Document 1 of 227

Kingston provincial election candidates wage a winter campaign

Author: Ferguson, Elliot

Publication info: Ferguson, Elliot. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/kingston-provincial-election-candidates-wage/docview/3171101373/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Kingston+provincial+election+candidates+wage+a+winter+campaign&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=Ferguson%2C+Elliot&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: KINGSTON Regardless of the outcome, the Ontario provincial election will be one for the record books, if for the weather only.
The last winter election was 142 years ago, in 1883, and candidates have faced campaign tasks taken for granted in warmer months, such as walking between houses while canvassing and setting up election signs, which are more complicated by deep snow and frozen ground underneath.
"Campaigning in a winter election certainly has its challenges," said Green Party candidate Zachary Typhair. "The cold weather means fewer spontaneous conversations at the door, and some residents aren't home as often in the evenings due to shorter day- light hours. For volunteers, staying warm and safe is a priority, so we've had to adapt our canvassing plans. That said, it hasn't slowed us down - voters are engaged, and we're having great conversations about the future of Ontario."
"Campaigning in the winter certainly comes with its own set of challenges freezing temperatures, snow-covered streets, and unpredictable weather have become part of our daily routine," New Democratic Party Kingston and the Islands candidate Elliot Goodell Ugalde said. "Coming from a grassroots activism background, I've always believed in making our voices heard, regardless of the season. Our volunteers, many of whom share this same passion and experience, are well accustomed to demonstrat- ing and picketing in all kinds of weather. Whether we're putting lawn signs into frozen ground or organizing winter rallies, our team is Ino stranger to the rigours of outdoor activism.
"The shared experience of campaigning in harsh weather has not only built a unique camaraderie among us but also reinforced our resolve to work tirelessly for Kingston and all of Ontario," Goodell Ugalde continued. "Every door knocked in the snow, every demonstration, and every picket line is a powerful reminder that together, we can overcome any challenge and build a more resilient future."
"Trudging through the snow, shimmying across the ice, with jackets and boots, and freezing extremities: these are the re- alities of campaigning in the dead of winter," said incumbent Liberal candidate Ted Hsu, nearing the end of his third campaign for office. "That has certainly restricted the number of volunteer-hours for door-to-door canvassing or sign installation. Unfortunately, the candidate doesn't get to restrict his hours in the cold.
"But we also have some memorable stories to tell, like driving around looking for a lost red mitten, which we found, or the heroics of installing large signs in the frozen ground as quickly as possible. We've had to help a large number of people, who were out of the country, successfully vote by mail. We're also hearing a lot more mobility-limited people saying that they'll need help getting to the polls on election day."

Subject: Provincial elections; Winter; Grass roots movement; Snow; Elections

Identifier / keyword: kingston; regardless; outcome; ontario; provincial; election

Document 2 of 227

Healthcare, cost of living top Kingston voters' concerns

Author: Ferguson, Elliot

Publication info: Ferguson, Elliot. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/healthcare-cost-living-top-kingston-voters/docview/3171101352/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Healthcare%2C+cost+of+living+top+Kingston+voters%27+concerns&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=Ferguson%2C+Elliot&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: KINGSTON It's less about tariffs, and more about health care and the cost of living, said Kingston and the Islands provincial election candidates.
At least, that is what voters on the doorstep are telling candidates in this week's provincial election.
"I hear about personal experiences such as not having a family doctor, or high and rising rents, that reflect Doug Ford's poor record on healthcare, housing, and cost of living. Many have also shared their valid fears about Donald Trump's threats to Canada, and I think they are looking for a local representative who has the experience to fight these threats," said incumbent Liberal candidate Ted Hsu. "My conversations at the door lead me to believe that more people want to vote. Many people bring up not having a family doctor. That is not surprising because, across Ontario, 2.5 million people can't get a family doctor. People want a properly funded public healthcare system that prioritizes patient well-being over for-profit care. That's why getting basic healthcare right is at the centre of the Ontario Liberal platform."
"Kingston voters are increasingly anxious about the economic impact of tariffs worries about job security, rising living costs, and a general sense of uncertainty dominate conversations at the doorstep," said New Democratic Party candidate Elliot Goodell Ugalde. "Many residents are not only concerned that tariffs will drive up the cost of essentials like groceries, heating and housing, but they're also worried about the future of local industries.
"What I'm hearing is a demand for real, tangible support: a need to re-stimulate our local economy by putting money back in people's pockets. Voters want policies that directly boost their household incomes, whether that's through targeted support for trade-exposed industries, initiatives that create new local supply chains, or measures to ensure government spending prioritizes Ontario-made goods," Goodell Ugalde said. "Essentially, Kingston families are asking for a government can both defend their jobs and actively enhance their economic well-being and that's the government the NDP will provide."
"At the doorstep, the number one issue I hear is the rising cost of living. Rent prices are out of control, grocery bills are climbing and many people are struggling to make ends meet," Green Party candidate Zachary Typhair said. "Healthcare is another major concern too many residents are waiting months for a doctor's appointment, and some don't have access to a family doctor at all. People are also deeply frustrated with how little progress has been made on housing affordability, especially when it comes to purpose-built rentals and non-market housing.
"As a Green MPP, I would push for stronger rent control, including vacancy control, to prevent landlords from driving up rents between tenants, expand funding for nurse practitioners and pharmacist-led clinics to improve access to healthcare, especially for those without a family doctor and support investments in deeply affordable, non-market housing - including cooperative and non-profit housing to ensure people have stable, affordable places to live."
elferguson@postmedia.com

Subject: Cost of living; Rents; Voters; Elections; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Cost of living Tariffs

Document 3 of 227

Kingston's candidates can't ignore the looming trade tariffs

Author: Ferguson, Elliot

Publication info: Ferguson, Elliot. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/kingstons-candidates-cant-ignore-looming-trade/docview/3171100392/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Kingston%27s+candidates+can%27t+ignore+the+looming+trade+tariffs&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=Ferguson%2C+Elliot&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario election was called because Premier Doug Ford said the government needed a new mandate to face the potential trade war with the United States.
And while most of the candidates vying for the Kingston and the Islands seat in the legislature reject that reasoning, the looming tariffs cannot be ignored.
"Tariffs present a serious threat to our economic stability across Ontario, including here in Kingston," said New Democratic Party candidate Elliot Goodell Ugalde. "They risk disrupting local supply chains, increasing production costs, and, most worryingly, causing job losses in key industries. In Kingston, where local businesses and manufacturing sectors form a critical part of our economy, tariffs could lead to signifcant financial strain on families and workers.
"The best response to these challenges is not just to brace for impact but to actively counteract it by re-stimulating the economy - putting money back in people's pock- ets," Goodell Ugalde said. "This means introducing measures that support workers and industries in real-time, such as our proposed direct income assistance programs and strategic investments in local infrastructure By doing sO, we can ensure that while tariffs may create headwinds, Ontario's economy remains robust, and our communities continue to thrive despite external pressures."
"Frontenac County is the most trade-exposed county in all of eastern Ontario. We export billions more than any other region in eastern Ontario," added incumbent Liberal candidate Ted Hsu. "So Trump's tariffs could have a lot of impact on jobs here. Take Invista for example. The factory in Kingston is one of the only places in North America that produces a special material used in airbags for cars. That's just one of hundreds of parts which travel back and forth across the border in the heavily-integrated Ontario-U.S. auto industry.
"As the only candidate in Kingston and the Islands with business and international experience, I feel that I would be best positioned to fght for our riding," Hsu said. "Even the threat of tariffs causes business to slow down investments. This is already causing job losses. The Ontario legislature should be sitting right now to look at ways to protect Ontario's economy. Unfortunately, we are in the middle of the early election called by Doug Ford."
"Tariffs on Ontario goods would put good jobs at risk, particularly in manufacturing and agriculture," said Green Party candidate Zachary Typhair. "Kingston has a strong post-secondary sector and growing green industries, but tariffs could still impact supply chains and local businesses that rely on trade. Instead of relying so heavily on U.S. markets, Ontario should invest in local, sustainable industries - supporting Ontario farmers, strengthening our domestic supply chains, and ensuring we produce more essential goods here at home.
"As a Green MPP, I would invest in Ontario's manufacturing sector to strengthen domestic supply chains and create jobs that aren't dependent on foreign trade policies, expand support for local farmers and food producers, reducing our reliance on imported food while lowering costs for consumers, push for clean energy investments, including a local energy grid for Kingston and the Islands - giving our community control over electricity costs, improving reliability, and creating local jobs in renewable energy."
elferguson@postmedia.com

Subject: Supply chains; Manufacturing; Local businesses; Elections; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Supply chains Manufacturing Local businesses Tariffs

Document 4 of 227

NDP And liberal leaders rally supporters in kingston ahead of ontario election

Author: Kulchar, Maegen

Publication info: Kulchar, Maegen. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndp-liberal-leaders-rally-supporters-kingston/docview/3171100278/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+And+liberal+leaders+rally+supporters+in+kingston+ahead+of+ontario+election&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Kulchar%2C+Maegen&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The City of Kingston was a hot spot for political visits over the weekend. NDP leader Marit Stiles and Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie both stopped in the Limestone City to meet with supporters in a final push leading up to provincial election day on Thursday.
In a last-minute change of schedule, Stiles was dropped off at Springer Market Square and was met by a group of cheering supporters.
With City Hall and the outdoor skating rink behind her, she spoke about health care, affordable housing, hospital wait times and the need to overcome the liberal threshold in Kingston and The Islands.
"I'm on the road for the next few days and my target are ridings that we need to flip," Stiles said. "The reason we need to flip them is because riding by riding by riding across this province, we are going to elect New Democrats and form an NDP government that is on your side."
Kingston and The Islands has been a Liberal stronghold since 2018 and she said her goal is to turn Kingston and Islands from red to orange.
"If Doug Ford wants to call an election in the middle of a crisis, that's an opportunity to make a change in the province of Ontario, and boy, do we need a change," Stiles said.
"In Kingston, there are 20,000 people without a family doctor. Rent has increased by 40 per cent since 2018. There are 26 people sitting right now being treated in hallways at Kingston Health Sciences Centre, that's the new normal. Right now, there are 8,000 people using the food bank here and 30 per cent of them are children. That is the cost of Doug Ford," Stiles told supporters.
To fix those issues, Stiles said the NDP is going to fight rising costs, deliver a grocery rebate program so that people have money in their pockets and provide a universal school food program.
"We have a really great plan that is going to help out lower to middle income people who are struggling with the cost of food," she said. "We have a grocery rebate program which is going to mean that families get $122 in their pocket every month to help them with groceries and foods."
By her side was Kingston and The Islands candidate Elliot Goodell Ugalde, who emphasized the need for helping Kingstonians.
"The Ontario Federation of Labour said that 13.3 per cent of Kingstonians live below the poverty line. People are upset, people have said that the average wait time at (Kingston General Hospital) is 22 hours. Wolfe Islanders, now that they've removed the paramedic service from that island and they've cut the amount of ferries that go back and forth. If you have a medical emergency and you live on Wolfe Island, you first have to wait for a ferry, which again wait times have been cut in half and when you make it to KGH you have to wait 22 hours just to be in a hallway," said Goodell Ugalde.
In the midst of when post secondary institutions such as St. Lawrence College are cutting programs, she vowed to invest in that level of education to help support jobs, the economy and communities.
Affordable housing is also top of mind for many people and rent control is one aspect her party plans to take control of.
"It's gotten completely out of control and people are losing their homes and that's why we are seeing so many people out on the street living in encampments," Stiles said. "We've got to bring back real rent control. The other thing we need to do build truly affordable housing. That means working with builders, working with municipalities, working with all the non-profits."
If elected, Stiles said her 100-day promise would be rent control and supportive housing.
Meanwhile, at Ted Hsu's campaign office on Princess Street, Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie laid out her political platform to supporters and said Ontario needs a leader who is accountable.
"The Toronto Star talked about a woman from Kingston that she waited 40 hours to get a hospital bed," Crombie said.
She pointed out that the woman with metastasized breast cancer waited in the hospital's emergency room, "vulnerable to whatever might be contagious in the ER room." "That's the state of Doug Ford's Ontario, even here in Kingston," Crombie said. "We want to redevelop the Kingston General Hospital, that's a big priority for Ted and myself. Affordability is key no matter where you are. It's all about health care, making life more affordable for people and investing in education," Crombie said.
Crombie visited Pan Chancho Bakery with Hsu prior to the rally and said their plan is to cut taxes for small business owners and reduce income tax for Ontarians.
"The first $50,000 to $75,000, we are going to reduce that," Crombie said. "Plus, we are going to take the HST off your home heating and hydro. Between those two things we are going to save you $1,150 a year, that's better than the $200 cheque that you got once," said Crombie.
Affordable housing was also on the list of promises by the Liberals.
"We're going to take off hidden taxes called development charges. That will reduce the price of homes by $170,000," Crombie said. "We'll remove the land transfer tax for anyone who is a first-time home buyer, a senior, a not-for-profit. We're going to fix the backlog in the Landlord and Tenant Board, and we're going to have phased in rent control."
Housing and health care are priorities in Kingston, Hsu said.
"Housing has been top ofmindin Kingston for almost 10 years, but then health care, we've made some progress I would say, but there is still a lot to go," he said. "For example, primary care, there's a lot of retirements coming sO we have to replace those family doctors. We still have days where ambulances are lined up at emergency. It's recruitment and retention. Some of the retention is making sure that the wages are competitive. So it's wages and working conditions.
"It's about getting the basics right," Crombie said. "It's very tough to attract investmentinto Ontarioi the company that's coming in, their employees can't afford the housing, their track to traffic gridlock, can't get a family doctor and can't afford groceries. So it's very tough."
Both Stiles and Crombie visited Kingston during the last weekend of advanced polls, which in Kingston drew in 11,000 voters.
mkulchar@postmedia.com

Subject: Islands; Hospitals; Emergency medical care; Provincial elections; Affordable housing; Rent control

Business indexing term: Subject: Hospitals Affordable housing Rent control

Document 8 of 227

Kingston and the Islands Liberal candidate Ted Hsu

Author: Ferguson, Elliot

Publication info: Ferguson, Elliot.

Document 9 of 227

Kingston and the Islands riding profile

Author: Ferguson, Elliot

Publication info: Ferguson, Elliot. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/kingston-islands-riding-profile/docview/3169625862/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Kingston+and+the+Islands+riding+profile&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Ferguson%2C+Elliot&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: WHERE IS THE KINGSTON AND THE ISLANDS RIDING?
Kingston and the Islands includes the portion of the city of Kingston south of Hwy. 401 and the Township of Frontenac Islands.
WHAT IS THE ELECTORAL HISTORY OF THIS RIDING?
Kingston and the Islands has, with one exception, been a solid Liberal riding for more than 30 years.
That exception was in 2018, when Ian Arthur of the New Democratic Party unseated incumbent Liberal Sophie Kiwala.
A 12-per-cent swing in votes from the Liberals to the NDP propelled Arthur to victory in 2018. The NDP vote in the riding surged by almost 10 per cent from 2014 levels, and the Liberal vote fell by more than 14.5 per cent. Prior to 2018, it was 1990 when the NDP last won here. Between those two elections was a string of six elections over 28 years won by Liberals John Gerretsen and Kiwala.
The PCs last won the riding in 1981.
In 2022, former MP for the riding Hsu retook the riding of the Liberals, recovering more than two thirds of the vote percentage the party lost in 2018.
WHO ARE THE CANDIDATES RUNNING IN KINGSTON AND THE ISLAN DS?
The riding of Kingston and the Islands has six candidates regis- tered for the Feb. 27 election. They include, in alphabetical order, Ian Chapelle of the Progressive Conservative Party of Ontario, Elliot Goodell Ugalde of the Ontario New Democratic Party, incumbent Ted Hsu of the Ontario Liberal Party, James McNair of the None of the Above Direct Democracy Party, Zachary Typhair of the Green Party of Ontario and Allan Wilson of the Ontario Party.

Document 10 of 227

Ford's hot-mic moments revealing, not endearing

Author: Baranyai, Robin

Publication info: Baranyai, Robin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-hot-mic-moments-revealing-not-endearing/docview/3169623529/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+hot-mic+moments+revealing%2C+not+endearing&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Baranyai%2C+Robin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Twenty years ago, the future King Charles was caught on a hot mic during a ski trip with his sons, William and Harry. Sitting for a few photos, the prince bristled at a question about his upcoming wedding, muttering: "Bloody people. I can't bear that man."
The unscripted moment of irritation was broadcast around the world. Instead of sparking a public relations crisis, people found it relatable. Endearing, even.
Doug Ford doesn't need help being relatable; the folksy premier speaks from the heart and governs with his gut, searching for evidence later. A smattering of hot-mic mishaps on the campaign trail have revealed the conservative leader in his less-guarded moments. The results are not endearing.
Ford likes to position himself as the leader who can deliver us from U.S. President Donald Trump. The entire rationale for this preposterously early election rests on the shaky premise that, with 15 months left in his term, Ford needed a new mandate to defend Ontario against Trump's threatened tariffs.
Set aside, for the moment, that every other premier in Canada is working to address the same threat, without the self-imposed distraction of running for re-election. Ford's tough-on-Trump credibility took a hit early in the campaign, when the premier was taped backstage chatting about the U.S. election. "Was I happy this guy won? One hundred per cent I was," he said, until the mercurial president "pulled out the knife."
Ten days later, a video surfaced from a London police chiefs gala. In it, Ford mused about bringing back the death penalty (abolished a half-century ago) for deadly home invasions. "I send 'em right to sparky," the premier boasted.
The law-and-order audience of about 1,000 people probably seemed like a safe bet for Ford's tough-guy rhetoric. Instead, the comment drew audible gasps.
It took six hours for Ford's campaign to walk back the remarks, calling the electrocution reference a "poortaste joke."
That's plausible, right? Ford's closed-door campaign speech had all the hallmarks of comedy.
A standard three-part joke contains a setup, a build and a punchline. Ford set up his remarks: "I was warned by my campaign, 'Do not go off script.' ... Well, that's just not who I am." The premier then described a four-part framework of escalating punishments, starting with 10 years "automatic" for a break-in, and building to "sparky." Classic.
A punchline is usually the end of a joke, but Ford kept the gag going. "Hopefully when a new government comes in, I think the provinces should take over some of the Criminal Code," he said, displaying the same masterful grasp of civics that has propelled the premier's for- ays into municipal bike lanes and federal interest rates set by the Bank of Canada.
"We need to straighten out these criminals ... Sorry for sidetracking, I just had to vent a little bit," Ford said. What a kidder!
The incident was an apt reminder, the man in front of the cameras doesn't always match the man behind the scenes. It echoed the infamous hot-mic mishap during Ford's first campaign for premier, in 2018. During a secret meeting with developers, Ford promised: "We're going to open up the Greenbelt for development. Not all of it. We're going to open a big chunk of it up, and we're gonna start building."
Facing significant voter pushback, Ford pledged to leave the Greenbelt untouched; however, once in office, the story changed.
Ontario's Feb. 27 election conveniently comes before the results of the RCMP's criminal investigation into the $8.28 billion land-swap scandal that would have benefited select developers. No joke. write.robin@baranyai.ca

Subject: Political campaigns; Scandals

Location: United States--US

What you need to know if you vote in the LanarkFrontenac-Kingston riding; Ontario election 2025 Seven candidates have registered to run in the riding in the Feb. 27 provincial election

Author: Duffy, Andrew

Publication info: Duffy, Andrew. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/what-you-need-know-if-vote-lanarkfrontenac/docview/3169623494/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+you+need+to+know+if+you+vote+in+the+LanarkFrontenac-Kingston+riding%3B+Ontario+election+2025+Seven+candidates+have+registered+to+run+in+the+riding+in+the+Feb.+27+provincial+election&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Duffy%2C+Andrew&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: LANARK-FRONTENAC-KINGSTON WHERE IS THE LANARK-PRONTENACKINGSTON RIDING?
Lanark-Frontenac-Kingsto is a large geographical riding west of Ottawa stretching from the Madawaska River in the north to Highway 401 in the south, from Pakenham in the east to Harlowe in the west. It encompasses much of the Ottawa Valley. Mostly rural, the riding includes the towns of Pakenham, Almonte, Mississippi Mills, Carleton Place, Smiths Falls and Perth and that portion of the City of Kingston north of Highway 401.
WHAT IS THE ELECTORAL HISTORY OF THIS RIDING?
Progressive Conservative John Jordan won the riding in 2022 with 50 per cent of the vote, more than doubling the vote count of his nearest rival.
His victory maintained the Tories' two-decade hold on the seat. The Liberals last won the riding in 2003, when it was known as Hastings-Frontenac-Lennox and Addington.
For 15 years before Jordan's victory, the riding was the political home of Randy Hillier, a controversial and outspoken MPP who was suspended from the Tory caucus by Premier Doug Ford in February 2019. Hillier, a brash advocate oflandowners' rights, later sat as an independent, fought against government measures to control the COVID-19 pandemic and embraced the truckers' occupation of downtown Ottawa.
Hillier was charged with nine criminal offences related to his actions during the February 2022 convoy protest, but those charges were thrown out of court in November 2024, when a judge ruled the case had taken too long to reach trial. The Crown has since appealed that ruling.
WHO ARE THE CANDIDATES RUNNING IN LANARKPRONTENAC-KINGSTON Progressive Conservative John Jordan, the incumbent, served as parliamentary secretary to the minister of agriculture in the Ford government. Before entering politics, Jordan was a health administrator, most recently serving as CEO of ConnectWell Community Health.
Liberal Party candidate Rob Rainer was elected reeve of Tay Valley Township in 2022 after first being elected as a councillor in 2018. He previously worked in leadership roles in the non-profit sector and as a consultant, letter carrier and outdoor education instructor.
New Democratic Party candidate John MacRae is a federal public servant with Health Canada who grew up in Lanark County. He graduated from the University of Ottawa with a Bachelor of Science degree and first sought the riding's NDP nomination in 2022.
Green Party candidate Marlene Spruyt is a retired family doctor and public health executive. Before leaving medicine, Spruyt served as medical officer of health and chief executive officer at Algoma Public Health. Spruyt, who serves as her party's health critic, ran in the same riding during the last provincial election in 2022.
David Motton is the candidate for the New Blue Party, which bills itself as a party dedicated to ending "woke" activism through the elimination of critical race theory, gender identity theory and all diversity, equity, and inclusion initiatives by government. The party wants to take down wind turbines and cancel all electrical vehicle subsidies.
Shane O'Neill is running as an independent candidate. A freelance researcher and writer, O'Neilllives in Carleton Place with his wife and children; he describes himself as a blood donor and an avid paddler and skier. O'Neill says he wants to give voice to young families looking for a safe place to live with hospitals and schools that are adequately staffed.
Wendy Dillistone- Whitaker is the candidate for the Ontario Party, whose slogan is "freedom, family and faith." The party says every citizen has an inalienable right to life, liberty and private property. Its platform espouses balanced budgets, small government, and rejects what it calls "the divisive politics of enVy, resentment, group identity, and ethnic exceptionalism."
Our website is your destination for up-to-the-minute news, so make sure to bookmark our homepage and sign up for our newsletters so we can keep you informed. QUICK FACTS Population: 111,424 (2021) Size: 7,063 square kilometres Population density: 17.5 per square kilometre Median household income: $90,000 (2021) Median age: 49.6 years Languages spoken: Englishonly 95,825, French-only 60, 13,000-plus both English and French, 100 neither English nor French.

Document 12 of 227

Kingston and the Islands Green Party candidate Zachary Typhair

Author: Ferguson, Elliot

Publication info: Ferguson, Elliot. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/kingston-islands-green-party-candidate-zachary/docview/3169615589/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Kingston+and+the+Islands+Green+Party+candidate+Zachary+Typhair&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Ferguson%2C+Elliot&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: More than a decade ago, Kingston and the Islands was one of the ridings where the Ontario Green Party had high hopes of getting elected.
With Bridget Doherty and Robert Kiley, two candidates who would later go on to win city council seats, the party peaked with more than eight per cent of the vote in the 2007 election.
In 2014, Kiley won more than seven per cent of the vote.
But despite the hopes, the local candidates did not break out of single digit vote percentages, let alone challenge for the win.
Zachary Typhair, the Green Party's candidate this time around, worked on all of those campaigns and said the party's message is still resonating with Kingston voters.
"It's incredible, actually," he said. "We're getting support across the political spectrum right now at the doors, and I think it's because conservatives don't feel like they're at home.
"The Ford government lost $340 million from the federal funding because they couldn't hit their housing targets. That's not responsible."
Typhair said the Green Party's environmental message remains the same, albeit with a few tweaks to address the current political landscape.
With a looming trade war with the United States, a threat Premier Doug Ford used as the pretext for the entire election, Typhair said the Green's environmentalist roots can help protect Ontario from tariffs.
"The Greens are going to invest into local," he said. "We're going to ensure that local business can get ahead because right now we're supporting big corporations and that's what you get with the other parties.
"We're the only party calling to break up the grocery monopolies. You want to know why food prices are going up? It's because we allow these monopolies to take control, declare what the price is, and small business owners can't compete."
Typhair said the party pledged to make it easier to build four-unit housing developments without needing to go through a lengthy zoning process, something many politicians and municipalities have been moving toward.
"It's definitely slow work," Typhair said of efforts to create more housing.
"You've got the not-in-my-backyard people, and I think climate groups tend to not want to build within the city," he said. "The choices are simple. You're either building within the city or you're continuing the sprawl, and as Greens, we're not anti-development. We're pro-development when it's sustainable and the more affordable choice."
elferguson@postmedia.com

Document 13 of 227

Kingston and the islands liberal candidate ted hsu

Author: Ferguson, Elliot

Publication info: Ferguson, Elliot. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/kingston-islands-liberal-candidate-ted-hsu/docview/3169615492/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Kingston+and+the+islands+liberal+candidate+ted+hsu&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Ferguson%2C+Elliot&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Like many opponents of the current Ontario government, incumbent Liberal candidate Ted Hsu has a pretty blunt assessment of why he and thousands of others are campaigning through a cold and snowy February.
"I think we're here because it's advantageous for Doug Ford to get more years in power by calling an election right now," Hsu said during a break in door to door canvassing. "Some people have sort of been influenced by what he says, that he needs this longer mandate and I can tell that because I knock on doors and people will tell me that directly."
But Hsu, a former MP for Kingston and the Islands and now seeking re-election for his second term as MPP, said Premier Doug Ford's election call was unnecessary.
Hsu was quick to point out that in the past eight months, the legislature has been in session for only seven weeks.
"If Doug Ford wasn't making use of his MPPs, why does he need a new mandate with new MPPs," he asked. "All three opposition parties, Green, Liberal and NDP, we've all pledged to work together, to stand together, to fight the Donald Trump's tariffs."
"Ontario is the only province that's having an election right now, so why does Doug Ford need a mandate when the other provinces aren't calling an election to do the same?" Instead, Hsu said, the election should be about issues closer to home.
"A lot of people are worried about our health-care system. They or their family or friends have experienced delays at the emergency room or they're waiting for surgery or diagnostics or they don't have a family doctor, they don't have primary care.
"The second thing that I hear a lot of is just the high cost of living," he said. "The high cost of rent, high cost of buying a house is very high on people's list, you just hear it over and over again.
"I have a volunteer on my campaign who's on Ontario Disability Support," Hsu said. "He shows me on his phone and how much he has left after the rent is is deducted. It's about $160 for the whole month.
"So we've known for a long time that Ontario disability support program has fallen far behind inflation."
Hsu said among the Liberal Party's campaign promises is to double ODSP payments.
Hsu served one term as MP for the riding from 2011 to 2015 and was then elected as MPP in 2022. elferguson@postmedia.com

Document 14 of 227

Ford close to his third majority

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-close-his-third-majority/docview/3169615417/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+close+to+his+third+majority&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: This is the third in a series of profiles of the leaders of Ontario's three main parties JOSEPH BREAN Eventually, voters get sick of politicians.
For Ontario Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, though, "eventually" is taking an unusually long time.
"He has a natural instinct that really serves him. He knows how to speak to people," said Geneviève Tellier, professor of political science at the University of Ottawa. "He doesn't have to listen to the comms team, he knows what to do, and it serves him well."
He has needed this instinct. Time grinds political popularity hard. Events dull the enthusiasm of even the most ardent supporters, while encouraging even the weakest opponents. Awkward details accumulate. Impatience sets in and change comes in due course.
But sometimes, an event comes along that is so disorienting it creates an exception to the rule that no Ontario premier wins three majorities in a row, not even Bill Davis, who won four elections but just two majorities, a decade apart (the '70s), or Dalton McGuinty, who won two majorities followed by a minority, followed by obliteration.
For long-serving leaders, then, looming catastrophe can look like opportunity. Ford's erstwhile federal ally, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, made a similar gamble in 2021, calling a pandemic election amid a major crisis with his opposition unprepared on the back foot.
It returned status quo, and was regarded as a failure to regain majority. If Ford achieves status quo in the Feb. 27 provincial election, however, he will have set himself quite apart.
"It would make Doug Ford one of the most successful politicians in Canadian history, period," said Tamara Small, professor of political science at the University of Guelph.
His opposition is certainly on the backfoot and in disarray, and has entered the strategic voting phase, with NDP votes bleeding to the Liberals.
"Ford is campaigning against Trump," said Elizabeth Goodyear-Grant, professor and graduate chair in political studies at Queen's University. "This whole looming trade war with U.S. has been handed to Ford on a platter," she said, and the fact that the actual tariff threat has been kicked just beyond the election "is such a gift to him."
Goodyear- Grant contrasts Ford's conservatism with American Trumpist conservatism as very different movements. Ford's is more like old-fashioned neo-liberalism, promoted by a dynast who presents to great popular effect as a roughneck.
"To me it's like George W. Bush. Like, why do you have a Texas ac- cent? You're from New England," she said.
"What it means to be on the right in Ontario is a much smaller narrower thing (than in the West) and I think Doug Ford is excellent at finding that balance," Small said. "He wants a relatively smaller government but he doesn't want a small government."
What this means in this crisis election, Small said, is that Ford is "playing out Ontario politics," re-establishing the Progressive Conservatives as Ontario's "natural governing party."
It is getting harder for politicians to get people to trust them. Voters are suspicious, often for good reasons. So one of Ford's strengths is "the capacity to reach out to people in a very open, honest way and invite them to trust him," said Cristine de Clercy, an expert in the study of political leadership and the inaugural Jarislowsky Chair in Trust and Political Leadership at Trent University.
She sees Ford as being in a race to the finish line to lock in his present popularity, to distract opponents and voters with "shiny things in the window" and "implausible" ideas like digging a tunnel under Toronto's main highway, which dominate the headline cycle and get Ford one day closer to victory.
"However they do it, it doesn't need to be pretty," de Clercy said. "Unhappy teachers, lack of doctors, universities in crisis, the actual tough problems aren't really the focus of the Ford campaign."
Ford's strategy, then, has been to sometimes say provocative things that sound outrageous to progressive ears, but which arguably put him on the side of popular opinion against an established legal and constitutional order.
Like "ol' Sparky" for instance, which is what he called the electric chair he wants to bring back to deliver capital punishment.
But that's easy stuff. That's grabbing a day's headlines for free. Ford doesn't control either parole board decisions or criminal code changes. He can fantasize righteous vengeance against murderers with no down side. On things that he actually does control, his approach has been more measured.
"I don't see him as a social conservative," said Goodyear-Grant. He added parental consent to sexed but the curriculum was more or less the same, she said. "He hasn't gone down the gender ideology rabbit hole."
He stepped into it briefly, with a speech that provocatively suggested teachers and school boards would "indoctrinate" children. But it came with political cover from other premiers pushing the same issue, he did not seem to do much about it, and the speech was read by many as an effort to change the channel from the escalating scandal about protected land being bought up for development.
So if progressive voters have a dastardly impression of him - and they do - it tends to be more for old-fashioned crony capitalist conservatism than for reactionary attitudes in the modern culture war.
Early in his premiership, for example, Ford gave loyalists and friends cushy jobs representing Ontario's business in Chicago, New York, and London, and even running the Ontario Provincial Police, then rescinded them all as it grew into a scandal.
The Greenbelt scandal fits firmly in that tradition, with Ford scrapping the plan as a "mistake."
"Walkback has been one of the themes of Doug Ford's time as premier of Ontario," Goodyear-Grant said, citing COVID restrictions as another example.
The curious thing, she said, is no other politician would have been given this kind of leeway to make mistakes and still take a third majority.
But Ford has a capacity to roll with his own mistakes before they cause him too much reputational damage.
"In fact sometimes it might play into this image of relatability. 'I made mistakes.' It grounds them," said Goodyear-Grant. "He's not unapologetic, but he's bold. 'Yeah that didn't work, I'm here now to change course.' Okay."

Document 15 of 227

Kingston's new democratic party candidate elliot goodell ugalde

Author: Ferguson, Elliot

Publication info: Ferguson, Elliot. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/kingstons-new-democratic-party-candidate-elliot/docview/3169273347/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Kingston%27s+new+democratic+party+candidate+elliot+goodell+ugalde&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Ferguson%2C+Elliot&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: K I N G S T O N After volunteering behind the scenes on several election campaigns, Elliot Goodell Ugalde put his name on the ballot for the first time late last month.
The New Democratic Party candidate for Kingston and the Islands, Goodell Ugalde is tasked with repeating the party's historic win here in 2018.
Born in Mexico, Goodell Ugalde and his family moved to Canada when he was four years old. After arriving, his family lived in Ontario and British Columbia, earning an undergraduate degree at the Uni- versity of Victoria and a master's degree at McMaster University.
Goodell Ugalde is now a PhD candidate in political science at Queen's University specializing in international relations and political economy, with a focus on health-care policy.
"It's different being in the spotlight, but I understand the sort of crises that this constituency is facing in terms of food insecurity, in terms of housing, and I do feel that I am the best prepared to advocate for it at Queen's Park," he said.
"I've been privileged enough to do policy work in a number of different constituents or constituencies, including in Toronto and I've seen first-hand the dangers of privatizing our public services and green spaces, as we saw with the Ford administration'sprivatizationofthegreenbelt, and I am scared that Ford has established a precedent that has made its way to Kingston and the Islands." Like many opponents of the current Progressive Conservative government, Goodell Ugalde said he doesn't accept Premier Doug Ford's decision to call a snap election in order to better position the government to address the tariff threat from the United States.
Instead, he said, the voters' focus is on more tangible issues, such as the cost of living, housing and the shortage of family doctors.
"Kingston is disproportionate- ly affected by the cost of food and food insecurity. We know that the city declared a state of emergenCYs that one in three Kingstonians struggle to put food on the table."
"I spoke with the executive director of the Food Sharing Project, which is an organization that feeds our school children, that their core provincial funding hasn't increased in 12 years, and that spans across two administrations, both the Liberals and the Conservatives, and that's quite shameful and that's part of the reason why I want to implement a universal food program for school children."
Goodell Ugalde said as a young person, and a student, he better understands many of the important issues facing the voters.
"Young people are disproportionately affected by the cost of living crisis. I'm coming at this as someone who will never own a home, who does not have a family doctor, is someone who, if I have an emergency, I will have to go to KGH and wait for 22 hours. That stunts my ability to continue going to work, which stunts my wage, which is again quite difficult when I'm struggling between paying my rent and putting food on the table.
"Ted (Hsu) simply does not have the insight into the needs and desires of Kingstonians like I do."
elferguson@postmedia.com

Document 16 of 227

Alberta's Smith pulls out of governors' meeting in D.C; Premier says she is putting the finishing touches on her party's upcoming budget

Author: Rahim, Mohamed

Publication info: Rahim, Mohamed. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/albertas-smith-pulls-out-governors-meeting-d-c/docview/3168913070/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Alberta%27s+Smith+pulls+out+of+governors%27+meeting+in+D.C%3B+Premier+says+she+is+putting+the+finishing+touches+on+her+party%27s+upcoming+budget&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Rahim%2C+Mohamed&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA Alberta Premier Danielle Smith is cancelling a trip to Washington, D.C., planned for this week, but says she's not giving up on her strategy of face-to-face engagement with U.S. lawmakers to head off the threat of tariffs.
Smith says she's skipping the 2025 Winter Meeting of the National Governors Association, set to get underway on Thursday, to put the finishing touches on Alberta's upcoming budget.
"We've decided to remain in Alberta this week to prepare for the budget that will be released next week," Smith said in a statement to the National Post. "We need to make sure Albertans have the resources they need to deal with the uncertainty we face from the tariff threat and the potential inflation that could cause."
Smith's cancellation comes one week after she joined her fellow premiers for a diplomatic mission to Washington that garnered middling reviews. The 13 premiers managed to score a White House meeting with two mid-level Trump officials, thanks to an assist from a well-connected lobbying firm, but the visit ended on a sour note when one of the meeting's hosts poked fun at the delegation on social media.
"To be clear, we never agreed that Canada would not be the 51st state," Trump aide James Blair said in response to a recap of the meeting attributed to British Columbia Premier David Eby.
The premiers' charm offensive also didn't stop President Donald Trump from singling out Canada repeatedly last week when announcing his plan for reciprocal tarifsf on America's trade partners.
"Canada has been very bad to us on trade, but now Canada is going to have to start paying up," Trump told reporters.
Trump repeated his canned line about Canada becoming the "51st state" at the Oval Office announcement, once again calling Prime Minister Justin Trudeau "Governor Trudeau."
Yet despite last week's mishaps, Smith is continuing to push for cross-border dialogue.
"Our diplomacy in the U.S. has been working and we'll continue to do that work," said Smith on Tuesday.
"We'll continue to meet with U.S. officials and I'll continue doing U.S. media to share our message with Americans that our economies are both stronger when we work together."
Smith added that she will be in Houston, Texas, next month for energy conference CERAWeek.
A source close to Smith who was not authorized to speak on this matter publicly said she's already built strong relationships with governors and other key state-level officials during previous visits.
Smith travelled to Las Vegas in December to attend the Western Governors Association Winter Meeting and signed an energy pact with 14 U.S. governors one month earlier.
Her cancellation means that just two premiers, Ontario's Doug Ford and Nova Scotia's Tim Houston, will be in Washington, D.C., for the governors' meeting.
Houston spokesperson Catherine Klimek said that the meeting presents a good opportunity to turn already wavering Americans against Trump's threat to impose tariffs on Canada.
"Many Americans are realizing the negative impacts these tariffs would have on their economy and Americans - we can't take the foot off the gas," said Klimek in an email to the National Post.
Jamie Tronnes, executive director of the Center for North America Prosperity and Security in Washington, said several governors will be disappointed by the low Canadian turnout.
"Governors are worried about how retaliatory tariffs on things like, for example, Kentucky bourbon, will affect their states and want to be seen as doing something about the issue," said Tronnes.
Tronnes added that some U.S. holiday destinations are already seeing a drop-off in tourism dollars from Canadian travellers.
"Just a 10 per cent drop in Canadian tourism would be a $2.1 billion (USD) loss to the American economy," said Tronnes.
"Right now, what we're looking at is a 25 per cent drop, depending on how things go and how long it will be sustained for." rmohamed@postmedia.com

Document 17 of 227

Clock ticking for Singh to prove himself; NDP dragged down by the supply-andconfidence agreement with Trudeau

Author: Taylor, Stephanie

Publication info: Taylor, Stephanie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/clock-ticking-singh-prove-himself-ndp-dragged/docview/3168136359/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Clock+ticking+for+Singh+to+prove+himself%3B+NDP+dragged+down+by+the+supply-andconfidence+agreement+with+Trudeau&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Taylor%2C+Stephanie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: to be a critique that has an impact on the voter themselves," he says.
"If Mr. Carney is out of touch with the struggles of everyday Canadians, it has to be put through the lens of how (being out of touch) affects them ... there has to be a little bit more depth."
Singh has been calling for Parliament to be recalled to pass a support package for workers and other Canadians who could be hurt by Trump's tariffs.
At the same time, he says nothing has changed about his intention to bring down the government at the first opportunity.
But with the possibility of an election call before that, Singh may have lost his chance. O T TA WA After Jagmeet Singh spent the fall struggling to prove he could be the change Canadians want, time is now ticking down for the leader of Canada's New Democrats to show he can offer it, against the man expected to replace Prime Minster Justin Trudeau.
For strategists and former staffers, including his past communications director, that means rethinking Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre as Singh's biggest threat.
"It would be worthwhile to spend some time defining Mark Carney before he is chosen as Liberal leader," said Melanie Richer, who left Singh's office in 2023.
"If he calls an election on March 10, it's too late."
That is the date Jennifer Howard, the NDP's national campaign director, gave in a memo circulated to candidates and campaign teams last week as to when they should be prepared to fight an election, should Carney, the front-runner of the Liberals' leadership contest, choose to trigger one instead of going to Parliament, once a leader is named on March 9.
Carney himself confirmed he was open to calling an early election.
Expectations that he may so do have only risen since polls began to show the Liberals surging in public opinion, the first signs the party may be climbing out of the hole it landed in under Trudeau.
Phillipe Fournier of the polling aggregator 338Canada.com said one survey suggests trouble could be brewing for Singh's New Democrats if they see their support drop even further as voters turn to a Carney-led Liberal party, which some polls suggest may be happening, despite the fact the contest is not yet over.
"The NDP would get really close to single digits," he told National Post in a recent interview.
The New Democrats are trailing both the Liberals and Conservatives and hold fourth-place party status in the House of Commons, behind the Bloc Quebecois.
Abacus Data CEO David Coletto says New Democrats "should be just scared completely" about the idea of their support dropping and agrees they would risk party status.
U.S. President Donald Trump's threat of a tariff war, coupled with Trudeau choosing not to seek re-election and Carney emerging as a fresh face, has left the main federal parties watching the political ground shift beneath their feet.
But even before that, the NDP was struggling.
Singh found the party dragged down by the supply-and-confidence agreement he struck with Trudeau after the last federal election in March 2022. In exchange for passing government bills - ensuring the Liberals' survival in the minority Parliament - the NDP saw some of their priorities enacted into law, such as dental care, pharmacare and rental supports.
There was some hope the party's fortunes would improve once Singh announced before the start of last fall that he had "ripped up" the deal, but those expectations never materialized in a significant way.
The reason? "They kept voting for them," said Karl Belanger, a former NDP national director and longtime political staffer who left Parliament Hill in 2016. "They kept supporting them and keeping them in power, so that distance that they were trying to create was never established."
At the same time, the federal Conservatives pummelled the freshly unshackled Singh with ads and attacks about him being a "sellout" by voting with the government, which the NDP argued was to deliver programs and help to Canadians.
An NDP strategist, speaking on the condition of background in order to speak freely, said when the Liberals "coughed up the ball" the NDP simply "didn't catch it."
Richer said the pact with the Liberals ended up costing the party voters who switch between casting a ballot for them and the Conservatives, in ridings where both are competitive.
Those who switch between voting Liberal and New Democrat liked the deal, she said. Now that those voters see Carney as the Liberal option instead of Trudeau, they appear open to leaving.
"Do I think that's a solid number? Do I think we've lost those folks for sure? Absolutely not," said Richer.
Carney is running his Liberal leadership campaign as an outsider, despite having advised Trudeau and served in a special role for the party.
Besides trying to appeal to Liberals concerned that the party did not prioritize the economy enough under Trudeau, the two-time former central banker is pitching himself to progressives who think the Liberals did not live up its promises on climate change.
Emily Williams, a spokeswoman for Carney's campaign, said he hosted an event with more than 1,000 people in Vancouver last week and staged another 500 person-event in Winnipeg and 400 in Kelowna.
Belanger said the NDP must stop Carney from painting himself "as Canada's saviour," when the country is anxious because of Trump.
"They need to go after him," he said. "They can't let him ... escape from his past, from his record, from who he is, from what he's defending, from what he's saying."
Belanger pointed to comments Carney made about bringing government spending into balance within three years.
"That's Liberal austerity, he says, "that's what's going to hit you."
The Conservatives, who have also seen some of their support slide to Carney, released their first ad against him last week, while the Liberals took out one against Poilievre.
Singh has so far attacked Carney along the same lines he has targeted Poilievre, saying both are leaders who choose to "stand with the billionaires" by making promises like repealing the capital gains tax hike, in contrast to the NDP, which Singh says stands with ordinary Canadians.
He also took aim at how Carney said he disagreed with the GST holiday the NDP helped pass, which ended Sunday.
Belanger says because Conservatives have been effective in attacking Singh for his expensive tastes, he believes it may be difficult for Canadians to see much of a difference between him and Carney on the issue of wealth.
"Jagmeet Singh wears a Rolex," he notes.
For Brad Lavigne, who worked as former national campaign director when Jack Layton delivered the NDP its "orange wave" back in 2011, Singh still has time to tell Canadians who Carney is "through a progressive lens" and outline why he is the wrong choice for non-conservative voters hungry for change.
"When you're defining your opponent, it is vitally important for it

Document 18 of 227

Over 2,000 graduate students at Queen's vote overwhelmingly in favour of strike action

Author: Kulchar, Maegen

Publication info: Kulchar, Maegen. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/over-2-000-graduate-students-at-queens-vote/docview/3168135940/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Over+2%2C000+graduate+students+at+Queen%27s+vote+overwhelmingly+in+favour+of+strike+action&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Kulchar%2C+Maegen&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Public Service Alliance of Canada (PSAC) Local 901 Unit 1, which represents over 2,000 graduate student workers at Queen's University, have secured both the highest turnout and strongest strike mandate in the union's history.
According to a news release, the potential job action directly impacts teaching assistants, research assistants, and teaching fellows, raising significant concerns about potential disruptions to tutorials, research activities, and teaching at Queen's University if members' demands are not met.
Members are fighting for fair wages and funding, tuition minimization, increased access to affordable housing, paid time to learn course content, and an equitable funding and labour balance. Graduate students work with some of the university's 26,000 undergraduate students.
"Our members voted yes in a strike vote. It is time for the employer to say yes to our members' demands. Our strike captains are ready," said Milka Njoroge and Justyna Szewczyk El-Jassem, members of PSAC 901 Unit 1 Bargaining Team.
The bargaining team said they are frustrated over what they perceive as continuous disrespect from Queen's administration at the bargaining table. They said despite the involvement of a Ministry of Labour-appointed conciliator, the university has repeatedly disregarded critical issues such as poverty wages, funding packages that have remained stagnant for decades, and rampant food and housing insecurity faced by graduate workers.
"Graduate student workers urgently need better funding to cover tuition, housing, healthcare, and basic living expenses," said Elliot Goodell Ugalde, the Ontario NDP candidate for Kingston, Frontenac and the Islands.
Goodell Ugalde is also a PhD student, teaching assistant, and PSAC 901 member. "The financial strain caused by historically low wages and funding relative to the cost of living is unsustainable. By voting, we are standing together to demand fair compensation and the respect we deserve."
Queen's alumni Dave Thompson, and former PSAC 901 said "it's well past time the university made movement on tuition minimization and an equitable funding/labour ratio. I was a PhD candidate in 2007. My funding was $20,000 per year. Inflation has gone up, tuition has gone up. Rents gone up. I don't know how people can afford to live if they work at Queen's."
According to the Graduate Funding Guide from the School of Graduate Studies and Postdoctoral Affairs at Queen's University, a full-time PhD candidate receives a guaranteed minimum funding package of $23,000 per year, which is available for up to four years. For comparison, a full-time minimum wage earner in Ontario makes approximately $36,000 annually. There is no minimum funding support for master's students at Queen's and each student worker must pay back tuition to Queen's, which starts at $7,308.73 per year, depending on the program and immigration status. Most graduate workers do not take any coursework beyond the first year of a program. Typically, a funding package includes an employment requirement as a teaching assistant, research assistant or teaching fellow, requiring approximately 10 hours of work per week. The PSAC 901 Unit 1 Collective Agreement expired on April 30, 2024. According to the union, they formally notified the university of their intention to bargain for a new contract on April 9, 2024. However, they said Queen's administration did not engage in any negotiation until November 2024. In January 2025, the union filed for conciliation in hopes of reaching a tentative agreement. Unfortunately, Queen's representatives have rejected all monetary proposals put forth by the union's team, according to the union.
"We know Queen's admin is listening, but we hope they're taking this seriously," said Jake Morrow, PSAC 901 President. "The members have made it clear that they stand behind the bargaining team, and the community has made it clear that they stand behind our members in their fight for dignity, respect and fair wages. If we do not receive a fair contract, our members are prepared for a strike. It's still unclear if Queen's is as prepared as our folks are."
PSAC 901 is part of the Unity Council of labour groups at Queen's University, which includes members from United Steelworkers Local 2010 (USW) and various CUPE locals. USW 2010 is engaged in negotiations with the university, while five CUPE units secured tentative agreements hours ahead ofa potential strike earlier this month. Notably, USW 2010 held a strike mandate vote concurrently with PSAC 901 Unit 1, the first in that union's history.
Ifboth unions fail to reach satisfactory agreements with the university over the coming weeks, over 3,500 workers including academic assistants and support staff, teaching assistants, research assistants, and teaching fellowscould strike simultaneously, the union said.

Document 19 of 227

Waves of support, but no pipelines in works

Author: Breakenridge, Rob

Publication info: Breakenridge, Rob. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/waves-support-no-pipelines-works/docview/3168135779/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Waves+of+support%2C+but+no+pipelines+in+works&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Breakenridge%2C+Rob&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's sudden outbreak of pipeline fever shows no sign of abating.
An Angus Reid poll out last week showed a solid majority of Canadians in every province support the idea that Canada needs oil and gas pipelines running across the country. Support, as high as 89 per cent in Alberta, was even an impressive 74 per cent in Quebec.
It's remarkable the extent to which Donald Trump's tariff threats have shifted the conversation around this issue.
Underscoring that, federal Energy Minister Jonathan Wilkinson last week suggested Ottawa and the provinces discuss a possible oil pipeline to Eastern Canada - not a headline you'd have expected a few years, or even months ago.
Unfortunately, there's no actual major pipeline proposal to benefit. Nor is it clear how serious or lasting the Liberals' presence on this bandwagon might be. But we should welcome this potential opportunity.
If we're smart, and if the will is there, we can make some meaningful progress on diversifying and expanding exports.
Wilkinson cited Canada's "vulnerabilities" exposed by Trump's threats, including that Canada is "so dependent on the United States for the export of oil." He also noted much of the existing energy infrastructure that serves Canada does pass through the U.S., including Enbridge's Line 5 pipeline - targeted for shutdown by Michigan's governor - which runs to Sarnia, then through Ontario and Quebec.
If there's a strategic need to build alternatives through Canada, that's a different issue. An Alberta-to-Sarnia pipeline through Canada would be a difficult, costly project. And we'd be no further ahead in overall production or access to other markets.
Much the same could be said of the Energy East pipeline. Wilkinson didn't mention that project, cancelled before the Liberals could make a fni al decision - though it's clear they weren't fans. Now, though, there seems to be an openness to something at least similar.
To supply more crude to Eastern Canada, there are better options. Enbridge, for example, has been looking at expanding its Mainline pipeline system's capacity. Ultimately, it needs to make financial sense, but it would all be within the existing right-of-way.
No doubt, it would be helpful to know there's federal and provincial support.
There's a similar opportunity for exports. The Trans Mountain pipeline now offers an outlet to offshore markets, and may expand. A top Trans Mountain exec has said plans were in the works for projects that could grow the pipeline's capacity by as much as 300,000 barrels a day.
If everyone agrees these projects have value, they could happen rather quickly. In the long term, if there's lasting commitment to new pipelines, new projects could be proposed.
Whether we get to that point, though, remains to be seen. Rob Breakenridge is a Calgary-based broadcaster and writer. He can be reached at rob. breakenridge@gmail.com

Subject: Pipelines

Location: United States--US; Canada; Quebec Canada

Document 20 of 227

Ford's impact on education defiles Davis's legacy

Author: Baranyai, Robin

Publication info: Baranyai, Robin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-impact-on-education-defiles-daviss-legacy/docview/3167233390/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+impact+on+education+defiles+Davis%27s+legacy&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Baranyai%2C+Robin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Across Ontario, college programs are toppling like dominoes. The cuts are sweeping: 18 programs at St. Clair; 40 at Sheridan; 49 at Centennial; two dozen at Loyalist; and 55 at St. Lawrence - the biggest hit so far - comprising 40 per cent of its lineup. Seneca has shut its Markham campus. This month, Algonquin will ask its board to suspend 37 programs and close a satellite campus in Perth. Meanwhile, Mohawk College sent out its third round of layoff notices this week.
The loss of wide-ranging programs, including skilled trades, are raising fears about colleges' ability to deliver on their mandate to meet local workforce needs.
The cuts are a response to new federal caps on international students. The bigger question, however, is why Ontario's publicly funded colleges have been forced to rely so heavily on revenue from international students, who pay about five times the tuition of domestic students for the same programs.
In 2019, Ontario Premier Doug Ford imposed a 10 per cent tuition cut, then froze domestic tuition, hamstringing post-secondary budgets. Ford touted lower costs as a win for students and families. It's not looking like a big win now.
The results for cashstarved colleges were predictable, and predicted. In 2021, Ontario auditor general Bonnie Lysyk called overreliance on international tuition a "risky formula," noting it had swelled to more than two-thirds (68 per cent) of tuition revenue collected by colleges, provincewide.
The community college system was introduced in the 1960s by Bill Davis, then minister of education for the Progressive Conservatives. Davis championed new education infrastructure in response to the technological revolution and disappearance of lower-skilled jobs.
Davis went on to serve four terms as premier of Ontario. Though sometimes at odds with teachers' unions, notably weathering a one- day strike in 1973, he became known as the "education premier." He once said: "There is no more important commitment that a government can make than to education."
That doesn't seem to be the philosophy of our current premier, whose attacks on education funding and delivery have provoked wave after wave of job actions, and destabilized the college system built by his own party.
Across the board, the two premiers really could not be much further apart.
Davis did not live to see Ford's bold assault on collective bargaining rights in 2022, using the notwithstanding clause to overrule job action from Ontario's lowest-paid educational support workers. Ford was forced to back down under threat of a general strike.
Davis, who had played a central role in negotiating the notwithstanding clause 40 years earlier, was notoriously tactful after retirement. However, he already had broken his customary silence to rebuke Ford for abusing the clause, conceived for "exceptionally rare circumstances."
Davis also created the first environment ministry in Canada, and intervened to protect the Niagara Escarpment from commercial development.
Ford, by contrast, infamously tried to open protected areas of the Greenbelt for housing development. The abandoned plan is the subject of an ongoing criminal investigation.
Davis invested in transportation infrastructure, but sagely halted the Spadina Expressway, memorably arguing: "We must make a decision as to whether we are trying to build a transportation system to serve the automobile, or one which will best serve people."
By contrast, Ford's "more roads, more cars" philosophy reached its zenith with the pie-in-the-sky promise to build the world's largest (uncosted) tunnel expressway under Highway 401.
Notably, the two premiers never met. Davis made it clear their versions of progressive conservatism were "quite incompatible," biographer and TVO host Steve Paikin observed shortly before Davis's death. The education premier must now be rolling in his grave. write.robin@baranyai.ca

Subject: Foreign students; College campuses; Education; Community colleges

Identifier / keyword: ontario; college; programs; toppling; dominoes; sweeping
