Document 61 of 227

Canada's leaders united, kind of, on Trump

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadas-leaders-united-kind-on-trump/docview/3156853794/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+leaders+united%2C+kind+of%2C+on+Trump&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Measured but robust is how Prime Minister Justin Trudeau portrayed the response he and the premiers agreed to in response to the Trump tariff threats. Instead of competing news conferences, he sat down next to Ontario's Doug Ford and most of the other premiers to say they're united, kind of.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, attending virtually, wouldn't sign a joint communique, issuing a blistering online statement instead. She said there was a constructive discussion, but she couldn't back everything being discussed.
"Federal ... officials continue to publicly and privately float the idea of cutting off energy supply to the U.S. and imposing export tariffs on Alberta energy and other products," Smith said on X. "Until these threats cease, Alberta will not be able to fully support the federal government's plan in dealing with the threatened tariffs."
Smith's take saw Ford step forward and say everyone needs to put Canada first, not their province. "Protect your jurisdiction, but country comes first. Canada is a priority."
The whole country must respond, nut no one part of the country should bear the full burden, Trudeau said. "We will be equitable and fair but we ... will be resolute."
That Smith stood alone was surprising because Quebec's François Legault and Saskatchewan's Scott Moe had expressed opposition to energy being on the table. Saskatchewan, of course, exports oil, potash, uranium and farm goods, Quebec sends electricity to New England states.
Alberta, though, is in a different position. Oil and gas are Canada's biggest export to the U.S. by far. As Smith has noted, halting those exports would hurt Ontario and Quebec because Enbridge's Line 5 pipeline goes through Michigan to supply Ontario and Quebec with an awful lot of energy - and there's no way to shut off Michigan without shutting off Canada's two biggest provinces.
Still, Ford has a point in saying you don't show your hand when playing poker. While Smith's stand can be understandable, it weakens Canada's argument as a whole.
Ultimately, it's not Smith's call. Export tariffs and shutdowns are federal decisions. Even Ford or Legault stopping power exports is a federal decision.
Perhaps if the Trudeau government hadn't spent its time in office shutting down other export pipelines such as Energy East and Northern Gateway or saying there was no case for LNG exports, this concept would be easier for Albertans to swallow.
Right now, the federal plan is to wait and see what the Trump tariff threat looks like.
"It's too early to know what we will do. We need to know what Mr. Trump will do," Quebec's Legault said.
If Trump does impose a 25 per cent tariff on all Canadian exports to the U.S., Canada won't respond in kind. The plan is to start with targeted tariffs and build up the response over time.
That's a middle ground: Alberta's Smith wants no retaliation, Ontario's Ford would like a stronger response.
Bottom line, though, as Legault said, we don't know what Trump will do Monday and until that's clear, any response is simply guesswork. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Exports; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Exports Tariffs

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; United States--US; Michigan; Canada; Quebec Canada

Document 62 of 227

Man behind Ford's hat sees bump in sales

Author: Knight, Chris

Publication info: Knight, Chris. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/man-behind-fords-hat-sees-bump-sales/docview/3156679970/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Man+behind+Ford%27s+hat+sees+bump+in+sales&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Knight%2C+Chris&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The founder of the Ottawa company that made Doug Ford's "Canada is not for sale" ball cap has seen a huge jump in sales thanks to the Ontario premier's patronage.
The tale of patriotic haberdashery began less than a week ago, when Fox News host Jesse Watters grilled Ford on Canada's "problem" with America.
"If I were a citizen of another country and I was a neighbour of the United States," Watters said, "I would consider it a privilege to be taken over by the United States of America. That's what everybody else in the world wants."
When Liam Mooney, founder of Ottawa-based Jackpine Dynamic Branding, heard that, it raised his hackles.
"We thought: It's actually a privilege to be Canadian," he told NewsTalk 580 CFRA's The Vassy Kapelos Show on Wednesday. "We jumped up out of our seats - we run a strategic design and branding firm - and we thought: Why don't we put our skills to work right now and come up with a rebuttal to the MAGA hat and make our own way forward?" He added: "Within a week it went from concept to on the premier's head."
That path included a call Tuesday morning from the deputy chief of staff in the premier's office. "And they said: We'd like the premier to wear this. Can you get a couple?" Frantic manufacturing and back-and-forth calls led to the hats being dropped off at about 7 p.m. that night. The next day, Ford was seen sporting the headwear in Ottawa while meeting with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and the other provincial premiers to discuss Donald Trump's threat of tariffs on Canadian goods.
Mooney told the radio show that what started off as "a cheeky rebuttal" to Trump's signature "Make America Great Again" hat has led to some $20,000 in sales in just the two hours before his interview.
The hats are available at strongandfreehat.ca for $45 in both English and French editions, with the French reading: "Le Canada n'est pas Ã vendre." There's also a "premier edition," which is not a reference to Ford but to the addition of "1867" on one side of the hat. A winterized version (a.k.a. a toque) sells for $40. All styles come in both red and blue.
Meanwhile, over at trumpstore. com, the MAGA baseball hat sells for US$50, equivalent to about $72 Canadian. Also, it's out of stock. Postmedia News

Document 63 of 227

Will Ford call vote over Trump tariffs? Maybe

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/will-ford-call-vote-over-trump-tariffs-maybe/docview/3156264661/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+Ford+call+vote+over+Trump+tariffs%3F+Maybe&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford might be kinda, possibly thinking about maybe calling an early election, but don't bet on it yet. Yes, Ford has been toying with the idea of an early election since last spring, but he's never been committed to it. Now, though, he may have no choice.
At the end of December, I wrote that despite some top advisers pushing Ford to call an early election this spring, he wasn't sold. He saw the prospect of an early vote as opportunistic and an unnecessary risk that voters may punish him over.
Smartly, I finished the column by saying, "Of course, this being politics, things could change. So, stay tuned."
So what has changed in the past two weeks? The looming threat of devastating tariffs from the incoming Trump administration, which takes office in Washington next week.
Ford believes the tariffs are coming and they will hit the province and the provincial economy hard.
"They're targeting Ontario.
They're targeting good-paying jobs in Ontario, and I'm going to fight with every tool I have in our toolbox to protect Ontario jobs or protect Canadian jobs," Ford said, even if that means spending billions to support companies while the tariffs are in place.
"I think if it comes to it, and we have to spend tens of billions of dollars, we go to the people, let the people decide. It's their money. It's not the government's."
Ford said nothing has been decided and his closest political advisers say he's not ready to push for an early election over the Trump tariffs yet, but he may get there soon.
His opposition leaders at the provincial legislature both made pitches for Ford to work with them rather than call an early election. Speaking in Windsor, NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Trump's tariffs are a real threat to Ontario's economy, but that requires everyone to come together.
"We are ready and willing to sit down and talk about anything that it's going to take ... to keep workers employed, to keep people well, and to push back against this threat to Ontario," she said.
"I think it's absolutely critical. I don't think Doug Ford needs to go to the polls to get that."
Speaking to reporters at Queen's Park, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie also called for Ford to drop the idea of an early vote.
"Absolutely," Crombie said when asked if she would support the government spending huge sums to combat the tariffs.
"I've said from the very start, we need to collaborate, and we need to work together. We need a wartime effort."
There is politics at play here on this issue from both parties, but there is also a very serious issue that must be addressed.
Obviously, Ford is well ahead in the polls and going to the voters in February with a vote in March would be advantageous to him. The opposition wouldn't be able to attack Ford on the only issue that matters, the tariffs, at a time when they are all saying money must be spent.
There is also the fact that key Liberal campaigners, including some who helped Crombie win her party's leadership, are occupied with the federal Liberal leadership race.
For the NDP and Liberals, they wouldn't want to go into an election now because they are weak in the polls, don't have as much money as Ford's PCs and wouldn't be able to effectively attack the premier at a moment of unity. They also know if Ford waits until the fixed election date of June 2026 and the tariffs are still in place, the economy will be hurting, making Ford vulnerable.
Politics aside, Ford does have a point that if he's going to spend $80 billion to $100 billion over the next year or so propping up the economy, he should ask the voters.
"That's where we differ," Ford said of himself and the opposition leaders.
Ford said he believes you've got to ask the voters before you spend their money in this way. We will see if voters buy that argument, if we end up with a late winter election. blilley@postmedia.com

Document 64 of 227

Alberta is now the real threat to Canadian unity

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/alberta-is-now-real-threat-canadian-unity/docview/3156264394/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Alberta+is+now+the+real+threat+to+Canadian+unity&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For years, it's been assumed the biggest threat to Canadian unity comes from the province of Quebec. It hosted a separatist terrorist movement in the 1960s and is home to the sovereigntist Parti Québécois. It held two referendums on separation, the last of which saw the country hang together by less than one per cent of the vote. Today, the party tops the polls and is threatening to hold another referendum if it takes power next year.
But the game has changed, thanks to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and U.S. president-elect Donald Trump. The biggest threat to the integrity of Canada no longer lies in the east, but in the west - namely, the province of Alberta. A showdown is looming between Edmonton and Ottawa over Canada's upcoming tariff war with the United States - one that will start this week, when provincial premiers meet with the federal government to hash out a "Team Canada" response to Trump's threats.
The battle lines are clear. Over the weekend, Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly said that "everything is on the table" when it comes to countering Trump's tariffs, including withholding the four million barrels of oil Alberta ships south every day.
This caused a backlash from Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, who spent part of the weekend meeting with Trump at his Mar-a-Lago resort. "We won't stand for that and you should never, ever threaten something you cannot do," she said. Smith reminded Joly that western Canadian crude flows to refineries in Ontario and Quebec through Enbridge's Line 5 pipeline, which runs through the U.S. Threats to cut off oil are thus "empty," she added, and would fuel a national unity crisis.
Smith is not wrong. If the federal government isn't careful, it could inflame the "Wexit" movement, which, for the first time, would be feasible, because it could find a willing partner in Washington. Trump thinks it would be great if Canada became America's 51st state. But what parts would he want? Downtown Toronto, with its core of NDP and Liberal voters? The province of Quebec, which speaks a different language? Nope. The prize would be Alberta, with its oil and conservative mindset. And its border with the Northwest Territories. If Trump got Alberta, his next stop would be striking a deal with the N.W.T., then on to neighbouring Yukon, to increase the U.S.'s access to the Arctic and its undersea oil reserves.
And how would Albertans feel about joining the U.S.? In a December poll, 30 per cent said the province would benefit, the highest percentage in the country. Thirty-eight per cent of Albertans also thought the province would be better off as its own country, while only 35 per cent of Quebecers felt the same about their province. A separate poll, released Tuesday, found that when asked if Canada as a whole should join the U.S., 18 per cent of Albertans agree - far from a majority, but the highest percentage in the country.
For this, we can thank Trudeau and Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault. Over the last decade, they've been determined to put Alberta's oil industry out of business. Between their federal carbon tax, incoming emissions cap and general denigration of the resource as dirty and shameful, it's no wonder the West feels disrespected.
It feels like a rerun of Pierre Trudeau's infamous National Energy Program, which caused thousands of Albertans to lose their livelihoods and spawned the bumper sticker, "Let the Eastern Bastards Freeze in the Dark."
But Trudeau the father also was concerned about national unity and would roll over in his grave if his son's legacy was the breakup of Canada. As Trudeau the son prepares to exit stage left, our leadership cannot allow that to happen. Canada needs to present a tough, united front that respects all its provinces.
If we don't hang together, there is a real risk we will break apart. Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Decades

Location: United States--US; Canada; Quebec Canada

Document 65 of 227

The West emerges as Liberal leadership race winners

Author: Rahim, Mohamed

Publication info: Rahim, Mohamed. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/west-emerges-as-liberal-leadership-race-winners/docview/3156264379/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+West+emerges+as+Liberal+leadership+race+winners&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Rahim%2C+Mohamed&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The past several years have been lean times for Liberals west of Ontario, but insiders are hoping that a spirited leadership race that happens to overweight western Liberal constituencies could help the party rebuild bridges in the region, after nearly a decade of frosty relations under Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's leadership.
"I'm already seeing tons of excitement for the upcoming leadership campaign," said Kent Hehr, a lifelong Alberta Liberal and ex-Calgary MP.
"Liberals around here have been shell shocked for the last little while, but they're looking rejuvenated of late," said Hehr. "It's been nice to see." (Hehr resigned from Trudeau's cabinet in 2018 amid sexual harassment allegations.)
Vancouver-based Liberal strategist Mark Marissen noted that the party's leadership vote's rules, which give each electoral district 100 points, regardless of the number of members, tilt the system in favour of western ridings with smaller membership bases.
"The one thing that does happen is that you hear from everybody across the country ... every riding has the same weight in this leadership race. So you're going to hear a lot more from places where Liberals haven't been elected," said Marissen.
Hehr estimates there are 1,000 Liberal members in his downtown Calgary riding. He expects this number to grow as contenders make their pitches to voters in the coming weeks.
"If people get out there and hustle with a strong message, anything can happen," said Hehr.
Corey Hogan, a Calgary-based strategist and former executive director of the Alberta Liberal Party, noted that the leadership's race's biggest names so far all have roots in Western Canada.
"You have Mark Carney, Chrystia Freeland and Christy Clark, all three of them grew up out West," said Hogan. "Maybe there's something there."
Carney, a former central banker turned Wall Street heavyweight, is set to launch his campaign in his hometown of Edmonton on Thursday. Clark, after organizing a planned leadership campaign, announced Tuesday she had changed her mind, claiming her French was too weak. Freeland, whose disgruntled resignation as Trudeau's top cabinet minister in December preceded the prime minister's decision to quit, was raised in Peace River, Alta., although she now lives in Toronto's Rosedale neighbourhood. She is reportedly planning to enter the race in the coming days.
North Vancouver, B.C., MP Jonathan Wilkinson is also rumoured to be weighing a leadership run.
Notably, none of the major candidates expected to come from Quebec have materialized: Labour Minister Steven MacKinnon, Foreign Minister Mélanie Joly and Industry Minister Francois-Philippe Champagne have all declared they don't intend to run.
Hehr said the rules give an edge to better-organized campaigns with stronger ties to local riding associations.
"You have to have people on your team who can pick up the phone and ask 'hey, can you search your database for a guy I met at the gas station in Zama City, (Alta.)?'" said Hehr.
Ex-Vancouver Liberal MP and former federal cabinet minister Ujjal Dosanjh said that much of the blame for the Liberal party's collapse in Western Canada lies at the feet of Trudeau.
"(Trudeau's) really severed all of his relationships with traditional Liberals before he came in," said Dosanjh. "He had new people that he recruited and put into those positions."
Dosanjh, who had been an NDP premier of B.C. later tapped by Paul Martin to run for the Liberals federally, said that Trudeau purposefully marginalized party elders. Postmedia News rmohamed@postmedia.com

Subject: Prime ministers

Location: Canada; Calgary Alberta Canada; Western Canada

Document 66 of 227

Barring Alta. oil exports to U.S. would lead to 'national unity crisis': Smith

Author: Dawson, Tyler

Publication info: Dawson, Tyler. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/barring-alta-oil-exports-u-s-would-lead-national/docview/3155463363/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Barring+Alta.+oil+exports+to+U.S.+would+lead+to+%27national+unity+crisis%27%3A+Smith&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-01-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Dawson%2C+Tyler&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith says that if the federal government imposes an export ban on Alberta oil going to the United States as a retaliatory measure against expected U.S. tariffs it would precipitate a "national unity crisis."
"Oil and gas is owned by the provinces, principally Alberta, and we won't stand for that," Smith said Monday. "I can't predict what Albertans would do."
She warned that Ottawa would have a "national unity crisis on (its) hands at the same time as having a crisis with our U.S. partners."
Smith made the remarks during a virtual press conference with reporters, which followed her meetings with U.S. president-elect Donald Trump at his West Palm Beach Mar-a-Lago resort and his golf club over the weekend.
Mélanie Joly, Canada's foreign affairs minister, was asked on CTV's Question Period talk show on Sunday if Canada would consider cutting off energy to the United States in the event of a tariff war, and responded, "Everything is on the table." Canada is preparing a list of U.S. products against which surtaxes could be levied, should Trump bring in his promised 25-per-cent tariff on Canadian goods after he's inaugurated next week.
Canada made similar moves during negotiations in 2019 with the first Trump administration, adding more than $16 billion in surtaxes to a variety of American imports, including steel, aluminum, yogurt, toilet paper, dishwashers and various other items.
Joly's response echoes a pitch made by Ontario Premier Doug Ford, who threatened in mid-December to cut off energy supply to the northeastern United States, should the Trump tariffs become a reality. Smith has staunchly opposed such a measure but said Monday morning that if Trump imposes tariffs, there will need to be a Canadian response.
"We have to wait and see what he does, and then we have to have a measured and mature response to it that hopefully does not damage the relationship or damage Canadian consumers and Canadian businesses too much," Smith said.
Smith, who said she attended Trump's Saturday soirée as a guest of celebrity investor and former Conservative party leadership candidate Kevin O'Leary, described her conversations with Trump as "constructive," but also seemed resigned that, come Jan. 20, Trump will be going ahead with tariffs, regardless of what Canadian politicians do.
She said that she is "not expecting" Alberta oil to be exempt from the Trump administration's tariffs, although she remains hopeful that the United States will want to purchase more oil from Canada for its refineries in the Midwest.
"I haven't seen any indication in any of the president's public commentary, or even in the comments that he had with me, that he's inclined to change his approach," Smith said.
The Alberta premier has been on an extensive hearts-and-minds campaign in the United States, meeting with governors and appearing on conservative-leaning news programs. She has been making the case directly that U.S. tariffs would increase energy costs to American consumers. She has, however, faced criticism that by boosting Alberta she may be undermining the broader Canadian response to tariffs.
"I'm hoping that by talking about the importance of energy, that we'll be able to avoid tariffs on every Canadian product," said Smith.
Since Trump first made threats of tariffs on Canadian exports, which would end more than three decades of free trade between the two countries, Canadian provinces and the federal government have scrambled to respond.
Trump has spoken of a "trade deficit" with Canada, and claimed that the United States is subsidizing Canada. He also suggested the tariffs were a pressure tactic to stop the flow of illegal migrants and drugs into the United States. Smith announced a $29-million border security plan, which would see a team of sheriffs patrolling Alberta's border with Montana.
In recent weeks, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has met with Trump at Mar-a-Lago, and a deputation of cabinet ministers has also travelled to Florida to meet with the incoming president's transi-tion team. So far, there has been little indication that Trump has been persuaded to abandon his plan for tariffs, and it's all occurring against the backdrop of a domestic political crisis in Ottawa.
Last Monday, Trudeau announced that he would be resigning as prime minister, kicking off a race for the leadership of the Liberal party and proroguing Parliament until March 24. Smith has called for an immediate election to avoid what on Monday she called the "leadership vacuum" in Ottawa.
"We need somebody at the table who is able to engage with the (Trump) administration saying, 'You got a four year mandate. I got a four year mandate, let's deal.'And we don't have that right now," Smith said.
Canada's premiers and representatives from the federal government are meeting on Wednesday to discuss the tariff response. As well, a joint mission of provincial premiers will travel to Washington, D.C., on Feb. 12 to meet with Trump administration officials and talk trade.
The Canadian economy, being orders of magnitude smaller than the United States', will be far more harmed by a prolonged trade war than the American economy will be, Smith said.
Research indicates that Canada would see tens of thousands of job losses, contractions in the country's GDP and, in the case of retaliatory tariffs, an increase in the cost of purchasing American-made goods.
"I think the solution is that we find ways to buy more American goods," said Smith. "We know the president's opening position, we've got to try to find a way to dig down and get to a resolution. And I think that will have to happen in earnest once the inauguration happens on Jan. 20."

Document 67 of 227

Extremists target Trudeau and Canada

Author: Johnston, Geoffrey

Publication info: Johnston, Geoffrey. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/extremists-target-trudeau-canada/docview/3153962680/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Extremists+target+Trudeau+and+Canada&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-01-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Johnston%2C+Geoffrey&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The far right is a transnational movement that seeks to destroy liberal democracy by spreading disinformation on social media, smearing democratic leaders, and causing chaos.Prime Minister Justin Trudeau was relentlessly targeted by the far right from the outset of his tenure as Canada's leader. From 'F*ck Trudeau'flags on pick-up trucks to far-right social media influencers harassing the prime minister while vacationing with his children, Trudeau has taken a horrendous amount of abuse.
According to a report by CBC News posted on Sept. 14, 2024, "a website at the heart of an international Russian disinformation operation has produced more than a dozen articles about Canadian politics in an apparent attempt to undermine support for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and boost his chief rival, Pierre Poilievre." In addition, CBC reported: "The website Reliable Recent News has been identified by officials in Europe and the U.S. as a repository for pro-Kremlin articles that are distributed through a network of affiliated sites disguised to appear as legitimate news outlets."
The European Union has acknowledged that Russia is waging hybrid warfare against Western democracies. Late last year, the European Council adopted a statement condemning Russia's hybrid campaign against European countries, which includes: "information manipulation and interference, and attempts to undermine democracy, including in the electoral process."
Many Canadians, traumatized by the COVID-19 pandemic and brainwashed by online disinformation, seem to believe that Trudeau is responsible for all of Canada's problems, even in areas of provincial jurisdiction.
Given the unjustified vilification of Trudeau, it's not surprising that his life has been threatened. For example, on July 2, 2020, Corey Hurren rammed his truck through the gate at Rideau Hall and attempted to make his way to Rideau Cottage, where the prime minister and his family were staying. Hurren was armed with five firearms.
According to a Canadian Press story, the gunman told the RCMP at the scene that he intended to demonstrate to Trudeau "how angry everyone was about the gun ban and the COVID-19 restrictions." Hurren was sentenced to six years in federal prison.
The assault on Trudeau's reputation was sustained over many years, eventually driving down his popularity, culminating in his Jan. 6th announcement that he will be stepping down as Liberal leader and prime minister in the coming months. Without a doubt, his successor will be similarly targeted.
CONVOY In a 2023 interview with the Whig-Standard, former Canadian diplomat and Russia expert Chris Alexander said that the so-called freedom convoy that occupied Ottawa in Feb. 2022 and blockaded Canadian-American border crossings at Windsor, Ontario and Coutts, Alberta, was heavily influenced and funded by Russia. The Russians "very much wanted Canada to be distracted as their full scale invasion was unleashed," said Alexander, a fluent Russian speaker and former federal cabinet minister under Conservative Prime Minister Stephen Harper.
Unfortunately, too many Canadians, "including the current leader of the (official) opposition (Pierre Poilievre) failed to see this 'active measures,'as the Russians call it, for what it was," Alexander said. "They failed to see its connection to larger geopolitical events...I've have never seen a case where Russian influence operations had a greater impact on the Canadian political arena," he added.
"The Russians have the gloves off in this current phase of information war," Alexander warned. Their goal is to radicalize Canadian politics, disorient the electorate, and "prevent us from giving Ukraine what it needs to have victory."
"I'm proud of the truckers and I stand with them," Pierre Poilievre, then an aspiring Conservative leadership candidate, said of the Ottawa occupation. He did more than just express support; Poilievre also met with participants "to offer coffee and donuts to the convoy," Charlie Senack of the Manotick Messenger wrote in a Feb. 4, 2022 story.
Jean Charest, a Conservative Party leadership rival, had harsh words for Poilievre. "For months, Pierre Poilievre has supported protesters who threatened to overthrow a democratically elected gov," Charest said in an April 9, 2022 post on X. "Now he's attacking the legitimacy of the Bank of Canada. He is stoking the flames of populism. We cannot take this lightly." RT Last year, the U.S. Department of State issued an alert to the world about Russia's global covert activities.
"Today, the United States is designating three entities and two individuals for their connection to Russia's destabilizing actions abroad," reads a Sept. 13 fact sheet issued by the State Department. "According to new information, much of which originates from employees of Russian state-funded RT (formerly 'Russia Today'), we now know that RT moved beyond being simply a media outlet and has been an entity with cyber capabilities." The Statement Department also alleged that RT is "engaged in information operations, covert influence, and military procurement."
In Oct. 2024, while testifying under oath before the Foreign Interference Commission, Prime Minister Trudeau spoke about Russian influence operations in Canada.
"According to a translation of Trudeau's testimony, 'We have seen that anti-vax messages during the convoy, during the pandemic, were amplified by Russian propaganda, especially in the media of the right,'" reads an Oct. 19, 2024 Vanity Fair article.
"As the pandemic waned, the focus of that alleged propaganda shifted from opposition to COVID-19 mandates and restrictions to straightforward messaging in support of Russian president Vladimir Putin. 'We've recently seen that RT is currently funding bloggers and other YouTube personalities of the right, such as Jordan Peterson,'Trudeau said, referencing the right-leaning internet celebrity."
Earlier this month, Poilievre posted a video on X of a lengthy conversation he had with Jordan Peterson. They talked about various issues, such as racism, housing, and the supposed shortcomings of the Trudeau government.
Poilievre is the only party leader in Parliament who does not have security clearance.
In an Oct. 17 written statement, Green Party Leader Elizabeth May said: "The only way for Canadians to know that the Official Opposition has not been compromised through foreign interference is for its leader to seek and obtain top secret security clearance...Pierre Poilievre is the only person in a position to clear the air about the Conservative Party and any potential favours owed to foreign interests." EL ON MUSK Elon Musk, the tech oligarch who used his wealth and social media platform to help Donald Trump win the U.S. presidential election, attacked Trudeau at the end of 2024. "He's such an insufferable tool," Musk said of the Canadian leader in a Dec. 11 post on X. "Won't be in power for much longer." And on Jan. 7, 2025, Musk issued two posts praising videos posted on X by Pierre Poilievre.
Meanwhile, not only does Musk allow RT to post on X, he also recently shared a post by Ian Miles Cheong, an RT International contributor.
The leaders of France, Germany, Norway and the United Kingdom recently declared in separate statements that Elon Musk exercises too much influence over politics, NBC News reported on Jan. 6. For example, French President Emmanuel Macron reportedly took a swipe at Musk.
"Who could have imagined, 10 years ago, that the owner of one of the world's largest social networks would intervene directly in elections, including in Germany?" Macron said in reference to Musk's endorsement of AfD, a far-right German political party.
In the winter of 2023, three members of Poilievre's Conservative parliamentary caucus- Leslyn Lewis, Dean Allison and Colin Carrie- met and dined with Christine Anderson, a German AfD politician.
"Anderson visited Canada as part of a tour organized by supporters of last year's 'Freedom Convoy'protests near Parliament Hill in downtown Ottawa, which she publicly supported," reads a March 6, 2023 story from CBC News. "Anderson is a member of the European Parliament representing the Alternative for Germany party, which has been under surveillance as a suspected extremist group in Germany and is accused of downplaying Nazi crimes, opposing immigration and pushing anti-Muslim ideology." Although Poilievre condemned Anderson's views, he refused to expel the MPs from his caucus. CONCLUSION It is clear that far-right extremism and foreign interference in Canadian politics pose grave threats to our liberal democracy and national security.

Document 68 of 227

This was the moment Trudeau told us he was done

Author: Kinsella, Warren

Publication info: Kinsella, Warren. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/this-was-moment-trudeau-told-us-he-done/docview/3152310697/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=This+was+the+moment+Trudeau+told+us+he+was+done&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kinsella%2C+Warren&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau didn't decide to resign this past week, sources say. The decision was made weeks ago, in plain view.
And few noticed. It happened Dec. 18. On that date, Trudeau's PMO abruptly cancelled interviews with multiple media outlets: Global News, CBC, CTV, Radio-Canada, TVA Nouvelles, along with what was to be a joint interview with CityNews and Omni Television. The move was unprecedented.
Few Canadians understood the significance of that decision. But senior Liberals knew it almost certainly meant the government end of Trudeau's reign had arrived.
One very senior Liberal, who has years of experience with different prime ministers and their offices, said that is the moment when Trudeau had truly decided to go. "No leader has cancelled year-end interviews, ever," said the Liberal. "They're a tradition. They're important, because lots of Canadians watch them. And Justin cancelled."
Trudeau's behaviour in the days that followed did nothing to alter that decision, sources said. Trudeau made some canned remarks at the Liberal Christmas party on the Hill, and then just a few words to the media after he shuffled his cabinet for the last time. He then got on a Challenger jet and flew to British Columbia to ski.
Few, if any, heard from Trudeau during the crucial days when he needed to be working the phones to save his leadership. Trudeau mainly communicated only with the small circle who remain loyal to him, and with family members. Some urged him to stay and fight.
But Trudeau had no fight left in him.
Everywhere he looked, sources said, his prospects were bad and getting worse. President-elect Donald Trump was mocking him on the world stage, calling him the governor of the 51st state. Caucus members - including the crucial Atlantic, Quebec and Ontario caucuses - started to demand publicly that he step aside. And an Angus Reid Institute poll was issued, suggesting the Liberals had fallen to only 16 per cent support nationally.
That's not all: Liberal leadership campaigns started organizing, more or less openly, and talking to the media - anonymously, of course - about their prospects in the post-Trudeau era. Among them was Dominic LeBlanc, who Trudeau considers one of his closest friends, and Chrystia Freeland, the former deputy prime minister who had dramatically quit Trudeau's cabinet two days before he cancelled his year-end interviews.
"He's human, you know," said another Liberal insider. "He was down. He asked his circle (of advisers) if there was any way to hold on and avoid a full caucus revolt. They came up with nothing."
"It had nothing to do with (Conservative Leader Pierre) Poilievre," said one Liberal. "He still thinks that Poilievre is a pipsqueak."
But the parliamentary holiday recess was like bankruptcy, another Liberal said. The process of unravelling is very slow, and then it suddenly reaches its grim conclusion very, very fast.
"He knew it was all over," said the senior Liberal. "His kids were saying to him, 'Dad, it's time to go home.'" Don't now expect an avalanche of patronage appointments, the sources agree. That was the main factor that defeated former Liberal leader John Turner in 1984. Liberals have been mad enough at Trudeau as it is, they say, and he doesn't want to make them any angrier.
As for the future, Trudeau is not worried.
His family's wealth have made him a multimillionaire, and he will be paid handsomely on the book and speech circuit. He has no shortage of job prospects, either. Some suggest a soft landing at the United Nations, but other sources doubt that.
Whatever happens, Justin Trudeau has finally taken what his father memorably called a "walk in the snow." He loved the job of prime minister, and he felt he was doing right by Canadians.
But Canadians clearly felt otherwise. And in mid-December, when no one was really looking, Justin Trudeau finally took note, and made his decision. Warren Kinsella is a Canadian lawyer, author, musician, political consultant, and commentator.

Subject: Leadership; Canadians; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

People: Trudeau, Justin

Identifier / keyword: justin; trudeau; decide; resign; sources; decision

Document 69 of 227

Growing up Gerretsen; The life of Liberal MP Mark Gerretsen

Author: Murphy, Jan

Publication info: Murphy, Jan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/growing-up-gerretsen-life-liberal-mp-mark/docview/3151476167/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Growing+up+Gerretsen%3B+The+life+of+Liberal+MP+Mark+Gerretsen&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-01-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Murphy%2C+Jan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There is a rich irony that tails Mark Gerretsen wherever he goes. And the lifelong Kingstonian and longtime politician admits he has embraced it.
Gerretsen, the longtime Member of Parliament for Kingston and the Islands and former councillor and mayor of the City of Kingston, said he can well remember his introduction to politics as a child, through his father, lifelong politician and public servant John Gerretsen.
"My brother and I being dragged around from event to event with my dad because he would go to events at City Hall and Memorial Hall, or to a ribbon cutting," Gerretsen, 49, said during an interview at his constituency office just before Christmas.
Gerretsen explained that his mother, Assunta, had gone back to school when he and his siblings, a younger brother and an older sister, were children, meaning their father, then the mayor of Kingston, would bring the boys along with him to many public events.
"I always remember my dad being at events and my brother and I being there with him," Gerretsen said, noting that he and his brother weren't exactly willing participants in those days. "Quite often, I can remember us tugging on him, like 'Come on, let's go,'" Gerretsen said with a chuckle. "My dad was a talker."
The irony, of course, is that for the last 19 years, since his election to city council representing Portsmouth District in Kingston, Gerretsen has become a handshaking, public appearance-making, smooth-talking politician himself.
And while Gerretsen can recount those early days of coming to understand his father's important political place in the city, he said he can also recall the first time he realized not everyone liked his dad, at least when it came to politics.
"I remember the first time that I learned that there are people out there who don't like my dad," he said, remembering a trip to Lake Ontario Park one summer, where visitors could buy balls to throw at a dunk tank. Inside that dunk tank? You guessed it, Gerretsen's dad. "My dad was going to sit in the dunk tank and people would buy balls to throw at it to try to dunk him," Gerretsen recalled.
At the time, to a child, this seemed particularly villainous.
"As a six-year-old, when this was being explained to me, I can remember saying 'But who would want to throw the balls to make you fall into the water?'" Gerretsen said. "I couldn't understand. Of course, now I'm sure it was for charity or something, but for me, I think that's when I first had my first kind of political thought."
However, Gerretsen said he doesn't recall a time when he wasn't aware that his dad was pretty special when it came to his public service, even if he didn't comprehend what the meant at the time.
"My dad became a city councillor in 1972," he said. "I was born in '75 so when I was born, he was already a city councillor. By 1980, he became mayor, so I really grew up in it. We couldn't go anywhere, although it's much like me now, the irony, but we couldn't go anywhere without someone wanting to talk to my dad."
Looking back, Gerretsen recalled his father as not only an excellent public servant, but an excellent dad.
"My dad was really good to us," Gerretsen said. "Every Saturday morning, he would take us swimming at Artillery Park. We had some ritual kind of things that my dad always did with us. He always took us to our soccer games, soccer practices in the evening." But his dad always had to balance his public persona with his private one. "Whenever we were at any of those things, people always wanted to talk to him. There were always people talking to him. Then when you start to have friends, you realized wow, so and so's dad isn't always being talked to all the time, always being asked questions and approached in the middle of a Canadian Tire or something like that."
Growing up Gerretsen, of course, meant a lot of time out in public, attending events and watching father John prepare for and run in elections. In a lot of ways, even though Gerretsen couldn't have known it, he was getting on-thejob training that would one day prove valuable.
"My dad running in elections, absolutely," Gerretsen answered when asked about the exposure the Gerretsen children got to politics in their household. "I can remember when he ran for mayor, I can remember his campaign offices on Princess Street. The city was much smaller so I can remember those, and I can remember some of the names. And there's a lot of names, like Flora MacDonald, Keith Norton, Alex Lampropoulos, these names that just pop into my memory. I can certainly remember being aware of all of that."
That was outside the home.
Inside the family home, as the kids became young adults, the political realm would work its way into dinner table discussion on occasion, too, Gerretsen recalled.
"I'd say that as we started to get older, we became more engaged in political discussion," he said. "My sister has always kind of been kind of apolitical ... she never really took a side. My brother was the huge lefty, and I was actually more on the right side of things."
In those early days, Gerretsen studied economics at Queen's University and fancied himself something of a young entrepreneur.
"I would do things to make money," he explained. "When I was younger, I deejayed dances and high school dances and parties. I was much more conservative than I am now, like big C conservative, as in I would have identified closer to being in the Conservative Party then. My brother was totally the opposite. He was the lefty."
All that made for some lively dinner discussions.
"We would get into debates," Gerretsen said. "I can still remember, I would have been probably in my early 20s, at one Christmas dinner getting into a fight over something to do with the economy and the best way to move forward and I can remember one of my dad's cousins being there and he was like, 'OK, well, thanks a lot for having me over for dinner,'" Gerretsen recalled with a chuckle.
And while John Gerretsen pieced together an iconic political career as he and Assunta raised their children - councillor, mayor, member of provincial parliament, cabinet minister - never did he attempt to push his children toward public servant life.
"My dad has always let his kids do their own thing, and we totally went in different directions," Mark Gerretsen said. "My sister is a social worker. I was an entrepreneur and, to some degrees, still am. My younger brother went into film in and lives in Toronto. We're totally different, but dad always kind of encouraged that."
Yet, by the early 2000s, following some discussions with a longtime friend of his who worked for the Kingston Economic Development Corporation, Gerretsen said he began to hear his calling whispering in his ear.
"(We talked) about what was going on in the city," Gerretsen said. In those days, the discussions around the horseshoe inside City Hall centred around building a large venue entertainment centre and the refurbishing of market square.
"I started to hear about what was going on and my friend and I were having conversations about it, I remember we sometimes would get into it over emails back and forth and debates over what we thought should be done," Gerretsen said. Before he knew it, the aspiring politician within him bubbled to the surface. "I said 'You know what, I think I'm going to run for city council.'"Gerretsen said he isn't sure it was any one thing that pulled him into the public servant life, but more the overall feeling that he could effect change.
"I just felt like I wanted to do it because I felt like I could influence changes and decisions," he said.
To say his rather abrupt decision to join the political fray came as a surprise to his parents would be a huge understatement.
"The two people who were surprised the most when I decided to run for city council were my parents," he said with a chuckle. "A lot of people just said, 'Oh yeah, makes sense.'My parents were the most blown away. Neither of them saw coming from a mile away, because it was just never something that I had expressed interest in. As a matter of fact, people would tease me when I was younger and say, 'Oh, you're going to be the mayor too one day'and I'd be like, 'No way, I'm not going to do that.'"Looking back now, Gerretsen said he can admit that politics was probably always his calling.
"It's funny how when we're younger, we think we're going to distance ourselves from that, but we find comfort in it," he said. "I think after I ran in that election in 2006, I started to find the comfort of being in this environment. I was used to it, having witnessed my dad do it, and being around my dad. It felt like it wasn't something I was doing for the first time really, even though I was. Although I might have been doing it for myself, I had experienced so much of it in the past that it came very natural to me after that."
Gerretsen's decision to enter the political realm came quickly, and his first victory came in his first attempt (to date, he's been elected in every race he's ever entered), but he said he knew very quickly that he wanted to establish himself as Mark Gerretsen, not John Gerretsen's son.
"You accept the reality, which is that people know (you're John Gerretsen's son), but it's not always necessarily all good," Gerretsen said. "People assume 'Oh, well, you have a strong family name, Gerretsen has been a political name since the '70s in Kingston.'"Gerretsen recalled an encounter during his first political campaign in 2006 that is still vivid in his mind, when he approached a homeowner in Calvin Park during canvassing.
"To this day, I can take you to the house and point to the house and say 'It was this house,'where, as I was walking up the driveway, I said, 'Oh, Hi, I'm Mark Gerretsen and I'm running for city council,'and (the homeowner) said, 'What's your last name?'I said, 'Gerretsen'and she said, 'Get off my lawn. There's no way I'll ever vote for Gerretsen.'And that's not the only time it happened. It happened a number of times. So yes, there are a lot of people out there that have embraced me because of my father and the fact that they knew him or the connection, but I've also been on the receiving end of a lot of people who won't even give me the time of day or even the opportunity to explain myself before they cast that judgment. That's just something that you kind of live with."
Right from the outset of his political career, Gerretsen was involved in some very transformative decisions in Kingston, including the building of what today is known as Slush Puppie Place. At the time, then mayor Harvey Rosen was adamant on building the arena downtown, while others wanted it situated closer to Hwy. 401. Gerretsen recalled just how divided council was at the time.
"When I was on city council, the very first thing that happened after that election, Harvey Rosen got re-elected, but a number of anti-LVEC individuals were also elected," he said. "I can remember six of them coming to me because council was 13 people and saying, 'There are six of us that are ready to cancel the LVEC'-steel was sticking out of the ground at this point - and asking will you be the seventh so we can cancel it? I remember being like, 'Guys, they're constructing it right now right, you can't stop it now.'"The project was completed, as was the construction of the Invista Centre, the revitalization of Springer Market Square, under Rosen.
"These were all really important things that I think that our city needed, and our city really benefits from today," Gerretsen said.
"When I was mayor, I used to get phone calls from other mayors throughout Ontario saying 'How were you successful at getting your arena in in your downtown area? We didn't do that, and we should have, and we're thinking about doing, doing something similar now.'I remember giving the credit to Harvey Rosen, that man had a vision, and he knew how to execute and get there."
Another contentious project at the time was the so-called third crossing, now the city's recently constructed Waaban Crossing, which had been debated and considered for decades. Gerretsen was a councillor when the city voted to perform an environmental assessment and mayor when council voted to start acquiring the land around the site. Later, he became MP and played an integral role in securing the federal funding needed to make the bridge a reality.
"There were some struggles, there was some back and forth, it took a couple of years, but once I was able to say the province is now on board, we have to do this, (the federal government) agreed, and decided to put the money in," Gerretsen said. "I got to see that in three different stages."
Though his dad was convinced the third crossing would never happen, Gerretsen was a key player in bringing it to fruition and it remains one of the things he's most proud of in his political career. Another, he said, was his role in the city offering free bus passes to high school students.
Gerretsen, then mayor, recalled a conversation with longtime Limestone District School Board trustee Helen Chadwick about how both school buses and city buses were only half full and costing both parties money.
"I said 'You know, Helen, I've come to the conclusion that if you want people to ride the city bus, you need people to want to ride it,'" Gerretsen recalled. "At the time, the only people who took the bus were people who needed to take it, not people who wanted to take it."
Gerretsen suggested a plan where all high school students would receive a free bus pass.
"That way, they could get exposed to it, and then when they were out of high school, they could become paying customers," he said of his rationale at the time. It would take some of the burden off the yellow buses at the time. "City staff were pushing back, and they're like, 'This is going to cost X number of dollars'and then I said, 'Fine, let's just start with Grade 9. What will Grade 9 cost?'"Council started with Grade 9, then eventually offered passes to students in grades 9 to 11. Before he jumped from municipal to federal politics, Gerretsen implored incoming Mayor Bryan Paterson and council to continue to expand the program, which it did.
"That's probably one of my proudest moments," Gerretsen of the bus program. "That, and keeping the tax rate to below two and a half percent the entire four years that I was mayor."
Following his first term as mayor, Gerretsen said he'd fully planned to seek re-election until he received a call from then councillor Sandy Berg, informing him that then Liberal member of Parliament Ted Hsu was not seeking re-election in order to spend more time with his family.
"(Running federally) was not on my mind," Gerretsen said. "I was coming to the close of the first term as mayor. I was fully intending to run again."
In fact, by the time Gerretsen learned of Hsu's decision, he only had five days to declare his intention to seek the federal nomination "The deadline to put your name forward was in August on a Friday," Gerretsen recalled. "I think I put my name forward on like the Monday and I had five days."
Following Berg's call, Gerretsen said, he started getting calls from others encouraging him to seek the federal nomination.
"People started to call me and say you should put your name forward, you should run, and some of the liberal establishment, for lack of better term, who were around my father, were really encouraging me. I had to decide really quickly, because if I didn't put my name forward for mayor, then nobody was going have their name in. So by the Wednesday, I had decided I was going to put my name in to run for the nomination for the Liberal Party."
Gerretsen made the jump to federal politics, winning the nomination and the election in 2015. He has subsequently been re-elected in 2019 and 2021.
In his first term, he was successful in having the prison farms reopened, as well as securing funding for the third crossing.
"I would say that as it relates to accomplishments federally, that's certainly one of the ones that I'm very proud of, is reopening those prison farms," he said. "We know how the community felt about the prison farms and it was really important for me to push to ensure that they could be reopened and that happened."
He's been representing his riding throughout the construction of the Waaban Crossing, the COVID19 pandemic right and everything in between.
Behind the scenes, Gerretsen balances the private life of a public figure, supported by his wife, Vanessa, and their three children Mason, Frankie and Vivian.
"I'm absolutely the luckiest person in the world because I have a spouse who's incredibly supportive and picks up the weight when I'm not around," Gerretsen of Vanessa, whom he says keeps him not only up to speed on the daily, but makes sure he knows when his presence is appreciated and required at home.
But public servant life isn't easy, especially when you have a young family.
"It's tough," Gerretsen said.
"It's really tough. It's tough when you're away four nights a week, even with Facetime, it's not easy. My dad didn't get into provincial politics, where he was out of the city, until I would have been 21. We were all we were all grown up by that point. My kids were born into it. Frankie was born in 2016, Vivian in 2018, Mason, my stepson, he's about 10 years older than Frankie, so he would have been about nine. You don't get to be there for all the same stuff, right? Like taking them to soccer or whatever it might be, or taking them to birthday parties or stuff like that. So I make a real concerted effort if I'm in town and I get the email from a school saying that one of their classes are looking for volunteers, I make an effort of saying I'll do that because I know that I don't have a lot of opportunities.
It's tough, but having somebody like Vanessa to be there to help with that stuff and to do it, and she does it so graciously and without any kind of complaint or anything, truly is ... I'm extremely lucky. I couldn't be any luckier."
While the life of an elected official has its challenges, those are outweighed by the many wonderful experiences, Gerretsen said, rejecting the notion that politics is a thankless job.
"I think that it is still an extremely rewarding job," he said. "Yes, sometimes I'll be somewhere, I'll go into Costco or something like that, and somebody will say, 'Oh, that Justin Trudeau.'I've had people yell at me in a store, 'Justin Trudeau sucks!'and I'll respond 'Oh, alright,'but there are also a lot of people who come up to me and say, I just want to thank you for what you're doing. They don't yell it across the store, sometimes they'll shake my hand or whatever."
The positive moments far outweigh the negative, he said.
"There are times when I've seen lives changed," he said. "There are times when I've gotten to participate in swearing-in ceremonies of new Canadian citizens, and to see the expression on people's faces and see them start to cry because they've just obtained their Canadian citizenship. There are times when my office has been successfully able to help people get something done, whether it's with CRA or immigration services or whatever, my staff here get more people thanking them for the work that they do. They are often showered with appreciation for the work that they do, and that is extremely rewarding.
There are rewarding aspects to the job. I genuinely, truly mean that."
Nearly 20 years into his political career, and with a political footprint that in many ways rivals that of his father's, Gerretsen is asked if he has any regrets.
"I definitely don't regret coming into the political world," answered. "Do I have any regrets along the way? Of course, I've made mistakes," he added "whether that was getting into fights with the principal of Queen's over Homecoming or something I learned along the way."
He recalled an early council meeting when he was particularly hard on a city commissioner during a council meeting.
"I really went after her and somebody came up to me afterward and said, 'You know, it's fair that you want to ask these questions, but your tone was wrong, and you were not nice to her.'It took me aback, and I thought about it and I dwelled on it for two weeks, and then at the beginning of the next council meeting, I asked if I could apologize, and I apologized to her. I have regrets along the way, 100%, and I learned a lot along the way. I'm not going to, by any means, suggest that I'm perfect, or I've never made mistakes, because I definitely have. But the benefits have always outweighed any of those regrets."
Gerretsen also believes he's carved out a name for himself politically, separate of that of his father's.
"One of my colleagues from the Barrie area a couple years ago, he's conservative, was sitting on the other side of the house and he said 'I knew your dad when he was the minister of municipal affairs and housing, you're nothing like your dad,'" Gerretsen recalled. "And I just yelled back 'You're damn right, I'm my mother's son!'I don't want to be like my dad. I've never tried to be like my dad. Now everybody thinks that I am, but those who are close to me, who are close to my dad, know the differences between us. My dad's a lot more laid back, he wants to kind of approach situations, I think, slightly more cautiously than I do, whereas I think I get a little bit more of my feisty side from my mother, from the Italian side."
As for which Gerretsen has laid the bigger legacy politically in the Limestone City, Mark Gerretsen said he has always been proud of his father's and he knows his father is proud of his.
"My dad watches CPAC (Cable Public Affairs Channel), and he's always following what's going on in the House of Commons," Gerretsen said. "Sometimes he'll call me and be like 'You know, I listened to that speech, your mom and I are really proud of you. Now, he's only started saying that I'd say in the last five years or so, but I think my dad is at that nostalgic, kind of reflective point in his life. I also know that he's equally proud of my brother for the path that he's chosen, and my sister."
With Trudeau's government expected to call an election as early as spring, Gerretsen could soon face another election campaign. Win or lose, he hopes to be remembered as someone who truly cared about his constituents.
"Whenever it's over, whether that's because I decide, or the people decide for me, I would like to be remembered as somebody who genuinely represented the values of this area and who fought for this area," he said. "I think a lot of times, people get caught up in one or two specific things they want to be remembered for. I'm not that interested in that. I don't want to be remembered as one of the people that helped build the third crossing. I want to be remembered as somebody who, when people look back at the entire time that I served, they agreed with the way that I represented them. I know that not everybody will. I get that because it's part of politics, but I hope that when it's all said and done, that people will have that ability."
Irony, of course, is abound when Gerretsen talks about his legacy.
"I want to be remembered as somebody who was approachable, somebody that people could go up to and speak to," he said. "I joked earlier how we used to always bug my dad because he'd be talking to people in the store when we just went in to get something, but I enjoy when people come up to me now. I want to be remembered as somebody who people felt like they could talk to." Gerretsen also chuckled as he recalled how some things have never changed. Recently, he and Vanessa attended a food sharing project event where he was to be recognized. En route, they stopped at his parents, who insisted they tag along, his father being a huge supporter of the cause.
"They ended up coming with us," Mark Gerretsen said.
When the event ended, and as the family readied to leave, John Gerretsen was nowhere to be found in the lobby.
"Sure enough, Vanessa and I and my mom were waiting out in the lobby for my dad while he was still talking to people in the room," Gerretsen said. "So my mom had to march back into the room and be like 'John, we're waiting for you, let's go'and drag him out of there." he said with a laugh.
Once a politician, always a politician.
Janmurphy@postmedia.com x.com/Jan_Murphy

Document 70 of 227

'The silence is deafening'; More Liberal MPs are demanding Justin Trudeau make a decision

Author: Taylor, Stephanie

Publication info: Taylor, Stephanie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/silence-is-deafening-more-liberal-mps-are/docview/3151475835/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27The+silence+is+deafening%27%3B+More+Liberal+MPs+are+demanding+Justin+Trudeau+make+a+decision&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-01-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Taylor%2C+Stephanie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With Prime Minister Justin Trudeau returning from vacation on Friday, some Liberal MPs say the time has arrived for him to face Canadians and say whether or not he intends to resign.
Trudeauhasspentthepasttwo weeks since former finance minister Chrystia Freeland's stunning resignation largely out of the public eye, at first staying behind closed doors and then setting off on his annual Christmas vacation, this time to B.C. Trudeau had said he would use the time to reflect on his future.
In speaking to fellow Liberals since Parliament broke for its holiday recess, Helena Jaczek, an Ontario MP who has called for Trudeau to quit, said what has become noticeable now is his silence.
"We want to keep our party together, and at this point, we're not hearing from the prime minister," she told Postmedia in an interview.
"I think everyone understood the need for some reflection, but it is what, more than two weeks now since Chrystia Freeland resigned. And so what I'm hearing that's a little different is, 'well the silence is deafening. Where's the leadership in a time of crisis?'" Another Ontario Liberal MP, who spoke on the condition of anonymity, said the calls for to Trudeau to resign are coming from every corner, whether it's from residents who are not usually close followers of politics or "dyed-inthe-wool Liberals."
That MP said time is running out before others in caucus get prepared to add their name to the roster of roughly 20 Liberal MPs who have gone public with their desire for Trudeau to resign, adding the fact that the prime minister has yet to make a decision "is almost as bad" as the blow Freeland dealt by resigning on the day she was set to deliver the government's fiscal update, announcing her move publicly in a scathing letter.
"This isn't just about Liberal political intrigue," the MP said. "Canadians are looking at a situation where they genuinely have a concern about who will be leading the country and what the plan is going forward on confronting the Trump threat."
Regional caucus chairs were set to meet on Friday to discuss the requests to hold a national caucus meeting.
Of the 51 out of 75 Ontario Liberal MPs who met after the House of Commons broke for the holidays, most said that Trudeau should resign - a message that Jaczek says has been communicated to the Prime Minister's Office, citing information from the group's chair. Ontario Liberal caucus chair Michael Coteau had yet to respond to a request for comment from Postmedia.
"As has already been widely reported, there was a consensus. Obviously, that's not unanimity, but there was a consensus that the prime minister should resign," said Jaczek.
A Quebec Liberal MP confirmed the sentiment that Trudeau must go is also shared by members within its provincial caucus, although its chair, Stéphane Lauzon, clarified in a statement posted to X that no letter has been signed or circulated by its caucus since they last met on Dec. 11, which was several days before Freeland resigned.
New Brunswick MP Wayne Long shared the letter sent by the Atlantic Liberal caucus chair, Nova Scotia MP Kody Blois, asking that Trudeau step aside, telling Postmedia that caucus members feel "disillusioned" that the prime minister has not yet responded to the growing calls.
"People are beyond mad now," Long says, "I think they're just really sad that the prime minister and in particular (the Prime Minister's Office) and those around him have allowed this to happen."
"Good leadership, of course, should have a lot of reflection, but good leadership also acknowledges when his caucus and, more importantly, Canadians, want to hear from him."
Long, who was one of the earliest to publicly call for Trudeau to quit following the party's surprise byelection loss in the former Liberal stronghold of Toronto-St. Paul's last June, said he expects Trudeau ultimately will resign.
"There is no other option," he said. "It's time to get out of fantasyland and get back to reality and reality clearly states that the prime minister's time is up."
Other Liberal MPs believe he will do the same, including B.C. MP Ken Hardie who said he believes Trudeau will do so "for the good of the country," as the Liberals enter an election year where they are set to face Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who has been riding high in public opinion polls since summer 2023, while Trudeau has been unsuccessful in his attempts to gain back any favour among Canadians.
Hardie pointed to a retreat the Liberal caucus had that year in London, Ont., where MPs "were assured at that time that there was a path forward."
"The leader and the Prime Minister's Office, they're pretty smart people so (we) said, 'OK, fine, let's see what that path forward is.'Well, so far we haven't seen it."
While Hardie said he believes Trudeau deserves to take the time he needs to reflect on his next steps, "the sooner the better" it would be to present a plan to Canadians. staylor@postmedia.com

Document 71 of 227

Trudeau goes radio silent; Anxious Liberals urge him to step down

Author: Platt, Brian

Publication info: Platt, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-goes-radio-silent-anxious-liberals-urge/docview/3151292191/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+goes+radio+silent%3B+Anxious+Liberals+urge+him+to+step+down&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2025-01-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Platt%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The holiday period is over and Canadian politics is gripped by a single question: when will the country hear from Justin Trudeau? The prime minister has largely disappeared from public view since Dec. 16, when Chrystia Freeland - his finance minister and once his most powerful ally in cabinet - resigned in devastating fashion, with a public letter that criticized him.
Trudeau spent much of the holidays at a ski resort in Western Canada and is not planning any official events or appearances by the end of the week, according to a person familiar with his schedule. He has given no public indication yet of when he'll announce a decision about his future.
But lawmakers in the Liberal party keep turning up the pressure on him to quit as the party's polling numbers slide further. A new survey by Nanos Research, taken over the holiday period, has the Conservative party extending its lead going into an election year.
Members of the Liberal caucus of Quebec have asked the prime minister to step aside, according to CBC, following a similar declaration by those in Ontario. Some Liberal members of the House of Commons have gone public, including Toronto lawmaker Rob Oliphant, who posted a letter asking Trudeau to make way for "a new leader elected through a robust, open leadership contest."
Liberal representatives from the Atlantic provinces said Donald Trump's imminent return to power in Washington makes it more urgent that Trudeau makes a decision.
"The country could face instability, notably from an economic threat in the potential of a 25 per cent U.S. tariff on Canadian imports from the incoming administration," said a letter from Kody Blois, who leads a group of Liberal members from the four easternmost provinces.
"Simply put, time is of the essence," Blois said, adding that it's "not tenable" for the prime minister to stay.
The longer Trudeau waits to address both his party and the country, the more he risks provoking a Liberal rebellion, said Nik Nanos, founder of Nanos Research.
"Until now it was primarily Justin Trudeau's personal brand that was damaged," said Nanos, referring to the long polling slump. "I think now the big issue is how much damage will he inflict on the party brand because he is dragging out what looks like will be his resignation at one point or another."
If the Nanos polling number were to hold up in an election, the Liberals would lose most of their 153 seats. The party has now launched pre-election advertising, focusing on concern that Conservatives will cut social programs.
But it would be difficult for the Liberals to smoothly transition to new leadership. The Trudeau government's hold on parliament is precarious, with the three largest opposition parties promising to vote "no confidence." If they do, it will trigger an election. The House resumes sitting in late January.
A Liberal leadership race may take three months to run - under different circumstances, it would take even longer. Even if the Liberals are able to avoid a forced election during a leadership contest, the winner would have a relatively short period of time, maybe only weeks, before being thrown into a national campaign. Bloomberg

Title: Trudeau goes radio silent; Anxious Liberals urge him to step down
