Document 72 of 227

Trudeau insulted Trump for years, now their roles are reversed

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-insulted-trump-years-now-their-roles-are/docview/3150440306/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+insulted+Trump+for+years%2C+now+their+roles+are+reversed&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-12-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's funny to watch Canadians in the commentariat class clutch their pearls at Donald Trump's insults aimed at Justin Trudeau. Not because the insults and online trolling are funny themselves, but because it comes after two years of Trudeau insulting Trump for his own political gain.
Trudeau has denounced Trump's MAGA movement as a threat to Canada, as dangerous, as something to be avoided, and has tried to paint his main opponent, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, as a "maple-syrup MAGA Conservative."
Since 2015, when Trump announced his bid to Make America Great Again (MAGA) by seeking the U.S. presidency, the term MAGA has been used in Canada's Parliament 127 times. Of those, 122 have been since the start of the 2023 fall session of the House of Commons and 115 of them have been by Liberal MPs.
In the Commons, Trudeau has accused the Poilievre Conservatives of adopting MAGA policies on Ukraine, housing and climate change. He's also taken shots at the MAGA movement in news conferences and media interviews.
"What we're seeing from these MAGA Conservatives is an approach on going back on fundamental rights in ways we shouldn't be seeing," Trudeau told The Canadian Press in a 2023 year-end interview.
He also tried to link the U.S. Supreme Court decision on Roe v Wade to the MAGA movement and Canada's Conservatives.
"We may think to ourselves, 'This will never happen in Canada and this is just the Liberals bringing up the usual fear that they do.'I'm sorry, it wasn't ever supposed to happen in the United States, either, and yet it did because of MAGA conservatism. The threat is real," Trudeau said.
That was Trudeau taking shots at MAGA and Trump on Canadian soil to try to score points with voters in this country. Unfortunately, he also took shots at Trump on U.S. soil.
In April 2023, Trudeau was speaking to the Council on Foreign Relations in New York when he took a veiled shot at Trump "You guys are the greatest democracy in the world," Trudeau said. "Right now, it's not just that it's being taken for granted by so many citizens, it's actually being devalued."
He took more shots at Trump, as reported by Reuters, while also praising Joe Biden's time as president. Biden took several hardline protectionist stances in areas like softwood lumber and the auto industry, and cancelling the Keystone XL project on his first day in office, but Trudeau never criticized Biden the same way.
About two weeks ago, Trudeau lamented that U.S. voters rejected Kamala Harris and chose Trump as a failure of feminism - a move noticed in Washington and by those near Trump.
Chrystia Freeland, until recently Trudeau's deputy PM and finance minister and the woman Trudeau wanted to put in charge of Canada-U.S. relations, had a habit of calling Poilievre's party "MAGA maple syrup Conservatives."
It wasn't just columnists like me warning Trudeau this was a bad tactic in case Trump won a second term.
David MacNaughton, 2015 Liberal Ontario campaign co-chair and ambassador to Washington from 2016-19 advised the Trudeau government, via the Toronto Star in February, to avoid blow-ups in the Canada-U.S. relationship.
"We need to be seen by the Americans as a trusted friend, ally, partner and, you know, right now, I don't think that feeling is as strong as it has been," he said.
They didn't listen to me or people like Mac-Naughton. Instead, Trudeau spent the last two years campaigning against Trump and MAGA and trying to link both to Poilievre's Conservatives in a bid to boost Grit poll numbers.
It hasn't worked. Instead, Liberal support among voters has fallen further.
But team Trump noticed what Trudeau has been doing and now are responding in kind. Yet, while Trudeau's power base and popularity have shrunk, Trump's power base is growing.
Trudeau has put Canada in a difficult position due to his shameless politicking and foolish actions. He will surely lose his job at the next election - how many Canadians, though, will lose their jobs because of Trudeau's stupidity before then? Sadly, it won't just be his cabinet ministers.
blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Political campaigns; Voters; International relations

Business indexing term: Subject: International relations

Location: United States--US; Canada

Document 73 of 227

Canadians ready to move on, Trudeau won't go

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadians-ready-move-on-trudeau-wont-go/docview/3149797302/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadians+ready+to+move+on%2C+Trudeau+won%27t+go&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-12-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau was off to B.C. for a ski vacation after spending Christmas in Ottawa. He shouldn't expect a warm welcome. In fact, as the latest Leger Poll shows, there are few places Trudeau would get a warm welcome because Canadians are done with him.
You've seen the numbers, 72 per cent of Canadians are dissatisfied with his government, 69 per cent think he should resign, only 11 per cent think he'd make the best prime minister, with 13 per cent picking Jagmeet Singh and 31 per cent choosing Pierre Poilievre. In party voting intentions, the Liberals are at 20 per cent nationally just ahead of the NDP at 19 per cent and far a behind the Conservatives at 43 per cent.
Trudeau's Liberals are now third in Quebec, Alberta and B.C. and if Leger didn't measure Manitoba and Saskatchewan as one group, you could likely add Saskatchewan to that group. Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives are at 50 per cent popular support or higher in Ontario, Manitoba, Saskatchewan, Alberta and above 40 per cent support in B.C. and Atlantic Canada.
Poilievre's Conservatives have a commanding lead in every demographic group looking at gender, age, or location. Any Liberal strategist or campaign worker knows these numbers spell disaster when the election comes.
And yet, Trudeau hangs on. Why wouldn't he think that, given how many times Canadians have forgiven him? Trudeau has been forgiven for interfering in criminal prosecutions to help a Quebec firm, for dismissing women who disagree with him, for groping a woman, for wearing blackface, for weaponizing vaccinations after vowing not to, for dividing Canadians while preaching unity.
If you're a trust fund baby who has never had to play by the same rules as anyone else, who has survived the scandals Trudeau has - and there are many more than I've listed - why wouldn't you think you could survive the internal party struggle now underway? So what if he lost a cabinet minister, who issued a scathing public letter denouncing his feckless policies as she quit. As Trudeau showed, there were enough greasy pole climbers in his caucus willing to look past his flaws to take up new titles and positions.
One of them, Nathaniel Erskine-Smith, Liberal MP for Toronto's Beaches East-York riding, went from being an outspoken critic of Trudeau, to a minister, to a secret critic of Trudeau with a whiplash inducing speed.
If he loses a minister, he'll find another.
He's probably also thinking that despite Jagmeet Singh's rhetoric, the NDP leader will back the Liberals with the right bribe when the time comes. If not, there is plenty of chatter the Liberals will simply prorogue Parliament to avoid any confidence votes in February or May.
Sure, Trudeau denounced prorogation as an opposition MP, but he's used it effectively in the past including to shut down committee hearings into the WE Charity scandal in 2020 and he will use it again to save his political life.
That's the entire focus of this government right now: protecting Justin Trudeau.
Canadians are ready to move on, Canada desperately needs new leadership at a time of crisis, but Trudeau and his enablers are doing all they can to cling to power. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Canadians

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; Manitoba Canada; Canada; Quebec Canada

Document 74 of 227

Drive to dump Trudeau has momentum, Liberal MP says; Prime Minister expected to shuffle cabinet on Friday

Author: Laura Dhillon Kane

Publication info: Laura Dhillon Kane. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/drive-dump-trudeau-has-momentum-liberal-mp-says/docview/3147565478/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Drive+to+dump+Trudeau+has+momentum%2C+Liberal+MP+says%3B+Prime+Minister+expected+to+shuffle+cabinet+on+Friday&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-12-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Laura+Dhillon+Kane&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau's political crisis is deepening, with more members of his Liberal party publicly calling for him to step aside and give a new leader a chance before an election in 2025.
Chad Collins, an Ontario member of Parliament, said that around 50 elected Liberals - perhaps more - are part of a growing group that wants the prime minister's resignation. Other Liberals in the anti-Trudeau camp have given similar numbers. That would be about a third of the 153-person Liberal contingent in the House of Commons.
Monday's resignation of Chrystia Freeland, Trudeau's finance minister and his longtime deputy, was a massive shock that has irreparably damaged the prime minister, Collins said in an interview.
Freeland said she quit after being told she would be moved to a different role in the cabinet. Trudeau delivered that news on Friday, she said - just three days before she was due for a major speech that would update the country on its fiscal and economic situation.
"I don't know who's giving him advice. I can guess. It's not good advice," Collins said. "But the buck stops with him with the decisions he makes, and we're now seeing the fallout that's come with what many would consider a very poor decision.
"In terms of who the successor is, I don't know at this point whether or not we could do much worse," he said.
Still, Trudeau's new finance minister insisted on Thursday that Trudeau has the full support of his cabinet to stay on as prime minister. Dominic LeBlanc also sidestepped a question about whether he would like to lead the Liberal party.
"If the prime minister has the full support of his cabinet, then why would we contemplate what happens when he decides to leave?" he said.
Trudeau is expected to shuffle his cabinet on Friday to replace five ministers who said they won't re-offer in the next election.
Earlier Thursday, Justice Minister Arif Virani avoided offering a clear defence of Trudeau when asked whether he thought he should resign.
"Decisions will be taken by the parties that are involved," he said, steering the discussion back to his announcement about preventing wrongful convictions. "I have absolute confidence in the prime minister in terms of what he has asked me to do. That is serve as a minister of justice who defends people's rights."
In a social media post, Toronto MP and foreign affairs parliamentary secretary Rob Oliphant said he called for an "urgent meeting" of his riding association executive Thursday before taking a public stand on the leadership question.
"What's best for Canada comes first and our party comes second," Oliphant wrote.
Trudeau has been under pressure to leave for months. In June, the Liberals lost a special election in a Toronto riding that they had held for decades. In September, they lost a seat in Montreal in similar fashion. Soon after, about two dozen Liberal lawmakers signed a letter asking him to go.
But Freeland's resignation - coming at a time when Canada's economy faces the threat of tariffs from the incoming Trump administration - has turned bubbling discontent into a full-scale crisis for Trudeau. The party also lost yet another byelection the same day she quit.
The prime minister has cancelled all of his usual year-end television interviews and said almost nothing publicly since her departure, other than some brief comments at two Liberal events.
Polls suggest the Conservative Party, led by Pierre Poilievre, is on course to win a majority government in the next election. "This isn't just about one man - it's about saving our party from a historic defeat," Wayne Long, another Liberal MP from New Brunswick, wrote in an open letter on Wednesday.
More Liberals will step away from politics if Trudeau tries to stick around, Collins warned. "I think the risk he runs is that he'll have a skeleton crew of experienced elected representatives."
Jenica Atwin, a Liberal who serves as parliamentary secretary to a cabinet minister, told a newspaper in her home province of New Brunswick that Trudeau should leave and that she won't run for re-election if he stays. Bloomberg, with a file from The Canadian Press

Document 75 of 227

Provincial premiers should take over tariff fight

Author: Braid, Don

Publication info: Braid, Don. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/provincial-premiers-should-take-over-tariff-fight/docview/3147565131/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Provincial+premiers+should+take+over+tariff+fight&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-12-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Braid%2C+Don&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Liberal death rattles grow louder and the regime of Justin Trudeau totters, certain realities become more obvious.
Canada is being ruined by the unrelenting centralism in this nation.
The Liberals have extended their powers in many areas without any growth in their ability to run the most basic services.
Nearly 200,000 people are waiting for passports.
The strike at Canada Post has derailed Christmas and damaged small business.
The government blew open the doors to immigration, overloading housing supply all over the country.
Heralded national programs such as child care and dental care are unavailable to many people. The holiday break on GST and HST is an expensive political dud.
The Liberals constantly expand federal power, especially in resources.
They try to impose social goals on foreign investment. The dollar has fallen nearly six per cent against U.S. currency during the last year.
They write unconstitutional laws - such as the Impact Assessment Act - to assume economic powers not rightfully theirs.
They ban firearms, but never get around to buying back guns. They lavish cash on their favourite economic sectors in Quebec and Ontario, while threatening the future of industries from Saskatchewan to B.C.
They are largely captive to the voters in three cities across a mammoth country, Toronto, Vancouver and Montreal. In wide swaths of the rural and urban West, as well as rural and suburban Ontario and Quebec, the government now seems alien.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau prizes performance above all else. Such leaders always leave a wreckage of expensive programs largely ignored after the spotlight switches off. The boondoggles live on, enriching consultants hired by well-paid bureaucrats.
With the Liberals deep in crisis after the resignation of Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland and the Trump presidency looming, we have a true national emergency.
(You know it is serious when Bloc Quebecois Leader Yves-François Blanchet, a separatist, says, "We have to stop fighting between ourselves.
We have to try to present a more united front.") U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's contempt for "Governor" Trudeau could not be more obvious.
The real work on that front is falling to the 13 premiers. They say they're taking leadership with a "Team Canada" approach. They've met twice and are promising to work together.
Their group is the Council of the Federation, under the working title Canada's Premiers. They've been meeting for years about irritants such as shortfalls in federal funding for health care.
The group always annoys centralists who don't think premiers should meddle in national matters.
But today, they're arguably more effective with the Americans than the federal government.
The premiers and their officials are lobbying vigorously in the U.S. against tariffs. Alberta Premier Danielle Smith has emerged as a key national spokesperson, and Ontario PC Premier Doug Ford, who currently chairs the group.
(Smith actually won a victory Tuesday in her battle over the 2035 federal target for a net-zero Alberta electricity grid. The Liberals pushed the deadline to 2050.)
As a group, the premiers represent Canada more accurately than the federal government does.
They include two Liberal provincial leaders, two New Democrats and a scattering of conservatives under various names. The territories send two non-partisan leaders and one Liberal.
Everybody at the table has an equal regional voice. You won't find that in the federal Liberal caucus or cabinet.
Maybe the premiers'group should be declared the formal national negotiator during this crisis, if only because they'd be far less likely to sell out one region.
At this point they're our best hope, the voice of collective sanity. Don Braid's column appears regularly in Postmedia publications X: @DonBraid

Subject: Leadership; Firearms

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Document 76 of 227

No-nonsense Ford ready to rumble with Trump

Author: Bell, Rick

Publication info: Bell, Rick. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-nonsense-ford-ready-rumble-with-trump/docview/3145910709/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No-nonsense+Ford+ready+to+rumble+with+Trump&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-12-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Bell%2C+Rick&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Two can play this game.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford looked like he had it up to here pussyfooting around with U.S. president-elect Donald Trump and the man's threats against Canada.
He obviously was not willing to stick to talking points coated in generalities. You know, we're going to have do something, but we're not saying what.
It sounded like Ford wanted to get down to the meat and potatoes, the bread and butter.
And when he spoke, Ford couldn't have said what he had to say more directly. Pull no punches would be the apt description.
Ford wants Trump to know Ontario, Canada's largest province, will go to the wall against a 25 per cent tariff charge on Canadian goods heading stateside.
Ford says he will "go to the full extent," if necessary.
What does that mean? "We will go to the extent of cutting off their energy going down to Michigan, going down to New York state and over to Wisconsin."
Say what?! You heard him right. Ford adds he doesn't want this showdown to happen. He doesn't want some cross-border game of chicken. He would rather skip this real-life high-stakes political poker.
He considers Americans our friends. He has good relationships with people in the U.S.
But the Ontario premier is also 100 per cent certain Trump will slam Canada with tariffs when he takes over as president.
He is asked if Trump is all bluster. Ford does not hesitate to say no.
So now he sees his job as protecting the people of Ontario and all Canadians.
"We need to be ready. We need to be ready to fight. The fight is coming 100 per cent on Jan. 20 or Jan. 21. 100 per cent these tariffs are coming," says the Ontario premier.
"Let's see what happens as we move forward.
We'll use every tool in our toolbox, including cutting them off energy we're sending down there.
"Folks, this is coming. It's not if it is. It's coming. We need to be prepared."
Just so you're picking up what he's laying down, Ford spells it out.
"We know it's coming," says Ford, of the Trump tariffs.
"Hopefully it's not going to come as hard as we think. If he comes out and he mentions he's going to tariff everything that's a problem.
"We'll use every tool in our toolbox to fight back. We can't sit back and roll over."
Ford does his talking after the virtual gabfest Prime Minister Justin Trudeau had with the country's premiers and there was plenty of talk of beefing up security at the Canada-U.S. border.
He is asked about Trudeau, who said when Trump defeated Kamala Harris in the election there were "regressive" and "reactionary" forces at play and women's rights and progress were under attack "overtly and subtly."
Trudeau's mouthings were brought up a couple of times by premiers.
Why does he become a dime-store analyst of American politics at this time of all times? "It's not helpful at all," says Ford. "Donald Trump was elected democratically.
You like him or you don't like him. That's not our issue. It was not helpful whatsoever."
Chrystia Freeland, the deputy prime minister, is questioned more than once about cutting off Canadian energy to the United States.
Freeland, in her not-willing-to-say-toomuch tone of voice, does mention how a number of premiers want Canada to play tough.
But what about cutting off energy? The deputy PM said some premiers named "critical minerals and metals their provinces produce and are exported to the (U.S.)" So why was Ford talking so tough? He says Trump is now "a little more aggressive." He is "a different type of cat."
One thing is also not in dispute if tariffs come to pass. "Canadians will get hurt but I assure you of one thing. The Americans are going to feel the pain as well." Rick Bell is a Calgary Herald columnist

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Document 77 of 227

Ford tramples vulnerable to appease privileged

Author: Baranyai, Robin

Publication info: Baranyai, Robin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-tramples-vulnerable-appease-privileged/docview/3144437452/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+tramples+vulnerable+to+appease+privileged&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Baranyai%2C+Robin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It seems everywhere you look these days, Premier Doug Ford is pointing a finger at the little guy. Cyclists are causing gridlock. Unhoused people in encampments are ruining parks. People who use drugs at supervised consumption sites (rather than in public spaces) are a threat to nearby schools.
All these challenges are clearly systemic, yet the "solutions" seem to target the people with the least power, and the most to lose.
Just in the last month, Ford's Conservatives have passed legislation to remove bike lanes from major Toronto roads; tabled a bill to close 10 supervised consumption and treatment sites (SCTs); and proposed new tools for municipalities to force people out of encampments.
The premier claims to speak for the little guy, but when he takes action, their actual needs - expressed by the communities themselves - come a distant second.
The Conservatives entertained just one day of public hearings on their controversial "reducing gridlock" bill. Then - anticipating cyclists could be injured or killed on roads where bike lanes are ripped out - they tacked on an amendment protecting the province from being sued.
Ford's Conservatives do show genuine concern for the well-being of people struggling with addiction, they just don't want any tedious public consultation informing their actions. They've committed $378 million for homelessness and addictions recovery treatment (HART) hubs across the province. Unfortunately, in focusing on treatment, the government is turning away from harm-reduction measures, though evidence shows both are needed.
Needle exchange services and drug testing at SCTs would complement the HART hubs, which will offer neither. Instead, SCTs near schools and daycares will be shuttered. A Toronto agency is suing to stop the cuts to lifesaving care.
Ford's own staff have warned SCT closings will increase the risk of needles in nearby parks and public spaces. Strategically, it's a head-scratcher for a premier eager to prove his commitment to keeping parks safer for families and kids to enjoy. At least, families with homes.
As many as one in four Canadians experiencing homelessness live in encampments, about 1,400 across Ontario, municipalities estimated last year.
Several of those municipalities have become increasingly frustrated by their limited ability to "regulate and prohibit encampments." An Ontario Supreme Court ruled people in such camps can't be forced out, until there is somewhere safe for them to go. Shelter is a human right.
In a scene that played out like a schoolyard fight, Ford invited griping municipalities to ask him publicly to invoke the notwithstanding clause, then showed up at recess with a dozen big-city mayors egging him on. The notwithstanding clause - intended as a rare mechanism to balance federal and provincial power - temporarily allows legislation to override guarantees in the Charter of Rights and Freedoms.
It is no less than the fourth time Ford has brandished this particular weapon in a fight. He's announced he doesn't expect to use it, but can't resist opening his jacket to remind us it's there, should his legislation steamroll any pesky human rights.
Nobody thinks tent encampments are a safe and sustainable solution to homelessness; however, a much worse solution is criminalizing people for having nowhere to go.
Experts widely advocate a housing-first approach to homelessness. The problem is, we don't have the homes. Ontario is lagging in new housing starts. Ironically, among the 12 municipalities requesting Ford's intervention, fully two-thirds won't come close to meeting their home-building targets for 2024, according to the province's own housing progress tracker.
Ford's signature policy initiative is more homes, faster. Perhaps that's why he's pointing a finger everywhere else. write.robin@baranyai.ca

Subject: Public spaces; Legislation; Homeless people; Housing starts

Business indexing term: Subject: Housing starts

Identifier / keyword: seems; everywhere; premier; pointing; finger; little

Document 78 of 227

'Coach Harty insisted on fast and furious play'; Local history

Author: Mcleod, Susanna

Publication info: Mcleod, Susanna. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/coach-harty-insisted-on-fast-furious-play-local/docview/3143460088/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Coach+Harty+insisted+on+fast+and+furious+play%27%3B+Local+history&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-12-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Mcleod%2C+Susanna&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's no getting around it. Even if one isn't a big sports fan, Kingstonians are familiar with the name of Jock Harty Arena. The venue was built in 1922 with funds from William Harty, a local entrepreneur and politician in loving remembrance of his son. An enthusiastic hockey player and coach, Dr. John "Jock" Harty's mark is infused in the history of Kingston and Queen's University.
Emigrating from Tipperary, Ireland, ancestors of the local Harty family settled in southwestern Ontario in the Lucan, Middlesex region. John Harty and his wife Mary Heenan welcomed William on March 8, 1847. The family moved east to Kingston, where young William attended school at Brothers of the Christian Schools and Regiopolis College. Constructed in 1839, the first Regiopolis College became the Sydenham Wing of Hotel Dieu Hospital in 1892.
"While in his teens (Harty) joined the wholesale grocery firm there of his uncle James Harty," said Andrew Thomson in Dictionary of Canadian Biography, Vol. 15, 2005. "In 1868, after his uncle's death, he took it over." Immersed in business, Harty also joined the Board of Trade and "rose to become its president in 1873."
Three years earlier, Harty married Katherine Mary Bermingham, the sister of Cornelius Bermingham (b. 1852 in Ireland), a young industrialist in the city. The Hartys lived on Sydenham Street, according to Directory of the City of Kingston for 1873-74. Their family grew quickly with two sons and one daughter. Their first child was John Joseph "Jock" Harty, born in Kingston in 1874.
Piqued by politics after attending a Dominion Board of Trade meeting in Saint John in the next year, Harty also expanded his commercial interests. Perhaps sparked by his brother-in-law's operations, Harty was a director and executive member with the Kingston and Pembroke Railway for four years, starting in 1875. The political bug finally took hold; Harty was elected as alderman in Kingston's city council in 1879. (Bermingham was later an alderman as well.)
In 1881, Harty helped organize the Kingston Charcoal and Iron Company on Clarence Street and left his grocery business. He had bigger plans in mind.
Learning that Kingston Locomotive and Engine Company-then in Montreal-was struggling, Harty joined with federal politicians George Airey Kirpatrick, Richard Cartwright, and previous mayors John McKelvy and John Breden to return the factory back to where it belonged in Kingston. Harty was made "managing director," said Thomson, "and operated the factory successfully for seven years before selling it to a Scottish firm, Dubs and Company" in 1887.
The manufacturing plant flourishing, Harty switched jobs, becoming the Canadian general manager for American firm, Equitable Life Assurance Society. Re-entering the political fray, Harty was first elected to the Legislative Assembly of Ontario in a by-election in February 1892 and was re-elected three more times. Stepping into federal politics, Harty was elected as the Liberal Party member for Kingston in January 1902, He remained the local representative until September 1911.
Throughout his time in Parliament, Harty was engaged in local business. The Canadian Locomotive Company was again on the ropes and the court appointed receivers. Harty and a group of men, including his brotherin-law, submitted an offer for the factory. Putting together $60,000, the group agreed to the stipulation of upgrading the locomotive works.
Armed with many business connections, Harty received a number of new contracts for the factory. Viewed as a Liberal advantage, on November 6, 1900 the Daily British Whig (then a partisan newspaper), published that Harty was again in charge, with "Orders for 32 engines. Liberals only friend of working man after all," noted Thomson.
At the Harty home, his children were growing and flourishing. Harty's oldest son, known as Jock, studied at Kingston Collegiate then attended Loyola College in Montreal, part of Collège Sainte-Marie. "He developed a love of hockey while playing for the Loyola team in Montreal, something that would stay with him the rest of his life," according to Queen's Encyclopedia. The young man chose medicine as his career and enrolled in the Queen's medical program. Back home in Kingston, "he brought a faster version of hockey that he had learned to play in Montreal."
Graduating in 1897, Dr.
Harty did not open a medical practice. Instead, he joined his father at the locomotive factory.
(The family residence was the brick two-storey home at 520 Princess Street, now the Maison Paul Coiffeur.) Learning the foundry business from the workshop floor to the offices, Harty "was secretary of the former company and vice-president of the newly organized company of a few years ago," said Daily British Whig, February 24, 1919.
Jock Harty's heart pulsed with sports. "At Queen's he was regarded as the cleverest hockey stick handler Ontario ever produced," stated Daily British Whig. He was "able to manoeuvre the puck in a smaller area than any other player and working the elusive rubber through a mix-up of players in marvellous fashion."
For a brief time, Harty played on the New York Crescents team when they defeated Yale University in 1897.
Harty also took delight in rugby, and sailboat and speedboat racing, participating in events at Alexandria Bay.
Coaching the Queen's and locomotive works'teams, Harty led the school team in 1909 in the senior ice hockey championship game against the cup-holder Ottawa. The win went to Harty and Kingston: Queen's University 5 to Ottawa Cliffsides 4.
The key to winning was, perhaps, the coach's training methods. "Coach Harty insisted on fast and furious play from his players, barking instruction to them through a megaphone from the bench," Queen's Encyclopedia mentioned.
Following his father's footsteps into local politics, Jock Harty served as Cataraqui Ward councillor for a few years. Offered nomination as the federal Liberal candidate, he declined, dedicating his time to the locomotive works.
Clashes at the re-incorporated Canadian Locomotive Company brought a strike in 1901 and a dispute with the machinist's local union that lasted several years. Production continued with replacement workers. Over time, the relationship between William Harty and Cornelius Bermingham was fractured over "Harty's refusal as president to consider offers to participate by American investors and his determination to keep the company in Kingston," Thomson wrote. Permanently damaging the family relationship, Harty "never spoke to his brother-in-law again."
While living at 107 Bagot Street, Harty lost control of the locomotive works in 1911 to Toronto investors. William Harty left the company, but his sons Jock and William remained with the firm. In 1917, Jock was appointed company president; his younger brother led the factory in 1924.
During a business trip to England, Jock Harty contracted the feared Spanish Flu. He did not make it home alive. On February 23, 1919, 44-year-old Dr. John "Jock" Harty died of the pandemic at the Savoy Hotel in London. His family was crushed.
In 1921, Harty's bereaved father donated $25,000 to Queen's University toward construction of a new campus arena. "In his memory, the 4,500-seat, all wood Jock Harty Area was built in 1922," said Queen's Encyclopedia, "with the money for its construction being largely supplied by friends, family, and classmates."
The first Jock Harty Arena was located on Arch Street, but fire in 1924 destroyed the building. A second arena was quickly rebuilt onsite. It featured an artificial ice rink, though seating was cut in half. Torn down in 1968 for construction of Humphrey Hall, arena users were irate at the loss. Jock Harty Arena number three rose up in 1971 at Union and Division Streets. "Hockey, skating, convocations, concerts and even tennis on its roof thrived at the new Jock Harty," described the Encyclopedia. It was torn down in 2007 for university expansion. The nearby Memorial Centre was the new home for Queen's hockey.
The friendly, generous, and passionate Jock Harty was buried at St. Mary's Roman Catholic Cemetery. His father, William Harty, died on April 1, 1929 at age 82. The Hartys were dedicated to Kingston and proudly advanced its status in society and sports.
Susanna McLeod is a writer living in Kingston, Ontario.

Subject: Presidents; Encyclopedias; Hockey

Location: Ireland; Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada

Document 79 of 227

Petition calls on government to drop case against kingston doctor

Author: Balogh, Meghan

Publication info: Balogh, Meghan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/petition-calls-on-government-drop-case-against/docview/3143459334/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Petition+calls+on+government+to+drop+case+against+kingston+doctor&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-12-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Balogh%2C+Meghan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Kingston family doctor has organized a petition to the Ontario government on behalf of a fellow family physician who is being ordered to repay more than half a million dollars to the Ontario Health Insurance Plan (OHIP). Dr. Veronica Legnini said that she "pounded the pavement" over the weekend, so that the petition could be present in the Ontario legislature on Monday.
Ted Hsu, Liberal member of provincial parliament for Kingston and the Islands, brought the petition on behalf of more than 100 signees that request the government stop demanding repayment from Dr. Elaine Ma, money that the petition says, "she earned for her heroic efforts to vaccinate Ontarians during the COVID-19 pandemic."
"We the undersigned petition the Legislative Assembly of Ontario as follows: To call on Premier Doug Ford and Health Minister, Sylvia Jones, to demonstrate their commitment to Ontario healthcare workers by ordering OHIP to set aside the (Health Services Appeal and Review Board) decision and to cease demanding repayment of earned billings," the petition reads.
On Nov. 26, Ontario Health Services Appeal and Review Board ruled that Ma should repay $600,962.61, plus interest, for billings that she made during a number of large-scale drive through COVID-19 vaccination clinics she organized between July 2021 and January 2022.
The clinics were organized by Ma in collaboration with health-care professionals, students at Queen's University, the local public health unit and others, many of whom volunteered their time to administer several thousand vaccines at the height of the pandemic.
Ma organized approximately 45 drive-through vaccine clinics that administered around 35,000 doses of COVID-19 vaccine. Her efforts were recognized by the Ontario Medical Association and beyond, and the Kingston region's medical officer of health, Dr. Piotr Oglaza, pointed to Ma's efforts as being pivotal in helping the KFL&A region achieve the best vaccine coverage rate in the province.
But the Ontario government has ruled that Ma's billings were made erroneously, due to the fact that the vaccinations were administered outside of her personal office, and by people who were not technically her employees, requirements for OHIP coverage for the billing codes she used.
Legnini said that the ruling against Ma is a terrifying prospect for other physicians, including herself, who fear that OHIP will be coming after them next. She and other doctors in Kingston have used the drive-through clinic model that Ma utilized, just not on such a large scale.
Legnini and others have cancelled planned community drivethrough vaccination clinics in the wake of Ma's experience with OHIP.
"I've done flu shot clinics and I've done COVID vaccine clinics, and I'll be very frank with you. We're all afraid of being targeted by OHIP," Legnini said. "So many doctors wouldn't even sign the petition.
Lots of people told me, 'I'm scared OHIP is going to target me for something.'" She is disillusioned with the mixed messaging that the ruling against Ma suggests.
"I remember what it was like on those provincial phone calls," she said. "You had General Hillier, the head of the COVID-19 task force, saying, 'Get it done. We'll make it right.'Doug Ford came on those calls and he said, 'Just get it done, whatever you have to do, we'll make it right later.'The messaging was really there from all levels of government."
Ma's clinics, praised as innovative, had support from the city, from local public health and other professionals, Legnini said.
"Many, many physicians came to those clinics, Queen's directed students to those clinics. People came and volunteered from all walks of life. Nurses came, even though they were tired and working hard at the hospital. It was really an amazing thing to see and be a part of."
Legnini said that it was "no secret" that drive-through clinics were being organized by other doctors in the Kingston community.
She doesn't know if or when OHIP will pursue action against others.
"I do worry that I'm going to be next," she admitted.
Legnini said that the ruling is also going to affect the training of medical students going forward. Ma is being penalized for billing for vaccine administration that was delegated to medical students and volunteers who were not technically her employees. OHIP representatives referenced a 2001 document during Ma's hearing that provides the supporting definition of "staff" that allowed the province to rule against Ma's billing.
"I have colleagues at other offices who, after this ruling, they're really looking hard at their staffing and saying, well, if OHIP is saying she can't go for that delegated act, I may have to let go of some staff because otherwise, what's the point?" Legnini said that most doctors do not get paid to teach student physicians.
"Honestly, it's a wonderful thing to do, but it's an investment in time and money. If you even lose that bit of billing (potential), it can make a difference for a doctor, and lots of groups are already looking at their structure, in terms of employees and medical students."
With Ontario doctors-in-training already having difficulty finding hands-on education, Legnini said that the ruling will have more negative impacts.
"If this (ruling) stands, then looking ahead beyond the next election we will see even worsening access to quality medical education for medical students, less employment opportunities in health care and less patient access to any care, let alone team-based care," Legnini said. "My clinic already did not run our traditional drive through clinics for flu shots, which we have done for many years, even before COVID."
Since the ruling at the end of November, community members have rallied to show support to Ma, with more than 4,000 signatures on an online petition asking the Ford government to reconsider Ma's case.
Kingston city council members voiced their support for Ma during a recent council meeting, unanimously backing a motion calling for the province to intervene in the Health Services Appeal and Review Board decision.
Hsu said that it is his job to present any petition brought to him by his constituents, but that he has also already spoken out in the house in support of Ma's pandemic-time efforts.
A petition formally presented in the Legislative Assembly of Ontario "asks the Legislature to act on or change its position on an issue," according to the government's website.
Once a petition is presented, the government is required to file a response within 24 sessional days.
In an interview with the Whig-Standard at the end of November, Hsu said that he is concerned about how this ruling will affect health-care professionals going forward.
"Doctors had to take the initiative to organize these completely new clinics, the sort of thing that had never been done before," he said. "The question in my head is, in the next public health emergency, will doctors step up and take the initiative when it is required?" Legnini praised Ma's unconventional approach to mass vaccination, which allowed people to stay socially distanced and safe during times of public health measures.
"The degree of personal liability Dr. Ma took on willingly is incredible," Legnini said. "I never would have spearheaded an operation of this scale. And if a large-scale corporation had offered to get this work done for the same cost per shot, but twenty times as quickly as a traditional clinic, and over the holidays in the cold, we'd be singing their praises. And their CEO would get a bonus."
In a supplied statement on Tuesday, the Ontario Medical Association said that doctors "were called upon to perform heroically in the midst of a crisis" during the COVID-19 pandemic.
"Now, years later, it's disheartening to our members to see this kind of reaction to their efforts and a lack of understanding of the depth and breadth of the sacrifices they made to keep us all safe. It is concerning that a physician who provided care to their community during the pandemic is now facing this outcome." mbalogh@postmedia.com

Subject: Review boards; Public health; COVID-19 vaccines; Community; Medical students; Billings; Clinics; Immunization; Employees; Health services; Pandemics

Business indexing term: Subject: Billings Employees

Document 80 of 227

Even Liberals running away from carbon tax

Author: Goldstein, Lorrie

Publication info: Goldstein, Lorrie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/even-liberals-running-away-carbon-tax/docview/3142730984/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Even+Liberals+running+away+from+carbon+tax&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-12-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Goldstein%2C+Lorrie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's carbon tax has become such a stink bomb to Liberal fortunes everywhere that even Ontario Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie is denouncing it.
At a Liberal fundraiser on Tuesday night she was reported to say: "I'm not here to tell the prime minister how to do his job. But I promise you, I will tell him when he's wrong. Like on the carbon tax."
That was in response to months of Ontario Premier Doug Ford and the Progressive Conservatives labelling her "the queen of the Carbon Tax" because of her previous support for it when she was a Liberal MP, plus describing her "as one of the only provincial Liberal leaders in Canada who won't speak out against the carbon tax."
Crombie previously pledged not to introduce a provincial carbon tax if she becomes premier.
Her credibility problem is the history of Ontario and federal Liberals saying one thing and doing another.
During the 2014 Ontario election that brought Kathleen Wynne to power, Wynne said nothing about imposing a provincial carbon tax.
Shortly after her victory, she said she had no plans to introduce a carbon tax Then, she introduced capand-trade.
Prior to the 2019 federal election, then-Liberal environment minister Catherine McKenna said the Liberals wouldn't raise their carbon tax beyond $50 a tonne of emissions in 2022.
After the 2019 election, in which the Liberals were reduced from a majority to a minority government, Trudeau announced he would raise the federal carbon tax every year after 2022 until it reached $170 a tonne of emissions in 2030.
The Trudeau Liberals have claimed the rebate system leaves 80 per cent of families with more money than they pay in carbon taxes.
Independent, non-partisan Parliamentary Budget Officer Yves Giroux subsequently pointed out this is true if one considers only the fiscal impact of the carbon tax.
But if you added in its drag on the Canadian economy, plus the fact the Liberal rebate system doesn't return revenue generated by their imposition of the GST on top of the carbon tax to Canadians, 60 per cent of families end up with less money in their pocket.
Trudeau says he introduced his carbon tax as an alternative to less efficient and more expensive ways to reduce emissions such as government regulations and government subsidies.
In reality, there are 149 government programs aimed at reducing emissions.
Finally the Trudeau Liberals said no political considerations went into the creation of their carbon tax.
But Gudie Hutchings, Trudeau's minister for rural economic development and the Atlantic Canada Opportunities Agency, said last year if provinces like Alberta wanted special deals to defray the costs of carbon pricing, as they had just announced for Atlantic Canada, "Perhaps they need to elect more Liberals on the Prairies so that we can have that conversation, as well." lgoldstein@postmedia.com

Subject: Emission standards; Carbon; Prime ministers; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Environmental tax

Location: Canada

Document 81 of 227

Obeying autocrat in advance a dangerous game

Author: Baranyai, Robin

Publication info: Baranyai, Robin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/obeying-autocrat-advance-dangerous-game/docview/3141979762/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Obeying+autocrat+in+advance+a+dangerous+game&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-12-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Baranyai%2C+Robin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A lot has happened since historian Timothy Snyder published his concise handbook, On Tyranny: Twenty Lessons from the Twentieth Century. The tiny book exploded in 2017. Today it's more relevant than ever. Among the lessons Snyder draws from our fraught history with authoritarianism, the most important is: Do not obey in advance.
So far, we're doing a terrible job.
When wealthy U.S. newspaper owners decided not to endorse a presidential candidate, they were obeying in advance - avoiding the ire of a grudge-holding nominee, in case he returned to power - thus making it more likely he would.
Seeking to appease an authoritarian by anticipating what he might want, and offering it before he asks, merely conditions us for more and greater concessions, Snyder warns. U.S.
senators who have pledged to support dangerously unqualified cabinet picks, long before confirmation hearings, are simply laying bare their obsequence.
Appallingly, we're doing the same thing in Canada.
A conga line of anticipatory obedience greeted the president-elect's threat of a 25 per cent tariff on Canadian imports, which he threatened as retaliation against the "Invasion" of fentanyl and "illegal aliens." Ontario Premier Doug Ford was first in line with a pre-emptive sacrifice, proposing to cut Mexico out of the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement. Alberta Premier Danielle Smith promptly lined up behind him.
The premiers'clunky attempts to curry favour only show the once and future president how easily he can push us around. More on point was Mexico's former chief free trade negotiator, Kenneth Smith Ramos, who told CBC's Power and Politics killing the trilateral deal would just play into the hands of a leader whose consistent approach to his trading partners is "dividing and conquer."
Meanwhile, federally, Opposition Leader Pierre Poilievre made up a new podium sign with the toadying slogan "fix the broken border." His new rhetorical target, "people who lie to come into our country" (while claiming to love "real" refugees), echoes the exaggerations and deflections of the president-elect. Such eagerness to obey in advance rather takes the air out of his criticism the prime minister returned from dinner at Mara-Lago without winning any concessions.
Plans for the visit, proposed by Trudeau, were hatched quietly behind the scenes. The leaders'dinner conversation touched on trade, border security and shared concern over fentanyl, along with a joke about Canada becoming the "51st state." Ha, ha.
Even less amusingly, on Nov. 29 - the same day Trudeau and Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc quietly flew to Florida - Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) announced an immediate "pause" on the private sponsorship of refugees. Claims already submitted will progress through the queue; however, no new sponsorship applications by community groups or groups of five will be accepted until after Dec. 31, 2025.
Of course, this could be a coincidence. IRCC did not cite a change in American leadership as a reason for the change. Rather, it referred to the backlog of applications from private sponsors, which, not surprisingly, exceed the shrinking targets set out in Canada's immigration levels plan.
Still, one wonders why it had to happen so abruptly, extinguishing hope for vulnerable refugees, along with groups of Canadians tirelessly pursuing legal channels to help them to safety. IRCC could have tapered the flow of applications, or given private sponsors a 48-hour window to submit paperwork near completion (as it has done in the past) before the new rules took effect. Instead, the pause was declared effective as of midnight the day before.
Another factor in the decision, IRCC noted, was "stakeholder feedback on potential application intake controls." It could be unrelated, but it sure sounds perilously close to obeying in advance. write.robin@baranyai.ca

Subject: Leadership; Fentanyl; Immigration; Corporate sponsorship

Document 82 of 227

Doug ford is leading the way; Opinion

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/doug-ford-is-leading-way-opinion/docview/3141979674/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+ford+is+leading+the+way%3B+Opinion&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-12-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford has had a successful run since being elected in 2018, but perhaps more than anything else, he deserves credit for expanding the subway system in Canada's largest city.
The Ontario Line will introduce nearly 16 kilometres of track to the fastest-growing city in North America, but it wasn't easy. Initially conceived as the Downtown Relief Line, it has been mired in politics and bureaucracy for decades - until Doug Ford came along and made it happen.
Without Ford's initiative, the Ontario Line would still be aspirational rather than under construction, although that seems lost on Toronto's chattering liberal class, for whom swallowing cyanide might be preferable to bestowing even a soupçon of credit upon a Conservative.
The Ontario Line is no doubt a legacy project for a premier who will be remembered for building the most significant infrastructure project the city has seen in decades.
Ford is presently being chided from all angles for his stance on bicycle lanes in Toronto, even though it's one of the most congested cities in the world. It doesn't take an urban planner to figure out that augmenting mass transit, not expanding underused bike lanes (which are used by a mere 1.2 per cent of the residents, according to census data), is the solution.
Nearly two decades after the Ontario Liberals introduced the Places to Grow Act, in part to curtail congestion, the tribulations of navigating Toronto's factory farm-like conditions have exposed kinks in the legislation's armour.
Bloor Street is down to a single lane in either direction along the hyper-dense expanse west of Spadina Avenue. Further to the north, Eglinton Avenue has been saddled with a bike lane that virtually no one uses but has somehow managed to make traffic around the notoriously congested Allen Road intersection even worse than before.
Ford's initiatives have nevertheless done precious little to stem the barrage of criticism he receives on a quotidian basis. And the Toronto Star, which dropped the mitts against the Ford family long ago, is unsurprisingly front and centre.
It recently ran a column accusing Ford of trying to hoodwink voters with trivialities like permitting grocers and convenience stores to sell alcohol. It's a shrill rebuke that reeks of the same patronizing paternalism that's mugged the province's hard-working taxpayers of the same consumer liberty that neighbouring Quebec has enjoyed from what seems like time immemorial.
The Star's petty, low-rent reprimand ignores the fact that Ford is merely adhering to arguably the most salient principle of free-market capitalism: competition between vendors results in lower consumer prices.
The Star must have forgotten that its columnists have also advocated for the exact thing Ford codified into law, having noted in the past that the Beer Store is a foreign-owned "cartel-cum-monopoly" and that its stores feel like "Stalinist relics ... designed by Communist apparatchiks for the beer-swilling lumpenproletariat."
The Star might need a primer on its own founding principles and mission statement, which says that it believes in "individual and civil liberties," and works to "focus public attention on injustices of all kinds." Those "injustices" apparently don't include corporations colluding to price-gouge increasingly immiserated consumers.
The reality is that such free market policies augment razorthin profit margins for entrepreneurs, many of whom pour their life savings into ventures like neighbourhood convenience stores, and in case the chattering liberal class in Toronto hasn't noticed, everything is more expensive.
That includes small businesses'overheads, but alcohol sales will help - and considering that you'd be hard-pressed to find a pint anywhere in Toronto for under $9, Ford's push to increase competition and choice is great news for consumers and businesses alike.
The portion of Highway 401 running through Toronto is North America's busiest and the bane of the city's drivers. Weekday or weekend, day or night, drivers can expect a slow commute.
Ford has proposed building a tunnel below the highway to augment traffic flow. It's an ambitious project that comes with considerable risk - just look at Boston's "Big Dig," which suffered years of delays and billions in cost overruns. Yet Ford's army of media critics have only admonished his idea instead of offering their own solutions to the city's traffic woes.
Ford is a lightning rod for Canadian media, most of which has a liberal bias that perhaps explains why they're more interested in tearing him down than proffering policies of their own. They offer criticism without any real solutions of their own, which reeks of partisanship and does little to further public discourse.
Urban planners and left-wing commentators are fond of gaslighting Canadians by pointing to bike-lane usage in cities such as Beijing, Paris and Amsterdam, where temperatures rarely dip below freezing, and using the "If you build it, they will come" argument.
But the fact remains that Canada does not have the bike culture of a country like China, and no matter how bad traffic gets, the prospect of riding a bicycle through the snow when it's -20 C outside will remain unappealing to most Canadians.
Unfortunately, Ford's critics seem completely devoid of solutions of their own, repeating tired clichés about how bike lanes are the answer to all our problems and building new roads will only invite more traffic.
Toronto fancies itself Canada's answer to Manhattan - and in a way it is, if exorbitant rent and gridlock factor into the equation - but if that dream ever becomes reality, Ford's push to modernize transit and ease congestion will have played an outsized role.

Subject: Consumers; Convenience stores; Roads & highways

Document 83 of 227

Runciman reappointed as parks commission chairman

Author: Zajac, Ronald

Publication info: Zajac, Ronald. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/runciman-reappointed-as-parks-commission-chairman/docview/3141685960/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Runciman+reappointed+as+parks+commission+chairman&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-12-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=Zajac%2C+Ronald&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former senator Bob Runciman will serve a third and final term as chairman of the St. Lawrence Parks Commission.
Ontario Minister of Tourism, Culture and Gaming Stan Cho confirmed the reappointment in a letter to Runciman on Monday.
"Your participation in helping the St. Lawrence Parks Commission to deliver on its mandate is a valued contribution in strengthening our economy and the quality oflife enjoyed by residents across the province," the minister adds in the note.
The St. Lawrence Parks Commission manages a range of local and tourism attractions extending over 200 kms, from Kingston to near the Quebec border. Its facilities include thousands of hectares of parkland, and attractions such as Fort Henry, Upper Canada Village and Crysler Park Marina.
Runciman's 45 years in political office began in 1972 as a member of Brockville city council. That was followed by a 29-year career in the Ontario Legislature as the Leeds and Grenville area's MPP, during which time he held six cabinet posts under three different premiers. He was Interim Leader of the Ontario PC Party and held the position of Leader of the Official Opposition for more than two years.
He concluded his political career in the Canadian Senate, where he served from January 2010 to August 2017, when he reached the mandatory retirement age.
Runciman's second three-year term as parks commission chairman ends in mid-January. Parks commission chairs are limited to three terms, so this next term will be his last.
The former senator on Monday said he was approached during the summer about whether he would be interested in the reappointment, and he readily accepted.
Runciman, whose current term saw the overhaul of Brown's Bay Beach in Mallorytown, said the parks commission chairmanship is a substantive job.
"It's not just where you warm a seat; you can actually make things happen," he said.
The reappointment is also welcome news to Runciman personally after his retirement from politics.
"I'm someone who likes to keep busy," he said.
Other milestones of his second term include the privatization of the once money-losing Skywood Eco Adventure zip line site.
Runciman noted that the private company that took over the site, Treetop Trekking, now makes money from the Front of Yonge Township location and returns a percentage to the parks commission.
"They came in and they knew how to operate this show," he added.
Immediate priorities for the coming years include continuing to work with municipalities in the Cornwall area to tie in the parks commission's infrastructure with municipal sewage systems, said Runciman.
When he assumed the chairmanship, most of the parks system's infrastructure was 50 or 60 years old, he noted, and both the Brown's Bay project and the work in Stormont-Dundas-Glengarry illustrate the need to update it.
"These are major projects," said Runciman.
"We're looking at ways that we can increase revenues throughout the system," he added.
Meanwhile, Runciman hopes the commission can refine its management of parking so that visitors to areas such as Brown's Bay can be charged for the length of their stay, acknowledging that some people only use such sites for short periods like a swim in summer.

Document 84 of 227

Push to generate nuclear power on the moon

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/push-generate-nuclear-power-on-moon/docview/3141242999/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Push+to+generate+nuclear+power+on+the+moon&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-12-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Nuclear energy is undergoing a bit of a renaissance in Ontario as the government of Doug Ford looks to expand the energy source for the first time in decades. It's even spurring a push by the private sector to build out on an energy source that is getting a second look.
The moves by the Ford government come as part of an expected surge for electricity demand in the province during the coming years. It's coupled with a desire by the premier to turn Ontario into an energy powerhouse that exports green energy across North America.
During the last several months, the province has announced a massive procurement for new energy sources, promoted the continuing use of the Pickering and Darlington nuclear generating stations and introduced legislation that prioritizes nuclear energy. The province also suggested former coal plants in Nanticoke and Lambton, plus a site in Wellesleyville owned by Ontario Power Generation, could be the sites of new nuclear power plants.
Since at least 2019, Ford has been promoting the use of small modular reactors, a new technology with great promise, as a way to move energy into remote areas. They also could be used to add additional capacity for domestic use and export.
"Energy policy will be fundamental to the future of our province and Ontario has a choice: A pro-growth agenda that attracts investments, creates jobs and keeps energy costs down for people and businesses, or ideological dogma that offers false choices," Ford said in a mid-October speech to the Empire Club.
Since being appointed minister of energy and electrification, Stephen Lecce has been pushing the idea of nuclear as a key anchor.
"To meet soaring energy demands, we're working with communities to plan ahead and build for our future so that we can generate more power that is reliable and affordable for our families today and tomorrow," Lecce said last week.
The Independent Electricity System Operator has predicted that the province will see an increase in demand of by 75 per cent by 2050. To meet that demand, which is driven by an increase in population and industries such as artificial intelligence and server farms - and an expected increase in the use of electric vehicles - the province will need to increase production capacity.
While once Ontario fully embraced nuclear, a new plant hasn't been commissioned in Ontario since 1985. A combination of opposition from environmental groups and fear after the Chernobyl disaster in 1987 made nuclear unpalatable for many years.
Now, the Ford government is putting nuclear forward as a clean and green alternative.
All of this activity from the Ford government has led to interest from the private sector in exploring nuclear reactors. A Toronto-based company, Canadian Space Mining Corp. (CSMC), announced a deal with Canadian Nuclear Laboratories to try to commercialize the Slowpoke nuclear reactor technology first developed by the federal government in the 1960s.
The plan is to not only use the technology for domestic uses but also to try to deploy a small nuclear reactor to the moon.
"We are confident in our ability to deploy the world's first reactor on the moon, contributing to international space exploration as part of NASA's Artemis missions," said Daniel Sax, chief executive of CSMC.
"At the same time, we see immense potential for commercializing this technology here on Earth, offering a sustainable and reliable energy solution to replace diesel generators in remote and Indigenous communities across the Arctic."
While nuclear energy provides more than half of Ontario's electrical energy supplies, plus much needed medical isotopes, the industry was on its way out. Plans to decommission Pickering were moving ahead until the Ford government changed course.
One thing is clear, that plan to change course has kicked off a rebirth for nuclear that no one would have seen coming five years ago. blilley@postmedia.com

Document 85 of 227

Ontarians want booze tax cuts, may get them

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontarians-want-booze-tax-cuts-may-get-them/docview/3138982772/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontarians+want+booze+tax+cuts%2C+may+get+them&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-12-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Consider me shocked. People across Ontario would like to pay less tax, in particular for their beer - craft beer, that is.
With the Ford government considering a realignment of provincial taxes and fees for all alcohol in the province, the Ontario Craft Brewers decided to take the pulse of the province on taxes.
A poll conducted last month shows that 71 per cent of Ontario residents believe that taxes on craft beer should be lowered and 75 per cent believe lowering taxes will help ensure craft brewers are able to compete.
The government will have trouble disputing the poll results as the brewers smartly hired Campaign Research. That's the same polling firm the government and the PC Party use and it's owned by Doug Ford's political adviser, Nick Kouvalis.
The questions detailed how Ontario charges the highest tax rates of any province for craft beer and that means "local craft beer companies in Ontario pay eight times more in taxes than craft beer companies in Alberta." The poll then asked if taxes should be lowered and, not surprisingly, 44 per cent strongly agreed taxes in Ontario should be more in line with other parts of Canada, 27 per cent somewhat agreed with that, eight per cent somewhat disagreed and seven per cent strongly disagreed.
Who are the seven per cent of people who want Ontario's taxes significantly higher on any product? If they like paying taxes that much, maybe they can pay my tax bill.
In addition to 75 per cent of Ontario residents saying it was important to ensure craft brewers were competitive, 64 per cent said the government should look at more measures to help "such as increasing shelf space for local beer." That policy idea may be popular, but is tricky for the government due to trade deals, even the language in their policy of currently advocating 20 per cent shelf space for local products has to be extremely precise to avoid trade challenges.
The craft brewing industry is likely to get their wish on an overhaul of the province's current system, which is antiquated and overly complex. When the province announced it was speeding up the sale of beer and wine last May, they also announced a total review of taxation.
"The government will also conduct a broader review of taxes and fees on beer, wine and alcoholic beverages with the aim of promoting a fairer and more competitive marketplace for Ontario-based products and consumers," a news release said at the time.
The Ford government has taken some heat with claims that it is needlessly obsessed with alcohol - odd given Ford doesn't drink - but that criticism missed the fact that booze is a major contributor to the province's coffers. The LCBO provided the province with nearly $2.6 billion last year, while excise taxes on beer, wine and spirits brought in another $593 million for a combined total of about $3.2 billion.
That's before the sales taxes on booze sold in bars and restaurants is considered, or all the other taxes generated by that industry.
This means the government will have to be careful that any move it makes on changing taxes and fees doesn't cut off revenue too deeply. That said, a study on Ontario's alcohol expansion commissioned by the Convenience Industry Council found provincial tax revenue could increase by as much as $147 million annually due to expanded sales.
While the government hasn't made its position known at this point, industry insiders expect that sometime in the new year, possibly in a pre-election budget, the government will announce tax changes on booze. Transparency is a key word that keeps being thrown around, so is simplicity.
"The problem is the government wants to make changes that don't cost them anything," said one industry rep.
Another said they expect the Ford government to drop some fees in coming months, in particular at the LCBO.
The poll also asked about support or opposition to the expansion of where alcohol can be sold. It found that 23 per cent strongly support the changes, 31 per cent somewhat support them while 16 per cent somewhat oppose, 23 per cent strongly oppose and seven per cent declined to answer. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Taxes; Tax rates; Alcohol; Beer
