Document 113 of 227

Voters fed up with stale governments

Author: Braid, Don

Publication info: Braid, Don. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/voters-fed-up-with-stale-governments/docview/3120930082/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Voters+fed+up+with+stale+governments&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-10-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Braid%2C+Don&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the days leading up to the B.C. election last Saturday a friend in Vancouver made a point of asking everyone she met how they would vote, and why.
Almost to a person, she said, Conservatives were voting to get rid of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
Yes, they thought their provincial vote would bring down a federal leader.
Trudeau wasn't any part of that election, of course. B.C. doesn't even have a provincial party with "Liberal" in the name, although it boasts the usual plethora of fringe parties.
In Ottawa Wednesday, the entire media corps wallowed in the drama of caucus discontent with the prime minister.
The main message from various MPs, during and after a caucus meeting, was the Liberals are fully united behind Trudeau.
If they are solidly with Trudeau, they're nuts. If not, they lie.
The Liberals cannot, under any circumstance, be forced into an election. Nor can they submit to a chaotic leadership campaign that would lack a key ingredient, leadership.
Their only hope is to survive deep into 2025, praying the political climate will shift and they will gradually recover.
Meanwhile, Trudeau is only part of their problem.
Today's political poison is incumbency.
Voters in provincial elections are showing they've had it with governments that were in charge during the COVID plague, rampant inflation and decaying services, especially health care.
Governments that failed to resolve these crises face revolts, no matter what their political stripe.
New Brunswick is a key example. Blaine Higgs'Progressive Conservative regime was clobbered Oct. 21 by the Liberals under leader Susan Holt, who becomes the province's first female premier.
Higgs is a dour sort who alienated key groups, especially francophones. He'd been elected for 15 years and was premier for nearly seven, through all the hard times.
He became more deeply conservative as time went on.
Higgs was just another politician who failed the big tests. His government was ripe for defeat by any competent opposition, Liberal or whatever.
The B.C. election brought a dead heat between the governing NDP and John Rustad's Conservatives, a party that barely existed only months ago.
Final results won't be known until Monday. NDP Leader David Eby could survive as premier, but only with the support of two Green MLAs.
That could pull the NDP even further from the concerns many British Columbians have about drugs and crime.
In Saskatchewan, voters go to the polls Monday.
Premier Scott Moe's Saskatchewan Party, proudly and deeply conservative, has often seemed all-powerful. Moe has been very adept since he took over in 2018.
Now, he appears to be in serious trouble. A recent CTV poll shows the NDP, led by Carla Beck, running slightly ahead.
Three provincial elections all bring a message to the Trudeau Liberals - don't let the voters anywhere near you. Don Braid's column appears regularly with Postmedia. X: @DonBraid

Subject: Provincial elections; Conservatism; Polls & surveys; Leadership; Election results; Voters; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; Canada

Identifier / keyword: leading; election; friend; vancouver; point; asking

Document 114 of 227

Samidoun group issues cease-and-desist to politicians

Author: Dawson, Tyler

Publication info: Dawson, Tyler. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/samidoun-group-issues-cease-desist-politicians/docview/3120693097/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Samidoun+group+issues+cease-and-desist+to+politicians&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-10-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Dawson%2C+Tyler&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Samidoun, the anti-Israel advocacy group based in Vancouver that was recently added to Canada's terror list, is threatening to sue Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre for "recklessly and maliciously" accusing Samidoun of being a terrorist entity.
The letter also threatens to sue Dominic LeBlanc, Canada's public safety minister, and demands a retraction and apology from each of the three politicians.
"The Liberals cannot legislate away our right to free speech. If they contend we are terrorists, let them prove it in court," said Charlotte Kates, one of Samidoun's founding members, in a statement.
Kates herself has been under a Vancouver police hate-speech investigation for months, although no charges have been laid, following a speech in which she praised the Oct. 7 Hamas attack on Israel.
"The mask has been ripped off, and now they are trying to desperately put the genie back in the bottle," said Michael Westcott, the CEO of Allies for a Strong Canada, a group founded to stand against rising antisemitism in Canada.
The letter, sent by Toronto lawyer Stephen Ellis, is a ceaseand-desist notice to Trudeau, Poilievre and LeBlanc, warning them that they may face a libel lawsuit if they continue to call Samidoun a terrorist group. Ellis, a co-founder of the Legal Centre for Palestine, also represented former Ontario NDP MPP Sarah Jama, who threatened libel action against Ontario Premier Doug Ford, who said Jama has a "long and well-documented history of antisemitism."
Last Tuesday, the Canadian government added Samidoun to its list of terrorist entities, where it joins groups such as the Proud Boys, the Islamic State, Hamas and al-Qaida. The United States also announced that it was designating Samidoun a terrorist entity and Khaled Barakat, who is married to Kates, has been designated a terrorist by the United States' government and identified as a member of the leadership of the Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine (PFLP).
In a statement posted to X, Trudeau called Samidoun "a front organization that acts in association with terrorist groups like the PFLP" and notes that it "is now a listed terrorist entity in Canada and the United States."
Poilievre, who had called on the Liberals to designate Samidoun a terror entity before the actual designation, wrote on X on Oct. 8 that Samidoun had "clear and direct ties to designated terror groups abroad." In a separate post to Instagram calling on the federal government to "Ban Samidoun," Poilievre claimed Trudeau and the federal NDP "have allowed terrorist organizations to operate freely across Canada." In the cease-and-desist notice, Ellis lists these and other allegedly libellous statements Poilievre made about the group.
Sebastian Skamski, a spokesperson for Poilievre, wrote in an email that "they are a banned terrorist group, under Canadian law. Mr. Poilievre will make no apologies for calling out terrorists who chant 'death to Canada'and burn our flag."
Gabriel Brunet, a spokesperson for LeBlanc, responded with an emailed statement that did not address the cease-and-desist letter, but rather said Samidoun has previously been banned in Germany and that it has links with the PFLP.
Postmedia sought comment from Trudeau's office about the letter. It said it had nothing to add to what LeBlanc's office said.
LeBlanc has been quoted by CBC News as saying "violent extremism, acts of terrorism or terrorist financing have no place in Canadian society."
The Samidoun letter says that between the three politicians, their "defamatory publications to date have been viewed many thousands of times." It says the three "recklessly and maliciously" stated that Samidoun is a terror group.
"The suit will force the biggest mouths in government to actually show proof of the allegation of terrorist activity. It is very dangerous if we allow a government to decide who gets to enjoy full rights and who doesn't without showing actual proof," Ellis said in an email, while conceding he couldn't think of a precedent for this sort of case.
In a statement posted last week, Samidoun condemned its designation in Canada and the United States and said, "Samidoun does not have any material or organizational ties to entities listed on the terrorist lists of the United States, Canada or the European Union."
David Cooper, vice-president of government relations for the Centre for Israel and Jewish Affairs, said that if Samidoun feels the designation itself is unfair, there's a process set out whereby they can seek a review of Public Safety Canada's decision in the Federal Court.
"Short of them doing that, they have no right to tell anyone to cease and desist from commenting on their organization," Cooper said.
The letter says that the statements from Poilievre, Trudeau and LeBlanc claim Samidoun incites hatred and violence, is engaging in violence and intimidation and is a danger to the Jewish population and broader Canadian public.
It calls on Trudeau, Poilievre and LeBlanc to refrain from publishing the allegedly defamatory remarks, a retraction of the posts and the publication of an apology.
If that is not done, a libel lawsuit could follow.

Location: United States--US; Canada; Israel

Document 115 of 227

Bloc has put itself in a bind with ultimatum

Author: Libman, Robert

Publication info: Libman, Robert. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bloc-has-put-itself-bind-with-ultimatum/docview/3119330898/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bloc+has+put+itself+in+a+bind+with+ultimatum&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Libman%2C+Robert&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "Politics makes for strange bedfellows" is a common maxim, apparently adapted from a similar line in Shakespeare's The Tempest. So, will the leader of the separatist Bloc Québécois soon be sharing a bed with the governing federal Liberals of Justin Trudeau, and pulling the covers? Last month, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh ripped up a supply and confidence agreement that committed his party to propping up the minority Liberal government in exchange for concessions. Without Singh's 25 MPs, the Liberal government could fall, sparking an election, if another non-confidence motion was proposed by the opposition Conservatives, who currently enjoy a big lead in the polls. But another party in the House - the Bloc, with 33 seats - also holds the balance of power. Its leader, Yves-François Blanchet, has spelled out conditions for backing the Liberals, invoking an Oct. 29 deadline.
There has always been something unsavoury and sinister about the Bloc to me, like a cat among pigeons: a party dedicated to the breakup of a country sitting in its Parliament, collecting salaries and pensions from taxpayers. Canadians are certainly tolerant. Imagine the outcry if a party in the National Assembly advocated the secession of Montreal.
That's democracy, though.
Founded in 1990 by nationalist MPs who defected from the Liberals and Progressive Conservatives after the Meech Lake constitutional accord collapsed, the Bloc has elected MPs in every election - ranging from a high of 54 and official opposition status in 1993 to a low of four in 2011.
Bloquistes used to claim they hoped to be ousted from their jobs by completing their mission - but that hasn't happened in 35 years. Without having attained their raison d'être, they justify their presence as the only party defending Quebec's interests in Ottawa, though it's hard to pinpoint a tangible accomplishment. Many Quebecers - primarily from the regions - identify more closely with them than the other parties, but balance that against the reality the Bloc can never form government.
With an election on the horizon, it's important for the Bloc to show relevance - that it can deliver. Blanchet's demands include government approval of the party's bill that would grant additional old age pension benefits for 65-to 74-year-olds (at a cost of $16 billion); better protection for supply management; greater immigration powers for Quebec; and less federal encroachment in provincial jurisdictions.
The latter two issues have been particular bones of contention for Premier François Legault, which puts the Bloc in a funny position. Its provincial political sibling - the Parti Québécois - is currently leading in the polls, doing battle with Legault's Coalition Avenir Québec. Legault is fighting a losing battle with the feds on the immigration question, which the PQ has been feasting on in opposition, without proposing concrete solutions of its own. Any short-term win for Quebec on immigration powers weakens the PQ's leverage on this matter.
On the other issues, including pension benefits, Quebec's financial dependence on the federation is undeniable. This was clear in the conclusions of an economic analysis released this week exposing the utter folly of the PQ's study last year on the finances of an independent Quebec, which deceptively minimized the reliance on federal transfers and pensions.
If the Bloc succeeds in gaining concessions, it undermines the party's raison d'être and that of the PQ by demonstrating the benefits of Quebec being part of the Canadian federation. If they fail, they show that even in a balance-of-power situation, they are impotent.
If Trudeau pulls the covers back from Blanchet anyway, I suspect that Singh, notwithstanding his transparent theatrics with the agreement - and knowing how vulnerable he will also be in an election - somehow crawls back into bed and votes against any Liberal non-confidence motion. Robert Libman is an architect and planning consultant who has served as Equality Party leader and MNA, mayor of Côte-St-Luc and a member of the Montreal executive committee. He was a Conservative candidate in the 2015 federal election. x.com/robertlibman

Document 116 of 227

Voters just waiting for Trudeau to move along

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/voters-just-waiting-trudeau-move-along/docview/3118390886/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Voters+just+waiting+for+Trudeau+to+move+along&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-10-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Behind all the noise about whether Liberal MPs want to oust Justin Trudeau, is one major factor we shouldn't forget: voters are done with Trudeau. In fact, that's one reasons Charlottetown MP Sean Casey gave for arguing Trudeau should resign.
"The message that I've been getting loud and clear - and more and more strongly as time goes by - is that it is time for (Trudeau) to go," Casey told CBC. "And I agree."
It seems he believes voters are tuning out Trudeau, no shock to anyone following polls over the last year or so.
The Conservatives under Pierre Poilievre started gaining voter support and pulling away from Trudeau's Liberals in spring 2023. By the end of September 2023, their lead had moved into double-digit territory and then, earlier this year, moved to a 15-to 20-point lead.
The latest poll from Abacus Data has Conservatives at 43 per cent voter support, the Liberals at 22 per cent and the NDP at 19 per cent. As they have for months, the Conservatives lead in every region except Quebec, and in Quebec, they are tied with the Grits at 24 per cent, well behind the Bloc Québécois at 36 per cent.
The Ontario numbers are where the Trudeau Liberals need to worry the most.
In the last federal election, the Liberals won 78 of the province's 121 seats. They dominated in Toronto, in the Ottawa area, in Southwestern Ontario and now ... all those seats are at risk.
According to Abacus, the Conservatives lead in every region of the province, including Toronto.
Abacus has the Conservatives taking 47 per cent of the votes across Ontario, the Liberals 25 per cent and the NDP 20 per cent. In Toronto proper, the Conservatives are polling at 47 per cent, the Liberals at 26 per cent and the NDP at 18 per cent.
Some seat projections say the Liberals could be reduced to just one of Toronto's 25 seats next election.
When voters, especially in what were once strongholds, are tuning you out, does changing the leader matter? If more MPs step forward and Trudeau is forced out, will anything change? It's tough to see the Liberals recovering. It's not impossible, but it is unlikely.
Just as it is unlikely that dozens of Liberal MPs will step forward and call for Trudeau to resign. If they felt that strongly, they'd have done so already.
We've been hearing about dissension in the ranks for months and before Casey, only New Brunswick MP Wayne Long had called for Trudeau to go. Montreal MP Alexandra Mendes said in September her constituents want Trudeau to go, though she felt he should stay on.
If Liberal MPs haven't found their spines by now, they are unlikely to do so in the near future.
Meanwhile, Trudeau is trying to use the foreign interference issue to change the channel. He came out hard against India Monday and appeared at the Foreign Interference Commission Wednesday. He clearly hopes to show that he's still prime minister and is still in charge.
Based on the polling, those optics may only matter to the Liberal caucus and Hill media. Voters have moved on, they're just waiting for Trudeau to move along. blilley@postmedia.com

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

Document 117 of 227

Four more Liberal ministers not seeking re-election

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/four-more-liberal-ministers-not-seeking-re/docview/3118113311/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Four+more+Liberal+ministers+not+seeking+re-election&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-10-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Four more Liberal ministers will not be seeking re-election in the next federal election, sources say, which will force Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to proceed with an imminent cabinet shuffle at a time when an internal caucus revolt is brewing.
As first reported by Radio-Canada, Minister of National Revenue Marie-Claude Bibeau, Minister of Sport and Physical Activity Carla Qualtrough, Minister of Northern Affairs Dan Vandal and Minister responsible for the Federal Economic Development Agency for Southern Ontario Filomena Tassi recently told Trudeau they will not be running again.
All four of them were first elected in 2015, and both Bibeau and Qualtrough have been in cabinet since then.
In a statement shared on X, Tassi said the reason for making this decision is "deeply personal" and that it is time for her to be closer to home with family. She had requested a lighter workload in 2022 after her husband had suffered two strokes.
Tassi called her decision to leave politics "bittersweet" and said she is "deeply grateful" to Trudeau for entrusting her with a variety of portfolios in cabinet.
"I am honoured that he believed in me and thank him for his understanding and empathy. I wish him the very best now and in the future. I believed in him in 2015 and I believe in him now," she added in her statement.
Government sources confirmed that Vandal and Qualtrough will not be seeking re-election, and that they would be publishing statements later Thursday.
Bibeau's office said she was out of the country until next week, but a government source confirmed that she is not running again. Bibeau had previously hinted that she might be interested in running for municipal politics in Sherbrooke but that she would first finish her mandate as an MP.
The source said that the four ministers each have "highly personal reasons" for not running again but they have nothing to do with Trudeau's leadership.
CTV and Globe and Mail have already reported that Liberal MPs will be asking Trudeau to step down at their weekly caucus meeting next week, but it is unclear if the perspective of a cabinet shuffle might discourage some vying to become minister from coming forward.
On Tuesday, Sean Casey, the MP for Charlottetown, became the first Liberal MP to publicly say he wants Trudeau's resignation since news broke this past weekend that some members of the party's caucus have been organizing behind the scenes to collect signatures and force the leader to step down.
Speaking in Winnipeg on Wednesday, Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault says Trudeau continues to have his support and suggested the same is true for most other Liberal MPs.
"I would argue that he has the support of the vast majority of the caucus," he said.
Over the weekend, Liberal MP Chandra Arya wrote an email to his colleagues urging them to not precipitate the party into a leadership race with no clear successor waiting in the wings.
"I foresee a vicious battle for leadership which will fracture the fragile party structure with vastly different ideas and personalities - the results will be disastrous."
Trudeau had already lost two senior ministers in recent months. Seamus O'Regan resigned from cabinet for personal reasons and was replaced as Minister of Labour by Steven MacKinnon this summer. Pablo Rodriguez resigned to run for the Quebec Liberal Party leadership and his position as Transport Minister was taken on by Anita Anand. with additional reporting from Stephanie Taylor

Document 118 of 227

Timing around India affair raises flags

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/timing-around-india-affair-raises-flags/docview/3117769127/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Timing+around+India+affair+raises+flags&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Monday, Canada announced the expulsion of six Indian diplomats over allegations of foreign interference. In a news conference, RCMP Commissioner Michael Duheme warned of widespread violence, homicides and a public security threat linked to agents of the Indian government. New Delhi has rejected the allegations, and sent six Canadian diplomats packing in return.
The accusations are appalling, and no country should be targeting the citizens of another on foreign soil. But why are these revelations coming out now? On the surface, they provide a great distraction from the weekend's attempted internal coup on the prime minister. The story of a revolt among Liberal MPs, 30 of whom apparently signed a letter asking the PM to quit, hit the news cycle on Saturday. Then, presto: on Monday, the RCMP dropped its bombshell, allowing the PM to take to the airwaves and sound all grave and solemn and prime minister-like, defending Canadian sovereignty.
But that would be too obvious. So let's look at what else is going on in Ottawa, namely the Hogue commission on foreign interference. On Friday, the commission heard from Public Safety Minister Bill Blair that his office sat for 54 days on a warrant to investigate Ontario Liberal MPP Michael Chan, accused of doing China's bidding in Canada. Blair offered "no explanation" for the delay, and neither did his chief of staff.
Three days later, boom: India stands accused of being the major agent of transnational repression in Canada and the focus switches from the government's failings to the murderous machinations of New Delhi. And who is responsible for ensuring that the RCMP is "effective, accountable and addresses the government's priorities?" You guessed it: Bill Blair.
According to Chapter 5 of the 2023 NSICOP report on the federal policing mandate of the RCMP, while there exists a core concept of police independence, the minister provides direction on "national security investigations in sensitive sectors," including issuing directives to the RCMP "to implement special measures when investigating terrorist offences or activities which have an impact on fundamental institutions in Canadian society, including academia, politics, religion, the media and trade unions."
I'm not saying India isn't engaged in transnational repression, but the timing of this exposure is highly convenient for both the minister and the government. It's also highly convenient for China, which relishes any chance to bash India, its chief rival for dominance of the Indo-Pacific.
For whom is it inconvenient? Not just India.
It's also inconvenient for Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives. Left-wing media outlets made noise last month about alleged Indian efforts to build a "war chest" of funds to disparage Trudeau and help elect Poilievre. The same month, the Hindustan Times reported that Poilievre told Canadian Hindu media that, "Anti-Hindu and Hinduphobia agendas have no place (in Canada). I detest how the prime minister has divided our people. We were getting along in this country. Now look, everybody is fighting."
And then there's NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, who, until August, was Trudeau's loyal coalition supporter. When he was deputy leader of the Ontario NDP in 2015, Singh spoke at a Sikh "sovereignty rally" in San Francisco that venerated violent Khalistani leader Jarnail Singh Bhindranwale, and accused India of "trying to wipe us out." In an interview on a Punjabi station in Canada in 2018, when asked to comment on Khalistani separatist referendums, Singh said, "In any place if people seek freedom, it is their right to seek freedom."
Why is India apparently willing to kill to crush the Khalistani movement? Sikhs have been demanding a separate state in the Punjab since India was partitioned in 1947. In the 1980s, the Khalistanis engaged in major acts of terrorism, including the bombing of Air India Flight 182 in 1985, which killed 329 people and was attributed to Sikh extremists in Canada. Today, Khalistanis regularly hold referendums in the U.S. and Canada on creating a separate state, with the goal holding an independence vote in the Punjab in 2025.
None of this is an excuse for murdering Canadian citizens. But nor should it be an excuse for this government to play diaspora politics and shield its own misdeeds. Postmedia News Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Criminal investigations; Referendums; Murders & murder attempts; Politics; Prime ministers; Transnationalism; Sovereignty

Location: China; Canada; India

Document 119 of 227

'Doubledouble' rural top-up helps Gananoque business

Author: Dempsey, Keith

Publication info: Dempsey, Keith. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/doubledouble-rural-top-up-helps-gananoque/docview/3117768971/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Doubledouble%27+rural+top-up+helps+Gananoque+business&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Dempsey%2C+Keith&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: During a chilly, windy and cloudy Tuesday in Gananoque, federal Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault took to the patio of Pistachio Café and announced the business would be receiving $10,000 as part of the Canada Carbon Rebate.
Jenn Lund, co-owner of the café, who was standing beside the minister, gasped. Then she did a double-take at Guilbeault.
"I didn't know the number until he said it," laughed an elated Lund afterwards. "And then I was like, 'oh my goodness', this is going to help so much!" Guilbeault, Canada's Minister of Environment and Climate Change, was in Gananoque to announce that the latest quarterly Canada Carbon Rebate payments are being delivered to their bank accounts and mailboxes, with a one-time "double-double" rural top-up.
In the 2024 federal budget, the government doubled the rural topup from 10 per cent to 20 per cent of the Canada Carbon Rebate base amount, in an effort to support Canadians who live in rural areas and small communities, as they often drive longer distances and have higher energy needs.
Given the delayed passage of the budget, on Tuesday, families received an added one-time 20 per cent to make up retroactively for the doubling of the rural top-up on the April 15 and July 15 payments, effectively adding a one-time 40 per cent top-up to the base amount of this quarter's Canada Carbon Rebate.
For a family of four in Gananoque, this means receiving a payment of $392 on Tuesday, and a total of $1,344 in Canada Carbon Rebates this fiscal year.
"The Canada Carbon Rebate is a cornerstone of our plan, which is holding big polluters accountable and putting money in the pockets of families - including here in Gananoque, Ontario, and other small towns across this country," said Guilbeault.
"The facts are clear - every day, Canada's economy is growing stronger as we drive down emissions, thanks in large part to Canadians and their Canada Carbon Rebates."
The minister's visit comes as the Trudeau government lags in the polls and a federal election could be imminent. Atop the polls are Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives, who have made "axing" the carbon tax a key campaign promise.
The Liberals argue the Canada Carbon Rebate and the rural supplement are part of a broader government effort to ease financial pressures on Canadians while also supporting the government's plan to combat climate change.
That's music to the ears of the Lund family, who run a vegetarian and environmentally friendly café.
"I could see the things (the rebate) could go towards," said Lund, still smiling from the news. "Because in the back of your mind, every day, you're thinking to yourself, 'how am I going to pay for that', or, 'is there interest on that?'It all gets stressful. But knowing that (rebate) is coming, allows me to breathe easy tonight."
Pistachio Café opened in 2021 on King Street East. The business is operated by Jenn and her brother, James, and the building is owned by their father, Jim.
"So, we're still new, still paying off the debts, the loaning of the equipment, etc., so this rebate can go towards so many things, helping keep us open, helping us be a safe space for the community," said Lund. Keith Dempsey is a Local Journalism Initiative reporter who works out of the Brockville Recorder and Times. The Local Journalism Initiative is funded by the Government of Canada.

Document 120 of 227

'They are not there yet'; Provinces should improve access before forcing addicts into treatment: Minister

Author: Taylor, Stephanie

Publication info: Taylor, Stephanie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/they-are-not-there-yet-provinces-should-improve/docview/3115843440/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27They+are+not+there+yet%27%3B+Provinces+should+improve+access+before+forcing+addicts+into+treatment%3A+Minister&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-10-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Taylor%2C+Stephanie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Provincial leaders ought to focus on improving access to treatment before deciding whether those struggling with addiction should decide to go on their own or involuntarily, Mental Health and Addictions Minister Ya'ara Saks said Friday.
Saks appeared before reporters to announce the launch of a $150-million fund that cities and First Nations can apply to access to fund programs that help them respond to the overdose crisis, which public health experts say is driven by an increasingly toxic drug supply.
Asked about growing calls for involuntary treatment on Friday, Saks said jurisdictions first need to ensure they have treatment services in place, "which currently they don't have."
"Before we contemplate voluntary or involuntary treatment, I would like to see provinces and territories ensuring that they actually have treatment access scaled to need," she said. "They are not there yet. They have the resources and tools to do it."
Leaders such as B.C. NDP Leader David Eby, who is campaigning for reelection in the province known as ground zero for the crisis, has promised to open facilities that offer involuntary care for those with serious addictions and mental illness.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith has also promised her United Conservative Party government would introduce a law allowing family members, police or physicians to seek involuntary treatment on behalf of someone else.
Federal Opposition Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre also told reporters on Thursday he supports involuntary treatment for minors and those in prisons struggling with mental illness and addictions, but is still studying the issue when it comes to adults.
Saks said the role of the federal government is to support the provinces when it comes to health care, which she underscored that provinces and territories are responsible for administering.
"Before we talk about involuntary or voluntary treatment, I would like to see them utilize and access the robust $200-billion worth of health-care agreements that have been signed across the country," she said, referring to the deal announced with jurisdictions last year.
"This is about political will and allocating the resources that they have to providing treatment access to those who need it in a way that is timely and accessible."
Poilievre has been leading the charge in criticizing Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's handling of the overdose crisis, deaths from which the federal health agency began tracking in 2016.
More than 47,000 people have died from opioid-related poisonings from January 2016 until March 2024, according to the Public Health Agency of Canada.
Many public health, addictions experts and other advocates say the staggering death toll comes from an increasingly toxic supply of drugs.
Poilievre says the federal government has failed to offer enough treatment options to help Canadians recover. He has slammed its support of supervised consumption sites, which he calls "drug dens," as fuelling addiction, and its funding of "safer supply" programs that provide users with pharmaceutical alternatives like hydromorphone to prevent them from using street drugs.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford has pledged to close 10 supervised consumption sites, which are facilities that allow people to bring their own drugs to use under the watch of someone else in the event they may overdose.
Ford cited public safety concerns in his decision to shutter the sites.
On Friday, Saks defended these facilities as having reversed around 58,000 overdoses since 2016, saying that when access to them are closed, it endangers people's lives.
She added there are examples of jurisdictions where such sites operate well within communities and that concerns around public safety are properly addressed.
"Jurisdictions are responsible for the health services that they provide," Saks said Friday.
"We continue to provide guidelines and standards. We provide the regulatory structures for safe consumption sites that are adhered to by the operators, but yes, provinces have a key part to play in this." staylor@postmedia.com

Subject: Provinces; Public health; Drug overdose; Addictions; Public safety; Pharmacists; Substance abuse treatment; Mental disorders

Identifier / keyword: provincial; leaders; ought; focus; improving; access

Title: 'They are not there yet'; Provinces should improve access before forcing addicts into treatment: Minister

Publication title: Kingston Whig - Standard; Kingston, Ont.

First page: A.8

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Oct 12, 2024

Dateline: Ottawa

Section: Canada

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Kingston, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 11974397

Source type: Newspaper

Document 121 of 227

UWindsor must rescind deal with protesters

Author: Cooke, Dave

Publication info: Cooke, Dave. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/uwindsor-must-rescind-deal-with-protesters/docview/3115843433/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=UWindsor+must+rescind+deal+with+protesters&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-10-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Cooke%2C+Dave&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I have been a proud member of the University of Windsor community since 1971, when I attended as a student and graduated in 1975.
Two years after that, I was elected to the Ontario legislature as the NDP MPP for Windsor-Riverside.
Later, as minister of education and training in the Bob Rae government, I was responsible for all levels of elementary, secondary and post-secondary education.
After leaving the political world, I was invited to join the university's board of governors in 2000, serving as chair from 2007-09. While we encountered some challenging times during those years, I always felt proud of our university.
This year has been different.
I am not only deeply disappointed, but also angry and embarrassed about the University of Windsor's negotiated agreement with pro-Palestinian protesters who established an encampment on the university grounds.
That decision has outraged not just the members of our Jewish community. It also has caused people of all faiths, from all walks of life, to become angry and disillusioned.
To read this agreement is to see that not only is it one-sided, it does not even acknowledge the terrorist attack of Oct. 7, 2023, in Israel by Hamas. And while the agreement calls for a ceasefire - which has widespread support - it makes no mention of releasing the hostages taken, or the 1,200 Jews who were murdered that day.
The agreement the university struck with pro-Palestinian protesters essentially blames all Jews for decisions made by Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu in the aftermath of the Oct. 7 assault by Hamas. An example of that is the sanctioning of Israeli academic institutions.
UWindsor president Robert Gordon exercised poor judgment entering into this agreement. As an afterthought, he offered to draft a similar agreement with the Jewish community. The Jewish community wisely declined.
When questioned, the university released a statement saying Gordon did not first come to the board of governors because he considered it an "operational matter."
At that point, the chair should have insisted it be brought to the board for discussion.
While he was out of the country, it was announced Gordon would resign at the end of the academic year.
A few weeks earlier, chancellor Mary Jo Haddad indicated she would be leaving in the middle of her second term.
The search for new senior leadership will be difficult because of the extreme damage that has been done to the university's reputation.
In fact, if these agreements are not rescinded by the board of governors, finding suitable candidates for the president's job might be impossible. These agreements will be an albatross around the neck, a reputational burden that will require a new president's utmost attention, with precious time spent dealing with this debacle.
As an alumnus, as well as a former board chair, and as someone who cares deeply about the future of the University of Windsor, I implore the current chair and all members of the board of governors to do the right thing.
Rescind these agreements.
Take the first steps to healing. Give the new president a chance for success. Students and the community are counting on you. Dave Cooke is a former University of Windsor board of governors chair, a former Windsor MPP and was Ontario's minister of education from 1993-95.

Subject: Agreements; Jewish people; Presidents; Jewish life & ethics; Governors; Education; Truces & cease fires

Document 122 of 227

Ex-minister says time to 'clear the air' about alleged traitors; Committee accused former and current MPs of assisting 'foreign state actors'

Author: Nardi, Christopher

Publication info: Nardi, Christopher. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ex-minister-says-time-clear-air-about-alleged/docview/3115601838/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ex-minister+says+time+to+%27clear+the+air%27+about+alleged+traitors%3B+Committee+accused+former+and+current+MPs+of+assisting+%27foreign+state+actors%27&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-10-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Nardi%2C+Christopher&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former public safety minister Marco Mendicino says there is a need to "clear the air" about allegations some MPs are wittingly participating in foreign interference in Canadian elections because the discourse about the claims is devolving into a "kangaroo court."
Testifying at the Public Inquiry into Foreign Interference (PIFI) Thursday, the MP and former Liberal minister said he was concerned about the discourse around the alleged presence of treasonous current or former MPs, as detailed by an intelligence review agency report earlier this year.
In June, the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians (NSICOP) reported that some MPs were "wittingly assisting" foreign state actors. The report noted unnamed MPs had provided "privileged information" to foreign influence actors or had responded to "requests or direction of foreign officials to improperly influence" Parliament to the benefit of a foreign state.
The shocking revelations have since sparked calls for the government to reveal the identity of the alleged "treasonous" parliamentarians, which the Liberals have so far resisted.
During his testimony, Mendicino was told by counsel for NDP MP Jenny Kwan that she has since been labelled a traitor by some individuals simply for being a parliamentarian of Chinese descent. That, despite the fact she is a frequent critic of the Chinese government.
Mendicino responded that the NSICOP report had gone "further" than what CSIS or the prime minister's national security and intelligence adviser had assessed when reading the intelligence the review body's report was based on.
Earlier this week, current national security and intelligence adviser Nathalie Drouin said she had seen no evidence there are "traitors" in Parliament and that some of the conclusions in the NSICOP report made her "very uncomfortable."
Mendicino said he believes there is a need to "clear the air" about NSICOP's findings.
"I am very worried that the entire conversation around foreign interference and parliamentarians is being transformed into a kangaroo court with very little regard for the process of understanding how we assess intelligence," he told the inquiry.
"I think it is extremely important that we heed the opinion and the evidence that has been given to this commission, from Ms. Drouin, from CSIS, around the fact that ... this NSICOP report has gone further than where they are at in the assessment of the intelligence," he added.
During his testimony, Mendicino also brushed aside a claim by Conservative MP Michael Chong that Canada has become a "playground" for foreign interference.
"I fundamentally disagree with that characterization," Mendicino told the inquiry. Canada is "an obstacle course for foreign interference" since the government enacted a series of new measures and laws to fight it in the last year, he added.
Mendicino was public safety minister from 2021 to 2023.
The inquiry has also spent considerable time discussing the fact that a CSIS warrant authorization allegedly targeting Liberal power broker and former Ontario cabinet minister Michael Chan sat in the office of Bill Blair - Mendicino's predecessor in the public safety portfolio - for 54 days before it was signed.
Without speaking to that warrant directly, Mendicino told the inquiry that he reviewed and decided on warrant authorization requests quickly every time one was brought to his attention.
"There were no undue delays in the approvals of warrants during my tenure as public safety minister," Mendicino said.
CSIS officials have previously testified that the average turnaround for a warrant authorization to be signed after it was sent to minister's office for approval was four to 10 days. Postmedia News cnardi@postmedia.com

Document 123 of 227

B.C. NDP regain lead in poll

Author: Chan, Cheryl

Publication info: Chan, Cheryl. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/b-c-ndp-regain-lead-poll/docview/3115241959/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=B.C.+NDP+regain+lead+in+poll&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-10-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Chan%2C+Cheryl&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With 11 days to go before the B.C. election, the governing NDP has regained the lead from the B.C. Conservative Party among decided voters, a new Leger poll suggests.
John Rustad's Conservatives had been polling ahead of David Eby's incumbent New Democrats since mid-September following the collapse of the B.C. United Party in late August.
But Leger's latest poll, conducted online among 1,002 British Columbians from Oct. 3 to 7, found 47 per cent of decided voters are supporting the NDP, while 42 per cent said they'll vote for the Conservatives.
That's a change from the previous Leger poll ending Sept. 30, which had the Conservatives leading the NDP by three percentage points.
The shift was due to a pretty clear "switcheroo" among two specific demographic groups, women and 18-34 year olds, said Steve Mossop, Leger's executive vice-president in Western Canada.
"It's really clear. Almost up to this point in the campaign, Rustad has been doing unexpectedly well among youth and female voters. All of a sudden, that balloon has popped."
The Leger poll suggests Tory support among female voters dropped eight percentage points from 42 per cent in late September to 34 per cent - a "pretty massive drop in that demographic within a short week," said Mossop.
That support shifted to the NDP. Its support among female voters jumped 10 percentage points to 56 per cent.
It's a similar situation among younger voters: The NDP has 50 per cent support among voters 18 to 34 years old, compared to 38 per cent for the Conservatives, the poll suggests. In the previous week, the Conservatives led this group by eight percentage points at 47 per cent with the NDP trailing at 39 per cent.
The poll indicates the Conservatives'early appeal among female and younger voters - groups that traditionally lean progressive - has faded, said Mossop.
Recent controversies, such as a video showing Rustad appearing to equate COVID-19 public health measures to the Nuremberg trials "blew up on Rustad and that doesn't help among progressive voters," Mossop said.

Subject: Females; Voters

Document 124 of 227

Arrogance blinds feds to hunger for tax cuts

Author: Braid, Don

Publication info: Braid, Don. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/arrogance-blinds-feds-hunger-tax-cuts/docview/3113188055/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Arrogance+blinds+feds+to+hunger+for+tax+cuts&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-10-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Braid%2C+Don&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It can get tiresome, watching Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre pop up in Parliament to cry "Axe the tax," and "we need a carbon tax election."
But he's smart to keep pounding the same stake into Liberal hearts. High taxes really are a huge issue countrywide, and every politician facing election knows it. The mystery is why the Liberals don't.
Signs of taxation toxicity abound in three provincial election campaigns now underway in British Columbia, New Brunswick and Saskatchewan.
New Brunswick PC Premier Blaine Higgs offers one key promise before voting day, Oct. 21.
He would lower the Harmonized Sales Tax by two points, to 13 per cent.
The Higgs tax cut would apparently cost the treasury $1.6 billion a year. That would be a direct benefit to taxpayers.
Poilievre told the Commons on Thursday federal taxes are depriving New Brunswick of funding for hospitals and schools He demanded a carbon tax election.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau gave the rote response: The Conservatives don't care about climate change, and the carbon tax works.
In B.C., NDP Premier David Eby made the most striking tax promise of all even before he called the election now set for Oct. 19.
He promised to ditch the province's consumer carbon tax, but only if Ottawa agrees not to impose the federal tax in its place.
That provincial tax, imposed in 2008, was the first of its kind in North America.
There's not the slightest chance Ottawa would give B.C. a blanket tax exemption. They'd have to do the same for Alberta and Ontario, for starters.
Eby hasn't answered the crucial question: Will he keep this promise if Poilievre wins the federal election and abolishes the federal tax? That pledge was just wind and everyone knew it. Its only value is to show how angry B.C. voters are about taxation levels.
With the campaign underway, Eby now comes up with a real-world promise. He would cut provincial income tax by $1,000 for an average family, starting next year.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith won a provincial election last year on just such a promise.
Her campaign gave the impression that the tax break would come in the next budget. But Smith reneged. The budget deferred the full break until 2027.
That made people very angry. She now promises to keep the promise soon.
The message was obvious.
In these times, a tax promise is the most dangerous one to break.
The Saskatchewan drive to an election Oct. 28 begins with duelling tax offers from both the ruling Saskatchewan Party and the NDP.
Premier Scott Moe promises income tax cuts that would save families about $1,000 a year for four years.
NDP Leader Carla Beck responds with a plan to suspend provincial gasoline tax collection for six months, and remove provincial sales tax from children's clothes and ready-to-eat foods.
The promises are all over the place, but politicians who fail to make them are doomed.
Liberal arrogance blinds them to reality. Don Braid's column appears regularly with Postmedia X: @DonBraid

Subject: Taxation; Provincial elections; Tax cuts; Sales taxes; Carbon; Income taxes; Environmental tax; Tax elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Taxation Tax cuts Sales taxes Income taxes Environmental tax Tax elections

Document 125 of 227

Ford's tunnel plan a way to change channel

Author: Baranyai, Robin

Publication info: Baranyai, Robin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-tunnel-plan-way-change-channel/docview/3113188023/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+tunnel+plan+a+way+to+change+channel&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-10-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Baranyai%2C+Robin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Changing the channel is a pretty familiar gambit. Predictable, even. When a public figure lands in the hot seat, expect them to schedule a funding announcement, unveil a big new plan or identify a threat; anything to change focus.
Remember when Donald Trump tried to "buy" Greenland? Six days before that story broke, a whistleblower filed a complaint detailing how the former U.S. president illegally pressured Ukraine to investigate his political rival, Joe Biden. By Christmas, the House would vote to impeach Trump. Nevertheless, news outlets diligently reported on Greenland's geopolitical importance, the potential for mineral extraction beneath its melting ice, and the consternation of the Danish prime minister, who seemed astonished to have to say: "Greenland is not for sale."
By now, you'd think we'd be wise to this timeworn tactic. Yet here we are, reporting on a mega-tunnel under Highway 401.
Not that we have much choice. Premier Doug Ford has proposed - indeed, promised - to tunnel under the busiest highway in North America. Like it or not, it's news. Ford suggested new vehicle lanes and transit could stretch from Mississauga to Markham. That's about 55 kilometres.
In quick succession, the premier announced both a feasibility study, and a determination to forge ahead regardless of what the study might say. This is a man desperate to change the channel.
Still, it must be reported.
Journalists are diligently putting Ford's "visionary" promise in perspective. They've plumbed comparisons to Boston's Big Dig, which delivered 12 km of underground highway for $14.8 billion. They've reported the reactions of other party leaders, from "silly" to "a complete joke." They've called on experts to test Ford's assumption that adding vehicle lanes will, in fact, relieve traffic congestion.
The argument for more and bigger highways is an enduring zombie of public policy, repeatedly shot down but very hard to kill. Former New York City traffic commissioner Sam Schwartz told CBC News what study after study has shown: "the more lanes you introduce, the more people are likely to drive."
Schwartz described proven alternatives, such as transit investments and high-occupancy toll lanes, which relieve congestion by reducing traffic. Even building transit down the centre of the 401 would make more sense than what Ford's proposing.
Meanwhile, away from the cameras, the RCMP continues its criminal investigation into the Greenbelt scandal.
Two provincial watchdogs already sharply criticized the government's improperly managed site selection process, which stood to enrich a handful of developers to the tune of $8.28 billion. The premier's office has denied any involvement in choosing specific sites for development. Ultimately Ford reversed course, leaving the Greenbelt intact.
During the summer, this government commanded a striking number of headlines, including funding announcements, alcohol in corner stores, a retreat on supervised consumption sites, and shuttering the Ontario Science Centre. Some of these actions are just the business of governing, and some may be unnecessary and ill-advised, but the premier kept that remote firmly in his grasp.
Through it all, the media reported on hints of an early election, perhaps next spring. Ontario could go to the polls ahead of the results of what is a criminal investigation. This government has no business contemplating an election before voters have answers.
A mega-tunnel under the 401 may strike voters as decisive, out-of-the-box thinking, or a boondoggle in the making. It's actually something else.
This is our Greenland, folks. write.robin@baranyai.ca

Subject: Voters; Roads & highways

Location: Greenland

Document 126 of 227

U.S. presidential election another 'watershed'moment for canada

Author: More, David

Publication info: More, David. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/u-s-presidential-election-another-watershedmoment/docview/3110818186/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=U.S.+presidential+election+another+%27watershed%27moment+for+canada&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-09-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=More%2C+David&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Donald Trump recently sounded off about the possibility of the "Canadian turned it back, all of that water would come right down here and right into Los Angeles."
Many, including experts of course, scoff at the very idea. They say he has no idea what he's talking about. Others shrug that it's illegal, anyway. After all, Canada's Parliament banned bulk water exports in 2013. A plethora of other laws regulate such things on both sides of the border at state, provincial and national levels, along with bilateral treaties such as the Shared Boundary Waters Treaties that also govern how we share this water with our gigantic neighbour. So, no problem, right? Take a deep breath.
Some of us remember the "National Security" excuse used by the previous Trump administration during the NAFTA 2 renegotiations, to cover slapping illegal trade tariffs on a wide variety of Canadian exports, and those stuck, for a time. Thoughtful observers could be found thinking this was a repeat run of the "weapons of mass destruction" fibs that George W. Bush made up to justify terminating the rule of Saddam Hussein. It is now clear that all of that National Security horsepuckey was simply and deliberately made up by Trump's trade negotiators to pressure Canada into knuckling under. Everyone knows Trump has a thing for tariffs. But playing hardball economics is one thing, war is another, right? Right? Wrong. Such a fine distinction is arguably moot. Consider that the deliberate fabrications of the younger Bush's administration were used to justify a war that piled up perhaps half a million dead (mostly Iraquis). A war that created ISIS, empowered Iran and destabilized the entire Middle East for a generation. No, National Security as an excuse for action is nothing to scoff at. And that particular excuse, among an infinite number of others can now be wielded at will by a president whom the right-wing, Trump-appointed majority U.S. Supreme Court has deemed 100 per cent immune from legal prosecution for acts performed as part of their official duties.
Enter those pesky rules and treaties about water. National Security could very easily be used to, er, trump all of those red tapeish, bothersome, socialist laws that in some eyes simply allow Canada to keep its "selfish" hold on more than 20 per cent of the world's fresh water. At present, more than 50 million of the United States'most ardent Republican MAGA voters live in Southwestern United States, if you include Nevada, Colorado and Utah. Parts of the region are desertifying at a very rapid rate, while at the same time the region's population is growing at almost triple the rate of the United States as a whole (2023). Much of the American Southwest is in the third decade of a megadrought now being predicted to last almost indefinitely. Underground aquifers such as the famous Ogallala are drying up. The Colorado River, which rises on the Continental Divide in north central Colorado, is the principal irrigation, potable water and power source for a very large fraction of this population. The Colorado is at historic low levels. For years, because of upstream diversions, the river's waters have no longer reached the Pacific Ocean at all. The Colorado River now just disappears into the desert, drying up and vanishing miles from the ocean along the U.S./Mexico border in Baja California.
Despite all this evidence of problems that may be stimulating some irrational thinking south of the border, the experts remain calm. One authority said, "There is growing consensus in both that water should generally be kept within its natural drainage basins and used more efficiently." Sure. How very reasonable sounding. Logical. Rational.
But I don't buy it. Ask the B.C.
Government how negotiations are proceeding with renewal of the Columbia River Treaty there.
Even Canadians are murmuring that our country is going to have to be, um, "flexible" about a new Treaty, even though our Arrow Lakes Reservoirs are a 20-year low, and the water diverted from the Arrow Lakes to the United States keeps their reservoirs, such as Franklin D. Roosevelt Lake, chock full.
Funny how that works. I guess that is an example of our legendary flexibility at work. The Treaty expires this month. Yes, this month; September 30, 2024. There is one full year beginning in October to negotiate a new one after this one expires. But let's be at ease, Canucks, we've been told negotiations with the big guys are under way.
In normal times that might be enough to reassure some. But there seems little doubt that Trump's recent verbal hallucination, in California, about a big, Canadian tap they can force us to turn was referring to the Columbia River. Evidently, someone has put this item on his radar recently. It is known that in 2016, an engineer placed on Trump's desk a comprehensive plan for a pipeline from Lake Michigan to Lake Mead to refill the Colorado. He ignored it then. Perhaps this time he will not.
To this Canadian, should Trump be re-elected in a few short weeks from now, it would be child's play for the man to create a pseudo national emergency over lack of fresh water to satisfy his voters in the Southwest. That done, almost anything you can imagine becomes entirely possible, along with some things I am very sure you and I have never thought about.
Shared Waters Treaties Bah! That was then and this is now. Canadian legislation? Surely you jest! Our domestic laws will be utterly meaningless if they impinge on U.S. security needs. American State legislation? Not a chance. National Security outranks that by a country mile, and if you don't think so, feel free to take it to Trump's Supreme Court! The chaotic cauldron of the U.S. Presidential election of 2024 presents a serious concern for Canadians, though many of us at this point continue to roll our eyes in disbelief, and perhaps denial. We cling to our long, northern summer twilight of humanitarian and at least half-rational impulses here despite the hard truth that a Trump victory is very likely to force us to make increasingly nasty decisions to defend ourselves and our principles. The retirement of Biden from the race and the entry into it of Vice President Kamala Harris has given many of us some hope that we may dodge the bullet of a second Trump presidency, but the issue remains very much in doubt.
One thing to consider is that a part of Canada's strong swing toward Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives, is a deep and well-funded cadre of articulate white evangelist "Christian" Canadians who are directly linked to their religious fellow travellers in MAGAland. They wholeheartedly and actively support Trump's 2024 candidacy, just as they did, rather successfully, in 2016. I should know; one of my many cousins is among them. A former successful businessman, he has boasted to me that he has been advising the Trump team through letters and telephone calls from his expansive farm in southwestern Ontario for years. What sort of advice? Well, mostly about crushing progressive movements on both sides of the border and working toward installing a quasi-religious autocracy, first there, then here.
I think we Canadians need to take our heads out of the sand and gird our loins in every way conceivable. Should Trump be re-elected, all bets on rational behaviour by our collective Cousin Jonathans to the south are off. David More is a Kingston author and historian (Queen's Ph.D. 2021). His ninth book, Dark Watershed, is a speculative, Tom Clancy-ish thriller set in the near future. It explores many of these issues. It is available at Novel Idea, Indigo/Chapters and on Amazon.

Document 127 of 227

Why we should consider Ford's tunnel idea

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-we-should-consider-fords-tunnel-idea/docview/3110817982/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+we+should+consider+Ford%27s+tunnel+idea&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-09-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford wants to build a tunnel under Highway 401 to relieve congestion.
There was no cost provided for building the highway or the feasibility study, but Ford spoke Wednesday as if the underground highway was getting built.
"We're going to get the job done, mark my words," Ford said.
The idea is for a tunnel for passenger vehicles and transit from Mississauga to Markham. Ford said one of the reasons for conducting a feasibility study was to determine the length of the project.
He said the cost of doing nothing and keeping people stuck in gridlock also comes with a steep price.
"You have to look at the cost of $11 billion a year it's costing our economy," Ford said.
The idea was immediately panned by the opposition parties at Queen's Park.
"This is a serious issue, gridlock, congestion, but this is not a serious proposal. This is not a serious Premier. It is a silly thought from a government that has run out of ideas," NDP Leader Marit Stiles said.
Stiles compared Ford's plan to Boston's Big Dig, which was years over deadline and significantly over budget. While the Big Dig had its problems, there are long, successful highway tunnels.
Norway has the Laerdal and Folgefonna tunnels, Japan has the Yamate tunnel, while Australia has the WestConnex around Sydney. These are all examples we could look to, but Stiles and other opposition leaders seem to want to throw up their hands and offer no solutions.
"We need to get transit built in a big way," Stiles said.
Perhaps Stiles didn't hear, part of the feasibility study is for a public transit component.
Beyond that, the Ford government is on a major transit expansion with a new subway line in Toronto, subway extensions north of the city, the Finch LRT, taking the LRT in Mississauga to Brampton and planning for an LRT in Hamilton. If Ottawa ever has a real return-towork plan, there will be LRT expansion there, as well.
Transit is part of the solution, but it is already being built; something needs to be done about roadways.
"I really think that this is a pipe dream of an idea," Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said.
Crombie suggested Highway 407 could be used, perhaps by allowing trucks on the highway without paying tolls. That could help, but it is by no means the solution to what is happening on highways in and around Toronto.
Crombie, like Stiles, opposes this project and they both oppose building Highway 413.
Along with Green Leader Mike Schreiner, they are the collective party of no. They don't want anything built; they believe we can add massively to the population, and we can get around with more bike lanes and subways.
That's not realistic. Perhaps Ford's tunnel plan isn't the right option - maybe there is a fatal flaw in the idea - but at least he's coming up with possible solutions.
So, let's study the idea.
blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Feasibility studies; Roads & highways

Document 128 of 227

Decoding why wasps in Canada are so seemingly angry

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/decoding-why-wasps-canada-are-so-seemingly-angry/docview/3110546714/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Decoding+why+wasps+in+Canada+are+so+seemingly+angry&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-09-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For a brief moment on Thursday, British Columbia NDP leader David Eby was upstaged by a wasp that flew down his shirt and stung him during a news conference.
Eby was delivering remarks in Chilliwack while on the election campaign trail when the insect interrupted him, prompting him to swat at his neck and identify it as a "bug."
But seconds later he was apparently stung, scrunching up his face in a grimace as he told reporters it was a bee that had made its way down his collar.
In fact, it was a yellowjacket that Eby eventually flung to the ground where it was later stepped on by a member of his staff.
The interruption was reminiscent of the moment in 2022 when Ontario Premier Doug Ford accidentally swallowed a bee during a news conference.
Eby said he was fine after being stung, but the encounter was probably "fatal" for the insect.
"The bee is not gonna make it," he said.
Spend any amount of time outside around this time of year in most of Canada, and you're likely to be besieged by wasps.
What is it about late summer and early fall that makes wasps so ubiquitous and seemingly so angry? MOTIVATED BY FOOD, NOT HATE The yellowjacket wasps most Canadians are accustomed to are one of several non-native species of the somewhat ironically named social wasps - meaning they live in a community in which they work together to build and defend the nest, hunt for food and raise the young.
Come late summer and early fall, the nest is home to several hundred worker wasps charged with feeding themselves and the queen wasp's final batch of eggs before winter, eggs destined to become the next generation of queens and unfertilized drones.
"You've got a whole lot of mouths to feed in the case of all these larvae that need to be fed to rear the next brood of reproductives," Dr. Gard Otis, professor emeritus from the University of Guelph's School of Environmental Scientists, tells Postmedia.
"But the numbers of workers that are being produced is actually starting to decline because they aren't being reared anymore. So, you've got huge food demands with smaller numbers to do the feeding." D ON' T PICK A FIGHT Given how hungry they are, they'll go after just about anything - including the food on your plate and the drink in your glass.
When this happens, Otis's advice is to not freak out. While wasps aren't necessarily looking for a fight, they won't back down.
"If you don't swing at them ... for the most part, they're just gonna completely leave you alone," he says. "Even if they're coming and foraging for food on your table, you can take your hand and gently wave it past where they are and move them out of the area without disturbing them."
As social creatures, they stick up for their own. Start flailing to take one down and it's likely to release an alarm pheromone calling nearby allies into battle. The same chemical is also released when a sting is landed.
Otis, who is often stung in his line of work, has a pro tip for quickly eliminating the threat of more attacks.
"The first thing I do is lick my fingers on one hand and then wipe the spot with my wet fingers because that will help to remove the water-based pheromone and prevent the target that's sitting there waiting to attract other wasps to sting the same spot."
ECO -WORKERS WITH A PURPOSE
As much as humans may deplore them infringing on our time outside, wasps are an important part of the ecosystem.
They're a food source for some mammals, play a role in natural pollination and hunt flies and insects.
"They do a pretty good job of biocontrol of some pest insects on some crops," adds Otis.

Subject: Press conferences; Food

Location: Canada

Document 129 of 227

Audit shows Ford increased Ontario spending

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/audit-shows-ford-increased-ontario-spending/docview/3108861191/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Audit+shows+Ford+increased+Ontario+spending&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-09-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie need to come up with new attack lines for Ontario Premier Doug Ford because claiming he is cutting spending just isn't true.
The province released their audited spending documents Thursday and cutting is a word that doesn't apply.
Total spending by the Ford government for the fiscal year that ended on March 31 went up by $7.8 billion compared to 2022-23.
Spending on government programs went up by more than $9 billion, including an extra $7 billion on health and an extra $2.7 billion on education. All of those spending increases were offset by the province's debt charges coming in at $11.4 billion instead of the expected $14.1 billion.
Health and education spending are both well above inflation and population growth combined.
"Doug Ford's cuts to education funding have led to larger class sizes, fewer EAs and support staff, a shortage of mental health professionals, and cuts to special education supports and programming," NDP education critic Chandra Pasma said in a statement last week.
"Doug Ford has gutted our health-care system," Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said in a statement last week.
Total spending between the time the Ford government took office and now is up by 34 per cent at a time when the Bank of Canada tells us inflation is up by 20.6 per cent.
The idea that Ford is a fiscal conservative who slashes budgets, is laughable. Ford spends with the best of them, he just doesn't get any credit for it.
This has been clear from Ford's first budget in 2019 until now. There has never been a desire to cut spending, especially in terms of core spending.
The best that can be said about Ford in terms of fiscal conservatism, and this is faint praise, is at least he hasn't raised taxes. In fact, Ford often brags about increases to spending in this province by more than $50 billion without having raised taxes.
"We've never increased a tax in six years," Ford said last week during a news conference in Windsor.
Ford likes to claim he runs the only government "that has never raised a tax."
It's not clear if that claim is true, but at least he can claim to have boosted spending without raising taxes and he's come close to balancing the budget. Ontario logged a deficit of just $600 million in fiscal year 2023-24 or less than one-third of one per cent of the total provincial budget.
Essentially, the current provincial deficit is a rounding error.
There is still room to improve and surely in a government spending more than $200 billion a year, savings of more than $600 million can be found without affecting core services. Let's hope that, a year from now, the government is announcing the budget is balanced or in surplus and taxes are coming down.
That would make real fiscal conservatives happy for a change. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Provincial budget; Taxes; Fiscal years; Budgets
