Document 161 of 227

Private Endoscopy clinic hopes to shorten wait times in kingston

Author: Balogh, Meghan

Publication info: Balogh, Meghan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/private-endoscopy-clinic-hopes-shorten-wait-times/docview/3082737014/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Private+Endoscopy+clinic+hopes+to+shorten+wait+times+in+kingston&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-07-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Balogh%2C+Meghan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Staff at a new endoscopy clinic in Kingston are hoping that their facility will help shorten regional wait times for endoscopic procedures.
Advance Endoscopy & Specialist Centre opened in Kingston in January 2024, its fourth clinic location in Ontario, with already-established clinics in Mississauga, Toronto and Bowmanville. Its first clinic opened in 2012.
The clinic, which held its grand opening event on June 26, offers a range of endoscopic procedures, including colonoscopies, gastroscopy, sigmoidoscopy, and minor procedures such as the removal of lipoma cysts, moles and skin tags and anorectal procedures.
Its doctors are certified by the College of Physicians and Surgeons of Ontario and all procedures at the clinic are covered by the Ontario Health Insurance Plan (OHIP) for those with an Ontario Health Card, or an out-of-province health card recognized by Ontario's public health-care system. Dr. Shahbano Rizvi is a general surgeon in Belleville and the medical director for the company's Bowmanville and Kingston clinics.
She sees firsthand the potential danger of requiring people who need non-emergent medical procedures like colonoscopies or gastroscopies to wait months for their procedure.
"My primary practice is in Belleville, and even though we have a lot of endoscopy time in Belleville (at the hospital), our wait times on average are about eight to 10 months," Rizvi told the Whig-Standard, pointing out that people with family history of disorders qualify for endoscopic screening, but often have to wait a long time.
"What happens in that scenario is that when something urgent comes through, then you have to accommodate those people ahead of the lesser urgent cases, so those cases get pushed back."
Rizvi said that based on information from colleagues, the wait-time situation in Kingston is worse.
"That's where our clinic comes in," she said. "We are able to accommodate those kind of (less severe) cases on a more basis, in comparison to the hospitals, where they obviously see more advanced cases and sicker patients." POTENTIAL CONSE QUENCES OF WAITING Working in hospitals, Rizvi sees patients with severe sickness. Those people are not able to utilize Advance Endoscopy.
"We're seeing the relatively more stable people who would be waiting in line to get their procedure done," she said.
While many people who wait several months for screening procedures or examinations for minor symptoms end up being healthy, and therefore suffer no consequences for having to wait, Rizvi said that others finally get their procedure and more serious issues are revealed, or, other serious issues arise during their wait.
"Last year in our other three clinics, we diagnosed over 100 cancers, both upper and lower," Rizvi said. "One patient with rectal cancer had been complaining of discomfort for about seven months. Another had anemia and had been waiting eight or nine months, and he also had cancer. So there is a real-life problem with missing or waiting on those cases."
For those who end up having few symptoms, but later have a cancer diagnosis revealed, the waiting can be life-altering.
"What happens is that if somebody has a cancer, that person is obviously going to get sicker (as they wait), and then they are potentially looking at metastatic disease. Or, they may have a massive bleed, or some of them may perforate, and then they are in a way more serious situation. They are now in the emergency department in need of intensive care unit-care.
"So now, you're spending hundreds of thousands of dollars on somebody who could have earlier. That's the reality."
In its 2023 report, "Waiting Your Turn: Wait Times for Health Care in Canada," in which it surveyed physicians across 12 specialities in 10 provinces, the Fraser Institute noted an overall increase since 2022 in the time patients are waiting for to see a specialist after being referred by a general practitioner - increasing the median wait time to 14.6 weeks from 12.6 weeks in 2022.
In 1993, when it began its survey, the average wait time was 3.7 weeks.
Ontario came out in the lead for weeks waited from referral to appointment with a specialist at only 10.1 weeks, on average.
However, those wait times vary by specialist and required treatment or screening.
"Research has repeatedly indicated that wait times for medically necessary treatment are not benign inconveniences," the report stated. "Wait times can, and do, have serious consequences such as increased pain, suffering and mental anguish. In certain instances, they can also result in poorer medical outcomes-transforming potentially reversible illnesses or injuries into chronic, irreversible conditions, or even permanent disabilities."
Wait times for endoscopic procedures in Ontario are unclear, but a spokesperson from the Ontario Ministry of Health pointed out that, for colonoscopies ordered following an abnormal fecal immunochemical test (FIT) result- the main screening tool for identifying colon cancer in Ontario-the provincial benchmark wait-time is eight weeks.
"From April 2023 to March 2024, approximately 83 per cent of Ontarians had their hospital-based colonoscopy within eight weeks of their abnormal FIT result," the spokesperson for the ministry said. PRIVATE CLINICS IN THE PUBLIC SECTOR Rizvi knows that the integration of private clinics into the public health-care system has come under fire, especially since Ontario Premier Doug Ford's announcement in early 2023 that more private facilities would be brought on board to address Ontario's growing waitlist for many surgical and diagnostic imaging procedures.
"The government is finding ways to help facilitate (the need for procedures)," Rizvi said. "There is not one penny coming out of the patient's pocket at our clinic. It is all covered by OHIP."
Most of those publicly-funded private facilities, formerly known as Independent Health Facilities (IHFs) and now identified by the Ontario government as community surgical and diagnostic centres, include more than 900 licensed clinics across the province offering a wide range of services including cataract and other eye surgeries, plastic surgeries, dialysis, routine diagnostic imaging such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and computed tomography (CT) scans, abortion, sleep studies and more.
However, private endoscopy clinics are not yet being licensed under the province's community surgical and diagnostic centres.
A spokesperson from the Ontario Ministry of Health clarified that there are currently 67 community-based centres that are providing OHIP-insured gastrointestinal endoscopy services in the province, which operate under the designation of "out-of-hospital premises" (OHPs).
According to the ministry, the College of Physicians and Surgeons of Ontario is responsible for overseeing quality assurance at OHPs, including conducting onsite quality inspections.
The province plans to add endoscopy clinics to its roster of community surgical and diagnostic centres in the near future.
"Under the leadership of Premier Ford, our government is making record investments in our publicly funded health-care system," Hannah Jensen, a spokesperson for Deputy Premier and Minister of Health Sylvia Jones, told the Whig-Standard in a written statement on Tuesday.
"Since 2018, we have increased our health-care budget by over 31 per cent, investing over $85 billion this year alone. We are connecting more people to the care they need when they need it by expanding capacity across our health-care system and reducing wait times for key services.
"Over the last year, our government has achieved some of the shortest wait times in Canada, with nearly 80 per cent of people receiving their procedure within clinically-recommended target times."
Jensen said that the province continues to work to eliminate surgical backlogs and reduce wait times.
"Starting with expanding cataract surgeries this year, adding an additional 14,000 cataract surgeries, our government is adding more publicly-funded MRI and CT scans and colonoscopy and endoscopy procedures in communities across the province," she wrote. "The call for applications for MRI and CT scans was posted earlier this summer, and we look forward to launching the call for applications for endoscopy community surgical diagnostic centres heading into the fall." WAITING FOR REFERRALS Advance Endoscopy hopes that Kingston doctors will utilize the services that they are making available to patients in the community.
On average, the expected wait time for a procedure at the private clinic is two to three weeks.
The facility is operating at about three days per week, but has capacity to run six days per week, conducting upwards of 30 scopes per day.
"It takes a little time for people to get comfortable," Rizvi admitted. "People have these thoughts about, are we going to be charged? Is it equal to the level of the hospital? We have people who are working in the hospitals, and we work with hospitals. We send patients right away, and we make sure that as much as we can deal with their issues, we treat those issues. If we can't, then we make the referral to the appropriate person." Dr. Michael Fitzpatrick, chief of staff at Kingston Health Sciences Centre (KHSC), said in a written statement provided to the Whig-Standard that the hospital organization is aware of the new private endoscopy clinic in the community, but has no formal affiliation with it.
"It is our understanding that primary care physicians can refer patients to this new clinic for routine cases, while KHSC continues to provide our full complement of both routine and complex procedures, as well as specialized follow-up care," Fitzpatrick wrote.
"It's important to note, that like many other types of care, endoscopy services were impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic, resulting in increased wait times. There are similar private clinics currently operating within the Greater Toronto Area, who are all responding to this increased demand."
Fitzpatrick noted that KHSC has invested in more resources to help support the needs of the Kingston-area community.
"Over the last two years we have recruited three new gastroenterologists to KHSC and have expanded the number of clinic days at our Hotel Dieu Hospital site," he wrote. "We are pleased to report that we are seeing an improvement in our wait times for these services, and we are confident that KHSC can continue to meet the needs of all patients in our community who require comprehensive care for gastrointestinal diseases, including endoscopic procedures." mbalogh@postmedia.com

Subject: Cancer; Patients rights; Cataracts; Surgeons; Colonoscopy; Clinics; Eye surgery; Magnetic resonance imaging; Medical referrals; Hospitals; Public health; Family physicians; Medical imaging; Endoscopy

Document 162 of 227

Ontario's groundbreaking plan; Local media needs support at all levels

Author: Taube, Michael

Publication info: Taube, Michael. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontarios-groundbreaking-plan-local-media-needs/docview/3082429329/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario%27s+groundbreaking+plan%3B+Local+media+needs+support+at+all+levels&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-07-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Taube%2C+Michael&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Recently, Ontario's Progressive Conservative government announced a plan to help provide financial support for newspaper publishers in the province. The best part about it: it doesn't come at any cost to the taxpayers.
Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy sent letters to several provincial Crown corporations, including the Ontario Lottery and Gaming Corp., Ontario Cannabis Retail Corp., and the Liquor Control Board of Ontario. These agencies were directed to allocate one-quarter of their annual advertising budget, which is reportedly over $100 million, to Ontario's news publishers by Sept. 3.
This means there will be roughly $25 million available for news organizations in Ontario, including the National Post's parent company, Postmedia Network Inc., to fund local journalism.
"These significant advertising budgets should help support Ontario-based publishers and the Ontario-based workers creating local news content for people in our province," reads Bethlenfalvy's letter. It also highlights the Ontario government's belief there is a "compelling opportunity to leverage the advertising spends of Ontario's major government agencies to support Ontario workers and content."
This is a creative and forward-thinking economic plan by Premier Doug Ford and the Ontario PCs. It will help ensure that a strong focus on local news content will be maintained, and significant job opportunities will be available for young journalists and established veterans alike.
Most importantly, it will play a vital role in the survival of Ontario newspapers.
The reality we all have to face is that there's going to be some level of financial involvement by federal and provincial governments in Canada's news industry, both now and in the future.
The vast majority of our newspapers, magazines and news websites currently receive some type of public funding. That being said, the use of direct subsidies can't be the only funding lifeline for Canadian publications, including newspapers. Ontario's directive to allocate part of the advertising budget of a number of provincial Crown corporations is, therefore, a step in the right direction.
This advertising budget is, unlike the introduction of direct subsidies, already earmarked and accounted for in annual government expenditures. It's an existing sum rather than an additional source of taxpayer money. Hence, the $25 million allocated for Ontario publications simply involves shifting one-quarter of the budget, while the remaining three-quarters can be used as the Crown corporations see fit.
The Ford government's policy will allow government agencies to continue to get their messages across to Ontarians, while investing in local journalism.
The money is to be used for Ontario-based newspapers, news content and journalists working in the profession.
This funding directive isn't being handed out with no strings attached, either. A review will apparently be conducted on a quarterly basis.
If the Ontario government feels that the main objectives of "promoting local content and culture and supporting Ontario jobs" aren't being met, it will consider withdrawing the funding altogether. Hence, it's up to Ontario newspapers to ensure that the money is being used effectively, wisely and, above all, properly.
Andrew MacLeod, Postmedia's president and chief executive officer, said, "I'm certainly extremely grateful to the leadership of Premier Ford and his team. My hope is this will serve as an example, a north star, not just for other provincial governments, but for the federal government and for major corporations."
I agree with MacLeod's sentiment wholeheartedly. There are ways for the public and private sectors to help and support Canadian journalism that don't involve direct subsidies. Ford and the Ontario PCs have identified one financial path to success. There are many others still to be considered and utilized. National Post

Subject: Journalism; Government agencies; Advertising; Newspaper industry; Funding; Subsidies

Document 163 of 227

Crowd the extremists out of our politics

Author: Pellerin, Brigitte

Publication info: Pellerin, Brigitte. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crowd-extremists-out-our-politics/docview/3081472648/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crowd+the+extremists+out+of+our+politics&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-07-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Pellerin%2C+Brigitte&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You need a lot of valuable skills to manage an organization like the Carp Agricultural Society. Nobody would expect that list to include "being able to spot political extremists from a mile away." Yet it is increasingly necessary to equip ourselves for this new reality.
As you may have heard, the Carp organization inadvertently rented a hall to Diagolon. For the hitherto blissfully unaware, this is a loose group of extreme right-wingers whose leader once "joked" about raping Pierre Poilievre's wife, which prompted the Conservative leader - despite having previously been photographed perhaps unknowingly shaking the group leader's hand - to call the lot of them "dirtbags" and "losers."
I heartily sympathize. There are plenty of well-documented reasons to avoid shaking hands with this character, even before he laughed on camera about sexual assault. But I wouldn't expect people whose lives are spent in productive pursuits outside the sewers of Twitter to be as extremist-aware as your favourite columnist is.
"We didn't know who they were when they booked," Jean Sullivan, co-president of the Carp Agricultural Society, told the media. "It's not within our values or mission. It was an unfortunate series of events."
Nobody can blame Sullivan for having been hoodwinked, especially as the Diagolon group didn't go out of its way to disclose who they were or what their purpose was. "From my understanding, they did not tell us what the organization was and I had never heard of them before Saturday night," Sullivan added.
I'm not as ready to absolve Goldie Ghamari, MPP for Carleton, who was kicked out of Doug Ford's Conservative caucus over a meeting with U.K. far-right extremist Tommy Robinson. Politicians have staff to do research for them and warn them not to meet someone like Robinson who, as Randall Denley reminded Citizen readers recently, has earned "various criminal convictions" for his activities.
At this point, you may be reading this and wondering, "Is it me, or is there a noticeable increase in run-ins with unsavoury extremists?" It's not you, and it's not just in Canada either.
In France, voters got a nasty scare after the first round of legislative elections yielded a victory for Marine Le Pen's extremist National Rally party, with 33 per cent support. After the second round of voting this past Sunday, the result was entirely different. The far-right party was not the winner; French voters gave first place to a brand-new coalition of four left-wing parties. Voters held their noses and cast their ballots for whichever candidate had the best chance of beating the National Rally contender.
As David Broder noted in the New York Times, one of the most remarkable developments in that second round of voting was how left-wing voters in contests where the two leading candidates were President Emmanuel Macron's centrist candidate or the far-right candidate, backed the centrist or abstained from voting.
The result may prove to be an ungovernable mess, but at least in the immediate future, French voters avoided electing a far-right party. Not that left-wing extremists would have been better, in case you were wondering. Extremism is always bad in politics, and the trick is for the rest of us who are scattered across the regular political spectrum to occupy as much space as possible so that extremists have nowhere to grow. The reaction of the people of Carp, who objected strenuously and publicly to Diagolon being in their lovely community, is exactly what we need to do.
Despite our disagreements on the best ways to achieve desirable goals, the vast majority of us agree on the fundamentals that make for a good and decent society. Individual rights (very much including reproductive rights), the rule of law and respect for our shared institutions of governance. And a baseline of properly financed social services such as health and education.
What we agree on is what makes this country such a desirable place to live. It's worth protecting it against extremists of all kinds. Brigitte Pellerin (they/them) is an Ottawa writer.

Document 164 of 227

Liberals blame local candidate, not Trudeau, for loss

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-blame-local-candidate-not-trudeau-loss/docview/3076125155/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+blame+local+candidate%2C+not+Trudeau%2C+for+loss&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-07-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Trudeau Liberals have done some deep thinking about why they lost the Toronto- St. Paul's byelection and have decided it was Leslie Church's fault. Forget the fact that the Liberals have been trailing badly in the polls for the last year, forget that the prime minister is unpopular, let's blame the local candidate.
That's what Liberal MP Karina Gould is doing. The Ontario co-chair for the next election, the person in charge of finding new candidates, is showing gratitude to Church by blaming her. Good luck with that recruitment for the next election! "I think the lesson here is that she was nominated a week or two before the byelection was called," Gould told The Canadian Press in an interview.
"And so she needed more time to be able to get out there and get known in the riding."
The way Gould and CP tell the story, the Conservatives had the advantage because they nominated their candidate earlier and so he could campaign and get to know voters in the riding. Meanwhile, says Gould, Church couldn't campaign because she wasn't nominated until May 1, a little over two weeks before the byelection was called.
That's a nice piece of fiction, a story to explain away a bad loss, but here is what really happened.
Carolyn Bennett, who had held the seat since 1997, announced in July of last year that she would not run in the next election, but would stay on as an MP. By December, she had resigned her seat and in January was appointed Canada's ambassador to Denmark.
Meanwhile, Church, who had been Chrystia Freeland's chief of staff, resigned from her job in October 2023 and moved back to Toronto to try to secure the nomination.
Despite being a loyal soldier as a party volunteer and high-level staffer for years, despite visiting Bennett to try to kiss the ring and get the retiring MP's endorsement, some in the party didn't want Church. The party delayed the nomination hoping in vain for a star candidate, they didn't get one and, in the meantime, Church was working the riding hard to secure the nomination.
The Liberals could have held the nomination meeting at any point, but they chose to delay. They also chose to hold the vote June 24, the day before capital gains tax changes took effect.
It was the Liberals who controlled every aspect surrounding the timing of this byelection and they completely screwed it up. Now, faced with a bad loss in a riding the Conservatives should never have won, the Liberals are blaming the local candidate.
There are calls from inside the Liberal fold for the PM to hold a caucus meeting to discuss the byelection loss, but Gould doesn't support that idea.
"I think what's important is that caucus members are really engaging their community, really hearing what's on people's minds," she told CP.
Well, in Toronto-St. Paul's, the local candidate and her volunteers just spent weeks knocking on doors and talking to residents to hear what is on their minds and one recurring theme is Justin Trudeau has to go. There were past Liberal voters telling Church's team that while they liked her, they couldn't vote for her while Trudeau was leading the party.
That's the message voters sent to the Liberals last week, but it's a message they don't want to hear which could cost them another safe Liberal seat. There is a coming byelection for the Montreal riding of LaSalle-Émard-Verdun to replace David Lametti.
It's been a Liberal seat for decades except for 2011-15, when the NDP represented the area after the Orange Wave. It could go NDP again with local councillor Craig Sauvé running as a candidate for the New Democrats.
The Trudeau Liberals lost the Toronto- St. Paul's byelection for many reasons, but Justin Trudeau and his policies were at the core of it all. The sooner they realize that, the better off they and the rest of the country will be. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Nominations; Voters; Elections

Document 165 of 227

Canada Day offers a tale of two nations

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canada-day-offers-tale-two-nations/docview/3075788990/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+Day+offers+a+tale+of+two+nations&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-07-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you want a preview of the next election campaign, just watch the Canada Day messages from Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre. They were a study in contrast in style, tone, content and vision. They also illustrate why the Conservatives are 20 points ahead of the Liberals in the polls, and likely to remain so. Trudeau began by exhorting Canadians to celebrate "the incredible people, the land and the story that is Canada." He acknowledged Indigenous peoples and honoured Canadian soldiers in the Second World War, but spoke not a word about anyone in between. He thanked "trailblazers, journalists, activists, organizers" for safeguarding Canadian rights and freedoms, and mentioned that Canada continues to confront injustices and learn from them "that we're stronger not in spite of our differences, but because of them."
In closing, Trudeau thanked workers and volunteers during the pandemic, firefighters protecting Canada from wildfires and the Canadian Armed Forces fighting for democracy and freedom. The entire video consisted of Trudeau addressing the camera for about 2½ minutes.
In contrast, Poilievre's four-minute message opened with images of Canada's natural grandeur set to dramatic music and the Conservative leader's voice-over. Poilievre acknowledged the stewardship of Indigenous people and described Canadian inventions, overlaid by grainy black-and-white historical footage. He described Canada as "a warrior nation" that "helped defeat ugly socialist ideologies like Nazism, fascism and communism."
The message then turned political. Poilievre criticized the federal government for the fact that "a quarter of our people live in poverty," while "crime, chaos, drugs and disorder reign in our once safe streets." He lamented that "weird, woke obsessions divide our people, destroy our education, disparage our history and degenerate into our streets." Poilievre spoke about how the "common people" will take back control of their lives, praising tradespeople, farmers and troops, and concluded with a quote by John Diefenbaker about freedom.
On every level, the two messages were polar opposites. Unlike Poilievre's inclusive montage of Canadian images, Trudeau's message featured only him speaking to the camera. In fairness, it was designed as an address to the crowds at Canada Day celebrations in Ottawa - Trudeau was in Newfoundland, so Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland addressed the crowd in the nation's capital, a fact that did not go unnoticed.
Nonetheless, it was a poor production choice, especially at a time when many accuse Trudeau of putting his ego ahead of the good of his party and country by staying on as leader.
For his part, Poilievre predictably politicized the moment. As he did at Brian Mulroney's state funeral, the Conservative leader didn't miss the chance to slam the government, whether appropriate or not. Poilievre could have mentioned the struggles people are facing without assigning blame, but chose to score political points and fundraising dollars instead.
It might not be statesmanlike, but Poilievre captured the national mood far more than the prime minister did. The fact that Trudeau failed to even acknowledge people's pain shows how out of touch he has become. It's as if he was speaking in 2015, when progressive causes were all the rage, and he was their beloved global champion. In 2024, Canada and the world are grappling with dire economic conditions and growing anti-immigrant sentiment. The populist right is on the march in Europe, the United States and here at home. A true leader would confront these sentiments and promise to do better, not double down on a message that is sorely out of date.
Trudeau also made it sound as if the byelection in Toronto-St. Paul's never happened. The activists and organizers he praised are one of the reasons his party lost that vote. The hateful antisemitic messages that flooded Canadian streets turned the Jewish community against the Liberal party, and angered Canadians of all faiths who prize Canada as a country of tolerance and respect. In 2024, diversity is no longer our strength, and the government's failure to address this is tearing our country apart. When faced with a choice between Trudeau's vision of kumbaya progressivity versus Poilievre's gritty realism, it's clear where voters land. They want change, and Trudeau's time is up. Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Native peoples; Activism; Prime ministers

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: preview; election; campaign; watch; canada; messages

Title: Canada Day offers a tale of two nations

Document 166 of 227

More baleful byelections are brewing for the Trudeau Liberals

Author: Tumilty, Ryan; Nardi, Christopher

Publication info: Tumilty, Ryan; Nardi, Christopher. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/more-baleful-byelections-are-brewing-trudeau/docview/3073392298/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=More+baleful+byelections+are+brewing+for+the+Trudeau+Liberals&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-06-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Tumilty%2C+Ryan%3BNardi%2C+Christopher&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Liberals'stunning defeat in the Toronto-St-Paul's byelection this week could be just the beginning of a year of pain for the party: several more byelections are expected; all of them could happen before 2024 is over.
The Liberals currently hold three of the four seats, with some previously considered safe for the party. But so was Toronto-St. Paul's, which the party held onto comfortably for 30 years until this week.
It may be that there are no longer any secure seats for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's party, which has been way behind the Conservatives in national polls for months. These are the looming battlegrounds that could either signal a turnaround for the Liberals, or add to their growing sense of demise. LASALLE- ÉMARD - VERDUN Previously: Liberal Expected byelection: Before Sept. 17 Since 2015, ex-Liberal MP and former justice minister David Lametti has had the central Montreal riding of LaSalle-Émard- Verdun in a chokehold.
Lametti and the Liberals have never won less than 42.9 per cent of the vote in the riding, which was created after an electoral redrawing in 2013. In the 2021 federal election, Lametti garnered over 20-per-cent more votes than the runner-up, Bloc Québécois candidate Raphaël Guérard.
Lametti resigned in January after being cut out of Trudeau's cabinet. By law, Trudeau must announce a byelection date by July 30 and it then must take place five to seven weeks after the announcement.
The question now: Is the riding loyal to the Liberals? Or was it loyal to Lametti himself? The former is most likely, the latter is a small possibility.
"I would say that's probably a safe seat for the Liberals to retain," said Andrew Enns, executive vice-president of pollster Leger.
One Liberal source said they're not as concerned about losing the riding as they were with Toronto- St Paul's and that the party has a "solid" team in Quebec. But the Liberals have yet to nominate a candidate for the Montreal riding. Neither the BQ nor the Conservative party has announced candidates, either.
All signs point to the Liberals'main challenger being New Democrat candidate Craig Sauvé, a longtime party member and former NDP staffer turned Montreal city councillor in 2013. Sauvé began campaigning after he was nominated as a candidate in late April. ELMWO OD - TRANSCONA Previously: NDP Expected byelection: Before Nov. 3 This race may be less of a test of the Liberal party's fortunes the an indication of the Conservatives' strengths.
The suburban Winnipeg riding has been an NDP stronghold since its creation in 1988, with two generations of the Blaikie family representing the riding. Former MP Bill Blaikie represented the riding from 1988 until 2008, and his son Daniel Blaikie has held the seat since 2015.
The riding went to the Conservatives only once between 2011 and 2015, when former prime minister Stephen Harper won a majority government, and the Liberals have rarely been competitive in the riding. Daniel Blaikie resigned the seat in March, moving to take a job with Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau must call the byelection by the end of September.
Enns said the riding could be very competitive without a Blaikie on the ballot and with the message Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has been using to appeal to some of the NDP's traditional working-class base.
Blaikie won the riding in 2021 by more than 20 points, but recent polling has indicated a strong Conservative lead overall in Manitoba.
The NDP has picked Leila Dance as their candidate in the riding. She previously ran a local business-improvement association and won a contested nomination to become the candidate.
The Liberals and the Conservatives have yet to name their candidates.
NDP MP Peter Julian said he believes the party's message is resonating in the riding and thinks New Democrats will hold the seat.
"They (voters) see the NDP getting things done and making a difference for people," he said.
The riding, like many in the Prairies, British Columbia and northern Ontario, is typically a battle between NDP and Conservative candidates.
Julian said when he talks to NDP supporters in the riding, they're nervous about what the Conservatives will do to government supports they rely on. He said they may be telling pollsters they support the Conservatives, but that will change when faced with the party's proposals.
"I'd say the Conservative support is a mile wide and an inch deep." CL OVERDALE-LANGLEY CITY Previously: Liberal Expected byelection: Before Jan. 4, 2025 The most daunting challenge for the Liberals could be holding onto the riding of Cloverdale-Langley City, in British Columbia's Lower Mainland. The riding has been hard fought between the Liberals and Conservatives for the last three elections, with only narrow margins of victory for either party. MP John Aldag announced he would be stepping down and formally resigned at the end of May. He is seeking to run for the British Columbia NDP in the provincial election expected this fall.
Trudeau will have to announce the date of the byelection before the end of November.
In 2021, Aldag and the Liberals won the riding by about three points, roughly the same margin that decided the race in 2019 when Conservative Tamara Jansen won, and has announced she will run again. It has become a much fiercer battleground over the years than it was in 2015, when Aldag won the riding by nearly 10 percentage points.
The NDP have finished a distant third in all of those elections.
"I think it'll be hugely challenging for the Liberals to hold that seat," Enns said.
Leger's public polling had the Conservatives with a 26-per cent lead in British Columbia, with the Liberals in third place.
By comparison, the Conservatives had a 18-per cent lead in Ontario where they pulled off the surprise upset in Toronto-St. Paul's. HALIFAX Previously: Liberal Expected byelection: Unknown Liberal MP Andy Fillmore announced in mid-June that he will be stepping down as MP, and he is expected to make a run at becoming mayor of the Halifax. He hasn't officially resigned his seat as an MP, so the exact timing of the byelection is unknown.
Fillmore first won the seat in 2015, upsetting longtime NDP MP Megan Leslie, and he held onto it in 2019 and 2021 with comfortable margins. The Conservatives have not traditionally been competitive in the riding, finishing third or even fourth.
Enns said that with the right candidate the Liberals could likely hold onto this riding.
He said that while public polling has the Conservatives ahead in Atlantic Canada, that won't necessarily be reflected in the Halifax riding. rtumilty@postmedia.com cnardi@postmedia.com

Subject: Conservatism; Nominations; Candidates; Prime ministers; Elections

Location: Manitoba Canada; Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada; British Columbia Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Document 167 of 227

Most Gen Z, millennials done with Liberals: poll; Party lost longtime Toronto stronghold to Conservatives in recent byelection

Author: Thomson, Stuart

Publication info: Thomson, Stuart. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/most-gen-z-millennials-done-with-liberals-poll/docview/3072924771/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Most+Gen+Z%2C+millennials+done+with+Liberals%3A+poll%3B+Party+lost+longtime+Toronto+stronghold+to+Conservatives+in+recent+byelection&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-06-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Thomson%2C+Stuart&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A majority of younger Canadians believe that older generations are rigging things to benefit themselves, while making life harder for millennial and Gen Z voters, and most aren't convinced the recent Liberal budget will help matters.
They also aren't big fans of plans for more government spending and higher carbon taxes. Nor are they confident that the government's recent massive subsidy announcements for EV and battery plants will benefit them.
A Postmedia-Leger poll released this week finds that 54 per cent of Canadians aged 18 to 39 agreed with the notion that their parents'generation is "rigging the system for their benefit."
The Liberal government had pitched its recent budget as ensuring "generational fairness," raising capital gains taxes and spending, to win back support from these cohorts, who polls suggest have deserted the Liberals en masse. But the poll finds only four per cent of younger Canadians strongly agreed that the budget "will make things fairer" for their generation, with 16 per cent of respondents saying they somewhat agreed.
Twenty-six per cent of younger Canadians either strongly or somewhat disagreed that the budget will make things fairer, however. And another 29 per cent said they don't know if the budget will make any difference in fairness.
The question also revealed a large gender divide, with only two per cent of women strongly agreeing that the budget will make things fairer, compared to six per cent of men. Women are far more likely to say they don't know if the budget will help them, with 37 per cent unsure, compared only 22 per cent of men.
The poll shows that millennials and Gen Z also aren't in favour of more government spending, something the Liberals had promised in the April budget. The poll shows that 56 per cent said the government shouldn't spend more money than it is currently spending.
Thirty-four per cent of Gen Z and millennial voters say want the government to spend less money, agreeing that they are "worried about generations like mine being overly taxed in future to pay for today's debt." Another 22 per cent say spending should stay at current levels.
And they're not particularly enthusiastic about the Liberal government's massive subsidies for electric vehicles and battery plants. Only 32 per cent of Gen Z and millennials agreed the handouts for auto and battery makers will "be of significant benefit" to their generation.
This week saw the Liberals devastated by a byelection loss to Conservatives in Toronto-St. Paul's, a formerly solidly urban safe seat, which is widely being seen as a clear sign that voters everywhere, even in once-friendly Liberal territory, are fed up with the government.
The Liberal candidate was the former chief of staff of Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland and Freeland had campaigned heavily in the riding.
The frustration of younger Canadians is heavily centred around runaway housing prices that have put ownership out reach for many in the younger generation, said Aaron Wudrick, the domestic policy director at the Macdonald-Laurier Institute.
"Housing is definitely part of it, because that's the most obvious symbol of your life stages, if you do all the things you're supposed to do. You get a job, you save your money, you should be able to buy a house," said Wudrick.
"And most Canadians now obviously can't do that. Even professionals - never mind working-class people - unless they have support from the parents," he said.
The poll shows younger Canadians are more likely to be pessimistic about their chances of ever buying real estate. Forty-eight per cent who don't already own a home think it is unlikely they will ever buy a house or a condo, with 45 per cent saying it's likely.
Younger people in British Columbia and Ontario, two provinces where housing is the least affordable in Canada, are far more likely to say that older generations are rigging the system and making it harder for younger generations. In B.C., 61 per cent of younger voters agree with that statement and 57 per cent in Ontario agreed.
In Alberta, where average incomes are higher and housing is cheaper, only 49 per cent of younger Canadians think the older generation is rigging the system.
Wudrick said the Liberals are "playing catch-up" on housing and other affordability issues against Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who has been campaigning on it since his party's leadership race in 2022.
"The (Liberals are) making big moves on immigration and housing but none of this stuff is going to bear any fruit for years. So I think it's not going to help them much politically," said Wudrick.
"The budget - if it was really directed at this generation - didn't really move the needle. We've seen that in some of the recent public polling on the federal ballot, including ours. The Liberal numbers didn't budge at all," said Leger vice-president Andrew Enns.
The poll finds that millennial and Gen Z voters are also unhappy with the Liberal government's plans to keep raising the carbon tax, with most saying it should be frozen or cut. Only 19 per cent of younger Canadians think the carbon tax should continue to increase as planned, with 23 per cent saying it should be frozen at the current level. Fifteen per cent of respondents said it should be reduced from the current tax of $80/tonne and 26 per cent said it should be eliminated entirely.
The poll was conducted May 24-26 via using an online panel method, with a sample size of 938 of Canadians aged 18 to 39 who are eligible to vote. A comparable probability sample would yield a margin of error of no greater than plus or minus 3.2 per cent, 19 times out of 20. stthomson@postmedia.com

Subject: Generations; Polls & surveys; Canadians; Millennials; Generation Z; Budgets; Voters; Government spending; Carbon; Environmental tax

Document 168 of 227

The Tory giant killer strikes

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tory-giant-killer-strikes/docview/3072924754/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Tory+giant+killer+strikes&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-06-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Monday, voters participating in the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection delivered a clear message to the incumbent Liberal government: it's time for change. They also unambiguously told Prime Minister Justin Trudeau that it's time to pack it in. St. Paul's has been a Liberal stronghold for 30 years. The last time it was blue was under the Progressive Conservative government of Brian Mulroney and Kim Campbell, when Barbara McDougall served two terms as its MP from 1984 to 1993.
Since then, the Liberals have won the riding by 10,000 votes or more in all but two elections. Liberal cabinet minister Carolyn Bennett held it for over 25 years before retiring in January, winning the 2021 vote by 23 percentage points.
This time, Conservative candidate Don Stewart took it by fewer than 600 votes, in a nail-biter that had the Liberals leading until early Tuesday morning. The last polls were reported at 4:30 a.m. ET, giving the seat to Stewart with 42.1 per cent of the vote against Liberal candidate Leslie Church, who took 40.5 per cent. The NDP's Amrit Parhar scored 10.9 per cent.
While many saw the contest as close, few predicted that the Tories would triumph. If their internal polls showed it, the Conservatives kept mum: even Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's key adviser, Jenni Byrne, said Monday night that she expected the Liberals to take it. This is the first urban Toronto seat the Tories have won since 2011.
To call Stewart a giant killer would thus be an understatement. But the giant he felled wasn't just the riding: it may be the prime minister himself.
The contest was widely described as a referendum on Trudeau's leadership - and the Liberals knew it.
Between the rising cost of living, the dearth of housing and the prime minister's have-it-both-ways approach to the Israel-Hamas war, voters sent a clear signal that they've had enough. The Jewish community, which makes up 15 per cent of St. Paul's, felt particularly abandoned and angry.
The chatter now isn't about whether Trudeau will quit, but when. The knives are out, and they are being sharpened both inside and outside the party. This isn't just about one man: there are now dozens of Liberal MPs whose seats are at risk.
If a downtown Toronto riding can turn Conservative, the thinking goes, then the entire 905 region of Toronto could flip, as well. And an Ontario tsunami could swamp Quebec, whose voters are notorious for going with the winner.
This is both good and bad news for the Conservatives. Good news, because it means that their strategy is working. Their consistent messaging on the economy and the prime minister's failings are resonating. Coupled with the populist wave that is sweeping the planet, from Argentina to France, there is no reason they should not get a crushing majority in the next election, whether it's held in the next month or the next year.
But it's bad news because, if Trudeau calls it quits, the Tories won't be able to muster quite the same level of vitriol that put them over the top on Monday. That's not to say they wouldn't win, but they would have to pivot to attack a new Liberal leader.
Who might that be? Trudeau's cabinet has no shortage of aspirants, many of whom campaigned in St. Paul's during the byelection. Deputy Prime Minister and Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland made a last-minute appeal to voters in the riding to not elect the "cold and cruel and small" Conservatives. It clearly did not work. If anything, it may have backfired, as the whiff of desperation is never attractive.
As is the whiff of association with Trudeau. Anyone who is part of the current Liberal inner circle will be promptly slammed by the Tories as Trudeau 2.0.
If not them, then who? Outsiders touted for the job include former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney and former B.C. premier Christy Clark. But it's a tough sell. Whoever takes over would have a monumental hill to climb - one that just got harder after Monday's loss. Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Conservatism; Leadership; Voters; Prime ministers; Elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Identifier / keyword: voters; participating; byelection; delivered; clear; message

Document 169 of 227

MPP HSU declares re-election intention

Author: Murphy, Jan

Publication info: Murphy, Jan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mpp-hsu-declares-re-election-intention/docview/3072089496/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MPP+HSU+declares+re-election+intention&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-06-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Murphy%2C+Jan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The weather on Sunday was a lot like the political situation in Ontario when it comes to an early election: a little uncertain.
Early Sunday, rain poured down on the Limestone City, an encore to Saturday's inclement forecast. But by early afternoon, the sun was shining, the warmth had returned, allowing a sea of Liberal supporters to gather at Lake Ontario Park to make official Kingston and the Islands MPP Ted Hsu's nomination to represent the riding in the next provincial vote, whenever that comes.
Hsu, the incumbent, who also has served as MP for the Kingston riding in the past, was reflective as he spoke about his life and political legacy hours ahead of his official nomination on Sunday.
"I was just six months old," Hsu answered when discussing when his parents packed up the family home in Bartlesville, Okla., some five-plus decades ago to set out for the small city of Kingston, Ont.
Hsu's father, a chemical engineer, accepted a position with Queen's University, moving his family to Kingston, where they reside to this day.
Hsu doesn't need to pause to think when asked about some of his earliest memories of Kingston.
"I remember my dad would make these handmade kites and we would go to Fairfield Historical Park to fly them," Hsu, 60, recalled. "There used to be more open space in Fairfield park 55 years ago or whatever. I also remember we lived near Churchill Park. Churchill Park is one of the places in Kingston that is still kind of the same. It's one of those rare places that has stayed almost the same all these years. I have vivid memories of that."
And who could forget the frigid waters of the old outdoor Memorial Centre pool, Hsu noted.
"(I have some) bad memories of freezing cold water in the outdoor Memorial Centre pool during swimming lessons," he said with a chuckle.
Much like his father, Hsu was a scholar, and a hard worker, attending high school at Loyalist Collegiate and Vocational Institute, before graduating from Queen's with a bachelor of science honours degree in physics. He attened Princeton University, where he completed his PhD in physics. Hsu went on to complete post-doctoral research in Chalk River, Ont., Vancouver and France. He later worked as a researcher and trader in Paris, France and in the United States for Banque Nationale de Paris, and as a director in the Tokyo office of Morgan Stanley.
Ultimately, he found his way back home, to Kingston, where from 2007 to 2011 he served as the executive director of SWITCH, a Kingston-based not-for-profit association that promotes local job creation and green economic growth.
"I took a couple of years off to be a stay-at-home dad, Hsu recalled when asked where his draw to politics traces back to. "My wife went back to school; we had a young daughter who's now 21. That was the time when I took my nose off the grindstone and started to look at what's going to happen 20, 30 years from now."
At the time, Hsu said, he took a hard look at what kind of future his children might be facing and felt the need to be part of the solution, not part of the problem.
When you look at what your kids might have to worry about when they grow up and the challenges, you see that there's a lot of common problems, like climate change or like how the government is managed. I felt like I should start learning about government and public policy because there are a lot of big problems out there. It's only gotten worse. I feel like we're living in a critical time right now where there's a lot of big, messy problems that we need to focus on and seriously tackle. That was how I felt 15, 20 years ago when I first got involved in politics, just thinking about public policy and government first, and then realizing that government had a role to solve these problems where we all have to work together to solve them."
Choosing to stay in Kingston to raise his own family and to pursue his political calling was made possible, he said, by his wife, Tara, being willing to plant roots here, where his parents also were and still are.
"Kingston is not too big, it's not too little, it's not too far from the big cities," Hsu said. "I love the city and the surroundings. It's just home, you know."
Other than his maternal grandmother, who once ran unsuccessfully for a city council position, Hsu is the first member of his family to chase a career in politics.
"I've gone out on my own," Hsu said. "It wasn't something that my mom or dad were particularly interested in."
Following his hiatus to spend time with his oldest daughter, Hsu made the leap into the sea of red that is, and traditionally has been, Kingston's political landscape. He acted as treasurer of the Kingston and the Islands Federal Liberal Association from 2007 to 2010, and was an active member of its policy committee. During this time, he supported the 2007 provincial and 2008 federal Liberal campaigns for John Gerretsen and Peter Milliken, the longest-serving Speaker in the House of Commons. Following Milliken's announcement in summer 2010 that he would not seek re-election, Hsu won the Liberal nomination in November to be the party's 2011 candidate.
On May 2, 2011, during one of the poorest showings in Liberal party history, Hsu defied the odds and was one of only two new Liberal MPS elected across Canada, narrowly defeating Alicia Gordon by a few thousand votes in a race that wasn't decided until after the polls closed.
"The federal elections are hand counted," Hsu said. "It was very, very close. Alicia and I were just a few hundred votes apart all night long."
The advance polls proved the difference maker for Hsu, giving him a late-night victory in his first election.
"It was very tense," Hsu recalled.
"I had no idea if I was going to win or lose. The lead kind of went back and forth all night long, just a few hundred votes, less than 500 votes most of the night. We just sat at home and (election team officials) said, 'Don't come yet, don't come yet, we don't know if it's going to happen.'I had two speeches ready and I didn't know what was going to happen."
And while it was a great political victory for Hsu, the first of many, it came at the expense of the party's failings, he said.
"We knew the federal Liberal party was going to do poorly, but not that poorly," Hsu said. "It turned out to be a big disaster, so many people who were expected to win didn't. Incumbent MPs who came to help me, because I was the new guy, instead of campaigning in their own ridings, they lost, and it was a real eye-opener and a wakeup call, I think, for the Liberal party."
That election outcome continues to serve as a reminder, Hsu said.
"You can't take anything for granted," he said. "I think ever since then, the Liberal party has had to just be more humble and not take any vote for granted. Justin Trudeau has won three elections in a row. I think it's good for government, but maybe that's not so good for the culture of the party. We really should be thinking that no election is given and no vote should be taken for granted. That's my lesson from getting elected in 2011."
From the day he was elected, Hsu was a political star in the making. He first served in the Liberal party shadow cabinet as the critic for science and technology, before serving as critic for the Federal Economic Development Agency for Southern Ontario and critic for the Federal Economic Development Initiative for Northern Ontario. In 2013, then-Liberal Leader Justin Trudeau appointed Hsu Liberal critic for post-secondary education. In 2011, he was the first runner-up as rookie of the year in Maclean's annual Parliamentarian of the Year awards. He was also the first runner-up for the Rising Star award for Maclean's in 2012. In 2013, he won Maclean's Parliamentarian of the Year and was voted by his fellow parliamentarians as the MP who best represents constituents.
Rightfully, Hsu's announcement in 2014 that he was not going to seek re-election in Kingston and the Islands caught many by surprise. At the time, he said, he felt torn between politics and being present with his family.
"That was a very difficult decision," Hsu said. "I wanted to keep serving, but it was really tough on the family. One thing I tell people is you think people say, 'Oh, you have to make sacrifices to serve,'but most of the sacrifices, I think, are from your family, my wife, who is holding up most of the family here in Kingston."
Looking back, and considering that he's back representing Kingston, albeit provincially, it all worked out for Hsu. But at the time, he laboured over the decision, he said.
"Maybe I could have done it differently and figured out how to do it differently, but I didn't," he said. "I spent a lot of time and energy on trying to do a good job as MP and I had a certain style, which maybe just took a lot of time and energy. Maybe I should have done it differently. We made the decision that it wasn't right for the family for me to run again. It was very tough. Half of me really wanted to keep serving. People always think that there is some other reason, but it really was just that. It wasn't until 2020, the kids were grown up - as I said, my older daughter is 21 now, she's left home - so that's what allowed me to run again when a provincial nomination opened in 2020."
Hsu's departure, of course, opened the door for then-Kingston Mayor Mark Gerretsen to jump into federal politics, where he has resided ever since. Had Hsu waited any longer to announce his intentions, Gerretsen may not have had time to declare for the federal post.
"We had no idea who would want to step up," Hsu said. "Mark was getting ready in 2014 for a municipal election. There was no reason to suspect that he was thinking of making that jump. It was a surprise to him, too."
In fact, Gerretsen confirmed on Sunday at the annual barbecue the Liberals host for supporters, where Hsu was nominated, that there were only eight days for him to declare for the federal Liberals and withdraw from the race to be mayor.
With Gerretsen, much like Milliken before him, having a stronghold on the local federal leadership, Hsu's reentry into politics came at the provincial level, where he topped the NDP candidate Mary Rita Holland and former Kingston mayor Gary Bennett to earn the MPP responsibility.
While there are obvious differences between federal and provincial politics, Hsu conceded, many of the issues are intertwined.
"People will sometimes say, 'Do you miss federal politics?'" Hsu said. "What I say is - and this is true - all of the really hard problems, all of these things that are making this time right now a critical time, whether it's our healthcare system or climate change or education system or the mental health and addictions crisis or affordability and housing or the state of our democracy - to tackle these problems, the different levels of government have to work together: federal, provincial, municipal, Indigenous government. Being in provincial politics doesn't remove me at all from the really, really tough things that people are facing right now and in the years to come."
Those issues continue to be the driving force behind his decision to seek re-election, as Ontario's Liberals look to push Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives out of power.
"I want to tackle these problems for people and I can definitely make a big contribution from the provincial level," Hsu said. "I have to say, when I watch the question period in the House of Commons in Ottawa, it has deteriorated since I was an MP. It's much worse than it is at Queen's Park Legislature. When I look at that, I say, 'OK, I can do some good at Queen's Park; I don't really need to go back to the House of Commons."
Last year, Hsu was a candidate in the Ontario Liberal Party leadership election, a bid he ultimately lost to then-Mississauga Mayor Bonnie Combie, but a valuable experience that Hsu said made him a better politician.
"I got a lot of good experience," he said. "I would say I got to know the province a lot better than I knew it before. If you want to change something, you need to know how it affects other people in other parts of the province. You need that support from other parts of the province. Knowing the whole province Is really important. Also, running for leadership, you sort of have to get up to speed on all the different parts of policy. You have to wrap your head around all these different things, and that is very useful, too. And that will make me a better MPP and, voters willing, a better government minister some day - voters willing and the leadership willing."
Hsu also said his leadership run put Kingston on the map.
"I was going around the province talking to everybody I could, thousands and thousands of people, and I would introduce myself as, 'Hi, I'm Ted Hsu from Kingston.'Sometimes you just need two words to tell people who you are so that they'll remember, even if they encounter you just for a minute. I was the 'Kingston guy.'That was the thing that made me a bit different from everybody else. I was campaigning in smaller towns, smaller cities, rural areas, northern Ontario, being the Kingston guy. You just need that two-or three-word phrase. I feel like that put Kingston on the map, which is a good thing. I'm kind of proud of that."
Premier Ford's actions inside Queen's Park, where he's hinted at a possibly early election next year, forced the opposition to go into election mode, Hsu said, necessitating his early nomination and to declare his intentions.
"The PC party is a lot stronger when it comes to organization and money, so the Liberal party and the NDP, we don't want to be caught flat-footed, because if we're not organized if an election gets called, we're not ready to campaign, that would be real bad for us," Hsu said. "So Bonnie Combie has decide that, 'OK, we're going to get ready,'and we're going to show that we're getting ready by starting the nomination process. And, you know, at least if we prepare, it actually reduces the chance of an early election because he won't be able to take us by surprise. Then we can get back to trying to get some work done and fix some things and not just have another election."
With the house not expected to return until October, Hsu will take the summer to prepare for whatever the future holds, he said. With Ontario, much like the entire country, facing a housing and homelessness crisis and Ontarians begging for help with rising costs of groceries, there will be no shortage of work to be done when parliament resumes.
Until then, the ever-smiling Hsu said he'll continue to do his best to represent Kingston and the Islands.
"I remember in elementary school, they were teaching us about optimists and pessimists, so from a very young age, I've always thought I was an optimist," Hsu said. "I like trying to solve problems. That's part of my political style. There are a lot of other things in politics that don't come so easily. You need to make connections with people, and some of the interpersonal skills I have to work on a lot, but problem-solving, I love to grab hold of a problem and sort of think about, 'How do we fix this? How do we make it better?'" Hsu said he's not naive enough to think he can solve everyone's problems all of the time, but it won't be for lack of trying.
"When somebody comes in with a challenge or a problem, I like to think about how we can do things better," he said. "If you want to run for a position like this, you need to enjoy helping people figure out solving some problems. You can't always solve problems. What do you do when you can't solve a problem for somebody? The best you can do is really understand, and I'm not so good at making people feel better if I can't get my hands dirty and solve a problem. But sometimes you have to do that as a politician. That's something I've had to wrestle with and try to learn how to do." janmurphy@postmedia.com x.com/Jan_Murphy

Document 170 of 227

Ottawa looks to stop flow of Chinese electric vehicles; Liberals haven't said if they will follow the lead of the U.S. with punishing tariffs

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ottawa-looks-stop-flow-chinese-electric-vehicles/docview/3071131991/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+looks+to+stop+flow+of+Chinese+electric+vehicles%3B+Liberals+haven%27t+said+if+they+will+follow+the+lead+of+the+U.S.+with+punishing+tariffs&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-06-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal government is "actively considering next steps" to stop the flow of Chinese-made electric vehicles into Canada, which could include new tariffs. But it will need to act fast to ensure that Canada is in lockstep with its economic partners.
Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland's office would not say Friday if Canada will follow the lead of the United States, which announced in May a plan to slap punishing tariffs of more than 100 per cent on low-priced Chinese EVs to prevent them from flooding the U.S. market.
But the issue has been top of mind for the Canadian government, which has been heavily subsidizing battery plants and EV manufacturing facilities to counter China's supply.
"Canada is set to be a global leader in electric vehicle manufacturing. Already, we rank first in the world in attractiveness to build EV battery supply chains - as a result of our abundant natural resources, talented workforce, and close collaboration with the U.S. on incentives," said Katherine Cuplinskas, Freeland's deputy director of communications.
"China has an intentional, state-directed policy of overcapacity. We are actively considering next steps to counter Chinese oversupply. Protecting Canadian jobs, manufacturing, and our free trade relationships is essential," added Cuplinskas.
In a statement on X on Thursday, Ontario Premier Doug Ford called on the federal government to "immediately match or exceed U.S. tariffs on Chinese imports."
"Taking every advantage of low labour standards and dirty energy, China is flooding the market with artificially cheap electric vehicles. Unless we act fast, we risk Ontario and Canadian jobs," he wrote, adding that Ontario secured $43 billion worth of investments.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said Thursday that his government is "watching closely" what the U.S. and other allies, like the European Union, have done and that he had "significant conversations" about this very subject just last week at the G7 summit in Italy.
Freeland also discussed the issue of Chinese EV oversupply with her U.S. counterpart, Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen, during a brief visit to Washington, D.C., on Monday.
Trudeau said the government will continue to protect Canadian workers and industry and that it will "look very carefully at what steps need to be taken to ensure that the Canadian auto industry and indeed Canadian consumers are well-supported for years to come."
A government source, speaking on background because they were not authorized to discuss it publicly, said that the No. 1 threat to Canada is not the flow of Chinese-made inexpensive EVs but rather becoming a "dumping ground" for the U.S.
With the U.S. tariffs kicking in Aug. 1, that means Canada will at least need to "align the outcomes" of the punitive measures set by its biggest economic partner, the source said.
The European Commission also said it will impose levies up to 48 per cent on imported Chinese EVs as of July. On Monday, China said it was opening an anti-dumping investigation into pork exports from Europe. While Beijing did not specifically mention EV tariffs, the move is widely seen as direct trade retaliation to Europe's measures.
The threat of a trade war explains why Canada, which saw its canola and pork exports halted by China following Canada's arrest of Huawei executive Meng Wanzhou in 2018, is taking such a careful and "measured approach" to EV tariffs, said the source.
There are two schools of thought as to how this might play out, said the source.
With the U.S. and Europe moving ahead with their tariffs, Canada may be a low priority for China in terms of retaliation. But with Canada being a middle power, China might want to make an example out of the country to prevent others from following suit. calevesque@postmedia.com

Subject: International trade; Manufacturing; Electric vehicles; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: International trade Manufacturing Tariffs

Location: China; United States--US; Canada; Europe

Identifier / keyword: federal; government; actively; considering; steps; electric

Document 171 of 227

Immigration a boon to separatists

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/immigration-boon-separatists/docview/3070111164/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Immigration+a+boon+to+separatists&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-06-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If times are good, immigration does not drive politics. If times are bad, immigrants fast become a lightning rod and a political football. This predictable pattern played out in recent European Parliament elections, where the economy, migration and war were the top issues. Nationalist right-wing parties promising to crack down on immigration, including those of Marine Le Pen in France and Georgia Meloni in Italy, won the day.
In Canada, a similar drama is unfolding. In the last two years, Canada admitted 900,000 permanent residents and 1.5 million non-permanent residents. During the same period, Canadians experienced record-high interest rates, inflation and a housing shortage. Toronto and Vancouver are "impossibly unaffordable" housing markets, with Vancouver third least affordable in the world after Hong Kong and Sydney, Australia. Toronto now has 7.9 per cent unemployment, a result of population growth outpacing job growth.
Are Canadians connecting the dots? Apparently so. Forty-four per cent of us believe immigration is having a mostly negative effect on Canada, up six points from last year, compared with 42 per cent who view it positively. Forty-six per cent want immigration to decrease.
Mario Canseco, whose firm The Research Company conducted the survey, said housing is a major factor: "(Some question) if we can't really have homes for everybody here, how are we going to deal with another influx of immigrants?" These effects were entirely predictable and mirror trends around the world. And they could produce a fresh national unity crisis.
Last week, Quebec Premier François Legault gave Ottawa a bill for $1 billion for immigration-related expenses. He trotted out charts of data showing the province received 80,151 requests for social assistance between March 22 and Nov. 21, 2023, compared with 72,221 for the same period in 2022, the largest increase in 25 years. During the same period, the monthly average of asylum seekers more than doubled, from 19,455 adults to more than 40,000. Legault also said "100 per cent of the housing crisis problem" is due to temporary immigration.
To assist, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau gave Quebec $750 million, which Legault qualified as "disappointing," despite it being the second such payment to Quebec in less than a year. In January, Quebec received $100 million when Legault and Ontario Premier Doug Ford complained about the cost of housing asylum seekers, and Ottawa tossed the two provinces a combined $362 million.
Cue the outrage in B.C., where this week Premier David Eby complained bitterly about the latest funding to Quebec coming "at the expense" of the West.
"That announcement today with Quebec, frankly, is the straw that broke this camel's back," Eby said Monday at the Western Premiers'Conference in Whitehorse. "I'll say for British Columbia how frustrated we are to see the money being showered down on Quebec and Ontario, and us scrabbling around for what's left over. It's not acceptable."
But in Quebec, the issue isn't just money, it's also powers. This week, Legault told Radio-Canada that Quebec should have a referendum on its constitution, to give it full powers over immigration. Why? Because immigration is also driving another potential referendum, this one on Quebec separation. Parti Québécois Leader Paul St-Pierre Plamondon declared that if elected, his party would hold another referendum on Quebec separation. The reason? Immigration is fuelling population growth in the rest of Canada and thereby reducing Quebec's share.
"Either we choose to raise our immigration thresholds to between 120,000 and 150,000 per year to try to maintain Quebec's proportion (of immigration to Canada), which means the decline of the French language and an acute housing crisis ... or we choose thresholds based on our capacity to receive, which means a significant drop in Quebec's political weight in Canada, possibly below 15 per cent," Plamondon said last month.
Federalists may shrug and say yes, but the polls show he'll never win. Separatism is polling at 35 per cent, while 54 per cent of Quebecers support remaining in Canada. To which I say, yes. For now. But no one thought French voters would endorse Le Pen's party, either. Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Population growth; Referendums; Immigration

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

Identifier / keyword: times; immigration; drive; politics; immigrants; become
