Document 172 of 227

Arthur Meighen at 150: A Kingston tribute

Author: Milnes, Arthur

Publication info: Milnes, Arthur. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/arthur-meighen-at-150-kingston-tribute/docview/3068493627/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Arthur+Meighen+at+150%3A+A+Kingston+tribute&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-06-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Milnes%2C+Arthur&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In this era of blindly partisan Tweets and asinine posts by MPs on Facebook and Instagram, it is a great tonic to recall a time when thoughtful political oratory still had a major role in Canadian politics.
And the 150th anniversary of the birth of Arthur Meighen on June 16 is a perfect opportunity to do just that.
Twice prime minister (1920-21 and then again in 1926), Meighen is known before history as one of the greatest House of Commons debaters and stump speakers of all time. And through the four decades he spent in the political arena, he delivered many of his finest addresses right here in Kingston.
Shortly after he first assumed the Canadian premiership, Meighen spoke to an important gathering of leading Kingstonians who assembled in September 1920 at the Frontenac Club to meet him.
Speeches of welcome were made by Mayor H.C. Nickle and Queen's University principal R. Bruce Taylor. Then Meighen's turn came and he reiterated his long-held belief that free trade with the United States was not on his government's agenda.
"I stand for the continuance of the protective principle" he said, "because I believe that any other principle of policy is manifestly unsound and unsuited to this country. A tariff is a consequence of separate national existence identity. A country that is less developed commercially than another cannot afford to put itself at the mercy of another."
What a far-off concept that seems today: A leader discussing a real issue, one that truly mattered for Canada, and doing so at length.
Editors at The Kingston Whig-Standard praised the rookie prime minister after this visit to the Limestone City. "No one privileged to hear Premier Meighen at the Frontenac Club on Thursday evening could fail to be impressed by the intense earnestness of the man, his sincerity of conviction and his determination to pursue a course in the administration of the affairs of the country best calculated to meet the needs of the people," the Whig reported in a front-page editorial.
"Aside from his eminent talents which are generally recognized, he showed the qualities of leadership needed in the present state of the country. He has taken a position in accordance with well-established principles, principles that have been proved to be sound by 50 years of our history and he lays them before the people of Canada for their approval or disapproval. In this he is not playing the part of political opportunist or demagogue but he appeals to the sound judgment of all thoughtful people who desire to promote the best interests of Canada."
In the summer of 1922, now leader of the Opposition, Meighen spoke before 15,000 Tories gathered at Lake Ontario Park. He took Mackenzie King's Liberals to task with the effective oratory he was famous for.
"A government born of misrepresentation cannot be a good government," he thundered as he accused the Liberals of pitting French against English to achieve their political goals. "Its very parentage is unwholesome. You cannot take the elements that for purely party purposes inflamed one great province (Quebec) against military service (during the recently concluded First World War) and the elements that appeal to class consciousness to further their political fortunes and make of those a combination that can really serve this country.
One sterile session may be followed by another, sterile or worse than sterile, but the end cannot be far. The people of Canada will demand men of candid, of dependable, of approved opinions, of consistent public conduct, men united as to what ought to be done and of courage and resource to do it. They will want a government that is a government and that has command of its task."
No wonder Mackenzie King feared Meighen more than any other opponent he faced in his own lengthy career.
More than a decade later, Meighen was back in Kingston to deliver an address at the graveside of Kingston's greatest son, Sir John A. Macdonald. Reading it today, one finds a lesson for politicians in 2024, one they continue to ignore at their own - and the nation's - peril as their childish activities on social media continue.
"It is much easier to convince people of grievances," he said, "than the grandeur of great conceptions. ... When one peers beyond the confusion of the moment, hears the threats, and watches political acrobats perform, one feels like the old English king who on a day before a battle prayed for the day's end before it came."
Three years later, in 1941 as the Second World War raged, he addressed more than 25,000 people who had gathered at Macdonald's City Park statue (now torn down on the orders of Kingston city council) to mark the 50th anniversary of the Father of Confederation's death.
Meighen's speech that day was destined to become one of the most famous of all the addresses he delivered in the Limestone City during his distinguished career.
"We turn aside for a mere moment to pay tribute where tribute is due and to gain inspiration, if we can, courage if we can, wisdom if we can, at the foundation of history," the ninth prime minister said. "Sir John Macdonald does not stand alone in the galaxy of our eminent men; but his greatest rival (Wilfrid Laurier), to his eternal credit, has awarded him the primacy among the founders and builders of our nation.
For the heavier tasks of today, there is more to be learned from him than from any other. If we govern ourselves, each one of us, by the principles which governed him; if we work as he worked; dare as he dared; and follow that star that lighted his life, we will serve our country as we ought to serve it and, with God's help, we will save it."
When Meighen died in 1960, it was his skills as an orator of uncommon ability and effect that were recalled by even those who had once stood against him.
And so it should be today as Arthur Meighen and his life of public service are remembered as his 150th birthday is celebrated this month. Kingston's Arthur Milnes, the in-house historian at the Frontenac Club Hotel, is the editor of 2011's Unrevised and Unrepented II: Debating Speeches and Others by Arthur Meighen, published by the Queen's University School of Policy Studies. He is a past speechwriter to then Prime Minister Stephen Harper, and was Brian Mulroney's memoirs' assistant.

Subject: Principles; Public speaking; Political leadership; Prime ministers; Social networks; Anniversaries

Business indexing term: Subject: Social networks

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: blindly; partisan; tweets; asinine; posts; facebook

Document 173 of 227

Doug Ford is all about 'sorry, not sorry'

Author: Baranyai, Robin

Publication info: Baranyai, Robin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/doug-ford-is-all-about-sorry-not/docview/3068493620/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+all+about+%27sorry%2C+not+sorry%27&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-06-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Baranyai%2C+Robin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Six years ago, then-Ontario premier Kathleen Wynne opened a candidates'debate with the memorable sentiment: "Sorry, not sorry." It was a bold acknowledgment that, after five years in office, she was not well loved by voters; still, she stood by her government's record without apology.
Six years later, "Sorry, not sorry" could serve equally well as her successor's slogan. Of course, the intonation would be rather different.
Premier Doug Ford is very good at saying sorry. He has a gift for dramatic course corrections, even if it takes a while to get there. I applaud the premier's ability to retreat from poorly laid plans, such as legislating away education workers'right to strike (nearly provoking a general strike), and opening parts of the environmentally sensitive Greenbelt for housing development. At such moments, Ford's signature move has been to dig in his heels, then pivot with athletic zeal. He owns the mistake, and assures us he has heard the people loud and clear.
Ford said sorry for the Greenbelt fiasco, but his actions say: Not sorry.
If the premier were sorry, would he promote the person in charge of the Greenbelt file - former housing minister Steve Clark - to government house leader, less than a year after Clark belatedly and reluctantly resigned the cabinet post he'd so egregiously mishandled? The RCMP is conducting a criminal probe of the $8.28-billion land-swap deal. Clark's former chief of staff, Ryan Amato, conducted a shockingly preferential selection process for favoured developers. Integrity commissioner J. David Wake found Clark's actions as minister had violated the ethics rules, and recommended a reprimand.
Whatever the outcome of the RCMP investigation, it's clear that in the very best case scenario, Clark's actions are marked by a total failure of oversight on a critically important file. Now, Ford has promoted him after a brief stint on the backbench. Clark's new job comes with a $21,000 salary bump, and responsibility for co-ordinating day-to-day business in the legislature - a job he'll have to conduct (rather unusually) from outside the newly appointed cabinet.
The cabinet itself, shuffled as the legislature rose early for the summer, has "not sorry" written all over it. In fact, "shuffle" is inadequate to describe this reorganized cabinet. When you shuffle a deck of cards, you still have 13 cards in each suit. This shuffle brought the number of seats on deck to an unprecedented 36, the largest cabinet in Ontario's history.
Remember how, in 2018, Ford named 20 ministers to his first cabinet? That was back when the new premier unapologetically focused on "efficient government."
So focused was the premier, he also slashed Toronto city council (a body where he no longer had any business) from 47 councillors to 25, invoking the notwithstanding clause to bully through the change during a municipal election.
"It's about reducing the size and cost of government." Ford said after convening a midnight session to ram the bill through. "The people are behind us... We will never, ever back down."
Of course, the premier has shown how well he can pivot after digging in his heels. His new cabinet pulls down a whopping $5.7 million in wages. Add in salary top-ups for 32 Conservative parliamentary assistants, and the cost tops $10 million. Rather than reducing size and cost, he has added several associate ministers, who enjoy a $22,000 salary bump, and the added profile of a cabinet position.
The visibility won't hurt, should the premier call an election next spring, a year ahead of schedule. Sorry, not sorry. write.robin@baranyai.ca

Subject: Local elections

Identifier / keyword: years; premier; kathleen; wynne; opened; memorable

Title: Doug Ford is all about 'sorry, not sorry'

Document 174 of 227

Opposition moans about more health success

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/opposition-moans-about-more-health-success/docview/3068491956/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Opposition+moans+about+more+health+success&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-06-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Since Bill 7, the More Beds, Better Care Act was passed in late August 2022, more than 20,000 people have been moved from a hospital bed to a long-term care home. Amid a seemingly never-ending health-care crisis, that should be good news, but the opposition parties at Queen's Park are outraged.
On Wednesday, The Canadian Press published a story laying out the details.
Since the act took effect less than two years ago, 20,261 hospital patients who had been discharged by their doctor and no longer needed hospital care were placed in long-term care homes. The government passed the More Beds, Better Care Act was in part to deal with patients who previously had refused to leave hospital.
The law allowed the government to move someone to a home that was not one of their preferred choices once all reasonable steps had been taken to place the patient.
Before this law, people could just refuse to leave hospital, taking up a bed when they no longer needed hospital care. The change in law asked patients to rank their top five choices for long-term care homes, but if none were available, the patient could be moved to another home.
Since the law took effect, The Canadian Press reports 424 people have been moved to homes not on their preferred list.
That's about two per cent more than 20,000 people moved and the opposition is outraged.
The New Democrats call this "callous and cruel," the Liberals describe it as "suffering," and the Greens say the government is "cruel and short-sighted."
Should people who went to a hospital but no longer needed hospital care have been allowed to stay indefinitely in a hospital bed, blocking a patient who does need hospital care? Because that is the logical outcome of opposition parties'calls to end this practice.
"Even with their track record, this is shocking," Niagara Falls NDP MPP Wayne Gates said. "I urge them to reconsider their actions and repeal this cruel legislation. Everyone should be free to live in a home of their choosing."
Really? To quote the Rolling Stones, you can't always get what you want.
"For every patient that has been forcibly transferred, many more have been coerced to leave 'voluntarily'under threat of financial penalty," Liberal MPP Adil Shamji said.
Well, we only know of one family refusing to leave a hospital and being charged per day to stay. The family says they won't pay and won't accept any home in the area with space available.
Without Bill 7, we'd have seen more than 20,000 people in similar situations, refusing to leave until they get their home of choice.
Hospitals are the most expensive care and for people discharged by their doctor, it's not even the best care. Letting everyone stay in hospital indefinitely would only ramp up hospital costs and make hospital care harder to get.
Ontario absolutely needs more long-term care homes and more beds and they're being built. There are currently about 76,000 beds with 31,000 new beds set to open up by 2028 while 28,000 existing beds are upgraded..
Meantime, we need hospital beds opened up and if there's a reasonable offer of a longterm care bed for someone discharged by their physician, they should take it, or move in with family.
You don't fix the problems in our health-care system by repeating past mistakes. Bill 7 was good news, not something to be repealed. blilley@postmedia.com

Document 175 of 227

Regional MPPS get new posts with provincial government; Quinte MPP Smith becomes minister of education; MPP Clark appointed government house leader

Author: Zajac, Ronald

Publication info: Zajac, Ronald. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/regional-mpps-get-new-posts-with-provincial/docview/3065634030/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Regional+MPPS+get+new+posts+with+provincial+government%3B+Quinte+MPP+Smith+becomes+minister+of+education%3B+MPP+Clark+appointed+government+house+leader&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-06-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Zajac%2C+Ronald&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Less than a year after he resigned as municipal affairs and housing minister amid the Greenbelt controversy, local MPP Steve Clark is finding his way back to the corridors of power.
The MPP for Leeds-Grenville- Thousand Islands and Rideau Lakes, who resigned from cabinet last September, was appointed government house leader in Premier Doug Ford's latest cabinet shuffle, announced late Thursday afternoon.
While it's not a cabinet position, the role of house leader is a key one within the government, as that person frequently fields the questions in question period.
"I just am really happy that I get the chance to serve and help deliver on our government's ambitious agenda," Clark said Friday afternoon in a telephone interview from Queen's Park.
Clark has served in previous years as the Opposition's house leader and deputy house leader before that, and he believes this past experience was behind the premier's latest decision.
The job of house leader requires frequent communication with one's counterparts from the other parties, he noted.
Clark, who had been Ontario's municipal affairs and housing minister since 2018, resigned from his cabinet role on Labour Day, only days after a damning report from the integrity commissioner found he violated ethics rules when the province opened up parts of the protected Greenbelt for development.
Last September, Clark said his decision to step down as Ontario's housing minister was the result of his own reflection and not done at the urging of the premier.
At the time, he signalled his intention to remain as the area's MPP and responded to questions about his hopes of eventually returning to cabinet by stating his focus remained on the riding.
In early March, Clark was named chairman of the Ontario government's Standing Committee on Social Policy, a position that has him overseeing debate on legislation related to health care, education and other government business. He retains that position after this latest shuffle.
Clark would not speculate on whether this latest promotion is a step toward his eventual return to a ministerial office.
"My focus has, for many months, been the riding. I'm excited that it now is expanding to the government house leader role," said Clark.
"I'm glad to serve wherever I can."
Ford's changes on Thursday come as the legislature rose for an extended summer break - to late October - and as the premier has been fuelling early election speculation by refusing to commit to waiting until 2026 to go to the polls.
The shuffle sees Stephen Lecce move from education, where he has served as minister since 2019, to energy, swapping portfolios with Todd Smith, the Bay of Quinte MPP who has been the minister of energy for the past three years.
Lecce, who butted heads with teachers'unions many times over the years, said he was honoured to serve in that role but is excited to lead the retitled Ministry of Energy and Electrification.
"In order to grow the economy, with millions of people coming to our shores and significant industry moving to Ontario, including the fact that under our previous leadership, we've put (Ontario) on the map as one of the primary destinations for EV construction," he said Thursday.
"We need a significant supply of affordable, reliable energy for the people of Ontario. We will build the energy capacity we will need to supply the energy to grow our economy."
Ontario's major teachers'unions are welcoming a change in education ministers in a cabinet shuffle, saying they hope it signals a new path forward with the Ford's government.
The Elementary Teachers'Federation of Ontario says it stands ready to work with Smith after six years of what it characterizes as "being misled, dismissed and undervalued by Minister Lecce."
Karen Littlewood, president of the Ontario Secondary School Teachers'Federation, says the education file is a really tough one, but she is hopeful that Smith will be more open to formal meetings with her.
Smith will not have to face the daunting task of contract negotiations, with deals secured under Lecce's leadership that will extend past the next election, but the unions say schools, teachers and students need greater levels of funding and supports. - with files from Allison Jones and Liam Casey, The Canadian Press

Document 176 of 227

Ford reduces surgical wait times, opposition complains

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-reduces-surgical-wait-times-opposition/docview/3065115459/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+reduces+surgical+wait+times%2C+opposition+complains&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the first year of Ontario expanding access to cataract procedures in community clinics, 32,000 extra people improved their eyesight with quicker access to the procedure. Now, the Ford government wants to take that success and expand it to MRI and CT scans to bring down wait times.
The province launched a call for applications for new clinics on Monday with new licences to be issued this fall.
"When it comes to wait times for surgeries and procedures, the status quo is not acceptable," Health Minister Sylvia Jones said in a statement announcing the changes.
The Ford government's plan is to roll out faster access for patients who don't need access to a hospital by using community clinics. These clinics have so far been a mix of public and private organizations, but the patient only pays with their OHIP card, not their credit card.
Still, the usual suspects are outraged that any private-sector involvement exists in health care. They would rather people wait and suffer with ill health than see the private sector involved in any way.
Both NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie claimed that Ford was forcing people to pull out their credit card to access health services or to get to the front of the line. That's simply not true and these leaders should be celebrating the success of these programs, not making false claims to scare people away.
Last year, ahead of these changes coming into effect but after the licences for new cataract surgeries had been issued, I interviewed Dr. Anuj Bhargava. In addition to being the chief of ophthalmology at Ottawa's Monfort Hospital, Dr. Bhargava performed cataract surgery at the Herzig Eye Institute.
"We have only one operating room at the Monfort hospital, which is shared by six surgeons in a week, so each of us has less than one day a week," Dr. Bhargava told me in January 2023.
Imagine, the head of eye surgery in a major city hospital gets less than one day of operating room time per week. He described how without getting their eyes fixed, his older patients were losing their independence, such as their ability to drive. Dr. Bhargava said expanding the surgeries to community clinics would reduce wait times and that is exactly what happened for 32,000 Ontario residents.
The opposition NDP and Liberals would prefer that those 32,000 Ontario residents were still on the waiting list and that those behind them were forced to wait longer still for care. As the province looks to expand this model to MRI and CT scans, and eventually endoscopies and hip and knee surgeries, the NDP and Liberals have one goal - make you to wait longer for your care.
To them, keeping a pure but failing system is more important than allowing the private sector to partner with the public health system to deliver you timely care.
"Increasing the number of MRI and CT scans being done each year is the next step we're taking to reduce wait times for more publicly funded surgeries and procedures, ensuring people get the care they need, when they need it," minister Jones said.
The goal of her plan is to reduce the time between when an MRI or CT scan is ordered and when the procedure happens to and average of 28 days in every region of the province. Right now, the average wait time for an MRI in Ontario is 90 days while the average for a CT scan is 81 days.
Both of those are unacceptable waits. Ford and Jones are doing something to fix this problem while Stiles and Crombie chirp from the cheap seats to try to maintain the status quo.
Ignore the critics, embrace the change and get your health treatment not only faster, but in a medically acceptable period of time. blilley@postmedia.com

Document 177 of 227

Crombie's false booze claims show her taxand-spend Liberal roots

Author: Lilley, Bria n

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crombies-false-booze-claims-show-her-taxand-spend/docview/3064383942/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crombie%27s+false+booze+claims+show+her+taxand-spend+Liberal+roots&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-06-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If Bonnie Crombie had her way, we wouldn't be moving to beer and wine in corner and grocery stores. Or, if we did, she'd impose taxes and fees so steep as to make the system unaffordable and doomed to fail.
The Liberals put out a news release last Monday claiming that Premier Doug Ford's plan to put beer and wine in convenience stores is going to cost the province $1 billion. A quick look at Crombie's claim and her breakdown of the costs shows it's not true, but she keeps repeating it.
Apparently, Crombie comes from the Trudeau Liberal school of communications.
"If you repeat it, if you say it louder, if that is your talking point, people will totally believe it," Catherine McKenna, Trudeau's former environment minister, said.
Crombie is following the same pattern of saying it louder, repeating it and making it her talking point with this $1-billion claim.
The Liberal leader is claiming that the province will lose $375 million because a fee the LCBO imposed when the Wynne Liberals put beer into 450 grocery stores won't be extended to beer sold in the estimated 8,500 other stores. The fee was invented out of whole cloth by the LCBO and wasn't needed, Crombie's claim of $375 million lost is based on a media report that extrapolates that fee across all new stores.
Dropping this unnecessary fee was a key ask of the craft brewing industry, which also lobbied hard for getting their products into convenience stores while Molson and Labatt opposed the idea.
She also claims that the province will lose $150 million by telling the LCBO to offer a 10 per cent wholesale discount to non-government retailers. In many instances, that 10 per cent discount will result in you getting beer or wine at cheaper prices, in other cases, it will mean the local retailer can make a living.
Either way, Crombie is demanding higher prices by insisting there be no discount to help get this new sales chain off the ground.
Finally, Crombie claims the province is forgoing $300 million in revenue by not charging a licence fee for convenience stores or grocers to carry booze in their stores. The province is charging a licensing fee for any new retailer selling alcohol, but it's not as big as Crombie wants.
Crombie points to Saskatchewan, where licence fees went for as much as $450,000. I'm not sure Crombie has spent much time in Saskatchewan, but the government there closed all their liquor stores as part of privatization.
Is she asking the Ford government to shut down the LCBO and let the private sector run all aspects of this sector? Crombie is advocating that independently owned convenience stores pay as much as $50,000 to the government to be able to sell beer and wine and that grocery stores pay as much as $100,000. A fee that size would make it unaffordable for the independent owners of these stores to enter the market, meaning we'd be left with the status quo.
The Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario is required by law to charge licensing fees on a cost-recovery basis, Crombie wants to use this government agency as a profit centre.
There is enough to be upset about with the Ford government paying The Beer Store $225 million without inventing new piles of money to create the false idea that the province is losing $1 billion.
Crombie's claims, repeated without any fact check by many in the media, also don't look at new revenue being generated.
The Convenience Industry Council of Canada projects 7,000 to 7,500 new jobs will be created by this change. The study also estimates $213 million in annual tax payments being generated with 69 per cent, or $147 million annually, going to the provincial government.
The province estimates the LCBO will gain somewhere between $895 billion and $1.2 billion from being the exclusive wholesaler to these 8,500 new stores.
We can have a debate about the merits of Ford's plan, including the timing and the $225 million to the Beer Store, without resorting to false and exaggerated claims. blilley@postmedia.com

Document 178 of 227

'How am i going to pay for that?'; Canadian woman, 93, wanted to help her family. Instead she got hit with $40K in capital gains tax

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/how-am-i-going-pay-that-canadian-woman-93-wanted/docview/3062956155/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27How+am+i+going+to+pay+for+that%3F%27%3B+Canadian+woman%2C+93%2C+wanted+to+help+her+family.+Instead+she+got+hit+with+%2440K+in+capital+gains+tax&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A gift to her daughter and grandson has left a 93-year-old Ontario woman with sticker shock.
Liz Diachun told CTV News she was planning to give her family two lots on her farm property in Warkworth to help them establish homes. However, her lawyer informed her that despite the land being a gift, it still needed to be appraised at fair market value for tax purposes.
The two lots were appraised at $125,000 and $145,000, totalling $270,000, leaving Diachun with a tax bill of about $40,000, an amount she said she cannot pay.
"I'm on pension. How am I going to pay for that?" said Diachun. "I'm not one of the wealthy. I'm 93 years old. Who is going to give me a mortgage? Who is going to give me a loan?" Diachun is hopeful she can still find a way to make the gifts happen, but the clock is ticking. Beginning next month, the capital gains tax inclusion rate will increase from 50 per cent to 67 per cent for amounts over $250,000. Here's what to know. Q A capital-gains
What is capital gains tax? tax applies to the profit made from selling a capital asset, such as stocks, real estate or other investments.
The current system includes an inclusion rate of 50 per cent, meaning that only half of the capital gain is considered taxable income.
The taxable capital gain is taxed at personal income tax rates, which vary depending on an individual's total income and the tax bracket they fall into. Q How is it changing?
A Under the new federal budget, the inclusion rate will increase from 50 per cent to 66.5 per cent for capital gains exceeding $250,000 per year for individuals.
The changes are scheduled to take effect on June 25, though the Liberals have yet to introduce the legislation to increase the capital gains inclusion rate. The delay means Canadians like Diachun are in a tricky situation as they make decisions about selling, or gifting, major assets.
Earlier this week Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland said the legislative process will begin in the "coming weeks and certainly before the House rises (on June 21)."
In the meantime, Canadians are mulling major decisions without firm details about how the new policy might affect their tax bill.
For corporations and trusts, meanwhile, the new 66.5 per cent inclusion rate will apply to all capital gains, without the $250,000 threshold.
There will also be a one-time increase in the lifetime capital gains exemption for small business shares and qualifying farm or fishing properties, increasing from $1 million to $1.25 million. Q When was capital gains tax established and how has it changed? A It was first introduced in 1972 as part of the tax reforms implemented by then Finance Minister Edgar Benson, a member of Prime Minister Pierre Trudeau's government.
The tax was intended to broaden the tax base and tax income from investments. The rate when first implemented was 50 per cent, though it has fluctuated over the years, including jumping to 66 per cent in 1988 and 75 per cent in 1990.
In 2000, under then Finance Minister Paul Martin, the rate dropped back to 50 per cent.
The upcoming changes are part of a strategy to make "the wealthiest Canadians pay their fair share," according to the Liberal government.

Document 179 of 227

No, Doug Ford isn't about to call an early election; But he likes the way the uncertainty frightens Bonnie Crombie's Liberals

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-doug-ford-isnt-about-call-early-election-he/docview/3062780371/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No%2C+Doug+Ford+isn%27t+about+to+call+an+early+election%3B+But+he+likes+the+way+the+uncertainty+frightens+Bonnie+Crombie%27s+Liberals&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We aren't headed toward an early election in Ontario any time soon, but Premier Doug Ford is having fun keeping people guessing.
Last week, Ford was asked at a news conference about early election speculation, and he wouldn't rule out an early vote.
He did the same thing while speaking to Jerry Agar on Newstalk 1010 on Tuesday morning.
"I can't answer that. I just can't right now," Ford said.
Understandably, Agar was puzzled and noted if Ford can't answer that, then no one can.
"As far as I'm concerned. We're gonna focus on our agenda, getting things done," Ford said.
"Stay tuned." So, Ford's not denying an early election, which must mean it's true. The Toronto Star has run several stories on the idea that Ford is going to the polls early, Agar had a column in the Toronto Sun on the issue and talked about it on radio, and Global New Radio's Greg Brady also is convinced it's happening.
With that many people pushing the idea, there must be some truth to it.
Not really. Mischief on the part of Ford and his team for sure, but why would they go to the polls early? Ford is old enough to remember what happened to Liberal Premier David Peterson when he called an election three years into a majority; he got clobbered by the NDP.
Does Ford want to be known as the next premier to give us an NDP government with Premier Marit Stiles at the helm? Not likely.
One theory being floated, pushed really, is that Ford will use the good news announcement of beer and wine in corner stores to convince voters he's delivering for them. As welcome as that promise is, it's hardly worthy of an early election.
Ford needs to show tangible progress on issues like Highway 413, the Bradford bypass, building new hospitals and more before he goes to the voters. He moved the beer and wine announcement up so that the changes and negotiations weren't happening so close to the next election, not so he could call an early campaign.
Another theory is Ford wants to get the election out of the way before the federal vote in October 2025. Some people believe in the theory of alternation, that when Conservatives are elected in Ottawa, for example, they will vote for Liberals provincially.
One big skeptic of that theory is Ford's own campaign manager, Kory Teneycke, who has never bought into the idea. Teneycke has been scoffing at the idea of an early election since it first started surfacing a few months ago, and his view hasn't changed as the whisper turned into a scream this week.
Those closest to Ford say there are no plans for an early election.
Too many people think politics resembles the TV series The West Wing with smart people devising grand plans, when in reality it's closer to the series Veep, and we should all be amazed anything gets done.
If I'm right on this, and trust me, I am, there is still the question of why he won't rule out an early vote. It comes down to two things: Ford doesn't want to rule anything out, and it terrifies Bonnie Crombie's Liberals and keeps them off balance.
A week is a lifetime in politics and a year is an eternity. Things could change.
Ford has been known to backtrack or change his mind a time or two, but at this point, don't start betting on an early vote. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Voters

Document 180 of 227

'I've just had it up to here'; Justin trudeau and doug ford decry 'vile and despicable acts of antisemitism'

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ive-just-had-up-here-justin-trudeau-doug-ford/docview/3062780357/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27I%27ve+just+had+it+up+to+here%27%3B+Justin+trudeau+and+doug+ford+decry+%27vile+and+despicable+acts+of+antisemitism%27&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Ontario Premier Doug Ford decried the shootings in the past week at Toronto and Montreal Jewish schools and vowed to do everything in their power to catch and punish the perpetrators.
Trudeau started off his remarks Thursday morning at the grand opening of Sanofi's $800-million vaccine manufacturing facility in Toronto by saying that, just a short drive away, parents were dropping off their daughters at a Jewish girls'elementary school that was fired on by gunmen over the weekend.
Another Jewish school in Montreal was also hit by at least one bullet Wednesday night.
"I'm, of course, relieved that no one was hurt. I'm disgusted by these vile and despicable acts of antisemitism. It needs to stop now," said Trudeau.
"To Jewish parents and students across the country, we stand with you. We will always work to safeguard your right to live proudly Jewish lives in Canada."
Trudeau said the federal government is working with local law enforcement and will use "all available federal resources to ensure that the perpetrators are held to account."
"We cannot and will not let this antisemitism stand in Canada," he added.
Ford said he agreed with Trudeau and promised that the province would "throw every single resource" to catch the perpetrators, and "throw them in jail, throw away the key."
"Folks, let's cut to the chase here.
What lunatic goes around shooting up schools? That is just unacceptable. Imagine that. Imagine a little kid in a school because they're from the Jewish faith, someone goes by and start shooting through the school windows?" "These guys need to be caught.
They need to be punished. They need to be thrown in jail, and I'll tell you, we have zero tolerance for this anywhere in Ontario," he said.
Federal Minister of Mental Health and Addictions Ya'ara Saks thanked Trudeau and Ford for their words of support and said that, as a Jewish MP, "allies matter."
"As a Jewish mother in this community, we have the right to be safe and feel safe each and every day," she said.
Trudeau and Ford weren't forthcoming with concrete details when asked by a reporter what resources their governments would use to jail the perpetrators, given that politicians are not supposed to direct the police's actions.
Trudeau pointed to his government's Security Infrastructure Program, which provides support for communities at risk of hate-related crimes and incidents at places of worship and schools, but he said there is "much more to do."
"The core thing that we have continued to call for is an end to hatred and intolerance in Canada, whether it be antisemitism or Islamophobia," he said.
"We are seeing that events overseas that are devastating and incredibly difficult to watch should not be spilling over into Canadians hating on other Canadians. We are all united in our strength and diversity and our desire for a better country in a better world." Ford said that Ontario "will throw every single resource of increased funding for the police" and has "given thousands of dollars to places of worship."
And he had a clear message to potential newcomers to Canada.
"Don't come to Canada if you're going to start terrorizing neighbourhoods like this. Simple as that. You want to come to Canada? You want to be a resident of Ontario? You get along with everyone. I don't care what background, what religion, what race you come from."
Ford touted Ontario's diverse population as its "No. 1 selling tool around the world."
"We have 110 nationalities here in Ontario, 200 languages being spoken, and guess what? Ninety-nine per cent of the people get along. There are wars going on all around the world. But we still get along," he said. "Enough is enough. I've just had it up to here." "People outside those communities, they've had it too. They aren't used to this happening here in Ontario. I'm done with this stuff."
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow said that the city has been conducting "safety walks" in collaboration with the police, local councillors and different communities. Chow said some places of worship or schools may consider putting up shatterproof glass, fencing or new lighting.
Ford expressed shock at hearing that shatterproof glass might be necessary in Ontario schools. "We have to have this in Ontario schools, like, where have we gone? Again, we're all just fed up with this nonsense. I'll tell you that. It shouldn't have to be that way."
He said that he was not criticizing Chow, adding " I love the mayor by the way." calevesque@postmedia.com

Subject: Antisemitism; Schools

Document 181 of 227

Ford delivers on beer and wine promise, but with a catch

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-delivers-on-beer-wine-promise-with-catch/docview/3060938506/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+delivers+on+beer+and+wine+promise%2C+but+with+a+catch&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You'll be able to get beer, wine and cider in corner stores by the end of the summer, Premier Doug Ford announced Friday.
The announcement moves up the changes by a significant margin with the province having previously only said the move to expanded sales would happen before Jan. 1, 2026.
The cost of that move, though, is raising questions, but proponents say the benefits are clear - more jobs and more tax revenue for the government.
Starting Aug. 1, grocery stores that already sell booze will be able to expand their offerings including ready to drink cocktails like a Caesar in a can and larger cases of beer. After Sept. 5, convenience stores will be able to do the same while new grocery stores and big box stores like Costco can come online as of Oct. 31.
One thing raising questions is a pledge to pay The Beer Store up to $225 million over the next 19 months for what are described as "added costs."
Those added costs will include ramping up distribution and recycling facilities to deal with all the new stores. A new agreement between the province and TBS states that "to facilitate the use of TBS and its efficient recycling and distribution systems for the benefit of Ontario on terms acceptable to the parties."
The agreement also specifically states that TBS must keep 386 retail stores open until at least Jan. 1, 2026.
In part, this $225 million is for services TBS will be providing to the province, like recycling. It will also in part be about subsidizing unprofitable stores and preserving jobs for the next 19 months.
Some of these costs were going to be incurred no matter when the expansion of booze sales happened. The province already pays TBS to run the recycling program for them, so those costs would have to be paid, but clearly some of this is paying off The Beer Store for ending their contract early.
Supporters of the plan to expand sales say the financial impact will be significant, including increased taxes for government.
A study on the economic impact of these changes conducted for the Convenience Industry Council of Canada predicts between 7,000 to 7,500 jobs created. Most of those would be in retail but they also project 500 to 800 in what are described as "middle income jobs."
The study also projects $165 million spent on installing coolers and other infrastructure. As for taxes, the study estimates more than $200 million in annual tax payments being generated with 69 per cent going to the provincial government.
"Our plan will create new growth opportunities for local brewers, wineries, retailers and small businesses. It's going to support local jobs and most importantly, it's going to give people more choice and convenience," Ford said.
The move to expand alcohol sales was fought for fiercely by the convenience store industry and by craft brewers who see this as a real opportunity for expansion. By contrast, the changes have been fought for years by The Beer Store and its owners Labatt, Molson and Sleeman.
Right now, Ontario has the fewest retail alcohol stores per capita, excluding bars and restaurants, in all of Canada. There are just 2.4 stores per 10,000 population in Ontario compared to 21.1 in Newfoundland and Labrador and 12.2 in Quebec.
After these changes, the province expects there to be 9.4 retail stores per 10,000 population.
Ontario is finally catching up to other provinces and many states south of the border. It's a change many politicians have promised but never delivered.
Ford now can claim credit for doing what others couldn't. Whether the public will toast him or have a sour taste in their mouths over the $225-million payment is an open question. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Beer; Convenience stores; Costs; Grocery stores

Document 182 of 227

Ford government blocks release of info on UP Express reversal

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-government-blocks-release-info-on-up-express/docview/3059656011/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+blocks+release+of+info+on+UP+Express+reversal&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-05-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On April 15, at 9 a.m., Premier Doug Ford announced his government would make every other UP Express train from Union Station to Pearson Airport a true express. Less than 36 hours later, the government had walked back that policy and apologized for upsetting people.
It took the Cabinet Office 31 days to tell me they needed another 35 days to look for documents to explain what happened. At least they aren't demanding search fees to look for these documents, the way the Ministry of Transportation and Metrolinx are.
Something happened to prompt the decision by the Ford government to make every other train an express and something prompted the reversal. The public deserves to know what happened and why.
But, as they always do when it comes to Metrolinx, the Ford government is being secretive.
There are parts of the story that are easy to figure out.
The province announced they were expanding GO Train service to Kitchener, which runs along the same line and provides service to the Bloor and Weston Stations. Clearly, they thought people would switch from using UP Express to GO Train service for those stops.
When the public backlash occurred, Toronto Coun. Frances Nunziata, a Ford ally, was inundated with complaints. So, too, was MPP, cabinet minister and the premier's nephew, Michael Ford, who represents the same area as Nunziata.
I'd hate to think that Ford was making policy decisions based on how his nephew and a political ally were feeling, but without evidence, that's the most plausible answer - and it's not a good one.
After the reversal was made, a reversal that was whiplash-inducing even for a government known to reverse course, several Freedom of Information Act requests were made. Ford's Cabinet Office, the Ministry of Transportation and Metrolinx all were asked for the same thing.
"Provide copies of any documents providing statistics on Metrolinx ridership and/or analysis in support of UP Express plans," the request read.
Metrolinx responded first May 13 with a demand for $225 in search fees for the documents. Next was the Ministry of Transportation on May 15, which demanded two different search fees, one for $120 and another for $105, to look for documents about UP Express plans.
Last Friday at 5:16 p.m., we got the final response from Cabinet Office. They shoved it out the door as they shut down for the Victoria Day long weekend.
"We wish to advise you that the time has been extended in accordance with Sect. 27(1)(b) of the Act for an additional five weeks to June 21, 2024," the letter from Cabinet Office read.
That extension, until June 21, means we won't receive anything from Ford's department until the deadline has passed for paying the search fees on the three other demands. If those fees aren't paid by June 12 and 14, respectively, they will be deemed abandoned and any attempts to get at the information on why this decision was made will need to start all over again.
It's all too cute by half. The Ford government is once again covering up for Metrolinx, as they have done for years. This is a troubled agency, which can't lay tracks on the Eglinton Crosstown properly and can't deliver projects on time - but one for which Ford, and his office, regularly run interference.
There is also the problem that Metrolinx just decides not to release information, claiming it is secret advice to government.
Last year, when they were forced to admit Crosstown's tracks were laid improperly, Metrolinx released 16 pages with almost all information redacted. The Transportation Ministry and Cabinet Office did the same thing.
The public deserves to know what is going on; we are paying for the mess that is Metrolinx.
If Doug Ford believes his own shtick - that he's in it for the little guy - he will tell his officials to release the information quickly and freely.
Sadly, the record shows that Ford and those around him are more interested in protecting Metrolinx chief executive Phil Verster than being honest with taxpayers. blilley@postmedia.com

Document 183 of 227

Liberals aim to give citizenship rights to Canadians born abroad; Conservatives changed law in 2009 to prevent passing down of citizenship

Author: Tumilty, Ryan

Publication info: Tumilty, Ryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-aim-give-citizenship-rights-canadians/docview/3059655958/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+aim+to+give+citizenship+rights+to+Canadians+born+abroad%3B+Conservatives+changed+law+in+2009+to+prevent+passing+down+of+citizenship&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-05-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Tumilty%2C+Ryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadians born abroad who have children overseas will be able to pass along their citizenship under new legislation introduced into the House of Commons on Thursday.
Immigration Minister Marc Miller tabled the legislation, which aims to close loopholes that have created so called "lost Canadians" - people with ties to the country who are unable to pass along their citizenship to their children.
Under current rules, Canadians who have children abroad can only pass along their citizenship if their children were born in Canada.
The then Conservative government of Stephen Harper changed the law in 2009 so that Canadian parents who were born abroad could not pass down their citizenship to children who were also born abroad.
The legislation would automatically confer citizenship rights to children born since 2009 who were affected by the Conservatives' changes.
It would also create a new test for children born after the legislation comes into force.
Parents who were born outside of Canada will need to have spent at least three years in Canada before the birth or adoption of their child to pass on their Canadian citizenship. The government has no idea how many people will be automatically granted citizenship if the legislation is passed.
"We're a country that supports human rights, equality, and respect for all people," Immigration Minister Marc Miller said in the foyer outside of the House of Commons after he tabled the bill.
Miller said there have to be rules for citizenship, but they must be reasonable.
"There's no doubt that Canadian citizenship is highly valued and recognized around the world. We want citizenship to be fair, accessible, with clear and transparent rules," he said.
NDP MP Jenny Kwan, who is supporting the Liberal bill, said it was past time the issue was addressed. She said Canadians travel the world to work and study and should be able to hold onto their citizenship rights.
"Canada is a global community of Canadians: people travel, people study abroad, people work abroad, people fall in love abroad, people have families abroad."
The Ontario Superior Court of Justice ruled last year that the 2009 change to the law was unconstitutional, and gave Ottawa until June 19 to fix the problem.
"This is an example of Conservatives having taken away Canadians'rights and something they hold most dear to them, in their citizenship," Miller said.
The Conservatives did not immediately respond to a request for comment. The government may have to request an extension from the court while the bill makes its way through the House of Commons, Miller said, but he doesn't want to wait long to fix the issue because people are being prejudiced in the meantime. Postmedia News, with files from The Canadian Press

Document 184 of 227

Greenhouse gas targets: where's the plan?

Author: Spears, Tom

Publication info: Spears, Tom. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/greenhouse-gas-targets-wheres-plan/docview/3057539661/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Greenhouse+gas+targets%3A+where%27s+the+plan%3F&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-05-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Spears%2C+Tom&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's great that the federal Liberals want to create more housing. Homes are good. But we can't ignore that each new home (and new car, and new person) creates more greenhouse gases. Energy-efficient new buildings don't replace old buildings'emissions; they add to them. And today the federal Liberal plan is to add up to 3.9 million homes.
As well, last month, Ontario presented the Cutting Red Tape to Provide More Homes Act, opening up more rural land for new subdivisions.
More people bring more greenhouse gas emissions, even as we pledge blindly, stupidly, to reduce them. So let's stop pretending we can cut emissions in a fast-growing population.
As a kid in 1967, I had to sing a dopey centennial song: "Caaa-naaa-daaa, now we are 20 million!" But we've pushed past 40 million now, with emissions rising even as we claim to lead the world in achieving net zero emissions - promising to reduce them by 40 to 45 per cent by 2030, only six years away, though we have made no movement in that direction.
Carmakers have vastly reduced the pollutants that cause smog, but can't change the fact that burning fuel produces carbon dioxide, period.
I was there with Catherine McKenna, then-minister of the environment, when she said in 2019 that technology would advance in ways we cannot foresee, as it has with cellphone tech (nice campaign newser, with falafels). On the carbon-burning front, we're still waiting. As with the Kyoto Protocol, we have signed up for ambitious targets with no idea how to reach them.
And here's a little-discussed side note: building new homes and the rest requires concrete. That alone is a big greenhouse gas problem.
Scientific American reports that "worldwide every year, cement and concrete production generates as much as nine per cent of all human CO2 emissions." The journal Nature puts it slightly lower at eight per cent.
Yes, but if we drop gasoline though electrification? It takes about 25 years (optimistically) to plan and build a new nuclear plant. Our last, Darlington, was budgeted at $3.9 billion and came in at $14.4 billion in the 1990s, and we need a whole lot more of these to be carbon-free. Also, communities need to welcome a new nuclear plant. You think anyone will accept one in urban Ottawa? We are committed, as a nation, to reduce emissions by 40 to 45 per cent by 2030 - a scale we have never even approached.
The federal website on greenhouse gas projections shows two things: First, our emissions dropped sharply in 2008-09 (major recession) and again in 2020-21 (pandemic lockdown). So when people are out of work, emissions drop. But later they pick up.
Second, the feds give us actual emissions figures through 2022, largely showing no significant reductions, but from 2023 on, emissions are forecast to drop dramatically. These figures are just projections. I can't wait to see what the actual 2023 numbers look like, but I bet projections for 2024 will look just dandy.
Please stop pretending. If you claim we can reach net zero despite accelerating urban sprawl, show me how. And save the bragging till you've done it. Journalist Tom Spears is a former Ottawa Citizen environment reporter.

Subject: Greenhouse gases; Emissions; Carbon

Document 185 of 227

Napanee's new CAO looking forward to small-town life

Author: Michelle Dorey Forestell

Publication info: Michelle Dorey Forestell. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/napanees-new-cao-looking-forward-small-town-life/docview/3056063239/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Napanee%27s+new+CAO+looking+forward+to+small-town+life&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-05-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Michelle+Dorey+Forestell&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a month, Matthew Grant will take on the role of Greater Napanee's chief administrative officer (CAO), and "the small-town vibe" is something he looks forward to.
The Town of Greater Napanee announced on May 2 that it had hired Grant and that he would step into his new role on June 17.
After being warmly welcomed to the neighbourhood, Grant said he's heard from "every single person" he's talked to about Napanee that he will enjoy his new home. And that is saying something.
Grant is a world traveller with significant education and work experience. Born and raised in Toronto, he completed his undergraduate studies at Western University in London, Ont. He then lived overseas for about seven years, first teaching English in Japan and eventually becoming a translator for a company there. "I got really interested in politics and political affairs and that sort of thing, so I moved to Australia to do my master's degree in journalism at the University of Queensland and lived there for a couple of years."
Returning to Canada, he first worked in Medicine Hat, Alta., as an education reporter, he said. "And then I saw this really interesting opportunity come up at the Whitehorse Star, which sadly is going to close its doors after 124 years a week from now. ... So I was a municipal affairs reporter and a political reporter there." This led to a job in municipal government as Whitehorse's first communications and government relations manager.
He also worked in the office of the premier of Yukon for a year, then moved to Alberta to be press secretary first for the minister of health and then for the minister of municipal affairs. In Edmonton, he met his wife, who hailed from the Niagara area.
When the "Orange Chinook" of the 2015 election ushered in that province's dramatic swing away from the Progressive Conservatives to the New Democratic Party, the government change left Grant looking for another position. After a stint in Kelowna working at the University of British Columbia, the couple moved back to Ontario to be closer to their parents. For almost four years, Grant worked at the University of Waterloo, but then the time came to return to the political realm because, he said, "I knew that was where my heart lies."
As communications director for the City of Hamilton, he performed chief of staff duties for the city manager and was a member of the senior leadership team during the COVID-19 pandemic.
With his boss's retirement, Grant decided to seek out his own leadership position, "and that's what brought me to Napanee."
"You know, when I started taking a look at the next step in my career, one of the things we wanted to look at was a smaller community," Grant explained. "When I lived in Whitehorse, I really enjoyed the small-community vibe."
They haven't moved to town yet, but so far, the Grants are excited at what they have seen. They are looking for a home for themselves and their beloved pet bulldogs.
"During the interview process, my wife and I spent some time there, sort of going around and chatting with people about the town - and what I've noticed is people are really proud of Greater Napanee," Grant said. "They're very interested in local affairs. They're very interested in where the community is heading, how it's going to grow, the recreation opportunities. And that's a great thing. I found people were really invested in it.
"And you know, candidly," he went on, "it's in a lovely setting in a place that's not quite as busy as the Greater Toronto/Hamilton area - which is a bit too busy for my wife and I. And so we're looking forward to actually having the ability to contribute to a town that's already well on its way. Also, living in a smaller community where you know the people that you're buying groceries from, you know the people's names who you're buying coffee from, that's appealing to us."
As for the future, Grant said it will be a "getting to know you" process. "So far, I've learned from people who've lived there and from friends, aside from being a historic and lovely community - which actually had one of the oldest furniture factories going - what people keep telling me is it's got a really diverse economy. It's got excellent infrastructure: it's got road, it's got rail, it's got water access, which is really advantageous.
"One thing I really noticed when I came into town is Napanee has got a very robust highway commercial area, and it's also got a vibrant downtown. Those two things don't always go together," Grant pointed out.
He also likes that the town is growing with lots of investment interest. "I think with the Goodyear plant expanding, the battery plants that are coming up, they're increasing the power generation ... it's a town that is poised for some pretty interesting things."
"I think the town of Greater Napanee has a lot to be proud of," said Grant. "My wife and I went and spent a couple of days there because we wanted to understand if this is a place that we want to live and invest our time in. The answer to that is 'Absolutely.' ... My experience of the people that I've talked with is that they're very, very proud of their town, and they have a reason to be." Michelle Dorey Forestell is a Local Journalism Initiative Reporter with the Kingstonist. The Local Journalism Initiative is funded by the Government of Canada.

Document 186 of 227

Again, Canada is spiralling into disorder

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/again-canada-is-spiralling-into-disorder/docview/3055648096/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Again%2C+Canada+is+spiralling+into+disorder&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-05-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Four people killed, including a baby and his grandparents. A horrific head-on collision on Canada's busiest highway that could have been even more deadly, according to eyewitnesses. All caused by a driver fleeing police after allegedly holding up a liquor store.
The driver, international student Gagandeep Singh of Milton, was out on bail for multiple charges of theft and robbery.
According to police, he was also under investigation for carjacking and subject to a driving ban.
The case sparked outrage not just because it took the lives of three innocent people, but because it feels like this was a tragedy waiting to happen.
A week prior, Nirmal Singh (no relation), of Brampton, was arrested for ignoring his fourth lifetime driving ban. Since 2017, Singh has been convicted of 35 Criminal Code and eight Ontario Provincial Offences Act charges, including impaired driving, dangerous driving, flight from police, theft of a motor vehicle, assault, criminal harassment, and obstructing a peace officer.
In November 2023, another man, Rafael Maia, of Mississauga, received a two-year sentence after ignoring nine lifetime driving bans, and committing 10 additional offences. He had been (finally) held in custody for 19 months leading up to his trial, where he was found guilty.
In the House of Commons last week, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre accused the government of favouring criminals and causing tragedies with their "catch and release" bail policies.
Asked about this, Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland accused the Conservatives of scoring "cheap political points" off a family's grief, and then cited recent reforms the Liberals made to the bail system, reforms that didn't prevent Singh from getting his freedom despite the string of charges against him.
The reality is this: The family of baby Aditya Vivaan Golkunath wouldn't be grieving if Singh hadn't been out on bail.
And for years, bail has become easier to get thanks to Liberal policies, specifically Bill C-75, which in 2019 legislated a "principle of restraint" for police and courts to ensure that "release at the earliest opportunity is favoured over detention."
The "unintended consequence of the reform" according to former B.C. Attorney General Murray Rankin, was that more repeat offenders, some accused of random violence, ended up on the streets, and committed more crimes. In December 2022, a man out on bail, with a warrant out for his arrest and a lifetime firearms prohibition, murdered OPP officer Grzegorz Pierzchala. That spurred provincial and territorial leaders to write the federal government asking they reverse the onus for bail in cases where a firearm is used.
The Liberals responded with Bill C-48, which this January brought in a reverse onus for violent crimes involving weapons, including firearms, knives and bear spray. The Liberals also enacted a requirement that courts consider if the accused's criminal record includes any past convictions for violent offending. But judges still have a lot of discretion when it comes to granting bail, and other laws have been undone by the courts on the basis they violated the charter rights of the accused.
In response, Poilievre has mused about using the notwithstanding clause to enact criminal justice reforms that would be immune to court challenges - leading the Liberals to accuse him of wanting to curb all manner of other freedoms in the process.
It's useful to remember why the Tories brought in tough-on-crime laws in the first place. They did so after another family lived a terrible trauma in 2005. Jane Creba, a 15-yearold high school student, was killed in a brazen gang shooting while out doing her Christmas shopping. Creba's killing capped a year of increasing violence, and voters said enough.
In 2005, there was a sense that Canada was spiralling into disorder.
Today, it feels the same. There is no consequence for holding up a liquor store, for failing to obey a driving ban, for spewing hate. There is no incentive to obey the rules. When anything goes, everything goes. People just do whatever the hell they want.
And a family is left grieving a loss they can never repair. Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Bans; Liquor stores; Robbery; Murders & murder attempts; Firearms

Business indexing term: Subject: Liquor stores

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: people; killed; including; grandparents; horrific; collision

Document 187 of 227

Lack of vision Ottawa transit's real problem

Author: Prier, Nate; Hersh, Sam

Publication info: Prier, Nate; Hersh, Sam. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lack-vision-ottawa-transits-real-problem/docview/3054648785/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Lack+of+vision+Ottawa+transit%27s+real+problem&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-05-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Prier%2C+Nate%3BHersh%2C+Sam&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ottawa Mayor Mark Sutcliffe, alongside Premier Doug Ford, recently announced a $543-million investment in Ottawa during the next decade. Most of this funding is allocated to highways, road use and policing efforts.
Though there is some funding for capital projects, when it comes to transit, there is still no funding announced, despite OC Transpo's multimillion-dollar budget deficit.
Ottawa's transit service has struggled to recover since the pandemic, with ridership in 2023 reaching only 66 per cent of pre-pandemic levels. This shortfall is the result of insufficient city investment and a lack of vision from all three levels of government. Yet Sutcliffe and Ford almost entirely place the blame for low transit ridership on remote work.
Meanwhile, Treasury Board President Anita Anand plans to increase mandatory in-office days for federal workers to three days a week. No unions or public servants were consulted.
Ford's insistence on increased transit ridership before allocating funds overlooks the benefits of remote work, which have improved quality of life for many workers and distributed spending to other parts of the city.
Ford and Sutcliffe's rhetoric also assumes transit ridership will increase if workers go to the office more. The issues and solutions, though, are a lot more complicated. Many have opted to drive and pay for parking instead of taking public transit, due to the unreliability of service.
The argument for increased transit ridership serves as a distraction, however, from deeper urban-planning issues.
Downtown Ottawa's post-5 p.m. desolation reflects outdated urban development strategies that prioritize office spaces over vibrant communities. The federal government, as a major landholder, holds significant influence over the city's future and must embrace progressive changes.
The recent federal budget signalled a shift toward repurposing federal buildings for housing and investing in childcare and climate initiatives. However, this was accompanied by a massive job cut of 5,000 workers on the heels of the ArriveCAN outsourcing scandal. We don't need to fall for the old scam that cutting public sector jobs saves taxpayer money when sketchy contractors simply replace accountable public servants.
All that is needed is a little vision.
The trend toward remote work is evident globally.
The federal government has an opportunity to lead this transition while revitalizing boring urban spaces and promoting worker welfare.
Ford's disconnect from Ottawa and disregard for worker preferences undermine efforts to create a city that prioritizes health and well-being. Transit improvements are necessary, but not at the expense of forcing workers into outdated office models. It's time to embrace a vision of downtown Ottawa that reflects the needs and desires of residents.
We have a chance to build a vibrant downtown. Sam Hersh is the co-ordinator for Horizon Ottawa, a municipal grassroots organization that advocates for progressive local change. Nate Prier is the president of the Canadian Association Professional Employees (CAPE), representing more than 25,000 public servants across Ottawa.

Subject: Work at home; Workers; Scandals; Funding; Pandemics

Business indexing term: Subject: Work at home Workers

Identifier / keyword: ottawa; mayor; sutcliffe; alongside; premier; recently
