Document 202 of 227

Liberals accuse premiers of 'screaming hypocrisy' over tax

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-accuse-premiers-screaming-hypocrisy-over/docview/3020238249/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+accuse+premiers+of+%27screaming+hypocrisy%27+over+tax&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-03-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.4&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA The premiers of New Brunswick and Alberta lambasted the impending federal carbon tax increase in a House of Commons committee and insisted that Canada needs to instead focus on reducing greenhouse gas emissions on the world stage.
But Liberal MPs accused the premiers of "screaming hypocrisy" for raising either electricity prices or provincial gas taxes in their respective provinces on the same date as the schedule increase, April 1.
New Brunswick Premier Blaine Higgs and Alberta Premier Danielle Smith were invited by the Conservative MP who chairs the government operations committee to explain, as Saskatchewan's Scott Moe did the day prior, why the federal government should pause its carbon tax increase on Monday.
"We need to think bigger," Higgs told MPs. "Our situation in Canada is that we're too isolated in our bubble. We're not reaching our potential to help the world and we're causing huge financial impact right across this country on our citizens, and it's unnecessary."
New Brunswick scrapped its own provincial carbon tax scheme just last year, and reluctantly joined the federal program arguing that it would provide relief to its citizens.
Higgs was repeatedly asked by Liberal MPs Wayne Long and Irek Kusmierczyk what his alternative to the federal levy would be in his province. Higgs said New Brunswick could instead ship liquefied natural gas (LNG) to Europe to replace coal plants and thus reduce global emissions.
"We don't have a gas supply currently, and that is the issue," he said.
There are currently eight LNG export projects in various states of progress across Canada, most of which are in British Columbia. LNG Canada, a major project in Kitimat, B.C., is aiming for first exports by 2025 while other projects are set to begin operations between 2027 and 2030.
"We have to reduce global emissions," said Smith. "The way I look at it is that if we can live up to the spirit of what we signed onto in (climate conference) COP28, which recognizes natural gas as a vitally important transition fuel, then we'll be able to reduce the overall global emissions profile."
"If we could simply reduce China's reliance on coal by 20 per cent, that would offset all of the emissions of all of Canada," she added.
Federal Conservatives have been maintaining the pressure to cancel the April 1 increase, which will see the federal carbon tax increase from $65 per tonne to $80. That means an increase of three cents per litre on gasoline, for a total of 17 cents for the federal levy.
At the same time, New Brunswickers will be hit by a nearly 13-per-cent increase of their electricity bills starting April 1, whereas Alberta is set to reinstate its provincial fuel tax at 13 cents per litre - an increase of four cents - after suspending it in 2023.
Kusmierczyk noted that Smith joined Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre at his "axe the tax" rally in Edmonton on Wednesday evening. "Was it a rally to axe your tax?" he taunted.
Smith shot back by saying that the provincial gas tax is used to build roads, and noted the federal government does not, referring to Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault's comments that the Liberals would stop investing in new roads. Guilbeault has since apologized.
Liberal MP Yasir Naqvi piled on and asked Smith if her provincial tax came with a rebate for Albertans, similar to the federal government's quarterly cheques. Smith said Alberta eliminated completely the tax last year when rising oil prices drove up gas prices.
"I would in fact love to see the federal government do the same thing," she said.
After the meeting, Kusmierczyk said the committee had become a "soapbox" for provincial premiers against the carbon tax, and said that the three premiers that they had heard from were "all talk and no action" on climate issues.
Conservative MPs attempted to pass a motion to get the other premiers who have spoken against the carbon tax - Ontario's Doug Ford, Nova Scotia's Tim Houston, Newfoundland and Labrador's Andrew Furey and Prince Edward Island's Dennis King - to appear.
The motion was ultimately rejected by Liberal, Bloc Québécois and NDP MPs.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said this week that Conservative premiers across the country are "misleading Canadians" and "not telling the truth" on the carbon tax.
He pointed to a report last year by the parliamentary budget officer showing that eight out of 10 households receive more money in quarterly carbon-tax rebates than they pay out directly and indirectly in taxes.
"But for ideological reasons, or reasons of pure partisanship, conservative politicians across this country are not telling the truth to Canadians," he said.
But another analysis by the parliamentary budget officer also shows that, once the economic impact of the carbon tax is taken into account, a majority of households will experience a "net loss" by 2030. calevesque@postmedia.com

Document 203 of 227

CSIS warned of 'foreign interference' in Toronto riding during 2019 election; Chief electoral officer had 'high confidence' that race was free and fair

Author: Nardi, Christopher

Publication info: Nardi, Christopher. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/csis-warned-foreign-interference-toronto-riding/docview/3020238244/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=CSIS+warned+of+%27foreign+interference%27+in+Toronto+riding+during+2019+election%3B+Chief+electoral+officer+had+%27high+confidence%27+that+race+was+free+and+fair&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-03-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.3&amp;au=Nardi%2C+Christopher&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's chief electoral officer said Canadian Security Intelligence Service raised "allegations of foreign interference" in the nomination contest of a Toronto-area riding during the 2019 election, but he ultimately determined "no action could be taken."
Speaking to the Public Inquiry into Foreign Interference Thursday, Stéphane Perrault said he has "high confidence" that the 2019 and 2021 federal elections were free and fair.
The same day, the Commissioner of Canada Elections - the country's election investigative watchdog - told the inquiry that her office struggles to investigate foreign interference complaints.
Commissioner Caroline Simard and her top investigator, Mylène Gigou, listed several reasons for those struggles, including a lack of resources for costly foreign interference investigations, challenges with accessing evidence and tracing money, and a lack of useful legal tools.
"I would expect that these are not just challenges that are unique to our office, but likely for most law enforcement agencies as well," Gigou told the inquiry.
Former commissioner Yves Côté also said that there are vast legal exceptions to "undue" foreign interference provisions in the Canada Elections Act. For example, it is legal for a foreigner to tell a Canadian how they should vote in an election.
Perrault's testimony at the inquiry and a summary of an interview with inquiry lawyers two weeks ago revealed that CSIS came to him "unsolicited" in 2019 with concerns about foreign interference allegations in the riding of Don Valley North, in Toronto.
"During the 2019 federal election, he discussed with the Canadian Security Intelligence Service (CSIS) allegations of irregularities related to the voting in the nomination contest in the riding of Don Valley North, Ontario," reads a summary of a Perrault's pre-hearing interview.
"At that point, Mr. Perrault determined that no action could be taken."
The Elections Canada head declined to say which party's nomination contest was the target of the allegations. But Don Valley North is represented by Han Dong, who was elected for the Liberals in the 2019 elections but stepped down from caucus after reports by Global News last year that he allegedly told a Chinese diplomat to delay the release of Michael Kovrig and Michael Spavor, being held prisoner in China, because it would benefit the Liberal party.
Dong has denied the allegations and filed a lawsuit against Global News.
In a report published last year, former special rapporteur on foreign interference David Johnston disputed Global News's story but did note there were "irregularities" discovered in Dong's nomination process in 2019.
He wrote that there was a "well-grounded suspicion that the irregularities were tied to the (People's Republic of China) consulate in Toronto, with whom Mr. Dong maintains relationships."
Perrault told commission counsel that after a routine audit of the Don Valley North nomination contest after the 2019 election, he referred the file to the Commissioner of Canada Elections, the country's election investigative body, for "other reasons that may be unrelated" to CSIS's allegations.
The document cited "accuracy, completeness or failure to meet filing deadlines."
Simard, the Commissioner of Canada Elections, told the inquiry that her office's investigation into Dong's nomination race is ongoing and is focused on compliance and enforcement of the Canada Elections Act political financing regime.
She also revealed that she is investigating an unspecified "electoral contest" in the Greater Vancouver Area.
Perreault frequently declined to answer specific questions about CSIS's allegations in Don Valley North, citing national security concerns, to the obvious displeasure of some parties'lawyers in the room.
Perrault said that as far as Elections Canada's mandate is concerned, he was fully satisfied that the last two federal elections were free and fair.
An undated briefing document from Canada's Security and Intelligence Threats to Elections (SITE) Task Force to Perrault that was tabled at the inquiry Thursday also revealed that security agencies appear to be monitoring attempted foreign interference by Pakistan.
It is one of the first, if not the first, time a Canadian government document identifying Pakistan as a potential source of foreign interference is made public.
But what level risk (if any) posed by Pakistan as well as any suspected methods, interests and targets are all redacted.
SITE was established in the lead-up to the 2019 election to co-ordinate intelligence and information between Canada's intelligence agencies, the RCMP and Global Affairs Canada.
In the undated briefing note, SITE says that "challenges remain" in dealing with foreign interference by China, Russia, India, Iran and Pakistan, though there have been improvements.
It noted that the People's Republic of China (PRC) was the task force's biggest concern and that the sophistication and intensity of its foreign interference (FI) activities in Canada "outpaces" that of other hostile countries.
"The PRC is highly capable and motivated against Canada, and acts in a sophisticated, pervasive and persistent manner in carrying out Fl activities against all levels of Canadian government and civil society. PRC Fl threat actors are pragmatic and tend to pursue paths of least resistance," the document said.
"The PRC continues its efforts to cultivate relationships with current MPs and influence their views on issues of strategic importance to the PRC."
The document notes that other state actors, including India, Russia and a third redacted country, had "not yet demonstrated a significant threat to the election process."
But Indian officials continued to act against Canadian politicians, democratic processes and the Indo-Canadian community in order to promote a positive image of their home country or counter "perceived threats to India" within Canada, reads the note.
SITE noted that Russia could, but at the time of the briefing note had not, engaged in foreign interference activities in Canada. It's efforts generally focused on cyber capabilities as well as discrediting democratic institutions and using disinformation to create social "wedge" issues. cnardi@postmedia.com

Subject: Nominations; Task forces; Canada Elections Act; Intelligence services; Elections

Location: China; Pakistan; Russia; Canada; India

Document 204 of 227

$42M spent on firearms confiscation program; Four years since Liberals announced sweeping changes to Canada's gun law

Author: Passifiume, Bryan

Publication info: Passifiume, Bryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/42m-spent-on-firearms-confiscation-program-four/docview/2986604669/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%2442M+spent+on+firearms+confiscation+program%3B+Four+years+since+Liberals+announced+sweeping+changes+to+Canada%27s+gun+law&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-03-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Passifiume%2C+Bryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Four years after the Liberals announced Trudeau Liberals announced sweeping changes to Canada's gun laws, the government has so far spent $42 million on a federal firearms confiscation program that doesn't yet exist.
In a response to an order paper question filed by Sen. Don Plett in September, Public Safety Canada revealed that $41,904,556 has been spent so far on the government's "firearms buyback program," and that 60 department employees are working on the project.
"This is a boondoggle, and it hasn't even begun," Plett said Friday during the Senate question period, directing his question at government representative Sen. Marc Gold.
"How can your government have spent $42 million on this, when not a single firearm has been bought back?" As well, the documents reveal that IBM has been awarded a $2.27-million contract to develop, design and implement the program.
That figure is nearly double what that contract was worth when Public Safety Canada announced the firm's involvement in the program in 2020.
In the documents, the RCMP say it is managing a team of 15 full-time employees devoted to the gun grab.
As well, Service Canada has assigned two employees to the program, and a response from Public Services and Procurement Canada said it has devoted "the equivalent of 5.825 full-time employees" to the project.
Last April, the federal government commenced the first phase of the program by entering into a $700,000 agreement with the Canadian Sporting Arms and Ammunition Association to confiscate so-called "assault rifles" from retailers.
That program is expected to start later this year.
Work on Phase 2, which involves confiscating legally purchased firearms from their licensed owners, began earlier this year.
The government issued an invitation to qualify earlier this year, seeking vendors interested in collecting and destroying the over 1,500 gun models summarily banned by the federal government under the May 1, 2020 order-in-council.
That order-in-council instantly reclassified around 1,500 popular firearm models to prohibited status, including so-called "military-style" or "assault" firearms.
Initially meant to happen by April 2022, amnesty for licensed owners was extended until Oct. 23, 2023, and again until 2025.
The government estimates that between 10,000 and 15,000 newly prohibited firearms are in the hands of Canadian businesses, and anywhere between 125,000 to 200,000 are owned by licensed individuals.
"These estimates could vary as the number of affected non-restricted firearms is unknown," the invitation remarked.
As many crime guns are committed with prohibited firearms smuggled into Canada from the United States - and therefore unlawful for Canadian licence-holders to own - critics say the government is preoccupying itself with an expensive program whose affect on Canada's crime epidemic will be negligible.
National Post contacted Public Safety Canada for comment.
Firearms policy expert Tim Thurley said that with such an unprecedented program, there's no question it will be expensive.
"Consider, for example, the logistics of confiscating firearms in rural and remote areas, where some of these firearms - such as the Ruger Mini-14 - were widely used by hunters, farmers and recreational or competitive sport shooters," he said.
"Expect the costs to climb significantly further as the business and personal stages of the program are implemented and as more firearms are prohibited."
He said he expects the cost to stretch into the billions.
Simon Fraser University Prof.
Gary Mauser mused that nearly $42 million is a lot of money to spend for what he said amounts to "grandstanding" by the federal government.
"Ottawa is not having much success with their efforts on this file," he said.
"Ontario has refused to co-operate with future confiscations. Saskatchewan has asked to join the appeal. Requests for co-operating organizations have fallen flat."
The fact that the now-banned guns sat safely with their owners without incident for the past four years suggests the 2020 bans - enacted after the 2020 mass shooting in Nova Scotia - were introduced for reasons other than just public safety, Mauser said.
Last October, National Post learned Quebec-based anti-gun group PolySeSouvient was so angry that mandatory confiscation wasn't part of the government's gun control scheme it disinvited Prime Minister Justin Trudeau from future commemorations of the 1989 École Polytechnique shooting.
The government added a confiscation program later that year.
Questions are still swirling around Parliament Hill over how much the confiscation will ultimately cost taxpayers.
Government documents obtained by National Post say costs could reach as high as $2 billion, far greater than election promises suggesting the program would only cost between $400 million and $600 million.
Tracey Wilson, spokesperson for the Canadian Coalition for Firearm Rights (CFFR), told National Post that skyrocketing costs so far don't bode well for the program's future.
"Not only has not a single firearm been confiscated, the program itself doesn't even exist - not even for willing retailers anxious to off-load unsellable inventory," she said.
"The whole premise of the 2020 gun ban was that these firearms, previously approved for sporting and hunting purposes by the RCMP lab, were simply too dangerous to own. Yet four years later, they are all exactly where they've been for decades, if not generations - in the gun safes of licensed, RCMP-vetted gun owners." National Post

Document 205 of 227

N.B. premier shows the way; Editorial

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/n-b-premier-shows-way-editorial/docview/2972949744/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=N.B.+premier+shows+the+way%3B+Editorial&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-03-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: New Brunswick's provincial government tabled its sixth consecutive balanced budget on Tuesday, which is virtually unheard of in Canada. This is a fiscal approach residents shouldn't take for granted, and policymakers across the country should adopt.
Since taking office in 2018, Premier Blaine Higgs has been a light of responsible, centre-right governance. He is not a flashy leader; rather, he's measured, determined and disciplined. His Progressive Conservatives have focused on stabilizing the province's precarious finances - turning New Brunswick from a warning sign to a success story.
These are real accomplishments. Perhaps the biggest indicator is the parliamentary budget officer's judgment that New Brunswick's finances are now sustainable over the long term - one of only five provinces to get this nod.
Change did not happen overnight. Instead, Higgs gradually improved the balance sheet and paid down debt - creating about $100 million of new fiscal room and reducing the impact of punishing debt-servicing charges. More than $2.3 billion in surpluses have accumulated since the PCs took power, according to Public Accounts. (For context, New Brunswick's entire budget comes in at about $13 billion.)
This is a stark contrast to the irresponsibility we've seen in Ottawa, where the federal government has moved its goalposts over and over again to justify ever-higher spending. This year's deficit is expected to reach nearly $50 billion.
It's the kind of policy-making that will saddle future generations with the effects of today's bad decisions. New Brunswick seems to get this point in a way few other provinces do.
Higgs is not without his critics on the left, who point out that surpluses could support higher social spending and that revenue increases owe to higher federal transfers.
Yet these ideas are exactly what got New Brunswick into trouble in the first place. The Liberals'"see a dollar, spend a dollar" routine sent the province careening toward a fiscal cliff, until cooler heads decided to break that cycle.
There have also been right-leaning critics who want Higgs to make substantive reforms. While the premier has delivered modest income tax relief, he hasn't slashed tax rates for a simple reason: it would be premature to do so with so much economic uncertainty, including low growth, high interest rates and inflation still running relatively high.
To be sure, deeper cuts will eventually be needed to make New Brunswick competitive. But the premier is putting his province on a fiscal footing to be able to afford them, without plunging back into deficit.
Others have criticized Higgs's social policy agenda, specifically around gender policies in public schools. But here, too, we find his approach to be the opposite of extreme.
His government's reforms have observed a common-sense distinction between older children aged 16 and up - who can choose to use a different name and pronoun in school - and those who are younger. The latter group needs parental consent to go ahead with such changes.
Far from being a revolution, this is a good balance between individual students'freedoms and parental rights. It's a dose of maturity and reason in a highly emotional, sometimes hysterical culture war.
This is the kind of leadership we need: not only in Ottawa, but in all our provincial capitals. New Brunswick's example shows that even small, less-advantaged provinces can get ahead through good policy. It's an approach that deserves emulation to get Canada back on track.

Subject: Provinces; Budgets

Business indexing term: Subject: Budgets

Location: Canada

Document 206 of 227

Motion would allow family doctors to see 'less paperwork,'NDP say

Author: Hendra, Peter

Publication info: Hendra, Peter. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/motion-would-allow-family-doctors-see-less/docview/2957615236/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Motion+would+allow+family+doctors+to+see+%27less+paperwork%2C%27NDP+say&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-03-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Hendra%2C+Peter&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's NDP believe they have a "simple" solution to help family doctors: hire administrative and interdisciplinary health staff to take care of the paperwork instead of doctors so they can instead focus on helping patients.
Provincial party Leader Marit Stiles stopped by Kingston Thursday morning to announce that the NDP will be making a motion Monday.
"Our motion calls on the government to fund primary care practices to hire more administrative and interdisciplinary health-care staff who can take on the paperwork, take that load off of our primary care providers so they can serve more," Stiles said while standing outside Queen's University's health sciences building and across the street from Kingston General Hospital.
Ontario Medical Association figures suggest that doctors spend, on average, 19 hours a week - 40 per cent of their work weeks - completing paperwork. If they could defer that task to others and give them access to an integrated primary care team, it would be the equivalent of adding 2,000 doctors to the system, Stiles said.
There are 2.2 million Ontarians without a primary care physician, Stiles noted, and 30,000 Kingston residents alone.
"I want to say as well that one of the reasons that we're coming forward with this is because it is a simple solution," the leader of the Opposition said, adding that she believes the provincial government is using the health-care crisis as an excuse to allow privatization to seep into the system.
Among those flanking Stiles at the podium Thursday morning were city councillors Paul Chaves, Jimmy Hussain, Wendy Stephen and Brandon Tozzo, who said he was "shocked" to see the long lineups of people hoping to roster at a new health clinic in his district.
"I would encourage the provincial government to support this motion," Tozzo said. "On behalf of Kingston and city council in Kingston, please, we need this desperately. Our citizens are literally dying waiting for family doctors."
Also standing beside Stiles were Daria Juudi-Hope, the new NDP federal candidate for Kingston and the Islands, and Dr. Richard Zoutman.
Zoutman, who's a specialist and not a family doctor, said he was speaking on behalf of the medical profession and their needs.
"Family doctors are doing an enormous amount of things that are not patient care-related," Zoutman said.
"It's paperwork that no doctor enjoys doing or they're not skilled at doing. But also, it's becoming financially unviable. The overhead to family doctors'clinics has shot up dramatically. And the fee schedule has not kept pace and family doctors are struggling to make ends meet and it is not an attractive option for our medical students, to be quite honest with you."
Zoutman said previous attempts to streamline the administrative side of family medicine have proven unsuccessful.
"In family medicine now, clinics are bare-bones operations, so the poor family doctor goes home with a gigantic briefcase stuffed with paperwork that they're going to do on the dining room table at night after their kids go to bed," Zoutman said. "It's gotten seriously out of control."
Stiles said the NDP's "more patients, less paperwork" motion puts Premier Doug Ford and his Progressive Conservative government "on notice."
"We will keep pushing this and we will continue to build a campaign around it," Stiles said.
"We have two years left before the next election, and I intend to defeat this government. But until then, we cannot sit back and rest on our laurels and wait for a government to solve the problem that they're clearly not interested in solving." phendra@postmedia.com

Subject: Primary care; Interdisciplinary aspects

Document 208 of 227

Liberals at Queen's Park show ignorance on guns and justice

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-at-queens-park-show-ignorance-on-guns/docview/2955954890/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+at+Queen%27s+Park+show+ignorance+on+guns+and+justice&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-03-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you ever needed proof that Liberals don't understand gun crime, gun control or gun policy beyond using it to scare people, Stephen Blais is walking proof.
The MPP for Orleans in Ottawa stood up to try and make a point this week on guns and judges getting tough on crime and utterly failed.
The Liberals at Queen's Park have been trying to make hay out of the fact that Premier Doug Ford appointed two people who used to work for him to the panel that vets lawyers seeking judicial appointments. Imagine that, the party that won the election appointing people who philosophically agree with them to advisory roles in the government.
This is a complete non-scandal because not only does every party do this, but they should do this. You advance the agenda you were elected on by relying on those who agree with you to get things done.
No matter that the previous Ontario Liberal government did this or that the federal Liberal government that Blais supports does this, he and his party want to make this an issue.
On Wednesday though, Blais decided to go a step further and point out that Matt Bondy, Ford's former deputy chief of staff who is now chair of the judicial appointments advisory committee, is also registered to lobby for a gun manufacturer.
"How can we believe the premier's tough-oncrime stance when he's asked the guy who sells the guns to appoint the judges?" Blais asked during question period.
And with that, he showed everyone that he knows absolutely nothing about guns.
Bondy is indeed a lobbyist for a gun manufacturer, a company out of Kitchener called Colt Canada that only makes and sells weapons to the Canadian military and law enforcement. In fact, Colt's only reason to exist with their facility in Kitchener is so that Canada has a secure domestic supply of rifles and other small arms for the Canadian military.
If Blais walked into his local gun store, he wouldn't be able to buy anything from Colt Canada. They make some fantastic firearms, but they do not sell into the retail market.
Nor are they guns found in the hands of criminals on the streets of Ottawa. No one is doing a drug deal with a C7 rifle, a C20 sniper rifle or Colt's EAGLE single-shot grenade launcher.
These are products designed for and sold to the Canadian military and police services across the country.
Colt produces the guns used by the guys who arrest the bad guys with guns. But why let the truth get in the way of scaring people? Doug Ford has expressed his frustration with judges and justices of the peace giving light sentences or letting repeat violent gun offenders out on bail for years now. This isn't a new thing for him, he's been saying this since before he was elected premier.
But now that he's appointed two people who used to work with him, and who agree with his judicial philosophy, to the panel that screens judges and the Liberals, the NDP and much of the media are losing their collectivist little minds.
Why? It's not because Liberals don't appoint like-minded people when they are in office, it's that they do it and claim they are all being non-partisan and neutral when in reality, they aren't. Ford is being upfront in what he wants.
"I'm proud to go out there and tell the people who are seeing violence in their homes, violence on the streets, violence in our subways, that we're going to get judges that are actually going to keep these criminals in jail," Ford said in response to Blais'bizarre question.
Ask the public what they care about, it isn't lobbyists, it's keeping criminals behind bars. That's something Blais'Liberal Party has been against for years. blilley@postmedia.com

Subject: Violence; Judicial appointments; Firearms

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: needed; proof; liberals; understand; crime; control

Document 209 of 227

How i came to love former prime minister brian mulroney; Tribute

Author: Milnes, Arthur

Publication info: Milnes, Arthur. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/how-i-came-love-former-prime-minister-brian/docview/2933844496/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+i+came+to+love+former+prime+minister+brian+mulroney%3B+Tribute&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Milnes%2C+Arthur&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I loved Brian Mulroney. His leadership. His political skill. His determination. His delicious sense of fun and friendship that, both inside and outside of politics, defined the man behind the headlines.
I loved these and so much more that I grew to know so well during the five years I served as the assistant on his memoirs.
I hardly knew Mr.
Mulroney when he invited me - over lunch in Montreal one day in December 2002 - to serve as his assistant as he began turning remembrances into writing. I had contacted him, asking for an interview, as a reporter from The Kingston Whig-Standard, months earlier. Together with my interview request, and with genuine interest, I also inquired why he had not yet written his story.
And then my mother-inlaw intervened.
Only a week or so after I mailed my letter to Mr. Mulroney, Mary Bogle of Toronto called me with excitement. "Guess who I saw at (Pearson) airport tonight," she said. "It was Brian Mulroney and I gave him a piece of my mind for not giving you an interview."
My life passed before me, fearing that she'd forever ended any chance I would ever have of interviewing the 18th prime minister of Canada. I asked Mary what Mr. Mulroney's reply had been.
"He said to tell you that now he has met your in-laws, he'll call you," she replied.
And sure enough, when I arrived at the Whig-Standard the next morning, the message light on my phone was already flashing. More than 20 years later, I can still recite Brian Mulroney's message from memory.
"Arthur," that deep baritone voice announced, "it's Brian Mulroney. I'm calling at the behest of my new best friends, your in-laws. Let's talk."
And that is how I ended up assisting a Canadian prime minister with his memoirs.
We were an unlikely pair.
Up until then, I'd always voted Liberal (except for the time I marked my ballot for Bob Rae's Ontario NDP in 1995). I was hardly a Tory partisan.
"Arthur, you f------Liberal," he said to me with a giggle, his voice nonetheless shaky and faint during a call he made from the ICU in 2005 as he faced a life-threatening illness, "how would you like to be your friend Jean Chretien's memoirs assistant about now?" The call came during particularly damning testimony for his nemesis, Chretien, during the Gomery Inquiry into the Liberals'sponsorship program. Despite his own illness, Mr. Mulroney was somehow still able to tease me about my Liberal voting record. I loved him for making the call.
Over almost two full years, I had reviewed his massive archival collection at Library and Archives Canada. More than 3,000 boxes in all.
And on page after page, particularly those documents involving his youth, I truly got to know Brian Mulroney. In the silence of my archives'office in Ottawa, I marvelled, day after day, at the young man from nowhere who would become prime minister.
"With regard to our telephone conversation of yesterday afternoon ... " he started a telegram to Prime Minister John Diefenbaker when he was all of 21 years of age. How many university students spend part of their day advising the leader of their country? Well, that was the Brian Mulroney I started to get to know.
But there were also the personal moments. With great emotion, he called me one night to say he'd finally written the passage about the death of his father, Ben Mulroney, in early 1965. Though it was late, he wanted my opinion on what he'd written.
He read me his words. "In the evenings I would take him in my arms like a child - he was losing weight very quickly - and carry him downstairs to the living room so he could watch TV and tune in to the CBC nightly news," he wrote. "In those days the lead announcer was Earl Cameron, who always concluded his newscast with the words, 'This is Earl Cameron saying good night from Toronto,'and my dad unfailingly replied with a smile, 'Good night, Earl.'He continued to do this right to the end, which came on February 16, 1965. He was sixty-one years old."
"Should we make any changes?" he asked me quietly.
"Sir, not one," I replied. A year later, he was the first person to call me when my own father died. He gave me a talk, telling me how my life had now changed forever. And that my responsibility was to honour my father's memory. Mr. Mulroney pulled no punches during that unsolicited call, talking me through a difficult time. Having read his account of his own father's death scant months before, I felt very close to the former prime minister that sad day.
But the work of preparing a prime ministerial memoir before history continued. As we moved into his public career, Mr. Mulroney had me prepare research packs in chronological form that contained the primary sourced documentation from his archive, quotes from the private journal he kept while prime minister, along with press reports of the day. The two of us used these packs to guide us as we started the long process of tape-recorded interviews that formed the foundation of the book.
These interview sessions, which always took place in his study at his Westmount home, lasted as many as four days straight. We'd work for seven or eight hours as he orally crafted what would become a written narrative. "Arthur," he'd always say when I'd start to pack up the recording equipment and papers, "we did the Lord's work today."
But what I grew to enjoy most were our personal conversations, which occurred when the work was done. Mr. Mulroney would pull his chair back and we would simply talk, often for hours.
We'd discuss our wives and families, the politics of the day and more. Once, we even watched a replay in full of that day's question period from the House of Commons. It was, for me, a dream come true to have had these moments with a prime minister of my country.
While I was never his friend (he was always the boss and the former prime minister), I felt I got to know him most these late afternoons.
That is how I remember him today. His legs up on the coffee table in his study, Irish blarney on full display, and me loving every second of his performances to me, an audience of one.
As he did for millions while campaigning, Mr. Mulroney held me in his spell each time. Often the crowds he described became larger and larger as the story continued, but that didn't matter to me.
Through him, and our private conversations, I too was soon in the arena alongside him as together we faced in triumph the mighty Grits of old.
And then he'd walk me to the door of his Montreal home, ask me to pass along his regards to my wife, and I'd drive back to Kingston. And I'd think then, as I do today upon learning of his death, how privileged I am to have known and loved this remarkable Canadian. Arthur Milnes, a public historian and political speechwriter, served as the memoirs' assistant to Brian Mulroney for five years during the early 2000s. He now serves as the in-house historian at the Frontenac Club Hotel. He lives in Kingston.

Subject: Archives & records; Autobiographies; Recording equipment; Prime ministers; Interviews

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada

People: Mulroney, Brian

Document 210 of 227

Pharmacare a scheme to keep power

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pharmacare-scheme-keep-power/docview/2933266425/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pharmacare+a+scheme+to+keep+power&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Is pharmacare doomed to fail? Last week, Quebec announced it would opt out of the Liberal-NDP plan for a national pharmacare program. This week, it was Alberta's turn. Health Minister Adriana LaGrange blasted the lack of provincial consultation and accused the federal government of overstepping its jurisdiction.
"We're not going to allow Ottawa to pick and choose what coverage should be available to Albertans based on issues they find politically palatable."
Instead of taking orders from Ottawa, Alberta will take the cash.
"Give us the dollars," LaGrange said. "Allow us to enhance the programs we actually have now, rather than create more bureaucracy."
In response, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh attacked Quebec Premier François Legault and Alberta Premier Danielle Smith.
"I'm not surprised at the Conservative governments that have cut investments in health care. These are the same premiers that, when you look at their province, they made massive cuts that hurt workers and patients."
Ah, the C-word. Never mind that Legault's government doesn't go by that name: it's a convenient excuse to attack the ideology, and by extension the federal Conservatives as well. Singh is eager to savour his pharmacare "victory" and position the NDP as the true alternative to the Tories. And since he has as much a chance of gaining seats in Quebec as he does in Alberta, bashing those two governments is a low-risk strategy.
But the NDP aren't the only ones playing politics with this deal. The Liberals are as well, starting with the choice of contraceptive and diabetes medication as the first drugs to be covered.
Contraception is a relatively low-cost item for the feds: it's already fully covered in B.C. and Manitoba, and partly covered in Ontario. Most private health plans - which four in five Canadians have - pay for it as well. More importantly, the coverage appeals to a key voter demographic - women - the Liberals need to get back. The latest polls show that 36 per cent would vote Conservative, compared to 26 per cent who would vote Liberal.
As for diabetes, that is also a numbers game.
Nearly 10 per cent of Canadians - six million people - suffer from the disease, with an additional six million classified as "pre-diabetic." Rates are even higher among seniors (20 per cent) and First Nations populations on reserve (17 per cent). Almost everyone knows someone with diabetes and knows that insulin is a lifesaver.
But that doesn't mean you need universal pharmacare to cover people who can't afford it. It's like bringing in a $27-billion dollar bazooka to kill a fly. A study released last week by the Montreal Economic Institute found only 2.8 per cent of Canadians currently have no drug coverage whatsoever. Twenty-five million people have private insurance, and existing provincial plans subsidize or cover a host of medications for persons who do not, including low-income patients and seniors.
The study also confirmed that imposing a single-payer universal drug insurance plan would jeopardize the quality of coverage for at least 21.5 million people. That's because a single-payer model would not fund many medications currently covered by private plans, including innovative medicines.
Based on the experiences of New Zealand, cutting-edge drugs won't be introduced into the Canadian market at all, because a universal plan would deem them too costly. A 2019 report commissioned by Medicines New Zealand ranked the country last for access to medicines and pharmaceutical investment among 20 OECD nations. Of 304 medicines introduced between 2011 and 2017, New Zealand funded only 17. Life-saving cancer therapies and other innovative medicines were rendered unavailable.
In other words, national single-payer pharmacare will consign Canadians to the same problems as national single-payer medicare: lack of choice, rationing of care and worse outcomes. Instead of keeping what works and fixing what's broken, the NDP chose to score political points, and the Liberals bought themselves another year in power.
So kudos to Alberta and Quebec for standing up and opting out. Let's hope other provinces join them and expose this deal for what it is: a political game that leaves patients in the cold. Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Diabetes; Canadians; Indigent care

Location: New Zealand; Canada; Quebec Canada

Identifier / keyword: pharmacare; doomed; quebec; announced; national; program

City in line for extra funding after exceeding 2023 housing target

Author: Ferguson, Elliot

Publication info: Ferguson, Elliot. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/city-line-extra-funding-after-exceeding-2023/docview/2931216882/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+in+line+for+extra+funding+after+exceeding+2023+housing+target&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-02-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Ferguson%2C+Elliot&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Kingston can expect to receive additional provincial funding for exceeding its 2023 housing target.
But how much additional money remains to be determined.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford this week announced almost $140 million in additional funding for Toronto and Brampton In 2023, the city's new housing target was 587 and it exceeded that goal by 250 per cent, with 1,465 new home starts.
"My message to mayors across this province is that we must continue to work together and build on our progress on making homeownership a reality for everyone. We will give you the tools you need to succeed in our shared goal." Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra said.
"Our government is committed to building at least 1.5 million homes by 2031 and I look forward to unveiling the next steps in our plan to build more homes with the release of our fifth housing supply action plan next month."
Kingston has a goal of building 8,000 new homes in the next 10 years.
Of 50 municipalities, 19 met or exceeded their 2023 housing target.
The provincial government's three-year, $1.2-billion Building Faster Fund is meant to encourage municipalities to meet or exceed their housing targets.
To qualify for the fund, municipalities must have reached at least 80 per cent of their housing targets.
Since 2022, Kingston has seen almost 1,900 new housing starts.
Last month, Kingston received $27 million from the federal housing accelerator fund.

Document 212 of 227

Poll shows Nenshi's recognition could upend NDP leadership race

Author: Braid, Don

Publication info: Braid, Don. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poll-shows-nenshis-recognition-could-upend-ndp/docview/2931011052/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poll+shows+Nenshi%27s+recognition+could+upend+NDP+leadership+race&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Braid%2C+Don&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A new poll shows Naheed Nenshi would create far more interest and recognition than any of the party insiders in the NDP race.
If he runs, that is. The ex-Calgary mayor isn't likely to announce anything for some days yet. But he's checking for support and calculating how he could sell enough memberships to win as a party outsider.
His friends and relatives quickly started pushing the poll around on social media. Done by Pollara Strategic Insights, some of the questions were developed by Stephen Carter and Corey Hogan of the Strategists podcast.
Carter's presence in the neighbourhood of any campaign always raises interest. He helped Nenshi win the mayor's office in 2010, went on to run PC Premier Alison Redford's leadership campaign, then helped Mayor Jyoti Gondek in 2021.
But Carter isn't boosting Nenshi or working with him. He says their egos are too big to fit in the same room.
Asked if Nenshi can win, Carter says, "I would not argue that at all. Think of the front-runner syndrome. Even Danielle Smith, who had run an excellent campaign as the front-runner who took all the oxygen in the UCP leadership, it still took her multiple ballots to actually win."
Carter says Nenshi would start late in selling memberships. The other candidates - MLAs Sarah Hoffman, Rakhi Pancholi and Kathleen Ganley - have been campaigning flat out for more than two weeks.
In the preferential ballot, Carter says, Nenshi would have trouble getting to a majority against three candidates, all with considerable support.
Still, the poll by Pollara gives Nenshi plenty of encouragement. In name recognition, he's well ahead of the three declared candidates in Calgary, Edmonton and the rest of the province. He scores much higher on the "excitement" index.
Pollster Dan Arnold wrote: "Albertans feel the best about a possible Nenshi victory, with 41 per cent feeling positive emotions."
Ganley gets 23 per cent positive, Hoffman 22 per cent and Pancholi 21 per cent.
"Nenshi also generates the most excitement, giving him the largest voter field to harvest votes from," Arnold adds.
Pollara says that 13 per cent of Albertans say they have or will buy an NDP membership.
"The figure is certainly overestimated, but this question shows there is more interest in the race in Edmonton (17 per cent) and Calgary (13 per cent) than the rest of Alberta (eight per cent.) Young and racialized people are the most interested in buying memberships."
Nenshi has appeared to be campaigning only once, when he spoke at a rally at Calgary city hall in favour of trans and LGBTQ rights. It was held Feb. 4, after the UCP's new policies were announced.
Nenshi's fierce opposition to the measures - he says they weren't worth "a few dead kids" - won him applause at the rally and surely approval among many NDP members.
Nenshi also talked about how he's known Smith for 30 years and always felt she was in favour of protections for gay and trans children and adults.
But now, he alleges, she has shown that's not true.
The brief appearance gave a taste of what to expect in the legislature if he won and went up against the premier. "It would be electric," says one Nenshi fan.
The three candidates already in the race are far ahead with policies. They've all voiced skepticism about the federal carbon tax, declaring it to be doomed. Ganley has presented an income tax scheme that would provide more exemption for low-income earners.
Pancholi has even called for divorcing the Alberta NDP from the federal party, a very controversial subject among provincial members.
Nenshi's presence certainly would bring more interest to a race that so far lacks Rachel Notley's star power.
But there are doubts about Nenshi. One longtime activist voiced a common view: "I think he'd be a great candidate - we'd get plenty of attention - but I'm not at all sure what kind of leader he'd be." Don Braid is a regular columnist for Postmedia. X: @DonBraid

Funds on the way to combat Belleville OD crises: Ford

Author: Baldwin, Derek

Publication info: Baldwin, Derek. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/funds-on-way-combat-belleville-od-crises-ford/docview/2927740419/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Funds+on+the+way+to+combat+Belleville+OD+crises%3A+Ford&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Baldwin%2C+Derek&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford said provincial funds are on the way imminently to help Belleville fend off crippling multiple overdose crises fuelled by tainted street drugs that are taxing first responders to near capacity.
Speaking to reporters in Mississauga, the premier said he will meet with Belleville Mayor Neil Ellis to discuss options moving forward after Ellis declared a state of emergency last Thursday.
Ford didn't elaborate on the funding amount following a request by Mayor Ellis Monday for $2 million needed to relocate a drop-in centre at Bridge Street United Church to a new integrated care hub called The Bridge at Alhambra Square, away from the city core.
Hastings-Quinte Paramedic Services responded to 35 reported overdoses over six days as of Monday, resulting in 20 people needing hospital attention.
"I personally talked to the mayor myself; he's doing everything he can. We have Minister (Todd) Smith on the ground in Belleville as well. We're going to try and support him. They need an influx of money right away," Ford said in a news conference livestreamed on YouTube by the Premier's Office.
"We're going to get that done and then we're going to sit down and talk to him about building. I always believe in rehabilitation centres and supporting communities," the premier said.
"But we have to go after these bad guys, these bad guys who are poisoning our towns, our cities, giving them tainted drugs that are killing people. We need to catch 'em, we need to throw 'em in jail and that's exactly what we're going to do."
Ellis said in a phone interview late Tuesday he is grateful for the relatively quick response by Ford and has high hopes the province will come through in his emergency request for $2 million for The Bridge funding as well as more dollars needed long term to establish a detox centre in the city.
The city has already donated the two storey, 10,000-square-foot building known as the banquet centre for The Bridge, but the money is needed for capital upgrades to build a kitchen, warming centre, and shower, medical and counselling amenities within the structure.
"This is great news what I'm reading. I haven't talked to the premier about the funding and I guess it will come. Our two asks were money for capital for The Bridge and the second ask for the detox centre. It's great the province has made this a priority and I wait to hear the announcement," Ellis told The Intelligencer.
"I'm waiting with anticipation for the announcement, it's what our community has been asking for."

Document 214 of 227

Trudeau needs to decide soon if he'll run again; His party needs time to elect a new leader to avoid the same fate as Kim Campbell's PCs

Author: Goldstein, Lorrie

Publication info: Goldstein, Lorrie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-needs-decide-soon-if-hell-run-again-his/docview/2927740015/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+needs+to+decide+soon+if+he%27ll+run+again%3B+His+party+needs+time+to+elect+a+new+leader+to+avoid+the+same+fate+as+Kim+Campbell%27s+PCs&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Goldstein%2C+Lorrie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Time is rapidly running out for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to decide whether he's going to lead the Liberals into the next federal election.
Assuming the Liberal/NDP accord holds, it has to be held on or before Oct. 20, 2025, 20 months away.
But Trudeau has to make a decision about his own future well before that.
If he's not going to run, he should be announcing it for the good of his party right about now.
Speculation about Trudeau's future to date has hinged on whether his confidence-and-supply agreement with NDP leader Jagmeet Singh until the fall of 2025 will hold, given Singh's frequent threats to break it, the latest on the issue of delivering pharmacare.
But the end of the accord wouldn't automatically mean a snap election.
In addition, does the NDP really want to provoke an election now, which, if the polls are accurate, would result in a huge Conservative majority where the NDP would have no say? The real deadline facing Trudeau is that the longer he waits to decide on his future, the more likely that decision will be made for him by default.
That is, he will have to stay on as leader because there won't be enough time to hold a Liberal leadership race to replace him, or for a new leader to put his or her stamp on the party and convince Canadians to vote for it.
Twenty years ago, then-Progressive Conservative prime minister Brian Mulroney waited until February 1993 to announce he was resigning as party leader, prior to a federal election that had to be called by October 1993, eight months later.
The party didn't elect its new leader - Kim Campbell - until June 1993.
In the ensuing federal election, four months later on Oct. 25, 1993, the PCs were reduced from a majority government to two seats.
That marked the beginning of the end for the PC party as a viable force in federal politics and the start of a Liberal dynasty that lasted 13 years.
The eight months between Mulroney resigning and Campbell leading the party into its disastrous showing in the 1993 election wasn't enough time for the PCs to put angry voter memories of the Mulroney years behind them, or for Campbell to create her own political brand and run an effective election campaign.
Given months of recent polling indicating Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives would win a huge majority government if an election was held today, the next Liberal leader, if there is one, is going to need a lot more than eight months to campaign for the Liberal leadership, put the memory of the Trudeau years behind him, or her, and convince voters a Liberal government under their leadership would be different from the current one.
Leadership races suck up money and resources that would otherwise be used in an election, and create internal divisions within the party the new leader has to be able to mend, which takes time.
Given these realities, if Trudeau is going to resign before the next election, he should be announcing it now, to give his party any realistic chance of winning.
Releasing a poll last week showing the Conservatives with a 19-point lead over the Liberals, Abacus Data chief executive David Coletto said the Liberals'root problem is "most people aren't listening anymore to Trudeau."
The question is, would they listen to any new Liberal leader? lgoldstein@postmedia.com

Subject: Leadership; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Identifier / keyword: rapidly; running; prime; minister; justin; trudeau

Title: Trudeau needs to decide soon if he'll run again; His party needs time to elect a new leader to avoid the same fate as Kim Campbell's PCs

Document 215 of 227

Protesters Target Mount Sinai Hospital

Author: Johnston, Geoffrey

Publication info: Johnston, Geoffrey. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/protesters-target-mount-sinai-hospital/docview/2927739720/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Protesters+Target+Mount+Sinai+Hospital&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Johnston%2C+Geoffrey&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Earlier this week, an angry mob of pro-Palestinian protesters engaged in an unlawful demonstration at the Mount Sinai Hospital in Toronto in an appalling display of blatant antisemitism that underscored how unsafe Canada has become for Jewish people.
"Tonight, the anti-Israel mob decided to pay a visit to Mount Sinai Hospital. I wonder who they were targeting," Michael Levitt, president and CEO of Friends of Simon Wiesenthal Center, wrote on X, formerly known as Twitter, on Feb. 12.
"The patients, or the families there to comfort them? The nurses, docs or first responders saving lives? Or maybe just the Jews, because Mount Sinai Hospital, right?" "Toronto's Mount Sinai Hospital was established in the 1920s by Jews against the backdrop of antisemitism," reads a Feb. 13 statement posted on X by the Friends of Simon Wiesenthal Center. "Last night, it was the target of protesters chanting for violence and terror against Jews. This was a deliberate attempt to intimidate not only the Jewish community, but also patients and staff at a hospital that serves people of all backgrounds."
Similarly, Marco Mendicino, Liberal MP for Eglinton-Lawrence, a riding with a large Jewish population, took to X to denounce the demonstration. "Targeting Mount Sinai Hospital to incite intifada against Israel and the Jewish people is flagrantly antisemitic and undermines the health and safety of the patients and medical professionals who work there.
The torrent of hatred against Jews and Jewish institutions must stop!" "We've been clear - the deplorable acts of antisemitism and intimidation we're seeing across Canada cannot stand. Targeting Mount Sinai, a place of healing, is unacceptable," Ya'ara Saks, minister of mental health and addictions and associate minister of health, posted on Feb. 13. The federal minister, who is Jewish, declared: "We must protect the safety of patients, health-care workers and the Jewish community who is hurting right now."
DEFINITION According to the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance's non-legally binding working definition, "antisemitism is a certain perception of Jews, which may be expressed as hatred toward Jews." In addition, the IHRA definition states that "rhetorical and physical manifestations of antisemitism are directed toward Jewish or non-Jewish individuals and/or their property, toward Jewish community institutions and religious facilities." Canada has adopted IHRA's definition as part of the federal government's anti-racism strategy.
According to the IHRA website, "holding Jews collectively responsible for actions of the state of Israel" is an example of antisemitism. Clearly, by targeting Mount Sinai, the demonstrators were holding Canadian Jews responsible for the ongoing conflict between Israel and Hamas.
LEADERS SPEAK OUT "The demonstration at Mount Sinai Hospital yesterday was reprehensible," Prime Minister Justin Trudeau declared in a Feb. 13 post on X. "Hospitals are places for treatment and care, not protests and intimidation. I strongly condemn this display of antisemitism. In Toronto and across Canada, we stand with Jewish communities against this hate."
"A hospital is a sanctuary, a place of healing for all," Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow said in a Feb. 13 news release. "My family has a long history with Mount Sinai. My father passed there, both my late husband and I had cancer treatments there. Yesterday's actions at Mount Sinai Hospital, a hospital founded by Toronto's Jewish community, were unacceptable. Targeting Jewish communities is antisemitic and hate has no place in our city."
"I think it is absolutely terrible," Premier Doug Ford said at a public event when asked by a journalist about the protest at the Mount Sinai Hospital. And Ford noted that it is against federal law to protest at a hospital. "Folks, get some decency. Do not break the law. Because that's exactly what happened."
If people want to protest, Ford continued, they should go to City Hall or Queen's Park. "Don't prohibit people going into a hospital," he said. "They're sacred, hospitals."
Anthony Housefather, MP for Mount Royal and a prominent member of the Jewish community, praised the leaders from the federal, provincial and municipal levels of government for speaking out. "Tone at the top is important," he posted on Feb. 13. "It is not acceptable to target Canadian Jewish institutions or businesses who do not control what happens abroad."
TORONTO POLICE SERVICE "We will be calling Toronto's mayor and chief of police today to discuss how they will put a stop to this despicable targeting and attempted intimidation of Mount Sinai Hospital and Jews across Toronto and Canada," Deborah Lyons, Special Envoy - Preserving Holocaust Remembrance and Combatting Antisemitism, declared in a Feb. 13 post on X. "It is antisemitic, it is illegal and it is indecent."
"Bill C-3, which was passed by Parliament in 2021, is designed to protect our health-care professionals, their places of work, and their patients," B'nai Brith Canada posted on X the day after the demonstration. "Such legislation was enacted to ensure Canadians'rights to access health care are not interfered with or impeded by others. It was specifically designed to prevent political protest from being used as a tool to intimidate health-care providers and their patients."
During the darkest days of the pandemic, anti-public health demonstrators engaged in protests outside of hospitals, impeding access of health-care workers and patients alike. In response, Parliament passed Bill C-3, which received royal assent on Dec. 17, 2021.
The legislation amended the Criminal Code, creating "an offence of intimidating a person in order to impede them from obtaining health services, intimidating a health professional in order to impede them in the performance of their duties or intimidating a person who assists a health professional in order to impede the person in providing that assistance."
In a post on X, the Toronto Police Service revealed that it is "investigating incidents that occurred in front of Mount Sinai Hospital." HATE CRIMES According to the Toronto Police Service, of the hate crimes reported between Oct. 7 and Dec. 17, 2023, 53 per cent targeted Jews. However, antisemitism is not unique to Canada's largest city. Jew hatred also poses a major threat to public safety in Canada's capital city.
"The Ottawa Police Service Hate and Bias Crime Unit just released their 2023 Annual statistics," Deborah Lyons said in a Feb. 13 post. "Unfortunately, but not surprisingly, the group most victimized by far was Ottawa's Jewish community who only make up just over one per cent of the city's population."
The Ottawa Police Service reported that hate crimes spiked in 2023. According to a Feb. 12 news release, "the Ottawa Police Service Hate and Bias Crime Unit 2023 Annual statistics show that the unit addressed 460 incidents last year, including 344 criminal and 116 hatemotivated incidents, which marks an increase of 19.5 per cent over 2022 (compared to 377 incidents that year)."
The report also states that Jews were the most targeted group in 2023; there were 92 reported incidents against Jews. The second "most victimized" group was the LGBTQ2+ community (65 reported incidents). However, the true number of hate crimes was probably much higher. "Hate crimes are extremely underreported," the release reads.
Prominent Ottawa family physician Dr. Nili Kaplan-Myrth frequently receives harassing communications and even death threats from Jew haters. Kaplan-Myrth, who is also trustee on the Ottawa-Carleton District School Board, rose to prominence during the early days of the COVID-19 pandemic for being a strong advocate for pandemic mitigation measures.
"It's already not safe for me to walk down the street in Ottawa," Kaplan-Myrth told the Whig-Standard in a recent interview. "I don't have any Jewish friends who feel the same sense of safety they felt before (the start of the Hamas-Israel war on Oct. 7). We don't feel that we can be ourselves, or that we can talk about our religion, or that we can talk about our culture without being attacked. And my children don't feel they can talk about who they are. And my colleagues don't feel they can talk about who they are."
"It has nothing to do with Israeli politics," Kaplan-Myrth said of virulent antisemitism. "It has to do with a deep hatred of Jews. And we aren't safe in any space." Follow Geoffrey P. Johnston on Mastodon @GeoffyPJohnston@c.im.
