Document 216 of 227

'It's a tragedy'; Kingston mayor reflects on Belleville drug poisonings

Author: Crosier, Steph

Publication info: Crosier, Steph. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tragedy-kingston-mayor-reflects-on-belleville/docview/2926957813/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27It%27s+a+tragedy%27%3B+Kingston+mayor+reflects+on+Belleville+drug+poisonings&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Crosier%2C+Steph&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the news of a rash of drug poisonings in Belleville made national headlines and forced the small city to declare a state of emergency, it came as a reminder to Kingston officials that it has been more than a year since a crisis was declared here, and little has been done.
"The conditions have not improved. We are still very much in a crisis situation here," Mayor Bryan Paterson said Monday. "We as cities, we see the effects before anybody else. We're on the front lines, together with our partner agencies, we see it. We see it day in and day out. We see what is going on, and the fact is, the status quo is not working."
Last week, emergency services in Belleville responded to 22 drug poisonings in two days. At one point it was reported that crews had treated 13 people in less than an hour. While crews worked feverishly around the city's downtown, residents were told to avoid the area.
Belleville Mayor Neil Ellis declared a state of emergency, calling on higher levels of government for aid. Last Friday morning, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Ontario Premier Doug Ford both pledged their support for the city of 50,720.
Trudeau said the federal government will be supporting the province move forward on more treatment and recovery centres, and Ford vowed a "multi-prong approach" that includes addressing those trafficking the illicit substances.
On Monday, the City of Belleville announced it had submitted a formal Request for Assistance from the provincial government. It is requesting investment for two projects: a local detox centre and a community hub for addiction, mental health and homelessness. The city is also asking for guidance and support in creating the latter.
When Paterson heard the news of what had happened in Belleville, he recalled thinking how "it was pretty scary stuff."
Paterson said he understands that Kingston, Frontenac and Lennox and Addington Public Health and Hastings Prince Edward Public Health have been communicating in an effort to support each other.
"KFL&A Public Health is continuing to monitor our local situation in collaboration with our partners," a statement from Daphne Mayer, manager of substance use health and mental well-being for the Kingston-area health unit, reads. "Our data sources do not indicate a change in the situation in our area this week. That said, with the contaminated drug supply across Ontario, we strongly urge people who use drugs to avoid using alone, use one drug at a time, go slow and get a naloxone kit."
Paterson has also offered support to his counterpart, Ellis, as the incident further demonstrates that this public health crisis doesn't respect municipal borders.
Justine McIsaac, co-ordinator of Kingston Community Health Centres'Consumption and Treatment Services located within the Integrated Care Hub, was also quick to offer her love and support to her Belleville peers. She admitted she wasn't surprised when she heard the news of the rash of drug poisonings.
"But I'm also very saddened that the community is having to experience that," McIsaac told the Whig-Standard. "It's stuff that we've been dealing with for a long time and it's just a reflection of how toxic the drug landscape is changing week by week."
Paterson said it is hard to know if what happened in Belleville is a foreshadowing for Kingston, and the only thing the city can do is to stay on guard. Ultimately, lives are at stake.
"(What happened in Belleville is) another example that highlights just how much these new resources, approaches and supports and treatments are needed to deal with this scourge of addiction," Paterson said. "It's terrible. It's a tragedy."
According to the Ontario Drug Policy and Research Network, the Office of the Chief Coroner reported that in the region covered by Kingston, Frontenac and Lennox and Addington Public Health, there were 52 people who lost their lives through a confirmed or probable case of opioid toxicity in 2022 - 46 within the Kingston census subdivision.
Between January and August 2023, 31 people's deaths were either confirmed or found to be probably caused by opioid toxicity.
In 2023, Kingston Health Sciences Centre's emergency department saw 399 visits by individuals suffering from an overdose. There were 807 visits by individuals who needed treatment for substance abuse or intoxication, which could have been from drugs or alcohol.
Statistics show that the number of confirmed opioid-related cases treated by the local hospital's emergency department has been continuously increased over the years from 83 in 2017 to 363 in 2022. That number dropped to 225 in 2023.
"KHSC's (emergency department) team feels these numbers could be only the tip of the iceberg as many individuals do not come to the hospital, for example using naloxone in a community setting instead," a statement from the hospital said.
The hospital reported that fentanyl continues to be the most common cause of drug poisonings treated in the emergency department.
A December 2023 report titled Opioid-and Stimulant-related Harms in Canada, authored by the federal, provincial and territorial Special Advisory Committee on the Epidemic of Opioid Overdoses, states 84 per cent of all apparent opioid toxicity deaths in the first six months of 2023 involved fentanyl. This is an increase of 47 per cent since 2016.
On Feb 9, Ganonoque Police Service alerted its residents of an increase in drug poisonings in the small town, and on Feb 2, Kingston's public health unit issued a similar warning in response to a 15 per cent increase in drug overdoses caused by a toxic supply of unregulated fentanyl circulating.
McIsaac said at the time that the supply had been cut with animal tranquilizers and benzodiazepines, causing dangerous hallucinations and prolonged sedation.
While it isn't the case for every resident of the encampment in Belle Park and along the K&P Trail, many chose to camp in the area because of its proximity to the Integrated Care Hub, which supports their sometimes-concurrent mental health diagnoses including substance addiction. In August 2021, the hub reported reversing more than 400 overdoses since it opened in November 2020.
McIsaac explained that because the unregulated substances are being laced, it has made responding to drug poisonings more complicated. Unlike those at the hub, many in the community don't have the right equipment to respond appropriately. There is naloxone, but that only reverses the effects of an opioid, not a tranquilizer. Untrained individuals generally don't know the difference between an overdose and a heavy sedation, colloquially known as "nodding."
The encampment grew over the course of the COVID-19 pandemic, and while the City of Kingston played a key role in establishing the Integrated Care Hub and the Consumption and Treatment Services site with the assistance of provincial and federal funding, the city has also been criticized for attempting to evict the campers living near it. When they took the case to the Superior Court, Justice Ian Carter decided it was only constitutional to evict the campers under the city's park bylaw during the day, not at night.
During this time, the city has also been working to create more shelter spaces, including those deemed low-barrier that would allow the presence of substances for personal use, and affordable housing for the vulnerable population living rough.
"We're all in this together, and a community functions best when we take care of each other," Paterson said Monday when speaking of substance dependency. "We all have different backgrounds and different experiences, but ultimately a healthy community is one where we care about each other, particularly those that are the most vulnerable."
But health care falls under the provincial government's purview, and despite more resources being "desperately needed," Paterson said little has changed since the city requested assistance in January 2023.
"There needs to be more (support); there needs to be a bigger push into treatment options. That's a critical piece, and meeting people where they're at," Paterson said. "At the end of the day, that takes money, it takes people, it takes resources that are beyond the ability of cities as a whole to cope with. This is beyond our area of expertise. ... "As a city we feel like we're putting band-aids on gunshot wounds. We are trying to thin the bleeding, but we are not, and it's not making the situation better."
Since January 2023, McIsaac said the situation locally has only gotten worse, and she agreed there isn't just one answer to the crisis. She questions why society and the parties elected to lead it seemingly accept the fact that the deaths of roughly 10 people a day are suspected to be drug-related in Ontario.
McIsaac suggests that some decision-makers may believe some people are more deserving of political support than others.
"When is the action going to come into play?" McIsaac asked. "To me the answer is very clear: it is who is using these drugs. ... People don't understand the trauma associated to people's substance use. They don't see the substances as a coping technique, and that people are being forced to use a very toxic supply."
She noted those needing to escape their trauma wouldn't have to use the dangerous, unregulated substances if the federal government provided an adequate safe supply and would decriminalize possessing substances.
"We could invest into people and into rebuilding their lives, building on their skills," McIsaac said. "We've chosen to criminalize, demonize and stigmatize people, instead of contributing to their wellness. This in turn helps an entire community. Our most vulnerable are a reflection of how we're doing as a community."
The Office of the Chief Coroner reported that in 2023 there were 3,628 suspect drug-related deaths across Ontario. In the final three months of 2023 alone, there were 836 suspect drug-related deaths in the province. That is a 44 per cent increase over the same time period four years ago (2019), and a 19 per cent decrease from the same time period two years ago (2021).
The December 2023 report from the Special Advisory Committee on the Epidemic of Opioid Overdoses states that between January 2016 and June 2023, 40,642 people were killed via apparent opioid toxicity.
In the first six months of 2023, 3,970 Canadians were killed by opioid toxicity, with 89 per cent of them in British Columbia, Alberta and Ontario, and 72 per cent were young men. The report highlights that of all those who were killed by accidental overdose, the majority were between the ages of 20 and 59.
"It feels like a whole generation of people are being wiped out," McIsaac said.
She said her clients are terrified every time they use, but their diagnosis forces them to continue. The words, "the goal is to get high, not die," are all too familiar. She said watching their friends and family overdose and die around them is only fuelling her clients'need to escape into that momentary relief.
"Every death is a tragedy, but I'm particularly gutted by young people whose lives are cut short by this and just the tragedy of what addiction can do," Paterson said. "Honestly, there has been a rallying cry that I've seen across the province. I speak with mayors across the province and this use comes up all the time.
"We desperately need better supports, and we need more treatment options. There's so much more that needs to be done to help people that are in these situations that are dangerous. It's a tragedy."
Information about reducing potential harms associated with substance use and where to get a naloxone kit can be found at kflaph. ca/en/health-topics/harm-reduction.aspx. scrosier@postmedia.com twitter.com/StephattheWhig

Subject: Public health; Emergency medical care; Integrated delivery systems; Drug overdose; Anesthesia; Mental health; Addictions; State of emergency; Fentanyl; Cities; Drug use; Narcotics

Document 217 of 227

SLC president says cap on international student visas 'short-sighted'

Author: Hendra, Peter

Publication info: Hendra, Peter. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/slc-president-says-cap-on-international-student/docview/2921588733/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=SLC+president+says+cap+on+international+student+visas+%27short-sighted%27&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Hendra%2C+Peter&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal government's announcement that it is capping the number of international student visas being issued this fall "threatens" the global reputation of Canada's post-secondary education system, the president and CEO of St. Lawrence College says.
Glenn Vollebregt, who oversees the college's campuses in Kingston, Brockville and Cornwall, said he is "frustrated" by the move.
"As someone who has been in education for 24 years, as someone who is a college graduate, as someone who believes in our students and the talent that they bring, I'm upset at what this does," he said in an interview with the Whig-Standard.
"We have the best system in the world and it's being threatened. And I think we, as educators, have a responsibility to speak up about this and ensure that the public is informed about what our grads do in our local communities. Our businesses know - they understand the talent that our college grads bring. So I'm very frustrated with the whole situation."
The federal government, which said it would cut the number of international student visas issued in the fall by 35 per cent, could have taken a more tactful approach in addressing their concerns, Vollebregt believes.
"A colleague of mine said it best: It was a policy decision delivered with a sledgehammer when a scalpel would have worked," he said.
With few details as to how the federal government's policy will unfold, Vollebregt isn't sure to what extent the federal government's move will affect St. Lawrence College - he just knows it will.
"There's no question that if you reduce the number of students you're allowed to have at your institution by 50 per cent, that's gonna have an impact," he said over the phone Thursday afternoon.
"But I keep coming back to this isn't about the college, this isn't about our financial system's sustainability. This is about student education, and this is about access to talent in Ontario.
"Who's going to build the houses that we need built? Who's going to help support our businesses and keep our economies running and thriving? ... And what we've basically said is we're going to cut the number of college grads and that, to me, is such a short-sighted decision for our country."
Cutting and capping the number of student visas - international students comprise roughly 45 per cent of St. Lawrence's enrolment - will further entrench the current labour shortage, Vollebregt believes.
"If we're going to have less students coming in to our institutions, there are going to be less graduates, which means there are going to be labour shortages. And it's just as simple as that," Vollebregt explained. "And I'm really worried about how we're going to be able to support our local economies throughout Ontario with this decision."
Vollebregt wanted to ensure the public was aware of the value international students bring to communities in Ontario and across the country. SLC grads can be found in every industry, he said, from health care to technology to tourism.
"Our students provide the talent that our local employers need, and decisions like this - to just sort of shut everything off and leave our students in limbo - are really going to hurt Canada's reputation on the international educational scene," he said. St. Lawrence, which has roughly 12,000 full-time students, is waiting to learn the details of the federal government's cap. In the meantime, students who have paid tuition and are awaiting visas are now saddled with uncertainty.
"We get into the education business to help students and that's what we're here for, (but) we don't have the answers for them anymore," he said. "Right now we have more questions for the federal government in terms of when is that (plan) going to be released? What's the allocation process? When can students join our institutions and start their educational journey?" The federal government said it is imposing the cap to protect students from being exploited by dubious colleges, but also because the influx of students has put increased pressure on the country's housing system. Vollebregt doesn't buy it.
"I'm upset that our students are being blamed for a housing crisis when, in fact, that is not the case," he said.
While he and other college heads have questions for the federal government, Vollebregt is hopeful the Ontario government can help. Premier Doug Ford said Wednesday that his government, which dropped tuition fees by 10 per cent in 2019 and then froze those fees, doesn't want to raise those fees right now.
"I understand and acknowledge the premier and he's acknowledging the struggles students face. I get that. But again, the reality is that colleges provide the talent Ontario needs, and we've been underfunded for so long now, and we're under incredible, incredible strain," Vollebregt said.
While he believes the cap will threaten Canada's global reputation in providing top-notch education, Vollebregt also feels it will threaten SLC's 15-year public-private partnership with Alpha College of Business and Technology in Toronto.
Vollebregt acknowledged that there are some colleges with poor reputations but that SLC's partnership with Alpha isn't one of them. They shouldn't all be grouped together, Vollebregt said.
"That tells me that folks really haven't stepped on a campus of a private partner because they would certainly see what these partnerships are like," he said. "They're excellent."
The college's other partnership, with Canadian College in Vancouver, is already winding down, Vollebregt said.
While Canada has earned a global reputation for its higher education system, he feels the federal government's decision may change that.
"Many, many countries in the world would love to be in our position where students across the world want to attend a Canadian college or university," Vollebregt said.
"And I think in terms of meeting the labour market needs of our communities, large and small, that's what our education system provides. We provide the talent for our local businesses, (from) the multinationals to the small business owner, which makes up 80 per cent of businesses in Canada. So we're extremely disappointed about the actions that have taken place." phendra@postmedia.com

Subject: Tuition; Federal government; College graduates; Community; Foreign students; Colleges & universities; Reputations; Education; Labor shortages

Document 218 of 227

NDP leadership race needs to find the ring road out of Edmonton

Author: Braid, Don

Publication info: Braid, Don. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndp-leadership-race-needs-find-ring-road-out/docview/2920914778/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+leadership+race+needs+to+find+the+ring+road+out+of+Edmonton&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Braid%2C+Don&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Alberta NDP won't win government again unless it breaks out of its comfy northern fortress.
But the leadership race to replace Rachel Notley already is looking like loads of Edmonton, not much Calgary or anywhere else.
There could be four Edmonton MLAs in the race: Sarah Hoffman, Rakhi Pancholi, David Shepherd and Jodi Calahoo Stonehouse.
Only one Calgarian, Kathleen Ganley, seems set to run.
Nobody has formally announced yet.
Application packages were sent out Monday. The official campaign starts Feb. 5.
Last May showed the NDP won't win another election without sweeping Calgary, or having a break through in rural Alberta.
During the campaign, the party fell over itself making pitches to the city.
The central campaign office was in Calgary.
Notley made dozens of local appearances.
It almost worked. The NDP captured 14 of 26 seats in the city.
But the fatal mistake - promising to raise the business tax - showed a deep disconnect with a crucial Calgary voting bloc at that pivotal moment.
And Notley carried every single seat in Edmonton.
The question now is whether the Edmonton wing is so dominant, and the local voting members so abundant, that a geographic outsider simply can't win the leadership.
Several Edmonton candidates might split that city's vote, and a sole Calgary candidate - Ganley - could win.
There have been eight Alberta NDP leaders since 1962. The only leader from outside the capital was Rachel Notley's father, Grant, from northern Spirit River-Fairview.
With a leadership record like that, it's hard to convince people you're a real provincial party.
Since the 1960s - and even before - Alberta's conservative governments have been led by an array of pan-Alberta premiers.
Ernest Manning (Edmonton) was followed by Harry Strom (Cypress) as the final Socred leaders. Then came PC premiers Peter Lougheed (Calgary); Don Getty (Edmonton, and then Stettler); Ralph Klein (Calgary); Ed Stelmach (Fort Saskatchewan), Alison Redford (Calgary), Dave Hancock (Edmonton) and Jim Prentice (Calgary).
The UCP premiers have been Jason Kenney (Calgary) and Danielle Smith (Brooks- Medicine Hat).
The NDP needs to develop this kind of reach and variety. Falling back on another comfortable Edmontonian won't get the job done.
In the 2015 election campaign, Prentice offended many Albertans when he said: "Alberta is not an NDP province."
He lost, but he wasn't entirely wrong. Notley won with 40 per cent of the provincial vote because of the conservative split between PC and Wildrose.
Last year's strong results in Calgary have convinced many New Democrats the party is permanently on the edge of victory.
It's not. Without serious signs they're reaching out to the whole province, the NDP is doomed to stagnate.
A couple of Edmonton hopefuls told me Monday they can fully champion Calgary (and by implication, rural Alberta, too).
They try. The NDP launched the new Calgary Cancer Centre that was stalled by PC dithering. But moves like that, while welcome, often seemed like foreign aid.
The party did take two essential steps at its weekend meeting in Red Deer.
First, they abandoned the rule that gave up to 25 per cent of leadership votes to "official organizations," meaning unions.
In this race, the standard will be one member, one vote.
The party also will ignore its longtime rule saying members can't also hold cards in other parties.
The party's chief returning officer, Amanda Freistadt, says they will keep an eye out for hostiles who try to flood the party, but will assume others genuinely support the NDP.
Most of all, though, this party needs a leadership divorce from Edmonton. Don Braid's column appears regularly with Postmedia. X: @DonBraid

Subject: Leadership; Elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: Canada; Calgary Alberta Canada

People: Notley, Rachel

Identifier / keyword: alberta; government; again; unless; breaks; comfy

Title: NDP leadership race needs to find the ring road out of Edmonton

Publication title: Kingston Whig - Standard; Kingston, Ont.

First page: A.4

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Feb 1, 2024

Section: Opinion

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Kingston, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 11974397

Source type: Newspaper

Language of publication: English

Document 219 of 227

Student cap will hit schools' finances hard; Colleges, universities rely on international students to balance books

Author: Tumilty, Ryan

Publication info: Tumilty, Ryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/student-cap-will-hit-schools-finances-hard/docview/2918375339/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Student+cap+will+hit+schools%27+finances+hard%3B+Colleges%2C+universities+rely+on+international+students+to+balance+books&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Tumilty%2C+Ryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Liberals'cap on international students could hit some universities and colleges in Ontario particularly hard, as schools there have become reliant on the higher tuition fees those students pay to keep their books balanced.
Immigration Minister Marc Miller announced the cap on Monday, saying his department will issue no more than 360,000 new visas this year, 35 per cent lower than last year, and keep them at that number for two years.
The visas will be handed out proportionally to provinces based on their population, which will force even larger cuts at some schools in Ontario. Schools in the province have been aggressive about bringing in international students and the new cap could mean a 50 per cent reduction in the number of new students schools can admit. Miller was still working with provinces on specific allocations, but Ontario, British Columbia and Nova Scotia were expected to have to make the biggest reductions. Other provinces that were not as aggressive about recruiting could actually be able to increase the number of international students they bring in.
The federal government specifically exempted Masters and PhD students from the change, but otherwise will leave the decision on how to use allocations to provincial governments, forcing premiers to pick whether to send more international students or dollars to schools.
Mikal Skuterud, an economics professor at the University of Waterloo, said provinces that have to reduce their international student numbers should see which schools produce the best long-term financial outcomes for graduates, which is data the government already has.
"Avoid the politics, look at the data, let the data do the talking. I really would encourage them to do that."
Skuterud said the international student program became a way to bring in low-wage labour in many cases, which it never should have been. "The student program is not about plugging holes in low skilled labour markets, but that's kind of what it's become in a lot of ways."
He said the federal decision to lower the number of hours an international student can work, brought in last fall, should also help.
Alongside the labour market implications, provincial governments will have to decide how to handle the impact on universities and colleges, which, especially in Ontario, have become reliant on international student money.
Ontario's auditor general Bonnie Lysyk warned in 2022 that the provinces'institutions were becoming too reliant on international students to keep their finances in order.
"A high reliance on international student enrolment by universities poses risks outside of the Ministry's and the universities'control," she said Lysyk examined the system as a whole with detailed audits of some schools.
She found many Ontario schools relied heavily on international students to make their finances work. Algoma University in Sault Ste. Marie, Ont., for example has a satellite campus in Brampton where 90 per cent of students were international students. She found that the Brampton campus was responsible for half the school's enrolment and 65 per cent of its revenue.
After Laurentian University sought creditor protection, Lysyk examined that school's books and found they took a significant hit when Saudi Arabia pulled students from the school during a diplomatic dispute with Ottawa.
"Laurentian lost 130 students from Saudi Arabia. These students would have paid an estimated $3 million in tuition revenues and ancillary fees over the full length of their degrees," she said.
Steve Orsini, President and CEO of the Council of Ontario Universities said the cut to international students is coming at a time when universities are struggling with a tuition freeze and no new money coming from the provincial government.
"Before (Monday's) announcement, there were at least 10 universities facing sizable operating deficits, totalling $175 million this year and growing to $273 million next year," he said.
Orsini said universities have been more responsible about bringing in international students and called on the government to give them more time to adapt.
Ontario's Advanced Education Minister Jill Dunlop wasn't available for an interview, but in a statement following Miller's decision she said the province would focus on bad actors in the system.
"We know some bad actors are taking advantage of these students with false promises of guaranteed employment, residency, and Canadian citizenship. We've been engaging with the federal government on ways to crack down on these practices, like predatory recruitment."
In 2021, Lysyk raised the same concerns she had with the province's universities with the province's colleges, noting they were growing primarily to serve international students.
"Between 2012/13 and 2020/21, public colleges experienced a 15 per cent decline in domestic student enrolments but a 342 per cent growth in international student enrolments," she said.
In addition to bringing international students to their own campuses, some Ontario colleges started partnerships with private colleges where the college's curriculum was taught at private colleges with a licensing fee in place.
Students would attend the private colleges but receive a diploma from the public college if they graduated. Algonquin College in Ottawa operates such an arrangement with CDI college in North York and Mississauga, bringing in $4.3 million a year. Fanshawe College in London has a similar program and it notes enrolment was almost 20 per cent higher than the school expected in 2022.
In addition to the cap, Miller announced people taking part in these programs would no longer be able to claim a postgraduate work permit, making them much less desirable for international students.

Subject: Provinces; Tuition; Foreign students; Private colleges & universities; Private schools; College campuses; Labor market

Business indexing term: Subject: Labor market

Document 220 of 227

Falling into the 'population trap'

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/falling-into-population-trap/docview/2916267268/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Falling+into+the+%27population+trap%27&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's immigration policies are damaging the economy. That's the finding of Canada's top bank economists, who issued a dire warning to the federal government. By admitting 455,000 new permanent residents and more than 800,000 non-permanent residents last year, Canada is on the brink of recession, or worse.
"I'll put it bluntly: We've fallen into the population trap," said Stéfane Marion, chief economist at National Bank of Canada. An increase in the standard of living is no longer possible because "you don't have enough savings to stabilize your capital-to-labour ratio."
We also learned the government was warned two years ago by its own officials about high immigration causing a housing shortage - and ignored those warnings.
Asked about this, Housing Minister Sean Fraser replied, "I don't think anybody needs a briefing note to understand that having more people in the housing market impacts the housing market, but having gone through in consecutive years the process of building Canada's immigration levels plan, I can tell you that there are an enormous number of factors that go into them."
OK then, so what are those factors? Why did the government decide immigration levels should increase at this pace? Why does it continue to allow hundreds of thousands of foreign students to work unlimited hours? Why did it admit so many people into the country in such a short time? The classic answer is we need to increase the labour supply. Businesses are short of workers. Canadians are not having enough children to replace them. That's the argument of the Century Initiative, the business community and immigration advocates.
But simply pulling in adult immigrants and temporary workers is not the same as boosting fertility in the existing population. Those workers have needs that are very different from those of children.
They won't live under their parents'roof; they need their own housing. They won't be ferried about by their parents; they need independent transportation. They will be buying adult levels of food, clothing and other necessities. In short, they will be competing with other adults for basic needs, and that increased demand means two things: price inflation and resource scarcity. Which is exactly what Canada is now experiencing.
Fraser is right. You don't need a study to figure this out. It's simple common sense. So again, why did the government do this - especially after it was warned not to by its own bureaucrats? The cynic in me sees one reason: votes.
Immigrants tend to settle in cities. City dwellers tend to vote for "progressive" parties. In Canada, since the 1960s, that has translated to increased Liberal support. According to research done at Western University, a oneunit shift in the "urbanity score" for a riding produces an increase of eight percentage points in the Liberal party's vote share in that riding. No surprise then, that in the 2021 election, the Liberals won 109 of the 150 most urban ridings, while the Conservatives took only 23.
Immigrants also are grateful to the party that brings them in. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau learned this from his father. Pierre Elliott Trudeau took credit for the citizenship of millions of Canadians, even though many had come to Canada under previous governments. Why? Because they took their oath under his watch. And when it came to elections, the immigrant communities of the day - Italians, Greeks, Haitians, Vietnamese - tended to vote Liberal.
The implications of Justin Trudeau's replication of this policy are dire. Studies show they are turning Canadians against immigration: even 62 per cent of current immigrants think we're letting in too many people. They are impoverishing Canadians, both current and newcomer, according to the bank study. And they are also helping fuel Quebec separatism, as francophones look with alarm at rising immigrant populations in the rest of Canada. By the end of the century, Quebec risks becoming a bit player in Confederation, with only 15 per cent of its population.
This cannot continue. Instead of flooding the country with newcomers, the government needs to boost domestic productivity. That's a harder fix - and one that won't give them more votes. But then again, if Canadians can't afford a decent life, the Liberals may not get their votes, either. Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Canadians; Immigrants; Immigration

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Document 221 of 227

'A very different job'; Familiar face to Kingston now leads South Frontenac Fire and Rescue

Author: Crosier, Steph

Publication info: Crosier, Steph. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/very-different-job-familiar-face-kingston-now/docview/2913780665/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27A+very+different+job%27%3B+Familiar+face+to+Kingston+now+leads+South+Frontenac+Fire+and+Rescue&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-01-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Crosier%2C+Steph&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A familiar face in the Kingston emergency services community has been named fire chief and director of emergency services at South Frontenac Fire and Rescue.
Chief Del Blakney had his first day on the job Monday, and last week he was very excited to get started.
"Del is a strong leader who is well respected within the fire services community in the region and the province," South Frontenac Mayor Ron Vandewal said in a news release.
"His deep knowledge, experience and success at developing effective working relationships with multiple stakeholders will continue to make South Frontenac Fire and Rescue a leader in fire services."
Not only does he bring decades of experience to the job, he also hopes to bring a little bit of what he learned working for Kingston Fire and Rescue for the past nearly 17 years.
"It is a very different job, and there are new challenges, so I'm really excited about that," Blakney said in an interview with the Whig-Standard.
Blakney has been working in some form of emergency services since his high school days, when he completed a co-op with the Kingston Township Fire Department and another at Cataraqui Town Centre working in security with the Cadillac Fairview real estate company.
After graduation, Blakney continued with Cadillac Fairview and eventually became the national manager of operations security services in Toronto. He managed 106 buildings across Canada, but with a young family, he decided to step away from all the travelling the job entailed in June 2001.
He never stepped away from firefighting, though. Throughout his time with Cadiallac Fairview and afterwards, Blakney worked as a volunteer firefighter for 15 years, working his way up to the rank of captain at the Woodbine station. The day he was promoted was the day the 1998 ice storm hit Kingston.
"It was like nothing I've ever experienced since," Blakney said.
He and his peers slept at the station for three days, and he recalled just driving around from one emergency to another. He'd written them down on a sheet of paper and, at the peak, he had 11 to respond to at once. He had to prioritize them and just go one step at a time.
"It was a very interesting night," Blakney said. "It was quite the introduction to my new role."
The ice storm reinforced the importance of staying calm in the thick of an emergency.
"The more that came at me, the calmer I got," Blakney said. "I had to keep my composure, keep my calm and just keep accomplishing what I can accomplish with the team."
In February 2006, he joined Kingston Fire and Rescue as a fulltime fire inspector. Since then, he also became a certified company officer, training officer, firefighter, public information officer, public educator and fire investigator.
Blakney is also a director for the Ontario Municipal Fire Prevention Officers Association. In the role for the past four years, the news release said he has been "working with the Office of the Fire Marshal and Emergency Management, Ontario Association of Fire Chiefs, Health Canada and over 230 member fire departments across Ontario to evaluate and direct the delivery of fire protection services in the province."
When Blakney first started full time with Kingston Fire and Rescue, provincial legislation wouldn't allow him to also work as a volunteer firefighter. That legislation changed, and just three years after moving his home to South Frontenac, Blakney signed up to be a volunteer firefighter of South Frontenac Fire and Rescue five years ago.
"I really missed the firefighting aspect," Blakney said of his decision to work as a volunteer firefighter.
"We had several excellent internal and external candidates but were very lucky to have someone like Del, with his vast expertise and knowledge within our own organization, to become our new fire chief," Louise Fragnito, chief administrative officer with the Township of South Frontenac, said. "I think it speaks to the talent of the people we have here in South Frontenac."
He said he will miss his role as a fire prevention officer with Kingston Fire and Rescue and working with the community. He said he was especially passionate about going into Kingston's more than 50 schools and sharing education with students from kindergarten to Grade 8.
All his work as a volunteer will help Blakney transition to chief as South Frontenac Fire and Rescue is made up entirely of 110 volunteers. The only full-time positions are chief, deputy chief, an administrative assistant and a contracted fire prevention officer/firefighter.
Blakney said running a department made up 100 per cent of volunteer firefighters is more challenging than a department with a mix of full-time and volunteer crews.
"It's trying to have adequate staffing available and then they need to have the same level of training on firefighting, auto-extrication, water rescues, and things like that," Blakney said. "Now you have to have people who are able to give up their own time to do training, whereas it is not your full-time job to get paid and do training."
Blakney added it is hard to attract volunteer firefighters right now. The Great Canadian Volunteer Firefighter Census 2021, completed by the Canadian Association of Fire Chiefs, estimated there were about 99,919 volunteer fire fighters, 21 per cent fewer than the previous census in 2016. The census also found 32 per cent of volunteers were over the age of 50.
In an effort to retain and attract volunteers, the Canadian Association of Fire Chiefs is working alongside NDP MP Gord Johns of Courtenay-Alberni in British Columbia to increase the federal tax credit from $3,000 to $10,000 for 200 hours.
"Staff is an ongoing issue for most rural fire departments," Blakney said. "Trying to attract good talent who are committed to the community."
Blakney knew his first assignment as chief before he even started: complete a community risk assessment. Township of South Frontenac has established the assessment as a top priority, which is good timing because the province has mandated all fire departments to have an assessment completed by the end of July of this year.
"That's a really excellent opportunity for me going in that it hasn't actually been done, so I get an opportunity to, you know, participate and facilitate that," Blakney said. "Completing that community risk assessment will give me a very, very clear understanding of what the risk is to the community and what the service levels would need to be to mitigate that risk."
From there, Blakney and his staff will conduct a "gap analysis" to present recommendations for improvements to council.
As a resident of South Frontenac, Blakney said he was already invested in the township's strategic planning. Now he gets to help execute it.
"I like the direction the township is taking," Blakney said. "They want to have data-driven, effective emergency fire services, and that's kind of, you know, right up the alley of things that I'm passionate about as well. So making informed decisions. That's why I like the risk assessment, because it really looks at what are the needs in the community and are you addressing those." scrosier@postmedia.com twitter.com/StephattheWhig

Subject: Fire stations; Firefighters; Fire departments; Censuses; Fire prevention; Emergency services; Volunteers; Evacuations & rescues; Risk assessment

Document 222 of 227

Gaza tweet tests university's free-speech policy

Author: Dawson, Tyler

Publication info: Dawson, Tyler. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/gaza-tweet-tests-universitys-free-speech-policy/docview/2913780587/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Gaza+tweet+tests+university%27s+free-speech+policy&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-01-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Dawson%2C+Tyler&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Wilfrid Laurier University, which has adopted a free speech policy that allows for "difficult, controversial, extreme, or even wrong-headed" ideas on campus, has seemingly not taken any action to discipline a professor who called for the support of Palestinians on the morning of the Oct. 7 massacre in Israel.
Jessica Hutchison, who focuses "on researching and disrupting racism, settler colonialism and gendered state violence," particularly in prisons, tweeted that day about land acknowledgments - now often standard practice before official events - saying that they should "include support for Palestinians who are taking their land back from settler colonizers."
Critics took that as an endorsement of the Hamas terrorists who crossed Israel's border with Gaza on Oct. 7, killing 1,200 Israelis and kidnapping 240 others.
Hutchison subsequently locked her Twitter account.
John Findlay, who's studying criminology at Wilfrid Laurier University, wrote in late October to the Office of Human Rights and Conflict Management that he was "profoundly and seriously disturbed by the post."
"Professor Hutchinson's post cannot be justified as a legitimate exercise of freedom of speech or academic discourse. It violates an inviolable Canadian principle - that the murder of innocent people cannot be tolerated or promoted," Findlay wrote.
In the letter, he argues that Hutchison's post was antisemitic and discriminatory, shows a lack of empathy for victims of the violence, may constitute participating in terrorism, encourages violence and dehumanizes Israelis.
"I would call upon Professor Hutchinson to disavow her post with a clear and unambiguous apology. I would further expect the university to publicly condemn the post in the strongest language," Findlay wrote.
In an interview, Findlay said the university has yet to respond.
"Basically, I've been told nothing," he said.
After Postmedia made multiple requests for comment, Vanessa Barrasa, the university's director of communications and issues management, said "Laurier is not commenting on this matter."
So far, it does not appear that Wilfrid Laurier University acceded to Findlay's demands. An email chain obtained by the National Post between Findlay and the university says "there will be no further action from this office with regard to the issues you have raised."
On Oct. 12, more than a week before Findlay sent his letter to the human rights office, he wrote to university president Deborah MacLatchy, asking whether there would be a public response to Hutchison's post.
"As an institute of higher learning, Laurier supports the expression of a range of perspectives, including those that may be deemed difficult, controversial, or extreme," said a reply from the president's office. "At the same time, it's important to note that the opinions of individual members of the Laurier community do not necessarily reflect the views and values of the university."
A further email to Findlay, this time from the Office of Student Affairs, reiterated the university's commitment to free expression, but also said "we strongly condemn acts of antisemitism and Islamophobia and have made it clear to our community we have zero tolerance for hate speech, racism, threats, and harassment."
Hutchison, who is teaching a course on social policy and activism in the winter 2024 term, did not return the Post's request for comment.
It is unclear whether Hutchison was spoken to formally or informally or why her tweet would not fall - if indeed it does not - under the university's harassment and discrimination policies. Findlay has yet to make a formal discrimination complaint about Hutchison's tweet.
"They've discovered freedom of speech," Findlay said, wryly. "Free speech has its limits. You can't call for the invocation of murder against somebody. That's not free speech."
The use of the term "settler" to refer to Israelis has been controversial. When founded in 1949, Israel was situated on the Biblical homeland of the Jewish people, which was around two-thirds Palestinian. Jewish immigration had increased considerably after the First World War, leading to conflict between Zionists and the Arab population.
For some Palestinians, the land Israel stands upon is their homeland; several hundred thousand were expelled from their homes in Israel's early years, in what Palestinians call the Nakba. Israelis, meanwhile, argue that it is their historic homeland. Max Nordau, one of the founders of the Zionist Organization, who died more than two decades before Israel became a reality, wrote in The Arabs and Us that Jews had a right to their "legal and historical inheritance, the land of their fathers, of which they were robbed 1,900 years ago by the Roman aggressors."
Today, roughly one-quarter of Israeli citizens were born in other countries but have since settled in Israel, moving to the country under the auspices of the 1950 Law of Return.
In 2018, Doug Ford's government required Ontario universities to adopt stricter policies to protect free speech on campus, similar to the Chicago Principles, which state that institutions of higher education "should not attempt to shield students from ideas or opinions that they disagree with or find offensive."
Wilfrid Laurier University adopted such a policy in 2019.
"The university supports the expression, testing, and challenging of a range of perspectives and ideas, including those that may be deemed difficult, controversial, extreme, or even wrong-headed," its free speech statement says.

Subject: Antisemitism; Violence; Zionism; Murders & murder attempts; Human rights; College campuses; Palestinian people; Freedom of speech

Document 223 of 227

Federal parties haunted by elections past

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/federal-parties-haunted-elections-past/docview/2913263675/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Federal+parties+haunted+by+elections+past&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-01-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Settle in, voters of Canada: the next federal election likely won't be until 2025. That's an eon away in political terms, and anything - literally anything - can happen in that time. If you had said in January 2023 that one year later, the Middle East would be a war zone, the federal government would call a foreign interference inquiry and the Conservatives would be trouncing the Liberals at the polls, most people would have called you crazy - or at least hoped you were wrong about the first one.
That said, there are a few certainties all three major federal parties will face in the lead-up to the next vote. And we got a taste of them in the past two weeks, a reminder that ghosts of elections past are always hanging around, ready to haunt you in elections future if you're not careful.
Let's start with the Liberals. Their Achilles heel is elitism, making them easy to brand as out of touch with the average Canadian. That elitism was on full display over Christmas when Prime Minister Justin Trudeau jetted off to Jamaica for a family vacation. And not just any vacation: this wasn't a Beaches or Sandals-level trip, but a $9,300-a-night epic getaway at a posh resort, gifted by owner and family friend Peter Green. Total tab: $84,000.
To put that into perspective, that same $84,000, if you had it lying around, would qualify you to buy a $660,000 Toronto condo at current mortgage rates - if your household income was $150,000 or more. The median Toronto household earns just over half that, according to the last census. In other words, for the price of Trudeau's holiday, they can't even afford to buy a home - a fact the Conservatives will likely point out at every opportunity.
But the Tories need to be careful, too.
Their Achilles heel is Uncle Marvin. As in your crazy Uncle Marvin, who trots out his collection of tin foil hats every time the family gets together for Thanksgiving. Uncle Marvin says a lot of nutty things, and a lot of not-so-nice things, too. He rants about gay people burning in lakes of fire and Jews running the world. He has surfaced in various political campaigns throughout the ages, and it has never ended well.
Uncle Marvin came out of the woodwork this week, in the form of a petition sponsored by Conservative MP Leslyn Lewis. The petition asks for Canada to quit the United Nations, and is the brainchild of a man named Doug Porter, who told the left-leaning website Press Progress that Lewis's staff helped him with the wording. Porter also said that if the UN gets what it wants, "It would be the elimination of a majority of the population and those that would remain would be slaves, and they would be repeatedly injected with mRNA injections to change their DNA so they lose their humanity and they are different creatures." Liberal attack ads, start your engines.
As for the NDP, their biggest issue is trust.
By 2025, they will have been propping up this government for a full four years. So when Leader Jagmeet Singh recently said, "that's off the table" next time around, it's kind of hard to believe him.
Of course, Singh's pension will have vested by then, so perhaps there is less risk in having the government fall, especially when his own party only gave him an 81 per cent approval rating at its last national convention.
Cynical? Yes. But this is politics, and cynicism is par for the course. The NDP will also need to keep its radical wing in check if it is to stop the labour vote from migrating to the Conservatives. This is where issues like the Hamas-Israel war come into play - if union voters feel the NDP has become too "progressive" for their tastes on issues like Palestinian statehood, they may tune out, and tune into the Tories'strong economic message instead.
Ultimately, the next election will be decided in the pocketbook. If the economy turns around, the Liberals could see a boost - but they will still need other issues to stay in power. So watch for those ghosts to keep haunting us throughout 2024 - and into the next new year. Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Document 224 of 227

Scott Moe tops in scaring, upsetting other Canadians

Author: Staples, David

Publication info: Staples, David. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/scott-moe-tops-scaring-upsetting-other-canadians/docview/2912140999/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Scott+Moe+tops+in+scaring%2C+upsetting+other+Canadians&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Staples%2C+David&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith no longer dominates the list of craziest conservatives in the imagination of other Canadians. There's a new contender, Premier Scott Moe of Saskatchewan, who is now scaring and upsetting many residents of Ontario, British Columbia, Quebec and the Maritimes with his refusal to collect Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's carbon tax on home heating and his recent use of the notwithstanding clause to ensure parents know if their child is taking on a new sexual identity at school.
I put this matter to you for two reasons.
First, I'm curious about how it all will play out politically and, second, because it's so unusual. I can't recall a time when it came to pushing a conservative agenda that Alberta and its leaders weren't front and centre.
Did anyone expect Smith - the raucous Wildroser, the fearless, even reckless radio talk show host, the vaccine-hesitant, Truckers Convoy-boosting, Sovereignty Act-thumping, Canada Pension Plan-bashing leader of Alberta's conservative movement - would shy from being Canada's most outspoken conservative leader? But that's what we're seeing. In October, Moe charged into the trans issue, bringing in legislation and invoking the notwithstanding clause to prevent children under 16 from changing their names or pronouns at school without parental consent.
Polls show support in the 60 to 70 per cent range from Canadians for parents knowing if their child has changed his or her pronouns at school. Trudeau seemingly conflated such support with hatred of trans people and other gender minorities. It blew up into an ugly wedge issue, but Smith kept out of it.
When cornered and asked if Alberta would follow Moe's lead, Smith said her party was monitoring the issue but had no plans to bring in legislation. Smith expressed compassion for the adults involved and also for children grappling with puberty and gender identity. "It's been my hope from the beginning that we wouldn't politicize this," she said.
Thus ended any major political uproar in Alberta on this issue.
On the carbon tax, when the Trudeau Liberals exempted home heating oil commonly used in the Maritimes, Smith and Moe asked Trudeau to do the same with natural gas home heating. When he refused, however, Moe had his province no longer collect the tax on home heating.
Globe and Mail columnist Andrew Coyne, a stalwart voice in the Eastern Canada establishment, now alleges Moe's action is pushing Canada to the brink. "We have reached, in short, a dangerous turn in the current epidemic of provincial lawlessness."
I'll suggest we reached a dangerous turn in the epidemic of federal lawlessness long ago, with the Trudeau Liberal power grab on assessing industrial projects and their bizarre law declaring all plastic toxic, two matters our courts have now deemed examples of illegal federal overreach.
Smith has spoken out in harsh terms against the feds'ongoing unlawful aggression, but she's not going so far as Moe in acting.
Is there some method to her lack of madness? Smith's only concern should be Alberta's success. That happens when Alberta wins major court cases against the Trudeau Liberals, but with a lot less heartache, disruption and bother when there's a Conservative government with heavy Alberta representation in Ottawa, one that doesn't bring in dark measures destructive to Alberta's success.
The less out-of-step Alberta is with most Canadians, the more likely we'll see such a government in Ottawa. That will mean other Canadians recognizing the wisdom of our agenda, but also that we take care to pay some small respect to their values and policies.
The more other Canadians are persuaded that a strong Alberta, free to produce oil and gas wealth to share with all regions, is in their best interest, the freer Alberta will be to build Canada's collective prosperity.
Essentially, if the Trudeau Liberals can paint Smith as crazy and Alberta as dangerous, they win.
Alberta can't bear such a defeat. The stakes are too high. Another Trudeau-Singh government will mean the full enactment of Trudeau's chaos-inducing, job-killing energy policies. Perhaps it's best if Smith folds now, presents Alberta's case as reasonably as possible, and backs away from fighting every small battle to win the greater war. dstaples@postmedia.com

Full text: 2: An Indigenous food sovereignty garden is this year's recipient of a grant from the Kingston Community Climate Action Fund. All Our Relations Land Trust operates a one-hectare garden on Highway 15 that includes a traditional food garden, medicine gardens, a pollinator garden and little forests. 5: Kingston and the Islands MPP Ted Hsu's bid to become leader of the Ontario Liberal Party ended on Saturday when he was eliminated in the first round of the party's 2023 leadership election vote.
5: A Central Frontenac man has been charged with first-degree murder in connection with the disappearance of a 59-year-old man in November. 7: City council is staying with the recommended strategy for population growth, housing and employment. Council voted for a medium-growth strategy that could see the Kingston area's total population grow to more than 220,000 by 2051. 7: A judge of the Ontario Superior Court of Justice has dismissed former MPP Randy Hillier's Charter of Rights and Freedoms challenge against Reopening Ontario Act charges laid against him in the spring of 2021.
8: After dropping during the first part of the pandemic, greenhouse gas emissions in Kingston bounced back in 2022, according to a report to city council. Overall community emissions increased by more than 42,000 tonnes - four per cent - in 2022 from 2021 levels. The 2022 emissions were still 3.5 per cent below 2019 levels.
8: The man accused of arson in the case of two suspicious fires in the Napanee area has been denied bail. Jay Bradley received the decision from justice of the peace Leona Dombrowsky on Thursday afternoon when he appeared virtually in Napanee bail court. His show of cause bail hearing began on Monday, with the justice reserving her decision until Dec. 7. 9: Kingston Police spent less than half of what they did to supervise mass gatherings in the neighbourhoods around Queen's University in 2023 than in 2022, while significantly increasing the number of fines doled out.
12: A number of local volunteers making a difference across Ontario have been recognized by the provincial government. The crisis and support volunteers of the Sexual Assault Centre Kingston and Ida Gavlas of Stella are two of just 14 individuals and two organizations that have been chosen to receive the 2023 June Callwood Outstanding Achievement Award for Voluntarism.
12: Three regional boards of health have been in discussions to investigate the potential to merge into one cohesive organization. Kingston, Frontenac and Lennox and Addington Public Health; Leeds, Grenville and Lanark District Health Unit; and the Hastings Prince Edward Public Health are examining whether a merger would be feasible.
14: An early design for a rural transit system includes bus routes between Kingston and Gananoque, Sydenham, Verona, Odessa and Bath. A draft proposal was presented to the city's environment, infrastructure and transportation policies committee Wednesday night.
16: A transport truck driver has been sentenced to nine months in jail and one year of probation after striking and killing a Sharbot Lake man with his vehicle, fleeing the scene and then trying to disguise the damage caused by the collision by posing a second one in January 2020. 16: Members and officials from the Napanee and District Curling Club gathered inside the 63-year-old building on York Street in Napanee on Friday to give thanks to the first responders who saved the building from a monstrous fire that destroyed the town's old rink in October.
19: Kingston Police Services Board has approved a proposed 2024 budget that will ask city council for a 6.1 per cent increase for the force, which would include extra cash to cover the costs of policing mass gatherings around Queen's University.
21: Scott Fraser was named chief of Kingston Police on Tuesday, a role he did not anticipate taking when he first came to Kingston from Brockville to become deputy chief in October 2021. 22: The early weeks of 2024 are to begin with some unfinished business from 2022. At the end of January, a planning appeal tribunal hearing is scheduled to decide the fate of the former Davis Tannery site.
23: Just as housing was the top city priority in 2023, so it will be in 2024, Mayor Bryan Paterson said. Speaking after the last council meeting of the year, Paterson said the need to build more housing has almost eclipsed other priorities. Date represents day story appeared in newspaper.

Document 226 of 227

New year, same inept leadership

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-year-same-inept-leadership/docview/2910030915/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+year%2C+same+inept+leadership&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-01-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The year is only a few days old, yet it feels like we're stuck in 2023. Russia remains at war with Ukraine. Hamas is still at war with Israel. China is meddling in Taiwan's election. Artificial intelligence is eating our jobs. Social media are filled with rage about inflation, Palestine, trans rights and Donald Trump. It's enough to make you hide under the covers, shut off your news feeds and watch cat videos until you fall into a fretful sleep.
Here in Canada, it also feels as though, despite the new year, we're grappling with the same problems, and no idea how to tackle them. In fact, our federal government's contradictory policies are making things worse.
Inflation continues to erode our standard of living, yet carbon tax increases will roll ahead on April 1. Housing remains in short supply, yet immigration levels are at historic highs. Geopolitical strife has increased, yet our military is short 16,000 personnel and if Canada were at war, we'd only have three days worth of ammunition to defend ourselves. Cue the cat videos, please.
In his year-end interviews, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau acknowledged Canada's military is woefully short on supplies, but blamed the situation on our contributions to Ukraine. On the carbon tax, he confirmed he will make no further exemptions beyond those announced for home heating oil.
As for immigration, while Trudeau acknowledged that, "We all bear some responsibility; this is a challenge that we have to work on all together," he added that it isn't the 500,000 new immigrants who are the problem: "It's the temporary immigrants that have spiked massively over the past couple of years that is putting so much pressure on the whole system."
Fair enough, but all these phenomena have happened under his watch. Eight years and three mandates have given the Liberals plenty of opportunity to assess the effects of their policies, and change course if things get worse instead of better. So given the mess we're in today, does Trudeau plan to change his tune? Apparently not. "This is exactly not the time to be slowing down," Trudeau told Global News. "The context we're in right now - where progress has become so fragile because of global and large macro events - is the time to be doubling down and rolling up our sleeves, and that's what I'm here for."
Actually, you're not here for that. As Albert Einstein purportedly quipped, "The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results." Canadians'mental health is bad enough; we don't need more nuttiness from Ottawa.
The government needs to start making the hard choices - the ones it doesn't want to make because they are politically unpalatable to its key voter bases, such as the progressive left and immigrant communities.
It should start by repealing the carbon tax to ease the pressure on Canadians' pocketbooks. It should not merely threaten to cap student visas to dubious diploma mills, but actually do so, and stop allowing these students to be used as cheap labour by working more than 20 hours a week off campus. It should increase military spending on not just hardware, but also on incentives for recruitment and support for military personnel, to make a career in the Armed Forces an attractive option for young people.
The irony is these policies may help the Liberals at the polls, not hurt them. Polls show a majority of voters in Liberal strongholds like Atlantic Canada want more carbon tax carveouts.
Sixty-two per cent of Canadians born outside of the country say immigration levels are too high. And two-thirds of Canadians - including 68 per cent in vote-rich Ontario - support increasing military spending to meet NATO's two per cent target.
This feels like a watershed year for many issues in Canada and around the world. Instead of doubling down, Trudeau should wise up. If these problems continue to fester into 2025, Canadians will roll up their sleeves and show him the door. Postmedia News Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

September & october; Year in review 2023
