Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=September+%26amp%3B+october%3B+Year+in+review+2023&amp;title=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;issn=11974397&amp;date=2024-01-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Kingston+Whig+-+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: SEPTEMBER 1: The city has not yet made a decision about what will happen to the statue of Sir John A. Macdonald, removed from City Park more than two years ago. The statue has been in storage since city council voted 12-1 in favour of its removal, and workers used a crane to lift it off its pedestal.
2: When Bhavana Varma stepped down from her longtime role as the head of the United Way of Kingston, Frontenac, Lennox and Addington at the end of August, she left it a transformed organization. When she arrived in Kingston 24 years ago, she took over leadership of an agency that was struggling to reach its annual fundraising target and lacked the trust of the community it served. Within a year, Varma and a new board of directors turned that around, creating what is now one of the most successful United Way branches in Canada. 5: Gregg Rosen, 64, a respected Kingston-area businessman and community leader, died in a boating accident on the St. Lawrence River east of Kingston.
The president of Kingston's Kimco Steel Sales Ltd. and a resident of Leeds and the Thousand Islands Township, Rosen was a father of three and an avid boat racer who is being mourned by his family, his local community and members of the boat racing world.
7: The city's plans for environmental remediation and brownfield cleanups may be delayed because of the need to make emergency repairs to Belle Park infrastructure. City council approved $1.5 million in emergency repairs to the former landfill property's groundwater management system that was vandalized about a month ago.
8: City council voted to begin planning the transition to new automated garbage trucks that will be operated by a person who will both drive the vehicle and also use a mechanical arm to pick up the garbage bins and empty them into the truck.
9: Volunteers at Sandy Pine Wildlife Centre in Napanee are busy tending to the last of the turtle hatchlings in their care. The turtle hatching season is coming to an end and volunteers are incubating the last few eggs they have and preparing the new hatchlings for release.
12: After getting off to a rocky start, Kingston-area Rotary clubs have wrapped up their annual nut drive fundraiser thanks to a flood of community support.
The fundraiser has taken place since 1965 in partnership with Queen's University engineering students.
The event was cancelled during its launch event last week on the Queen's campus when only 20 students of the needed 200 stepped forward to help with the door-to-door sales of chocolate bars, leaving Rotary partners with 300 cases of Laura Secord chocolate bars.
With community support, however, all 300 cases sold in just three days.
14: Kingston's two French-language secondary schools are now united under one roof. The Conseil des écoles publiques de l'est de l'ontario's Mille-iles public secondary school and the Conseil des écoles catholiques du Centre-est's Sainte-marie-rivier secondary school officially opened their new shared building on Wheathill Road.
19: Kingston and the Islands MP Mark Gerretsen was appointed deputy government House leader, a role that will see him work with government House leader MP Karina Gould and the government whip to manage the government's operations.
21: Protests over the teaching of 2SLGBTQIA+ in schools unfolded, drawing the attention of the local school board and member of provincial parliament.
The Slow Roll 1 Million March 4 Children rolled into the city, protesting the teaching of 2SLGBTQIA+ issues in schools, even encouraging parents to keep their children home from school. Across Ontario Street from City Hall, a counterprotest on behalf of Kingston's 2SLGBTQIA+ community was held. 21: An agreement has been reached between a developer of a proposed building at the intersection of Barrie and Queen streets and a neighbourhood community group.
Among the terms of the agreement, which city council approved, Podium Developments agreed to lower the building by one floor, from 15 storeys to 14 storeys. 22: Kingston goaltender Amanda Leveille, 29, is still taking it all in after being selected by Minnesota in the very first Professional Women's Hockey League draft. Leveille, a graduate of Frontenac Secondary School who grew up playing both girls and boys hockey in the city, was drafted in the 11th round (61st overall) by the league's Minnesota team. 23: Kingston's long-running blues festival has sung its last song. The Limestone City Blues Festival, a staple of the summer season for 26 years, will not return for a 2024 edition.
The Downtown Kingston Business Improvement Area, the group that put on the festival, announced its end with a commitment to create a new event in its place. 29: Regiopolis-Notre Dame Catholic High School's sports field is getting a $1.3-million facelift that will be ready for the fall 2024 sports season. Those driving by the school, especially on the very busy Division Street, would have noticed the construction.
The project, funded by the Ministry of Education, includes a new grass field and sprinkler system, a new asphalt track and a new fence.
OCTOBER 3: It's a sunny Saturday morning along Bath Road and the parking lot is packed at Ramekins. The only difference from almost any other sunny Saturday the last quarter-century-plus is that this time, it will be the last.
Joe de Couto's establishment, known for casual dining, fine cuisine and much more, closed its dining room for the final time Saturday night, opting to only offer takeout, catering and to continue its Heat and Eat Meals program going forward. 5: Food trucks will be limited to the Queen's University campus during late night Homecoming festivities.
Council amended a motion that would have seen food trucks allowed to remain open until 2 a.m. and set up in neighbourhoods around the campus. 5: A Kingston-area organization is receiving a $98,000 grant via the Government of Canada's Community Services Recovery Fund to provide assistance to women affected by the COVID-19 pandemic. Girls Incorporated of Limestone, Algonquin and Lakeshore, also referred to as Girls Inc. Limestone, provides programs and support for girls and women in Frontenac, Lennox and Addington, Hastings and Prince Edward counties. 7: Some four decades later, it's the final act for Audrey's Costume Castle & Dancewear, as owner Scott Perrett, who took over the family business with his sister back in the '90s, is frantically rifling through decades of merchandise and fixtures in hopes of selling them off before he must vacate the longtime retail store for good.
10: After spending some time out in the country, the Kinsmen Club of Kingston Foundation has brought its Dream Home back to the city.
12: Habitat for Humanity Kingston Limestone Region is preparing to move four nearly completed tiny homes to their final location in the city's Rideau Heights neighbourhood next week.
12: Gananoque Police Service and Ontario Provincial Police Biker Enforcement Unit are reminding residents that the Outlaw Motorcycle Gang is considered a criminal organization ahead of its expected arrival in the small town on Friday. 13: Kingston Police are recognizing a local scrap metal business after it helped to solve a case of theft in Loyalist Township.
Police said in a news release that ANS Scrap Metals, located on McAdoo's Lane, reported suspicious activity to Kingston Police that led them to help solve a case of stolen aluminum stadium benches from the J.
Earl Burt Memorial Park Ball Diamond in Wilton last Thursday. 17: The company behind what is set to be a first-of-its-kind electric vehicle battery manufacturing facility has officially committed $2.1 billion to its construction. Quite something consideringthat when Umicore chief executive officer Mathia Miedreich and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau first announced the project, it was expected to cost roughly $1.5 billion.
17: Ontario Premier Doug Ford stopped by Kingston Health Sciences Centre on Monday morning to tour its Kingston General Hospital site.
Dr. David Pichora, KHSC president and chief executive officer, extended the invitation to the premier in 2022 after Ford extended thanks to KHSC for its role in treating an influx of young children suffering from serious respiratory illnesses during last year's respiratory virus season. 19: Frontenac Paramedics say nearly every one of their medics has experienced violence while working, and 18 per cent report being sexually assaulted. 19: After an emotional discussion, city. council voted unanimously to declare intimate partner violence an epidemic in the city.
The declaration came out of inquiries into mass killings in Eastern Ontario and Nova Scotia. 20: Jim Jefferies saw more of the city that most touring comedians when her performed here last month. The morning after his Sept. 22 show, the Australian comedian had another gig, this time in the emergency room at Kingston Health Sciences Centre. The interview was posted on the Jimmy Kimmel Live YouTube channel.
20: Representatives from Queen's University's Alma Mater Society appeared before a critical Kingston Police Services Board Thursday afternoon to request officers stop issuing Part 1 Court Summons for provincial offences when the University District Safety Initiative is not in place
. 21: During an event fit for a multibillion-dollar announcement complete with champagne and live jazz music, Umicore announced that the first customers for its electric vehicle battery components would be the Automotive Energy Supply Corporation and the BMW Group.
24: Ontario Provincial Police are investigating two suspicious fires that took place in the Napanee area on Monday and are asking for anyone with information or video footage to contact them. A fire in the early hours of Monday morning destroyed much of the Lennox Agricultural Memorial Community Centre in Napanee. Firefighters also responded to a fire at a residence in Stone Mills early that morning, which has been confirmed as the home of Lennox Agricultural Society president Carol McKinley.
27: The SS Keewatin finished its final voyage across open water Thursday, tying up at its new home at the Great Lakes Museum Thursday afternoon.
27: Troy Mann is being pulled in every direction at the Leon's Centre on a Wednesday afternoon, some 24 hours after he was announced as the 22nd head coach in Kingston major junior hockey history. 31: Kingston's most well-known philanthropist, lawyer and business leader has died. Arthur Britton (Brit) Smith, whose success in the residential rental sector put him in a position to donate millions of dollars to local charities and educational institutions, died Saturday. He was 103. Date represents day story appeared in newspaper.
